topics.title: Solar Thermal Collectors - Flat Plate Collectors - Solar Concentrators (Parabolic Trough, Parabolic Dish, Central Tower Collector) - Solar Air Heaters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT862 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solar Thermal Collectors - Flat Plate Collectors - Solar Concentrators (Parabolic Trough, Parabolic Dish, Central Tower Collector) - Solar Air Heaters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Solar Thermal Collectors — The Engineering Definition

A **Solar Thermal Collector** is a heat exchanger specifically engineered to absorb incident solar radiation, convert it into thermal energy (heat), and transfer this energy to a working fluid (air, water, oil, or molten salt) without converting it to electricity. The fundamental performance metric is the **Concentration Ratio (C)**, defined as the ratio of the effective absorber area to the receiver/aperture area through which useful energy is extracted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Solar thermal collectors are broadly classified into **Non-Concentrating (C = 1)** and **Concentrating (C > 1)** types. The choice dictates the achievable temperature, tracking requirement, and cost.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Greenhouse Bucket" Model

Imagine a black painted bucket placed in sunlight with a glass lid on top. Sunlight enters through the glass, hits the black interior, gets converted to heat, but the heat cannot easily escape back through the glass (greenhouse effect). The water inside warms up. A **Flat Plate Collector** is essentially this bucket scaled up to industrial dimensions — a blackened absorber plate beneath a glass cover. A **Parabolic Trough** is like bending that bucket into a curved mirror shape so sunlight focuses onto a small tube — like a child using a magnifying glass to focus sun rays onto a leaf.

### 1.3 Classification Hierarchy

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification of Solar Thermal Collectors:**
> - **Non-Concentrating (Stationary):**
>   - Flat Plate Collector (FPC)
>   - Evacuated Tube Collector (ETC)
>   - Solar Air Heater
> - **Concentrating (Tracking Required):**
>   - Line-Focus: Parabolic Trough, Compound Parabolic Concentrator (CPC), Linear Fresnel
>   - Point-Focus: Parabolic Dish, Central Receiver (Power Tower), Heliostat Field

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Solar Constant (I_sc) = 1367 W/m²** (extraterrestrial radiation at top of atmosphere)
- **Air Mass 1.5 (AM1.5) Reference = 1000 W/m²** (standard terrestrial testing condition)
- **Stefan-Boltzmann Constant (σ) = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴**
- **Thermal Efficiency Range:** FPC (30–50%), Parabolic Trough (60–70%), Solar Tower (35–45% net)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Flat Plate Collector (FPC) — Deep Analysis

#### 2.1.1 Constructional Components
The FPC consists of six functionally distinct layers arranged in a precisely engineered sandwich:

1. **Transparent Glass Cover(s):** Low-iron tempered glass with transmissivity (τ) ≈ 0.91, reduces convective and radiative losses.
2. **Air Gap (typically 25–40 mm):** Suppresses convection between cover and absorber.
3. **Absorber Plate:** Metallic (copper/aluminum) with high absorptivity (α ≈ 0.95) and low emissivity (ε ≈ 0.05–0.15). Often coated with **Black Chrome** or **Selective Absorber Coating (SAC)** such as Titanium Nitride-Oxide (TiNOx).
4. **Fluid Tubes/Risers:** Bonded to absorber plate carrying the heat transfer fluid (HTF).
5. **Insulation (Back & Sides):** Polyurethane foam or rock wool with thickness 50–100 mm, thermal conductivity k ≤ 0.04 W/m·K.
6. **Casing/Housing:** Galvanized iron or aluminum structural frame.

#### 2.1.2 The Hottel-Whillier-Bliss Equation (KTU High-Yield)

The thermal performance of an FPC is governed by the **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation**, which relates the **Useful Heat Gain (Q_u)** to solar radiation, fluid inlet/outlet temperatures, and ambient temperature.

$$Q_u = A_c \cdot F_R \left[ S - U_L(T_{fi} - T_a) \right]$$

Where:
- $A_c$ = Collector aperture area (m²)
- $F_R$ = Heat Removal Factor (dimensionless, typically 0.7–0.9)
- $S$ = Absorbed radiation per unit area = $(\tau \alpha)_e \cdot I_t$ (W/m²)
- $(\tau \alpha)_e$ = Effective transmittance-absorptance product
- $I_t$ = Total incident solar radiation on tilted surface (W/m²)
- $U_L$ = Overall heat loss coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $T_{fi}$ = Fluid inlet temperature (°C or K)
- $T_a$ = Ambient temperature (°C or K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Why:** The factor $F_R$ accounts for the fact that the absorber plate temperature is always higher than the fluid temperature. $U_L$ lumps together **top loss ($U_t$)**, **bottom loss ($U_b$)**, and **edge loss ($U_e$)**.

#### 2.1.3 FPC Efficiency Equation

$$\eta_c = F_R (\tau \alpha)_e - F_R U_L \frac{(T_{fi} - T_a)}{I_t}$$

This is a **linear equation** in the parameter $\frac{T_{fi} - T_a}{I_t}$, which is why FPC performance is plotted as a straight line on a graph of $\eta$ vs. $\frac{\Delta T}{I_t}$.

### 2.2 Solar Concentrators — Geometric Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Concentration Ratio (C) — The Cardinal Parameter

$$C = \frac{A_{\text{aperture}}}{A_{\text{receiver}}} = \frac{\text{Area of mirror/reflector}}{\text{Area of absorber}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermodynamic Upper Limit (Ries Limit):** For a 3-D point-focus concentrator, $C_{max} = \frac{1}{\sin^2 \theta_s}$, where $\theta_s$ is the half acceptance angle (Sun's half-angle ≈ 0.27°). For 2-D line-focus concentrators, $C_{max} = \frac{1}{\sin \theta_s}$.

#### 2.2.2 Parabolic Trough Collector (PTC) Analysis

The PTC is a **2-D line-focus** concentrator. The reflector is a section of a paraboloid of revolution truncated to a trough shape. The receiver is a linear absorber tube placed along the focal line.

**Geometric Equation of the Parabola:**

$$y = \frac{x^2}{4f}$$

Where $f$ is the focal length and $x, y$ are coordinates with vertex at origin.

**Edge Ray Principle:** All rays parallel to the optical axis reflect and converge at the focal point. The receiver tube diameter is chosen such that the angular acceptance covers the solar disc (≈ 0.53°) plus tracking errors.

**Optical Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{optical}} = \rho \cdot \tau_{\text{gl}} \cdot \gamma \cdot \alpha_r$$

Where $\rho$ is mirror reflectivity (≈ 0.93 for silvered glass), $\tau_{\text{gl}}$ is glass envelope transmittance, $\gamma$ is the intercept factor, $\alpha_r$ is receiver absorptivity.

**Heat Loss from Receiver Tube:**

$$Q_{\text{loss}} = \pi D L U_L (T_r - T_a)$$

Where $D$ is the outer glass envelope diameter, $L$ is the length, $T_r$ is receiver surface temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Point:** Modern PTCs use **Selective Coated Receiver Tubes** (e.g., Schott PTR 70) with evacuated annulus (vacuum < 0.001 Pa) to suppress convection. Achievable fluid temperatures: **300–400 °C** with thermal oil (Therminol VP-1) or molten salt.

#### 2.2.3 Parabolic Dish Collector (PDC) Analysis

The PDC is a **3-D point-focus** concentrator with the highest concentration ratio among single-unit concentrators. It consists of a parabolic dish reflector (typically fiberglass or spun aluminum) that focuses sunlight onto a receiver mounted at the focal point.

**Concentration Ratio Range:** C = 600 – 2000 (Sun)

**Receiver Types:**
- **Stirling Engine Receiver:** External combustion with hydrogen/helium working fluid; electrical efficiency 30–40%.
- **Microturbine Receiver:** Brayton cycle with air as HTF.
- **Thermophotovoltaic (TPV) Receiver:** Emits IR to PV cells.

**Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{\text{dish}} = \eta_{\text{opt}} \cdot \eta_{\text{receiver}} \cdot \eta_{\text{engine}}$$

$$\eta_{\text{dish}} \approx 0.85 \times 0.85 \times 0.40 \approx 29\%$$

#### 2.2.4 Central Tower Collector (Solar Power Tower)

The Central Tower system uses a **field of sun-tracking flat mirrors called heliostats** to reflect sunlight onto a single receiver mounted atop a tall tower.

**Heliostat Field Layout:**
- **Radial Stagger:** Heliostats arranged in concentric rings around the tower.
- **Spiral Layout:** Optically efficient but tracking complex.
- **Field Size:** 50,000 – 100,000 heliostats for 50–200 MW plants.

**Cosine Law Loss at Heliostat:**

$$\eta_{\text{cos}} = \cos(\theta_i)$$

Where $\theta_i$ is the incidence angle between sun ray and heliostat normal.

**Attenuation & Spillage Loss:**

$$\eta_{\text{attenuation}} = \frac{\int_0^R r \cdot \cos(\text{elevation}) \, dr}{\text{Total heliostat area}}$$

**Tower Receiver Types:**
- **External Receiver:** Cylindrical or cavity type, surface-mounted.
- **Molten Salt Receiver:** 60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃ (Hitec salt), operating up to 565 °C.
- **Supercritical CO₂ Receiver:** For next-gen Brayton cycles.

### 2.3 Solar Air Heaters (SAH) — Detailed Analysis

A **Solar Air Heater** is a low-temperature variant of the FPC where **air** is the working fluid. It is extensively used for **crop drying, space heating, and industrial process heat** below 80 °C.

#### 2.3.1 Single Pass Conventional SAH

The absorber is a flat metal plate. Air flows in a channel beneath/above the absorber. The governing heat transfer equation:

$$Q_u = \dot{m} \cdot c_p (T_{fo} - T_{fi}) = A_c F_R [S - U_L(T_{fi} - T_a)]$$

The **thermal efficiency** is typically **40–60%**, lower than water-based FPC due to air's low thermal conductivity (0.026 W/m·K) and low heat capacity (1005 J/kg·K).

#### 2.3.2 Performance Enhancement Techniques (KTU High-Yield)

| Enhancement Technique | Working Principle | Efficiency Gain |
|---|---|---|
| **V-Corrugated Absorber** | Creates turbulence, increases surface area | +15–20% |
| **Transverse Wire Mesh Insert** | Artificial roughness, disrupts boundary layer | +20–30% |
| **Perforated Fins** | Promotes mixing and secondary flows | +25% |
| **Matrix Absorber (Packed Bed)** | High surface area-to-volume ratio | +40–50% |
| **Phase Change Material (PCM) Backup** | Thermal storage integrated | +30% (effective) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to compare SAH with FPC, emphasize that SAH eliminates **freezing, corrosion, and leakage** problems, but suffers from lower heat transfer rate.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_u = A_c F_R [(\tau\alpha)_e I_t - U_L(T_{fi}-T_a)]$ | Hottel-Whillier-Bliss (FPC) | W |
| 2 | $\eta_c = F_R(\tau\alpha)_e - F_R U_L \frac{\Delta T}{I_t}$ | FPC efficiency equation | dimensionless |
| 3 | $U_L = U_t + U_b + U_e$ | Overall heat loss coefficient | W/m²·K |
| 4 | $C = \frac{A_{\text{aperture}}}{A_{\text{receiver}}}$ | Geometric concentration ratio | dimensionless |
| 5 | $y = \frac{x^2}{4f}$ | Parabola equation | m |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{opt}} = \rho \cdot \tau_{\text{gl}} \cdot \gamma \cdot \alpha_r$ | PTC optical efficiency | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{dish}} = \eta_{\text{opt}} \cdot \eta_{\text{rec}} \cdot \eta_{\text{eng}}$ | Dish-Stirling efficiency | dimensionless |
| 8 | $q'' = \epsilon \sigma (T_s^4 - T_{\text{sur}}^4)$ | Radiation heat flux | W/m² |
| 9 | $Nu = 0.023 Re^{0.8} Pr^{0.4}$ | Dittus-Boelter (turbulent HTF) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $R_a = \frac{g \beta \Delta T L^3}{\nu \alpha}$ | Rayleigh number (natural convection) | dimensionless |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **FPC:** Domestic hot water (DHW) systems, swimming pool heating, building-integrated solar thermal (BIST).
- **PTC:** Andasol (Spain), Mojave Desert (USA) — grid-connected solar thermal power plants generating 50–250 MW.
- **PDC:** SES Dish-Stirling systems, McDonnell Douglas MD-60, suitable for off-grid distributed power in arid zones.
- **Central Tower:** Ivanpah Solar Power Facility (392 MW, USA), Gemasolar (24/7 solar, Spain with 15-hr molten salt storage).
- **SAH:** Rice, wheat, and coffee drying in rural agricultural applications; passive ventilation in greenhouses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Hottel-Whillier-Bliss Equation (Full Derivation)

This is a **mandatory 14-mark derivation** in KTU exams. Every step is shown explicitly.

**Step 1: Energy Balance on the Absorber Plate**

The absorber plate receives solar radiation, loses heat to the fluid underneath and to the environment through the cover by radiation + convection. At steady state:

$$S = U_L(T_p - T_a) + q_{\text{fluid}}$$

Where:
- $S = (\tau\alpha)_e I_t$ is the absorbed solar flux (W/m²)
- $T_p$ = Mean absorber plate temperature (°C)
- $T_a$ = Ambient temperature (°C)
- $q_{\text{fluid}}$ = Heat flux transferred to fluid (W/m²)

**Step 2: Heat Transfer to Fluid Through the Tubes**

The fin-and-tube geometry of an FPC requires a **Fin Efficiency Factor (F)**:

$$q_{\text{fluid}} = \frac{h_{fi}}{F} (T_p - T_{fi})$$

Where $h_{fi}$ is the internal convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K) and $F$ is the collector efficiency factor:

$$F = \frac{1/U_L}{W[(1/(h_{fi} \cdot \pi D_i)) + (1/(C_b \cdot \pi D_o))] + 1/U_L}$$

Actually, the standard textbook form (Duffie & Beckman) is:

$$F = \frac{1/U_L}{W \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{h_{fi} \pi D_i} \right] + \frac{1}{U_L}}$$

This is for tube spacing $W$ between tubes.

**Step 3: Definition of Heat Removal Factor (F_R)**

Since the fluid temperature rises along the flow path, $T_{fi}$ is not the entire plate temperature. Hottel-Whillier introduced the **Heat Removal Factor** to relate useful heat gain to the **inlet** fluid temperature:

$$F_R = \frac{\dot{m} c_p}{A_c U_L} \left[ 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{A_c U_L F'}{\dot{m} c_p}\right) \right]$$

Where $F' = F \cdot \left[ 1 + \frac{F \cdot h_{fi} \pi D_i}{2 \dot{m} c_p / N} \right]$ accounts for temperature variation across the plate.

**Step 4: Substitution into Energy Balance**

The useful heat gain per unit area becomes:

$$\frac{Q_u}{A_c} = F_R [S - U_L(T_{fi} - T_a)]$$

**Step 5: Final Equation (Multiplied by Area)**

Multiplying both sides by $A_c$:

$$Q_u = A_c F_R [(\tau\alpha)_e I_t - U_L(T_{fi} - T_a)]$$

**Step 6: Derivation of Efficiency Equation**

Dividing by $A_c I_t$:

$$\eta_c = \frac{Q_u}{A_c I_t} = \frac{F_R (\tau\alpha)_e I_t}{I_t} - \frac{F_R U_L (T_{fi} - T_a)}{I_t}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_c = F_R(\tau\alpha)_e - F_R U_L \frac{(T_{fi} - T_a)}{I_t}}$$

This is the **first-order linear efficiency model** — a straight line with:
- **y-intercept** = $F_R(\tau\alpha)_e$ (peak efficiency at zero temperature difference)
- **Slope** = $-F_R U_L$ (negative slope)
- **x-axis** = $\frac{\Delta T}{I_t}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation is valid for FPC with **single-glazed, well-insulated** designs where $T_{fi}$ is the inlet temperature. For double-glazed or evacuated FPC, corrections apply.

### 3.2 Derivation of Optical Efficiency for Parabolic Trough

**Step 1: Total Optical Path**

Sunlight undergoes: Reflection from mirror → Transmission through glass envelope → Absorption at receiver.

**Step 2: Mirror Reflectance Loss**

For a freshly silvered glass mirror, $\rho \approx 0.93$. Soiled mirror: $\rho \approx 0.88$. Per month degradation ≈ 1–2%.

**Step 3: Intercept Factor (γ)**

Not all rays reflected from the parabola strike the receiver. Some miss it. The intercept factor:

$$\gamma = \frac{\text{Reflected rays hitting receiver}}{\text{Total reflected rays}}$$

$$\gamma \approx 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{D_{\text{abs}}}{D_{\text{image}}}\right)$$

Where $D_{\text{image}}$ is the sun's image size at focal plane (≈ 8–10 mm for a 1.5 m aperture).

**Step 4: Glass Envelope Transmittance**

Borosilicate glass: $\tau_{\text{gl}} \approx 0.96$ (new), 0.92 (after dust).

**Step 5: Receiver Absorptivity**

Selective coating (e.g., Cermet): $\alpha_r \approx 0.95$, $\epsilon \approx 0.12$ at 400 °C.

**Step 6: Combined Optical Efficiency**

$$\eta_{\text{opt}} = \rho \cdot \gamma \cdot \tau_{\text{gl}} \cdot \alpha_r$$

$$\eta_{\text{opt}} = 0.93 \times 0.95 \times 0.96 \times 0.95 = 0.805 \approx 80.5\%$$

**Step 7: Receiver Thermal Loss**

Receiver loss at 400 °C with vacuum annulus ≈ 150 W/m. Total loss:

$$Q_{\text{loss}} = \pi D_o L U_L (T_r - T_a)$$

**Step 8: Useful Heat Gain per Unit Length**

$$q_u = \eta_{\text{opt}} I_t W - \pi D_o U_L (T_r - T_a)$$

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example — FPC Efficiency Calculation

> **Problem (KTU Style):** A flat plate collector has $A_c = 2.5$ m², $F_R = 0.82$, $U_L = 5.8$ W/m²·K, $(\tau\alpha)_e = 0.86$. Operating conditions: $I_t = 800$ W/m², $T_{fi} = 35$ °C, $T_a = 28$ °C. Calculate: (a) Useful heat gain, (b) Efficiency.

**Solution:**

**Step (a): Useful Heat Gain**

Using the Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation:

$$Q_u = A_c F_R \left[ (\tau\alpha)_e I_t - U_L(T_{fi} - T_a) \right]$$

$$Q_u = 2.5 \times 0.82 \left[ 0.86 \times 800 - 5.8 \times (35 - 28) \right]$$

$$Q_u = 2.05 \left[ 688 - 40.6 \right] = 2.05 \times 647.4$$

$$Q_u = 1327.17 \text{ W}$$

**Step (b): Efficiency**

$$\eta_c = \frac{Q_u}{A_c I_t} = \frac{1327.17}{2.5 \times 800} = \frac{1327.17}{2000}$$

$$\eta_c = 0.6636 \approx 66.36\%$$

Alternatively, using the direct formula:

$$\eta_c = F_R(\tau\alpha)_e - F_R U_L \frac{(T_{fi} - T_a)}{I_t}$$

$$\eta_c = 0.82 \times 0.86 - 0.82 \times 5.8 \times \frac{7}{800}$$

$$\eta_c = 0.7052 - 0.0416 = 0.6636$$

$$\boxed{\eta_c \approx 66.36\%}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flat Plate Collector — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeSun[Sun] --> nodeRad[Solar Radiation I_t]
    nodeRad --> nodeGlass[Glass Cover Transmittance tau]
    nodeGlass --> nodeAbs[Absorber Plate alpha]
    nodeAbs --> nodePlate[Plate Temperature T_p]
    nodePlate --> nodeFluid[Fluid in Riser Tubes h_fi]
    nodePlate --> nodeTopLoss[Top Loss U_t via Radiation and Convection]
    nodePlate --> nodeBotLoss[Bottom Loss U_b via Insulation]
    nodePlate --> nodeEdgeLoss[Edge Loss U_e]
    nodeFluid --> nodeOut[Useful Heat Gain Q_u to HTF]
    nodeTopLoss --> nodeAmb[Ambient T_a]
    nodeBotLoss --> nodeAmb
    nodeEdgeLoss --> nodeAmb
    nodeOut --> nodeResult[Hottel-Whillier Equation Output]
```

### 4.2 Solar Concentrator Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeSTC[Solar Thermal Collectors] --> nodeNC[Non-Concentrating C equals 1]
    nodeSTC --> nodeCC[Concentrating C greater than 1]
    nodeNC --> nodeFPC[Flat Plate Collector 30 to 80 C]
    nodeNC --> nodeETC[Evacuated Tube Collector 50 to 150 C]
    nodeNC --> nodeSAH[Solar Air Heater 30 to 80 C]
    nodeCC --> nodeLF[Line Focus 2D]
    nodeCC --> nodePF[Point Focus 3D]
    nodeLF --> nodePTC[Parabolic Trough 300 to 400 C]
    nodeLF --> nodeLFR[Linear Fresnel Reflector 250 to 350 C]
    nodeLF --> nodeCPC[Compound Parabolic Concentrator 100 to 200 C]
    nodePF --> nodePDC[Parabolic Dish 500 to 1500 C]
    nodePF --> nodeCTC[Central Tower Collector 500 to 1000 C]
```

### 4.3 Parabolic Trough — Optical Path Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSun2[Parallel Sun Rays] --> nodeMirror[Parabolic Mirror Reflectivity rho equals 0.93]
    nodeMirror --> nodeFocus[Convergence at Focal Line]
    nodeFocus --> nodeRec[Receiver Tube Selective Coating alpha equals 0.95]
    nodeRec --> nodeLoss[Thermal Loss U_L via Vacuum Annulus]
    nodeRec --> nodeHTF[Heat Transfer Fluid Temperature T_f 300 to 400 C]
    nodeLoss --> nodeAmb2[Ambient]
```

### 4.4 Central Tower Collector — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeHeliostatField[Heliostat Field 50000 to 100000 Mirrors] --> nodeTrack[Solar Tracking Algorithm]
    nodeTrack --> nodeReflect[Reflection to Tower Top]
    nodeReflect --> nodeReceiver[Central Receiver Cavity or External]
    nodeReceiver --> nodeHTF2[HTF Molten Salt or Steam]
    nodeReceiver --> nodeRadLoss[Re-radiation Loss sigma epsilon T4]
    nodeRadLoss --> nodeSky[Sky Temperature]
    nodeHTF2 --> nodeStorage[Thermal Storage 15 hr Capacity]
    nodeStorage --> nodeTurbine[Steam Turbine Rankine Cycle]
    nodeTurbine --> nodeGen[Generator 50 to 200 MW Output]
```

### 4.5 Comparative Schematic — All Four Collector Types

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeCompare[Collector Comparison Framework] --> nodeA[Type A Flat Plate Stationary No Concentration]
    nodeCompare --> nodeB[Type B Parabolic Trough Single Axis Tracking]
    nodeCompare --> nodeC[Type C Parabolic Dish Dual Axis Tracking]
    nodeCompare --> nodeD[Type D Central Tower Field Heliostat Tracking]
    nodeA --> nodeParamA[T_max 80 C, C equals 1, eta 40 to 60 percent]
    nodeB --> nodeParamB[T_max 400 C, C equals 15 to 80, eta 60 to 70 percent]
    nodeC --> nodeParamC[T_max 1500 C, C equals 600 to 2000, eta 25 to 30 percent net]
    nodeD --> nodeParamD[T_max 1000 C, C equals 300 to 1000, eta 35 to 45 percent net]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define the term 'Concentration Ratio' of a solar collector. State the geometric and thermodynamic limits.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Concentration Ratio (C)** of a solar collector is defined as the ratio of the effective aperture area (area receiving solar radiation) to the area of the receiver/absorber where collected energy is utilized.

$$C = \frac{A_{\text{aperture}}}{A_{\text{receiver}}}$$

- **Geometric Limit:** For a point-focus concentrator, $C_{max,\text{geometric}} = \frac{1}{\sin^2 \theta_s}$ where $\theta_s$ is the half acceptance angle.
- **Thermodynamic (Ries) Limit:** For a 3-D point-focus, $C_{max} \approx 46{,}200$ for $\theta_s = 0.27°$ (sun's half-angle). For 2-D line-focus, $C_{max} \approx 215$ (since $C_{max} = 1/\sin \theta_s$).

(3 marks)

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is a Solar Air Heater? List any two advantages and two disadvantages.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Solar Air Heater (SAH)** is a low-temperature solar thermal device that uses air as the heat transfer fluid to capture solar energy and deliver heated air for applications like space heating, crop drying, and industrial drying.

**Advantages:**
1. No freezing, boiling, corrosion, or scaling issues (unlike water-based systems).
2. Simple construction, low cost, and leak-proof operation.

**Disadvantages:**
1. Low thermal conductivity of air (0.026 W/m·K) results in lower heat transfer coefficient.
2. Larger duct areas are required due to air's low density and heat capacity.

(3 marks)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working principle of a **Flat Plate Collector (FPC)**. Derive the **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss equation** for useful heat gain.

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (Diagram as in Section 4.1):**

An FPC consists of the following components:
1. **Transparent Glass Cover:** 3–4 mm thick low-iron glass; transmittance τ ≈ 0.91.
2. **Air Gap:** 25–40 mm; suppresses convection losses.
3. **Absorber Plate:** Copper or aluminum with selective coating; α ≈ 0.95, ε ≈ 0.12.
4. **Riser Tubes:** Carry the heat transfer fluid; bonded to absorber.
5. **Insulation:** Polyurethane foam at back and sides; k ≤ 0.04 W/m·K.
6. **Casing:** GI sheet frame for structural support.

**Working Principle:** Solar radiation passes through the glass cover and is absorbed by the selective coating on the absorber plate. The absorbed energy raises the plate temperature. Heat is conducted through riser tubes to the HTF (water/glycol). The glass cover traps IR radiation (greenhouse effect), reducing thermal losses.

**Derivation of Hottel-Whillier-Bliss Equation (refer to Section 3.1 for full derivation):**

$$Q_u = A_c F_R [(\tau\alpha)_e I_t - U_L(T_{fi} - T_a)]$$

(7 marks)

**(b) [7 Marks]** A flat plate collector has $A_c = 3$ m², $F_R = 0.78$, $(\tau\alpha)_e = 0.85$, $U_L = 6.2$ W/m²·K. The incident radiation is $I_t = 900$ W/m², ambient temperature $T_a = 30$ °C, and fluid inlet temperature $T_{fi} = 45$ °C. Calculate (i) useful heat gain and (ii) collector efficiency.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Useful Heat Gain:**

$$Q_u = A_c F_R \left[ (\tau\alpha)_e I_t - U_L(T_{fi} - T_a) \right]$$

$$Q_u = 3 \times 0.78 \left[ 0.85 \times 900 - 6.2 \times (45 - 30) \right]$$

$$Q_u = 2.34 \left[ 765 - 93 \right] = 2.34 \times 672$$

$$Q_u = 1572.48 \text{ W}$$

**(ii) Efficiency:**

$$\eta_c = \frac{Q_u}{A_c I_t} = \frac{1572.48}{3 \times 900} = \frac{1572.48}{2700}$$

$$\eta_c = 0.5824 = 58.24\%$$

**Verification using direct formula:**

$$\eta_c = F_R(\tau\alpha)_e - F_R U_L \frac{(T_{fi} - T_a)}{I_t}$$

$$\eta_c = 0.78 \times 0.85 - 0.78 \times 6.2 \times \frac{15}{900}$$

$$\eta_c = 0.663 - 0.0805 = 0.5825 \approx 58.25\%$$

(7 marks)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **Parabolic Trough**, **Parabolic Dish**, and **Central Tower Collector** with respect to: (i) concentration ratio, (ii) tracking mechanism, (iii) operating temperature, (iv) receiver type, (v) typical application, and (vi) thermodynamic efficiency.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Parabolic Trough | Parabolic Dish | Central Tower |
|---|---|---|---|
| Concentration Ratio | 15–80 | 600–2000 | 300–1000 |
| Tracking | Single-axis (N-S or E-W) | Dual-axis | Heliostat field, dual-axis per mirror |
| Operating Temperature | 300–400 °C | 500–1500 °C | 500–1000 °C |
| Receiver Type | Linear evacuated tube | Cavity or direct illuminated | Cavity or external cylindrical |
| Application | Grid-connected solar thermal plants | Distributed power, Stirling engine | Large grid plants, with thermal storage |
| Net Efficiency | 60–70% thermal | 25–30% electrical | 35–45% net (with storage) |

(7 marks)

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a schematic diagram, explain the **Central Tower Collector** system. What is the role of the **heliostat**? Explain the cosine loss.

**Model Answer:**

**Schematic Diagram (refer to Section 4.4)**

A **Central Tower Collector (CTC)** consists of:
- **Heliostat Field:** Thousands of sun-tracking flat mirrors distributed over a circular area.
- **Tower:** A tall structure (typically 80–200 m) hosting the receiver at the top.
- **Receiver:** Cavity or external type where concentrated sunlight is absorbed.
- **Heat Transfer Fluid:** Molten salt (60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃) or steam.
- **Thermal Storage Tank:** For 24/7 operation.
- **Power Block:** Steam turbine + generator.

**Role of Heliostat:**
A heliostat is a flat, sun-tracking mirror that continuously reflects solar radiation toward a fixed target (the receiver atop the tower). Each heliostat has a dual-axis tracking mechanism (azimuth + altitude) controlled by a central computer using astronomical algorithms.

**Cosine Loss:**

The cosine loss arises because the effective area of a heliostat as seen by the sun's rays is reduced by the cosine of the incidence angle $\theta_i$ (the angle between the heliostat's normal vector and the sun ray direction).

$$\eta_{\text{cos}} = \cos(\theta_i)$$

For a heliostat at distance $r$ from the tower base, the incidence angle depends on the sun's position, the heliostat's orientation, and the tower's height. Cosine losses typically range from 0.7–0.95, with heliostats near the tower suffering higher losses.

$$Q_{\text{incident}} = A_h \cdot I_b \cdot \cos(\theta_i) \cdot \rho$$

**Total Field Efficiency** (combined):

$$\eta_{\text{field}} = \rho \cdot \cos(\theta_i) \cdot \eta_{\text{shading}} \cdot \eta_{\text{block}} \cdot \eta_{\text{atm}}$$

Where $\eta_{\text{shading}}$ accounts for mutual shadowing between heliostats, $\eta_{\text{block}}$ accounts for blocking of reflected rays, and $\eta_{\text{atm}}$ accounts for atmospheric attenuation over the slant range to tower.

(7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by $A_c$** when computing $Q_u$ — leads to loss of 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $F_R$ (Heat Removal Factor) with $F$ (Collector Efficiency Factor)** — they are NOT the same; $F_R$ depends on flow rate $\dot{m}$.
> 3. **Mixing up $(\tau\alpha)_e$ and $\tau \alpha$** — the effective product accounts for multiple reflections inside the cover system.
> 4. **Incorrect units in $U_L$** — must be W/m²·K, not W/m·K.
> 5. **Skipping the temperature scale consistency** — use either all °C or all K; differences are unaffected, but mention explicitly.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- [ ] **Solar Constant:** $I_{sc} = 1367$ W/m²; Standard terrestrial test: AM1.5, $I_t = 1000$ W/m².
- [ ] **FPC Components:** Glass cover, air gap, absorber with selective coating, riser tubes, insulation, casing.
- [ ] **Hottel-Whillier-Bliss Equation:** $Q_u = A_c F_R [(\tau\alpha)_e I_t - U_L(T_{fi}-T_a)]$ — most important equation.
- [ ] **FPC Efficiency Equation:** $\eta_c = F_R(\tau\alpha)_e - F_R U_L \frac{\Delta T}{I_t}$ — linear in $\Delta T/I_t$.
- [ ] **Concentration Ratio:** $C = A_{\text{aperture}}/A_{\text{receiver}}$; limits ~215 (2D), ~46,200 (3D).
- [ ] **Parabolic Trough:** Line-focus, single-axis tracking, T = 300–400 °C, evacuated receiver tube, C = 15–80.
- [ ] **Parabolic Dish:** Point-focus, dual-axis, T = 500–1500 °C, Stirling engine pairing, C = 600–2000.
- [ ] **Central Tower:** Point-focus via heliostat field, dual-axis, T = 500–1000 °C, molten salt storage for 24/7 operation.
- [ ] **Cosine Loss:** $\eta_{\text{cos}} = \cos(\theta_i)$; affects heliostat field efficiency.
- [ ] **Solar Air Heater:** Air as HTF, low temperature (≤ 80 °C), no freezing/corrosion issues, but low heat transfer rate.
- [ ] **Enhancement techniques for SAH:** V-corrugated absorber, wire mesh, perforated fins, matrix/packed bed absorbers, PCM integration.
- [ ] **Selective Coatings:** High $\alpha$ (≈ 0.95), low $\epsilon$ (≈ 0.12); e.g., Black Chrome, TiNOx, Cermet.
- [ ] **Ries Limit:** Maximum achievable concentration ratio based on thermodynamic considerations.
- [ ] **Plant Examples (memorize for KTU viva):** Andasol (Spain, 50 MW PTC), Ivanpah (USA, 392 MW Tower), SES Dish-Stirling.

**Mnemonic for Concentrator Types — "LD-PC-Tower":**
- **L**inear (Parabolic Trough, Fresnel, CPC)
- **D**ish (Parabolic Dish, point-focus)
- **P**roject (Heliostat + Tower)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Thermal Electric Power Generation - Thermal Energy Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT862 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solar Thermal Electric Power Generation - Thermal Energy Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Energy Storage (TES)** in a solar thermal power plant is an engineered subsystem that captures, retains (for a finite duration), and releases the thermal energy harvested by the **solar collector field** so that electricity generation in the **power block** can be decoupled in time from solar insolation. The stored energy acts as a buffer that converts an inherently **intermittent** energy source into a **dispatchable, firm, and schedulable** electrical output.

In the context of Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) plants — namely **Parabolic Trough**, **Solar Tower (Central Receiver)**, **Linear Fresnel**, and **Dish-Stirling** systems — TES is the single most important enabling technology that grants solar thermal electricity its unique grid-stability advantage over Photovoltaics (PV).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Hot Water Bottle" of a Power Plant:**
> Imagine boiling a kettle at noon using a parabolic dish. If you run the steam turbine *only* when the kettle is on the stove, you get electricity for 4–5 hours a day. Now place a **giant insulated hot water bottle** (the TES tank) between the kettle and the turbine. The kettle heats the bottle during the day; at 7 PM when the sun sets, the bottle continues to feed the turbine for several more hours. The TES literally **time-shifts** solar energy from the "collection window" to the "dispatch window."

## 1.3 Why TES is Indispensable in CSP

| Plant Component | Operating Window | Problem Without TES |
|---|---|---|
| Solar Collector Field | 6–10 hrs/day (clear sky) | Excess heat dumped if generation continues |
| Power Block (Turbine) | Needs to run 16–24 hrs/day | Idle at night → no revenue |
| Grid Demand | Peak demand: 6–10 PM | Solar peak ≠ demand peak |

TES solves **all three mismatches** simultaneously: temporal mismatch, capacity mismatch, and demand mismatch.

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bold Constants)

- **Storage capacity rating:** MWh_th (megawatt-hours thermal) — typical CSP plant: **1,000 to 15,000 MWh_th**.
- **Discharge duration:** **6 to 15 hours** at full-load power-block rating.
- **Storage media melting point (Solar Salt, 60% NaNO₃ + 40% KNO₃):** **220 °C**.
- **Storage media maximum operating temperature (Solar Salt):** **565 °C**.
- **Round-trip efficiency (charged → stored → discharged):** **≈ 0.85 – 0.95** for sensible heat in molten salts.
- **Capacity factor boost:** Without TES ≈ **25%**; with 6 h TES ≈ **40%**; with 15 h TES ≈ **70%+**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme PESFT862 Module 1 explicitly requires students to *"describe the need for thermal storage in solar thermal plants and identify the major TES technologies with their governing heat-transfer equations."* The remainder of this note is built strictly around that requirement.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Physical Mechanisms of TES

Thermal energy is stored by exploiting one (or a combination) of three thermodynamic mechanisms:

### (a) Sensible Heat Storage (SHS)
Energy is stored by raising the temperature of a single-phase storage medium. The energy is **sensible** because it manifests as a measurable temperature change.
- **Low-temperature range (< 100 °C):** Water, thermal oil.
- **Mid-range (100–300 °C):** Engineered concrete, molten salt mixtures, sand-rock beds.
- **High-range (> 400 °C):** Solar salt, liquid sodium, ceramic particles, graphite.

### (b) Latent Heat Storage (LHS) — Phase Change Materials (PCM)
Energy is absorbed/released during an isothermal solid–liquid (or solid–solid) phase transition at the **melting point**. Energy density is roughly **5–10× higher** than sensible storage per unit mass for a small ΔT window.
- Salt hydrates (e.g., Glauber's salt, melting point ≈ **32 °C**) — building HVAC.
- Paraffins (45–70 °C) — building applications.
- Metallic alloys (e.g., Al-Si, eutectic ≈ **577 °C**) — high-temp CSP.

### (c) Thermochemical Storage (TCS)
Energy drives a reversible endothermic chemical reaction. Products are stored separately and recombined on demand to release heat. Theoretically the **highest energy density** (e.g., metal oxide redox cycles: ZnO ⇌ Zn + ½ O₂ at **> 1700 °C**). Still at pilot stage in 2024.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind choosing one mechanism over another:** Sensible storage in **molten salt (HITEC / Solar Salt)** dominates commercial CSP today because of its high specific heat (~**1.55 kJ/kg·K**), low vapor pressure, chemical stability, and proven 30+ year operational track record. LHS and TCS are research frontiers for the next generation of CSP.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipes inside table cells** — using `\vert` and `\mid` everywhere to preserve markdown integrity.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Description | Typical Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{SHS} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat stored | J or kWh | $m$ = mass, $c_p$ = specific heat, $\Delta T = T_{hot}-T_{cold}$ |
| 2 | $Q_{LHS} = m \cdot L$ | Latent heat stored | J or kWh | $L$ = latent heat of fusion |
| 3 | $Q_{total} = m\!\left[c_p\!\left(T_{m}-T_{i}\right) + L + c_p\!\left(T_{f}-T_{m}\right)\right]$ | Combined sensible + latent | J | For medium heated from $T_i$, melted at $T_m$, to $T_f$ |
| 4 | $q_{vol} = \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Volumetric energy density | kJ/m³ | $\rho$ = density |
| 5 | $Q_{loss} = U \cdot A_{tank} \cdot (T_{avg}-T_{amb}) \cdot t$ | Tank standing heat loss | J | $U$ = overall heat-transfer coefficient |
| 6 | $LHV_{s} = \dfrac{U \cdot A_{tank}}{m \cdot c_p}$ | Storage loss parameter (per hour) | h⁻¹ | Used in transient tank models |
| 7 | $f_{solar} = \dfrac{Q_{storage}}{Q_{demand,total}}$ | Solar fraction | dimensionless | $0 \le f \le 1$ |
| 8 | $CF = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{P_{rated} \cdot 8760}$ | Capacity factor | dimensionless | Boosted by TES |
| 9 | $\dot{Q}_{hx} = U_{hx} \cdot A_{hx} \cdot LMTD$ | Heat-exchanger duty (TES–power block) | W | $LMTD$ = log mean temperature difference |
| 10 | $T_{strat}(z,t)$ | Thermocline temperature profile | °C | Function of height $z$ in stratified tank |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dispatchability:** A 50 MW_e CSP plant with **7.5 h** of TES can deliver firm power from 8 AM to 12 AM, replicating the duty cycle of a coal plant.
- **Grid ancillary services:** Provides **spinning reserve**, **ramp-rate control**, and **frequency regulation** — services that PV alone cannot supply without expensive batteries.
- **Capacity credit:** Dispatchable CSP receives the same capacity payment as a thermal plant in many deregulated markets (e.g., Spain, California, Morocco).
- **Hybrid integration:** Pairs naturally with biomass, waste heat, or natural-gas back-up for 24/7 operation.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** In KTU descriptive answers, always quote numerical values for typical storage capacities (e.g., "The 280 MW Gemasolar plant in Spain has a 15-hour molten-salt TES supplying **2,840 MWh_th**"). Numerical anchoring scores top marks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Total Sensible Heat Stored in a Two-Tank Molten-Salt System

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Setup:** A two-tank direct molten-salt TES system consists of a **Cold Tank** (salt at $T_c$) and a **Hot Tank** (salt at $T_h$). During charging, salt is pumped from cold to hot via the solar field; during discharging, hot salt is pumped to the steam generator (power block), then returned to the cold tank. The "working inventory" $m_{inv}$ circulates between the two tanks; an additional **"inactive" mass** $m_{inactive}$ may sit permanently in each tank.

### Step 1 — Mass balance on the salt inventory
Let $m_c$ and $m_h$ be the salt masses in the cold and hot tanks respectively. The total mass in the storage subsystem is:

$$
m_{total} = m_c + m_h
$$

For a **direct two-tank** system in steady cyclic operation, $m_c = m_h = m_{inv}$ (symmetric inventory). The minimum inventory required to deliver a usable storage energy $E_{usable}$ is:

$$
m_{inv} = \frac{E_{usable}}{c_p \cdot (T_h - T_c)}
$$

**Logic:** Setting $E_{usable}$ equal to the maximum extractable sensible heat from the hot tank minus the residual energy kept in the cold tank.

### Step 2 — Heat balance on the hot tank
During discharge over a time $t_d$ at constant power $\dot{Q}_{dis}$:

$$
m_{inv} \cdot c_p \cdot \dfrac{dT_h}{dt} = -\dot{Q}_{dis} - \dot{Q}_{loss}
$$

Where $\dot{Q}_{loss} = U A_h (T_h - T_{amb})$. For an ideal **adiabatic tank** ($\dot{Q}_{loss} \to 0$):

$$
m_{inv} \cdot c_p \cdot \dfrac{dT_h}{dt} = -\dot{Q}_{dis}
$$

### Step 3 — Integrate from $T_h$ to $T_c$ over the full discharge window

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{t_d} \dot{Q}_{dis}\, dt &= -\int_{T_h}^{T_c} m_{inv} \cdot c_p \, dT \\
E_{usable} &= m_{inv} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_h - T_c)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical calibration for Solar Salt
Take $c_p = 1.55 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$, $T_h = 565 \; °C$, $T_c = 290 \; °C$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= 565 - 290 = 275 \; \text{K} \\
q_{mass} &= c_p \cdot \Delta T = 1.55 \times 275 = 426.25 \; \text{kJ/kg} \\
q_{mass} &= \frac{426.25}{3600} = 0.1184 \; \text{kWh/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **2,000 MWh_th** storage:

$$
m_{inv} = \frac{2{,}000{,}000 \;\text{kWh}}{0.1184 \;\text{kWh/kg}} \approx 16.9 \;\text{×}\; 10^{6} \;\text{kg} \approx 16{,}900 \;\text{tonnes}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation II — Thermocline (Single-Tank Stratified) Storage Capacity

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept:** A single tank with a thermal **thermocline** — a sharp temperature gradient separating hot (top) and cold (bottom) regions — saves capital cost. The temperature profile $T(z)$ is approximately a **sigmoid** with the hot region in the upper portion.

### Step 1 — Define the height of the thermocline
Let $H$ be the tank height, with hot region thickness $h_h$ (top) and cold region $h_c$ (bottom). The volume of hot usable salt:

$$
V_h = A \cdot h_h
$$

### Step 2 — Energy stored in the stratified tank

$$
E_{strat} = \rho \cdot A \cdot c_p \int_{0}^{H} \left[T(z) - T_c \right] dz
$$

### Step 3 — Idealised linear thermocline (approximation)
Assume $T(z) = T_h - \dfrac{T_h - T_c}{\delta} (z - z_0)$ across a thermocline thickness $\delta$ centred at $z_0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{strat} &= \rho \, A \, c_p \!\left[ (T_h - T_c)(H - \delta) + \tfrac{1}{2}\,(T_h - T_c)\,\delta \right] \\
&= \rho \, A \, c_p \, (T_h - T_c) \!\left[ H - \tfrac{\delta}{2} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The first term is the contribution of the fully-hot (or fully-cold) region; the second is the average contribution of the gradient zone. A **thinner thermocline** (smaller $\delta$) → higher usable energy.

### Step 4 — Filling-fraction efficiency
Define $\eta_{fill} = \dfrac{H - \delta/2}{H}$. For well-designed thermocline tanks $\delta/H \approx 0.1$, giving $\eta_{fill} \approx 0.95$.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Standing Heat Loss and Lumped Loss Parameter

### Step 1 — Surface heat loss
For a cylindrical tank of radius $r$, height $H$:

$$
A_{tank} = 2\pi r^2 + 2\pi r H
$$

The instantaneous thermal loss rate:

$$
\dot{Q}_{loss} = U \cdot A_{tank} \cdot (T_{avg} - T_{amb})
$$

### Step 2 — Daily energy loss
Over 24 hours:

$$
Q_{loss,24h} = U \cdot A_{tank} \cdot (T_{avg} - T_{amb}) \cdot 86{,}400 \; \text{s}
$$

### Step 3 — Lumped parameter (used in hourly simulation)

$$
LHV_s = \frac{U \cdot A_{tank}}{m \cdot c_p} \quad \left[\text{h}^{-1}\right]
$$

**Logic:** A typical Solar-Salt cold-tank with $U A \approx 8 \; \text{W/K}$ and $m c_p \approx 6 \times 10^6 \; \text{kJ/K}$ gives $LHV_s \approx 4.8 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{s}^{-1} \approx 0.017 \; \text{h}^{-1}$, i.e., about **1.7 %** standing loss per hour — manageable over 15 h.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Storage Media Comparison

| Property | Water (H₂O) | Solar Salt (60-40) | HITEC XL | Liquid Sodium | Engineered Concrete |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $c_p$ (kJ/kg·K) | 4.18 | 1.55 | 1.42 | 1.30 | 0.88 |
| Density $\rho$ (kg/m³) | 1000 | 1870 (liq.) | 1990 | 850 | 2200 |
| Operating range (°C) | 0 – 100 | 220 – 565 | 120 – 500 | 100 – 760 | 200 – 600 |
| Latent heat $L$ (kJ/kg) | 334 | 161 | — | — | — |
| Corrosivity | None | Mild | Mild | High (reacts with H₂O) | None |
| Cost (USD/kg) | < 0.01 | ~ 0.9 | ~ 1.5 | ~ 3.0 | ~ 0.1 |
| CSP use | Direct steam trough | Tower, Trough | Industrial | Demo only | Trough (concrete block) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — TES Integration in a Solar Tower (Central Receiver) Plant

The following Mermaid block diagram traces the energy flow from sunlight to the grid, highlighting the **dual path** through TES that creates dispatchability.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sunA[Sunlight] --> helioA[Heliostat Field]
    helioA --> receiverA[Central Receiver on Tower]
    receiverA --> hotSalt1[Hot Molten Salt at 565 C]
    hotSalt1 --> valve1A{Operating Mode}
    valve1A -- Charge Mode --> hotTankA[Hot Storage Tank]
    valve1A -- Direct Mode --> hx1A[Steam Generator Heat Exchanger]
    hotTankA --> hx1A
    hx1A --> turbineA[Steam Turbine Rankine Cycle]
    turbineA --> genA[Electrical Generator]
    genA --> gridA[Electrical Grid - 24x7 Dispatchable]
    hx1A --> coldSalt1[Cold Molten Salt at 290 C]
    coldSalt1 --> valve2A{Operating Mode}
    valve2A -- Discharge Mode --> coldTankA[Cold Storage Tank]
    valve2A -- Bypass --> hotSalt1
    coldTankA --> pumpA[Charging Pump]
    pumpA --> receiverA
```

**Caption:** During sunshine, the heliostats focus radiation on the receiver, heating molten salt. A three-way valve routes salt to either the hot storage tank (charge) or directly to the steam generator (direct generation). When sunlight fades, hot salt is drawn from storage, sent to the steam generator, and the cooled salt returns to the cold tank. The cycle closes through a charging pump that lifts cold salt back to the receiver next morning.

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Two-Tank TES Charge / Discharge States

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Charging: Sun available and tank not full
    Charging --> DirectGen: Demand exceeds storage capacity
    Charging --> Full: Hot tank reaches T_max
    Full --> DirectGen: Sun continues
    DirectGen --> Discharging: Sun fades, hot tank has inventory
    Discharging --> DirectGen: Sun returns
    Discharging --> Empty: Hot tank depleted
    Empty --> Idle: Wait for next sunrise
    Full --> [*]: Maintenance
    Empty --> [*]: Maintenance
```

## 4.3 Process Schematic — Thermocline Single-Tank (textual block representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ThermoclineTank[Stratified Single Tank]
        regionH[Hot Region - 565 C - low density salt floats on top]
        regionT[Thermocline Zone - sharp gradient - 5 to 10 percent of height]
        regionC[Cold Region - 290 C - high density salt sits at bottom]
    end
    inPipe[Hot Salt Inlet from Receiver] --> regionH
    regionC --> outPipe[Cold Salt Outlet to Steam Generator]
    regionH --> outPipe2[Hot Salt Outlet to Steam Generator]
    outPipe2 --> hxBlock[Steam Generator]
    hxBlock --> inPipe
```

**Caption:** A single thermocline tank uses filler material (quartz sand, ceramic balls) to establish a stable vertical temperature gradient. Hot salt is charged/discharged from the top; cold salt from the bottom. Capital cost is ~**35 % lower** than a two-tank system for the same storage capacity.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU University Exam – May 2024 Set B**

**Q1.** Define Thermal Energy Storage. List the three physical mechanisms of TES and state which mechanism is dominant in commercial Concentrated Solar Power plants today.

**Model Answer:**
Thermal Energy Storage (TES) is a subsystem of a solar thermal power plant that stores the heat collected by the solar field and releases it on demand, decoupling energy collection from electricity generation. The three mechanisms are (i) **Sensible Heat Storage** (temperature change of a single-phase medium), (ii) **Latent Heat Storage** (isothermal phase change, e.g., solid–liquid), and (iii) **Thermochemical Storage** (reversible endothermic reactions). In commercial CSP, **Sensible Heat Storage in molten salt (Solar Salt, 60 % NaNO₃ + 40 % KNO₃)** dominates because of its proven reliability, low vapour pressure, and 1.55 kJ/kg·K specific heat over the 290 – 565 °C operating range. *(3 marks)*

> **KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Set A**

**Q2.** What is a *thermocline* in a single-tank molten-salt storage system? Why is it preferred over the conventional two-tank design?

**Model Answer:**
A thermocline is the **sharp vertical temperature gradient** that forms in a single-tank storage unit, separating the hot molten salt at the top from the cold salt at the bottom. It is typically stabilised by a packed-bed filler (silica sand or ceramic balls) that suppresses convective mixing. Compared with the conventional two-tank design, the thermocline single-tank system offers a **35–40 % lower capital cost**, a smaller physical footprint, and a comparable round-trip efficiency of ~90 %. Its main engineering challenge is maintaining the steepness of the gradient during repeated charge–discharge cycles, which is mitigated by proper filler selection and controlled flow rates. *(3 marks)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Mixing kJ, kWh, MJ, and kcal in the same numerical step. Always convert to **kJ or kWh first**, then maintain consistency.
> 2. **Forgetting ΔT = T_hot − T_cold, NOT the absolute temperature.** Many students write 565 K instead of 275 K and lose 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Omitting the round-trip efficiency** when asked to compute the *electrical* energy output of a storage-equipped plant.
> 4. **Skipping the LMTD calculation** in heat-exchanger sizing — KTU expects the full derivation, not a one-line answer.
> 5. **No numerical illustration:** In 14-mark questions, KTU mandates at least one **worked numerical example** with all steps visible. A purely descriptive answer caps at 8–9 marks.

### Question A — 14 Marks *(Internal Choice Option A)*

> **KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **total sensible heat stored** in a two-tank molten-salt TES of inventory mass $m_{inv}$ operating between cold-tank temperature $T_c$ and hot-tank temperature $T_h$. Hence compute the salt mass required to store **2,500 MWh_th** of thermal energy using Solar Salt (take $c_p = 1.55 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$, $T_c = 290 \; °C$, $T_h = 565 \; °C$). *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **charging and discharging sequence** of a two-tank direct TES in a solar-tower plant. State two advantages and one limitation of this configuration. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Solution to Q.A (a) — Model Answer

**Step 1 — Governing equation:**
The usable energy stored in the working inventory of a two-tank direct system is the difference between the energy in the fully charged hot tank and the energy in the fully discharged cold tank:

$$
E_{usable} = m_{inv} \cdot c_p \cdot T_h - m_{inv} \cdot c_p \cdot T_c = m_{inv} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_h - T_c)
$$

**Step 2 — Invert for mass:**

$$
m_{inv} = \frac{E_{usable}}{c_p \, (T_h - T_c)}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**
Convert storage to kJ: $2{,}500 \; \text{MWh} = 2{,}500 \times 3{,}600 \; \text{MJ} = 9 \times 10^{6} \; \text{MJ} = 9 \times 10^{9} \; \text{kJ}$.

$$
\Delta T = 565 - 290 = 275 \; \text{K}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{inv} &= \frac{9 \times 10^{9} \; \text{kJ}}{1.55 \; \text{kJ/kg·K} \times 275 \; \text{K}} \\
&= \frac{9 \times 10^{9}}{426.25} \\
&\approx 2.1118 \times 10^{7} \; \text{kg} \\
&\approx 21{,}118 \; \text{tonnes of Solar Salt.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check / typical density conversion:**
Volume of hot salt (liquid density 1870 kg/m³):

$$
V = \frac{2.1118 \times 10^{7}}{1870} \approx 11{,}293 \; \text{m}^{3}
$$

This corresponds to a single hot tank roughly **25 m diameter × 23 m tall** — a reasonable industrial scale. *(7 marks — full credit for derivation + numerical + units + interpretation)*

---

#### Solution to Q.A (b) — Model Answer

**Block Diagram (text representation — to be redrawn in the exam):**

$$
\text{Sun} \rightarrow \text{Heliostat Field} \rightarrow \text{Receiver} \rightarrow \text{Hot Salt} \rightarrow \text{3-way valve} \rightarrow \text{Hot Tank} \rightarrow \text{Steam Gen.} \rightarrow \text{Turbine} \rightarrow \text{Grid}
$$

$$
\text{Cold Salt} \rightarrow \text{Cold Tank} \rightarrow \text{Charging Pump} \rightarrow \text{Receiver}
$$

**Charging sequence (daytime, surplus heat):**
1. Heliostat field concentrates sunlight on the central receiver mounted atop the tower.
2. Cold salt from the cold tank is pumped through the receiver, heated from 290 °C to 565 °C.
3. A three-way diverting valve routes the hot salt to the **hot storage tank** until the tank is fully charged.
4. Excess hot salt, once the tank is full, is sent directly to the **steam generator** to run the turbine at full load.

**Discharging sequence (cloudy period or after sunset):**
1. The valve switches, drawing hot salt from the **hot storage tank** and routing it to the steam generator.
2. The salt transfers its heat to the working fluid (water/steam), cools back to 290 °C, and is sent to the **cold storage tank**.
3. The cycle continues until the hot tank is depleted or solar radiation returns.

**Two Advantages:**
- **Dispatchable power:** Generation can be scheduled for peak demand (6–10 PM), securing higher tariffs and capacity payments.
- **Capacity factor boost:** A 6-hour TES raises the annual capacity factor from ~25 % (no storage) to ~40 %; 15-hour TES pushes it beyond 70 %.

**One Limitation:**
- **High capital cost** of molten-salt inventory (~USD 1,500–2,500 per tonne) and freeze-protection systems required to keep salt above its 220 °C melting point during plant shutdown. *(7 marks — full credit for diagram, sequence, advantages, and limitation)*

---

### Question B — 14 Marks *(Internal Choice Option B)*

> **KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the principle of **latent heat thermal energy storage** using a Phase Change Material (PCM). Derive the expression for the total heat stored when 1 kg of PCM is heated from initial temperature $T_i$ through its melting point $T_m$ to a final temperature $T_f$. Use the equation to evaluate the energy stored in 1 kg of a paraffin PCM ($c_{p,s} = 2.0 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$, $c_{p,l} = 2.5 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$, $L = 200 \; \text{kJ/kg}$, $T_i = 30 \; °C$, $T_m = 60 \; °C$, $T_f = 90 \; °C$). *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare **Sensible, Latent, and Thermochemical** energy storage mechanisms in terms of energy density, technology readiness, cycle stability, and typical CSP application. Justify why molten-salt sensible storage is preferred in current commercial plants. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Solution to Q.B (a) — Model Answer

**Step 1 — Physical description:**
In a Latent Heat Storage (LHS) system, the storage medium undergoes a **phase change** (usually solid → liquid) at a constant temperature $T_m$. Heat is absorbed as **latent heat of fusion** $L$ during the transition, with no temperature change. The medium is encapsulated in containers, panels, or macro-encapsulated spheres and arranged in a heat-exchanger geometry. *(2 marks — for diagram and description)*

**Step 2 — Total energy in three sub-stages:**
When 1 kg of PCM is heated from $T_i$ to $T_f$ passing through $T_m$:

$$
Q_{total} = \underbrace{m \, c_{p,s} \, (T_m - T_i)}_{\text{Stage 1: Solid sensible}} + \underbrace{m \, L}_{\text{Stage 2: Phase change}} + \underbrace{m \, c_{p,l} \, (T_f - T_m)}_{\text{Stage 3: Liquid sensible}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values (per kg, $m = 1$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{total} &= (1)(2.0)(60 - 30) + (1)(200) + (1)(2.5)(90 - 60) \\
&= (2.0)(30) + 200 + (2.5)(30) \\
&= 60 + 200 + 75 \\
&= 335 \; \text{kJ/kg.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kWh:**

$$
Q_{total} = \frac{335}{3600} = 0.0931 \; \text{kWh/kg}
$$

**Comparison note:** This is **comparable to** molten-salt sensible storage over a 60 K window (which would give $1.55 \times 60 = 93 \; \text{kJ/kg}$) but PCMs achieve it at a **single temperature**, ideal for isothermal dispatch. *(7 marks — full credit for description, derivation, numerical, and interpretation)*

---

#### Solution to Q.B (b) — Model Answer

| Criterion | Sensible (Molten Salt) | Latent (PCM) | Thermochemical |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Volumetric energy density** | Moderate (250–400 kJ/L) | High (300–500 kJ/L) | Very high (500–1500 kJ/L) |
| **Technology Readiness Level** | 9 — Commercial | 6–7 — Pilot | 3–4 — Lab |
| **Cycle stability** | 30+ years, 10,000+ cycles | 1000–3000 cycles, encapsulation degradation | 100–500 cycles, reactor design issues |
| **Operating temperature** | 220 – 565 °C (Solar Salt) | 30 – 600 °C (depending on PCM) | 500 – 1700 °C |
| **Commercial CSP use** | Andasol, Gemasolar, Noor III, Crescent Dunes | Demonstration only (e.g., ENEA Italy) | Pilot / none commercial |
| **Cost (USD/kWh_th)** | ~20–30 | ~40–80 | ~100+ |

**Justification for molten-salt dominance in current CSP:**
1. **Proven reliability** — 30+ years of operational data, no encapsulation issues.
2. **Decade-long performance data** at Gemasolar, Andasol, Noor III, and Crescent Dunes validate the technology.
3. **Favourable thermophysical properties** — non-flammable, low vapour pressure at 565 °C, chemically stable.
4. **Well-developed supply chain** — solar salt is commodity-produced in thousands of tonnes annually for fertilizer industry.
5. **High cycle stability** — molten salt survives 10,000+ thermal cycles without degradation, whereas PCMs suffer from sub-cooling, phase segregation, and container failure. *(7 marks — full credit for table + justified reasoning)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist — print and pin to your study wall.**

- **Definition:** TES = heat storage subsystem that decouples collection from generation.
- **Three mechanisms:** Sensible (SHS), Latent (LHS / PCM), Thermochemical (TCS). Know one example + governing equation for each.
- **Master formula:** $Q_{SHS} = m \, c_p \, \Delta T$. Memorise it cold.
- **Combined formula:** $Q = m [c_p(T_m - T_i) + L + c_p(T_f - T_m)]$ — frequently appears in 7-mark derivations.
- **Solar Salt:** 60 % NaNO₃ + 40 % KNO₃; $c_p = 1.55 \; \text{kJ/kg·K}$; $\rho_{liq} = 1870 \; \text{kg/m}^3$; range **290 – 565 °C**; freezing point **220 °C**.
- **Two-tank vs Thermocline:** Two-tank = gold standard, simple, reliable. Thermocline = lower capex, single tank, sand filler.
- **Performance lift:** 6 h TES → CF ~40 %; 15 h TES → CF ~70 %+.
- **Round-trip efficiency:** ~0.85–0.95 for molten-salt sensible storage.
- **Standing heat loss parameter:** $LHV_s = U A_{tank} / (m c_p)$ — a small value (0.01 – 0.02 h⁻¹) is desired.
- **Solar fraction:** $f_{solar} = Q_{storage}/Q_{demand,total}$.
- **Real-world plants to memorise:**
  - **Andasol 1 (Spain):** 50 MW_e, 7.5 h TES = 1,031 MWh_th.
  - **Gemasolar (Spain):** 19.9 MW_e, 15 h TES = 2,840 MWh_th — first 24-h solar plant.
  - **Noor III (Morocco):** 150 MW_e, 7.5 h molten-salt tower TES.
  - **Crescent Dunes (USA):** 110 MW_e, 10 h molten-salt TES.
- **Top three reasons molten salt dominates:** proven reliability, high cycle stability, established supply chain.
- **Common unit conversion to internalise:** 1 MWh = 3,600 MJ = 3.6 × 10⁶ kJ.
- **Examiner dislikes:** (i) mixing units, (ii) skipping numerical example, (iii) forgetting the latent-heat term in a combined-storage problem.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensible Heat Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT862 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sensible Heat Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sensible Heat Storage (SHS)** is a thermal energy storage (TES) methodology in which the thermal energy is accumulated within a storage medium by raising its measurable, macroscopic temperature, without undergoing any phase change (solid–liquid, liquid–gas, or solid–solid transition). The term *sensible* is derived from the Latin *sensibilis*, signifying that the stored energy can be *sensed* by a conventional thermometer as a rise in temperature of the storage material.

From a thermodynamic perspective, sensible heat storage exploits the **sensible heat capacity** (also called the *volumetric heat capacity*) of a working substance, denoted as:

$$C_{vol} = \rho \cdot c_p \quad [\text{kJ/(m}^3 \cdot \text{K)}]$$

where $\rho$ is the mass density and $c_p$ is the specific heat capacity at constant pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 – Thermal Energy Storage):**
> Sensible Heat Storage is the *first* sub-topic under Thermal Energy Storage. It is a guaranteed 7 to 14 mark question in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE) under Part B.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – The Solar Water Heater Bucket**
> Imagine a black painted bucket filled with water placed in the afternoon Sun. After a few hours, the water becomes hot enough to take a warm shower in the evening. What happened? The Sun's radiant energy was absorbed by the water, raising its temperature from (say) 25 °C to 65 °C. No ice melted, no water boiled, no chemical reaction occurred — the water just got *hotter*. That stored thermal energy, which we can measure simply by putting a finger (or a thermometer) into the water, is **sensible heat**. The water is the *sensible heat storage medium*.

In the same way, large engineering systems store heat in **water tanks**, **rock beds**, **molten salts**, or **concrete blocks** — all of them get hotter when charged and release that heat when discharged.

## 1.3 Governing Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range / Value | Unit |
|-----------|--------|----------------------|------|
| Specific heat of water | $c_{p,w}$ | **4.186** | kJ/(kg·K) |
| Specific heat of rock (granite) | $c_{p,r}$ | 0.84 | kJ/(kg·K) |
| Specific heat of molten salt (Solar Salt) | $c_{p,ms}$ | 1.55 | kJ/(kg·K) |
| Specific heat of concrete | $c_{p,c}$ | 0.88 | kJ/(kg·K) |
| Density of water at 20 °C | $\rho_w$ | **998.2** | kg/m³ |
| Density of granite | $\rho_r$ | 2750 | kg/m³ |
| Universal gas constant | $R$ | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle – The "Why" and the "How"

The fundamental physics of Sensible Heat Storage rests on the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to a control mass (the storage medium). When a quantity of heat $Q$ enters the medium at constant pressure:

1. **Energy Influx:** Thermal energy from the source (solar collector, waste heat stream, heat pump output, etc.) flows into the storage mass via conduction, convection, or radiation.
2. **Internal Energy Rise:** The internal energy of every infinitesimal mass element $dm$ increases proportionally to its specific heat capacity $c_p$.
3. **Macroscopic Temperature Change:** The aggregated increase manifests as a measurable rise in the bulk temperature from $T_i$ (initial) to $T_f$ (final).
4. **Energy Retrieval (Discharge):** When demanded, the stored energy is extracted by allowing a colder heat-transfer fluid to flow through (or past) the storage medium, which cools it back toward $T_i$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called "Sensible"?** Because a standard mercury or alcohol thermometer can detect the temperature change. This is in direct contrast to **latent heat**, where energy is absorbed/released *without* a measurable temperature change (e.g., during melting of a PCM).

## 2.2 The Governing Equation

For a homogeneous storage medium of mass $m$ and constant specific heat $c_p$, the stored sensible heat is:

$$Q_S = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_f - T_i)$$

Expressed volumetrically (preferred for engineering sizing):

$$Q_S = \rho \cdot V \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = V \cdot C_{vol} \cdot \Delta T$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption for exam purposes:** $c_p$ is assumed constant over the temperature range $[T_i, T_f]$. If temperature dependence is significant, integration $\int_{T_i}^{T_f} \rho V c_p(T)\, dT$ is required.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units | When to Use |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | $Q_S = m\,c_p\,\Delta T$ | Basic sensible heat | kJ | Quick mass-based calculation |
| 2 | $Q_S = \rho V\,c_p\,\Delta T$ | Volumetric form | kJ | Tank-sizing problems |
| 3 | $C_{vol} = \rho c_p$ | Volumetric heat capacity | kJ/(m³·K) | Material selection |
| 4 | $\eta_{SHS} = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$ | Round-trip efficiency | dimensionless | Performance evaluation |
| 5 | $\dot{Q} = \dfrac{m c_p \Delta T}{t}$ | Rate of charging | kW | Time-bound problems |
| 6 | $T(t) = T_\infty - (T_\infty - T_i)e^{-t/\tau}$ | Lumped capacitance transient | °C | Charging/discharging time analysis |
| 7 | $\tau = \dfrac{\rho V c_p}{h A_s}$ | Thermal time constant | s | Lumped-capacitance validity |
| 8 | $\dot{Q}_{loss} = U A (T_{avg} - T_{amb})$ | Heat loss to ambient | W | Insulation design |
| 9 | $LMTD = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log mean temp difference | K | Heat exchanger rating |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU valuation, if you use a wrong sign convention for $\Delta T$, **2 marks are forfeited immediately**. Always define $\Delta T = T_{final} - T_{initial}$ explicitly in the solution.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) Plants:** Molten-salt SHS enables round-the-clock electricity generation by storing solar heat collected during the day for use at night.
- **District Heating Networks:** Large water-based SHS tanks (often called *accumulators*) buffer supply and demand mismatches in city-scale heating grids.
- **Building HVAC:** Strapped concrete cores or pebble-bed stores decouple peak daytime cooling/heating loads from chiller/boiler operation.
- **Industrial Waste Heat Recovery:** Refractory-lined SHS units capture intermittent flue gas heat for later use in preheating combustion air.
- **Aquifer Thermal Storage (ATES):** Natural groundwater aquifers are used as seasonal sensible heat stores in Scandinavian and Northern European countries.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Fundamental SHS Energy Equation from First Principles

**Starting point:** First Law for a closed system (no mass inflow/outflow) at constant pressure.

$$Q - W = \Delta U$$

For a constant-pressure process, the work done by expansion is $W = p \Delta V$, so the First Law becomes:

$$Q_p = \Delta U + p \Delta V = \Delta H$$

where $H$ is the enthalpy. For a homogeneous mass $m$:

$$Q_p = m \int_{T_i}^{T_f} c_p(T)\, dT$$

Assuming $c_p$ is constant over the operating temperature window:

$$Q_p = m\,c_p \int_{T_i}^{T_f} dT = m\,c_p\,(T_f - T_i)$$

$$\boxed{Q_S = m\,c_p\,\Delta T \quad \text{(Sensible Heat Stored)}}$$

Dividing both sides by volume $V = m/\rho$:

$$Q_S = \rho\,V\,c_p\,\Delta T$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 – Material Selection Index (Volumetric Heat Capacity)

Two candidate media may store the same energy per unit mass, but one may occupy a much smaller volume. The engineering selection criterion for SHS is the **volumetric heat capacity**:

$$C_{vol} = \rho\,c_p$$

**Comparison Table for Common SHS Media (at ~300 K):**

| Medium | $\rho$ (kg/m³) | $c_p$ (kJ/kg·K) | $C_{vol}$ (kJ/m³·K) | Ranking |
|--------|---------------|------------------|---------------------|---------|
| Water (liquid) | 998 | 4.186 | **4178** | 1 |
| Magnetite (rock) | 5080 | 0.68 | 3454 | 2 |
| Concrete | 2300 | 0.88 | 2024 | 3 |
| Sand (dry) | 1500 | 0.84 | 1260 | 4 |
| Granite | 2750 | 0.84 | 2310 | 5 |
| Solar Salt (molten) | 1800 | 1.55 | **2790** | — |
| Cast iron | 7200 | 0.46 | 3312 | — |

**Inference:** Although molten salt has a higher $c_p$ than granite, water wins because of its exceptionally high density of heat *per unit volume*, making liquid water tanks the most compact SHS at low-to-moderate temperatures.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 – Charging Time Under Lumped Capacitance

When a storage medium is heated by a hot fluid at constant temperature $T_\infty$ with convective heat-transfer coefficient $h$ and surface area $A_s$, the energy balance on the lumped mass is:

$$\rho V c_p \frac{dT}{dt} = h A_s (T_\infty - T)$$

Separating variables:

$$\frac{dT}{T_\infty - T} = \frac{h A_s}{\rho V c_p}\, dt$$

Integrating from $T_i$ at $t = 0$ to $T(t)$ at time $t$:

$$-\ln(T_\infty - T) \Big|_{T_i}^{T(t)} = \frac{h A_s}{\rho V c_p}\, t$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{T_\infty - T_i}{T_\infty - T(t)}\right) = \frac{t}{\tau}$$

where the thermal time constant $\tau = \dfrac{\rho V c_p}{h A_s}$.

Solving for the temperature evolution:

$$\boxed{T(t) = T_\infty - (T_\infty - T_i)\, e^{-t/\tau}}$$

The **charging time** to reach 95 % of the source temperature ($T - T_i = 0.95 (T_\infty - T_i)$) is:

$$t_{95\%} = -\tau \ln(0.05) \approx 3.0\,\tau$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The lumped-capacitance model is valid only when the **Biot number** $\mathrm{Bi} = \dfrac{h L_c}{k} < 0.1$, where $L_c = V/A_s$ is the characteristic length and $k$ is the thermal conductivity.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 – Heat Loss from an Uninsulated Cylindrical Tank

A cylindrical storage tank of radius $r$, height $H$, and overall heat-transfer coefficient $U$ experiences ambient losses. The steady-state loss rate is:

$$\dot{Q}_{loss} = U A (T_{avg} - T_{amb})$$

Surface area for a vertical cylinder (side + top + bottom, neglecting bottom if on ground):

$$A = 2\pi r H + \pi r^2$$

Energy lost over a holding period $t_h$:

$$Q_{loss} = U A (T_{avg} - T_{amb})\, t_h$$

The fractional loss:

$$f_{loss} = \frac{Q_{loss}}{Q_S} = \frac{U A (T_{avg} - T_{amb})\, t_h}{\rho V c_p \Delta T}$$

This metric directly drives the economic selection of insulation thickness, as $U$ scales approximately as $1/x$ where $x$ is the insulation thickness.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture of a Generic Sensible Heat Storage System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Heat Source: Solar Field / Waste Stream] --> B[Heat Exchanger / Charging Loop]
    B --> C[Storage Vessel: Water / Rock Bed / Molten Salt]
    C --> D[Thermocline Layer]
    D --> E[Discharge Heat Exchanger]
    E --> F[End User: Turbine / District Heat / Process]
    F --> G[Cooled Return Fluid]
    G --> C
    C --> H[Ambient Losses via Insulation]
    H --> I[Environment]

    subgraph Monitoring
        J[Temperature Sensors]
        K[Flow Meters]
        L[Control System]
    end

    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> B
    L --> E
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow of Charging & Discharging Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ChargingPhase
        S1[Hot HTF enters at T_high] --> S2[Convective heat transfer]
        S2 --> S3[Solid or liquid medium warms]
        S3 --> S4[Energy stored as Q = m cp ΔT]
    end

    subgraph HoldingPhase
        H1[Insulated tank] --> H2[Losses U A T_avg minus T_amb]
    end

    subgraph DischargingPhase
        D1[Cold HTF enters] --> D2[Medium cools]
        D2 --> D3[Heat transferred to HTF]
        D3 --> D4[Useful work or heat delivered]
    end

    S4 --> H1
    H1 --> D1
```

## 4.3 Material Selection Decision Tree for SHS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Choose SHS Medium] --> Q1{Operating Temperature Range?}
    Q1 -->|Below 100 deg C| Q2{Compactness critical?}
    Q1 -->|100 to 400 deg C| Q3{Max temp?}
    Q1 -->|Above 400 deg C| Q4[Refractory concrete or molten salt]

    Q2 -->|Yes| Water[Liquid Water Tank]
    Q2 -->|No| Earth[Earth or Aquifer Storage]

    Q3 -->|Below 250 deg C| ThermalOil[Thermal Oil + Concrete]
    Q3 -->|Above 250 deg C| MoltenSalt[Solar Salt or Carbonate Salt]

    Q4 --> Q5{Repeated thermal cycling?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| Ceramic[Castable Ceramic]
    Q5 -->|No| SolidBlock[Refractory Block]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define sensible heat storage. Mention any two sensible heat storage materials used in solar thermal applications.**

**Model Answer:**

Sensible heat storage is the method of storing thermal energy by raising the temperature of a storage medium, without any phase change occurring in the medium. The amount of heat stored is given by $Q_S = m\,c_p\,\Delta T$. The stored heat can be sensed as a temperature rise using a thermometer, hence the name "sensible".

**Two commonly used SHS materials:**
1. **Water** – used in low-temperature (< 100 °C) applications such as domestic solar water heaters and district heating accumulators.
2. **Molten salt (e.g., Solar Salt, a binary mixture of 60 % NaNO₃ and 40 % KNO₃)** – used in high-temperature (~290–565 °C) concentrated solar power plants.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 each for two materials)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List four desirable properties of a sensible heat storage material.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **High specific heat capacity ($c_p$)** – more energy per unit mass per degree rise.
2. **High density ($\rho$)** – compact storage footprint.
3. **Good thermal conductivity ($k$)** – faster charging and discharging.
4. **Chemical stability & non-corrosiveness** – long operating life without degradation.
5. *(Bonus: low cost, non-toxicity, compatibility with containment materials.)*

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write only the formula $Q = m c_p \Delta T$ and substitute numbers without (a) specifying units, (b) justifying why their chosen medium is appropriate, or (c) computing heat losses. This typically costs **3–5 marks** even when the arithmetic is correct. Always include: problem data → formula → substitution → result → engineering comment.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** – [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

A solar thermal collector delivers 1500 kJ of heat per minute to a cylindrical water-storage tank. The tank holds **500 kg** of water initially at **30 °C** and is charged until the bulk water temperature reaches **80 °C**. Take $c_{p,water} = 4.186$ kJ/(kg·K).

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the sensible heat stored and the time required for full charging.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 – Identify the data.**
- Mass of water: $m = 500$ kg
- Initial temperature: $T_i = 30$ °C
- Final temperature: $T_f = 80$ °C
- Specific heat: $c_p = 4.186$ kJ/(kg·K)
- Heat input rate: $\dot{Q} = 1500$ kJ/min

**Step 2 – Compute the temperature rise.**

$$\Delta T = T_f - T_i = 80 - 30 = 50\ \text{K}$$

(Note: a temperature *difference* in °C equals the same value in K.)

**Step 3 – Compute the stored sensible heat.**

$$Q_S = m\,c_p\,\Delta T = 500 \times 4.186 \times 50$$

$$Q_S = 500 \times 4.186 \times 50 = 104{,}650\ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 4 – Compute the charging time.**

$$t = \frac{Q_S}{\dot{Q}} = \frac{104{,}650}{1500} = 69.77\ \text{min}$$

**Step 5 – Present the answer.**

$$\boxed{Q_S = 1.0465 \times 10^5\ \text{kJ} \quad ; \quad t \approx 69.77\ \text{min} \approx 1\ \text{h}\ 10\ \text{min}}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for correct formula, 2 for substitution, 2 for final answer, 1 for unit consistency)*

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** If the tank stands for 8 hours losing heat to ambient at an average U·A of 8 W/K with $T_{amb} = 25$ °C and the average tank temperature during the hold period is 55 °C, calculate the heat lost and the residual energy.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 – Temperature driving force.**

$$\Delta T_{loss} = T_{avg} - T_{amb} = 55 - 25 = 30\ \text{K}$$

**Step 2 – Convert holding time to seconds.**

$$t_h = 8\ \text{h} = 8 \times 3600 = 28{,}800\ \text{s}$$

**Step 3 – Compute heat loss.**

$$\dot{Q}_{loss} = U A\,(T_{avg} - T_{amb}) = 8 \times 30 = 240\ \text{W} = 0.240\ \text{kW}$$

$$Q_{loss} = \dot{Q}_{loss} \times t_h = 0.240 \times 28{,}800 = 6912\ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 4 – Compute residual sensible heat.**

$$Q_{residual} = Q_S - Q_{loss} = 104{,}650 - 6912 = 97{,}738\ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 5 – Equivalent residual temperature drop.**

$$\Delta T_{loss,\,eq} = \frac{Q_{loss}}{m c_p} = \frac{6912}{500 \times 4.186} = 3.30\ \text{K}$$

**Step 6 – Present the answer.**

$$\boxed{Q_{loss} = 6912\ \text{kJ} \quad ; \quad Q_{residual} = 97{,}738\ \text{kJ} \approx 9.77 \times 10^4\ \text{kJ}}$$

**Engineering comment:** The tank loses only **6.6 %** of its stored energy over an 8-hour hold, demonstrating the effectiveness of insulated water-based SHS for diurnal storage.

*(7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for numerical evaluation, 2 for residual energy calculation, 1 for engineering commentary)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice)** – [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]

A concentrated solar power plant uses a **molten-salt** sensible heat storage system. The salt mixture has $c_p = 1.55$ kJ/(kg·K) and density $\rho = 1800$ kg/m³. The salt is heated from **290 °C** to **565 °C**.

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** Determine the mass and volume of salt required to store **350 MWh** of thermal energy.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 – Convert energy to kJ.**

$$Q_S = 350\ \text{MWh} = 350 \times 3600\ \text{MJ} = 1.26 \times 10^6\ \text{MJ} = 1.26 \times 10^9\ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 2 – Compute the temperature rise.**

$$\Delta T = 565 - 290 = 275\ \text{K}$$

**Step 3 – Solve for mass.**

$$m = \frac{Q_S}{c_p\,\Delta T} = \frac{1.26 \times 10^9}{1.55 \times 275} = \frac{1.26 \times 10^9}{426.25}$$

$$m = 2.956 \times 10^6\ \text{kg} \approx 2956\ \text{tonnes}$$

**Step 4 – Compute volume.**

$$V = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{2.956 \times 10^6}{1800} = 1642\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 5 – Present the answer.**

$$\boxed{m \approx 2.956 \times 10^6\ \text{kg} \quad ; \quad V \approx 1642\ \text{m}^3}$$

*(7 marks)*

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** A circular cross-section storage tank of diameter 12 m is to hold the molten salt. Find (i) the required height of the tank, and (ii) the volumetric heat capacity of the salt.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 – Compute cross-sectional area.**

$$A_c = \frac{\pi}{4}\,d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}\,(12)^2 = 36\pi = 113.10\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 – Required height.**

$$h = \frac{V}{A_c} = \frac{1642}{113.10} = 14.52\ \text{m}$$

**Step 3 – Volumetric heat capacity.**

$$C_{vol} = \rho\,c_p = 1800 \times 1.55 = 2790\ \text{kJ/(m}^3\cdot\text{K)}$$

**Step 4 – Verification using volumetric formula.**

$$Q_S = V \cdot C_{vol} \cdot \Delta T = 1642 \times 2790 \times 275 = 1.26 \times 10^9\ \text{kJ} \checkmark$$

**Step 5 – Present the answer.**

$$\boxed{h \approx 14.52\ \text{m} \quad ; \quad C_{vol} = 2790\ \text{kJ/(m}^3\cdot\text{K)}}$$

**Engineering comment:** A 12 m diameter tank standing ~14.5 m tall is a typical footprint for commercial CSP molten-salt stores (e.g., Gemasolar, Spain).

*(7 marks: 2 for cross-section, 2 for height, 2 for $C_{vol}$, 1 for verification + comment)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist – Sensible Heat Storage**

- [x] **Definition:** SHS stores heat by *raising* the temperature of a single-phase medium; no phase change is involved.
- [x] **Core Equation:** $Q_S = m\,c_p\,\Delta T = \rho\,V\,c_p\,\Delta T$
- [x] **Selection Metric:** Volumetric heat capacity $C_{vol} = \rho c_p$ (favours dense, high-$c_p$ materials).
- [x] **Best low-temperature medium:** **Liquid water** ($C_{vol} \approx 4178$ kJ/m³·K).
- [x] **Best mid-temperature medium:** **Solar Salt (60 % NaNO₃ + 40 % KNO₃)**.
- [x] **Lumped Capacitance Model:** Valid only when Biot number $\mathrm{Bi} = h L_c / k < 0.1$.
- [x] **Thermal Time Constant:** $\tau = \rho V c_p / (h A_s)$ — dictates charging/discharging time.
- [x] **Loss Equation:** $\dot{Q}_{loss} = U A (T_{avg} - T_{amb})$; insulation reduces $U$.
