- [x] **Advantages:** Simple, low cost, mature technology, reversible over many cycles.
- [x] **Disadvantages:** Lower energy density than latent heat or thermochemical storage; temperature drops during discharge (unless thermocline-managed).
- [x] **Engineering Examples:** Solar water heaters, district heating tanks, CSP molten-salt stores, ATES, pebble-bed air heaters.
- [x] **Common Exam Mistake:** Confusing °C with K in $\Delta T$ — they are *equal in magnitude* but must be acknowledged.
- [x] **Sign Convention:** Always $\Delta T = T_{final} - T_{initial}$ when computing stored energy.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Latent Heat Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT862 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Latent Heat Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Latent Heat Storage — Section 1: Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Latent Heat Storage (LHS)**, also called **Latent Heat Thermal Energy Storage (LHTES)**, is a thermal energy storage technique that exploits the **high enthalpy change associated with a reversible phase transition** of a material to store, retain, and release energy at an approximately **constant operating temperature**. The energy is absorbed or released **isothermally** during the phase change (most commonly the **solid–liquid** transition), making LHS a high-energy-density alternative to conventional sensible heat storage.

The material that undergoes the phase change is termed a **Phase Change Material (PCM)**. The energy stored per unit mass during the phase transition equals the **specific latent heat of fusion (L_f)** of the PCM.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT] Concept Picture: "The Ice Cube in a Glass of Water"
> Imagine placing an ice cube (at 0 °C) into a glass of warm water (at 25 °C). The ice *absorbs* a huge amount of heat from the water — yet **the ice temperature does not change** while it melts. That absorbed heat is "hidden" inside the molecular rearrangement from a solid lattice to a liquid — this hidden (latent) heat amounts to **334 kJ/kg for water**, which is the energy required to break the hydrogen bonds in the crystalline ice structure. LHTES uses this exact principle **in reverse** — solar or waste heat *melts* a PCM during charging, and the stored latent heat is *released* on solidification to provide useful thermal energy at a near-flat temperature.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants (KTU High-Yield Values)

| Property | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Latent heat of fusion of water/ice | L_f,water | **334** | kJ/kg |
| Latent heat of fusion of paraffin (typical) | L_f,par | **150 – 250** | kJ/kg |
| Latent heat of fusion of salt hydrate (Glauber's salt) | L_f,sh | **250 – 330** | kJ/kg |
| Density of water at 4 °C | ρ_w | **1000** | kg/m³ |
| Specific heat of liquid water | c_p,w | **4.18** | kJ/(kg·K) |

> [!NOTE] Why LHS matters in Renewable Energy
> Solar radiation is **intermittent** — sunshine hours do not match peak electricity / heating demand. LHS decouples supply from demand by storing solar thermal energy in PCMs (paraffin, salt hydrates, fatty acids) and releasing it during the night or cloudy periods — a cornerstone technology for **solar water heaters, building HVAC, and Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) plants**.

## 1.4 Sensible vs Latent Heat Storage (Foundation Concept)

> [!NOTE] Critical Distinction
> - **Sensible Heat** — energy stored as a *temperature rise* in a medium: **Q = m·c_p·ΔT** (e.g., water in a solar tank, rocks in a storage bin).
> - **Latent Heat** — energy stored during a *phase change at constant temperature*: **Q = m·L_f** (e.g., melting ice, solidifying paraffin).
> - **Thermochemical Heat** — energy stored via *reversible chemical reactions* (highest density, but technically complex).

For a typical 20 K temperature swing, water can store only **~83.6 kJ/kg** of sensible heat, whereas melting the same mass of ice (PCM) stores **334 kJ/kg** — a **4× advantage**. When volumetric energy density is considered (ρ·L_f), this advantage grows to **~5–10×** over sensible water storage.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Latent Heat Storage — Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Phase Change Materials (PCMs)

PCMs are classified on the basis of the **type of phase transition** and the **chemical nature** of the material.

### (a) Solid–Liquid PCMs (most commercially relevant)

1. **Organic PCMs**
   - *Paraffins* (C_n H_{2n+2}) — chemically stable, non-corrosive, predictable melting; e.g., n-docosane (T_m ≈ 44 °C), n-eicosane (T_m ≈ 36 °C).
   - *Fatty acids & esters* — caprylic acid (T_m ≈ 16 °C), capric acid (T_m ≈ 31 °C), stearic acid (T_m ≈ 69 °C).
   - *Advantages*: no supercooling, chemically inert, reproducible cycling.
   - *Disadvantages*: low thermal conductivity (~0.2 W/m·K), flammable, moderate latent heat.

2. **Inorganic PCMs**
   - *Salt hydrates* — Glauber's salt (Na₂SO₄·10H₂O, T_m ≈ 32 °C), Calcium chloride hexahydrate (CaCl₂·6H₂O, T_m ≈ 29 °C).
   - *Metallic alloys* — low-melting eutectics of Bi–Pb–Sn, used for high-temperature storage.
   - *Advantages*: high latent heat, low cost, higher thermal conductivity.
   - *Disadvantages*: supercooling, phase segregation, corrosiveness, volume change.

3. **Eutectic Mixtures**
   - Mixtures of two or more PCMs (organic–organic, organic–inorganic, inorganic–inorganic) engineered to give a *sharp, single melting point*.
   - Example: 65 % naphthalene + 35 % benzoic acid → T_m ≈ 67 °C.

### (b) Solid–Solid PCMs
   - Phase change occurs between two solid crystalline forms (e.g., polyethylene glycol transitions, layered perovskites).
   - *Advantage*: negligible volume change, no leakage.
   - *Disadvantage*: lower latent heat than solid–liquid PCMs.

### (c) Solid–Gas and Liquid–Gas PCMs
   - *Not used in practice* due to the enormous volume change (gas occupies ~1000× the volume of liquid), requiring heavy containment.

## 2.2 Desirable Thermophysical Properties of an Ideal PCM (KTU Board-Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT] The 10 Commandments of a "Good" PCM
> 1. **Suitable melting temperature** in the desired operating range.
> 2. **High latent heat of fusion** (L_f > 200 kJ/kg desirable).
> 3. **High specific heat** to provide additional sensible storage.
> 4. **High thermal conductivity** (k > 0.5 W/m·K) — to enhance charge/discharge rates.
> 5. **Chemical stability** over thousands of melt–freeze cycles.
> 6. **No supercooling** (or minimal, < 1–2 K) during solidification.
> 7. **No phase segregation** on repeated cycling.
> 8. **Small volume change** on melting (< 10 %).
> 9. **Non-toxic, non-flammable, non-corrosive**.
> 10. **Low cost & abundant**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Table)

> [!NOTE] Use this table as the single source of truth for numerical problems.

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Q = m·L_f | Latent heat stored (mass m, latent heat L_f) | J or kJ |
| 2 | Q = m·c_p·ΔT | Sensible heat stored | J or kJ |
| 3 | Q_total = m·[c_{ps}(T_m - T_i) + L_f + c_{pl}(T_f - T_m)] | Total heat (solid sensible + latent + liquid sensible) | J or kJ |
| 4 | q' = ρ·L_f | Volumetric latent heat storage density | J/m³ or kJ/m³ |
| 5 | Ste = c_p·ΔT / L_f | Stefan number (ratio of sensible to latent) | dimensionless |
| 6 | Fo = α·t / L_c² | Fourier number (transient heat conduction) | dimensionless |
| 7 | Bi = h·L_c / k | Biot number (surface vs internal conduction) | dimensionless |
| 8 | s(t) = √(2·k·ΔT·t / (ρ·L_f)) | Solid–liquid interface position (semi-infinite slab, Stefan solution) | m |
| 9 | τ = s²·ρ·L_f / (2·k·ΔT) | Characteristic solidification time | s |
| 10 | η = E_out / E_in | Round-trip storage efficiency | % (decimal) |
| 11 | T(t) ≈ T_m ± (ΔT)·exp(-h·A·t / (m·c_p,eff)) | Lumped-capacitance temperature decay | K |

where
- α = k / (ρ·c_p) is the **thermal diffusivity** (m²/s)
- L_c is a **characteristic length** (e.g., volume / surface area of PCM container)
- h is the **convective heat transfer coefficient** (W/m²·K)
- ΔT = T_m - T_s is the **driving temperature difference** for the Stefan problem

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of LHTES

- **Solar Water Heaters (SWH)**: paraffin wax PCMs in encapsulated spheres store daytime solar heat for night-time water heating.
- **Building Thermal Management**: PCM-impregnated wallboards, ceiling tiles, and plaster flatten indoor temperature swings.
- **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP)**: high-temperature salt eutectics (e.g., Solar Salt — 60 % NaNO₃ + 40 % KNO₃, T_m ≈ 220 °C) provide dispatchable thermal power.
- **Electronics Cooling**: low-melting metallic PCMs absorb transient heat from CPUs/IGBTs.
- **Cold-Chain Logistics & Vaccine Storage**: ice-based PCMs maintain 2–8 °C during transport.
- **Spacecraft Thermal Control**: solid–solid PCMs regulate instrument temperatures in orbit.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Latent Heat Storage — Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Energy Stored per Unit Mass of PCM

**Scenario**: A PCM initially at temperature **T_i** (below its melting point T_m) is heated to a final temperature **T_f** (above T_m). The heating proceeds in **three sequential stages**: (1) heating of solid, (2) melting at T_m, (3) heating of liquid.

### Step-by-Step Algebraic Expansion

**Stage 1 — Sensible heating of the solid phase (T_i → T_m):**

The amount of heat absorbed per unit mass equals the specific heat of the solid times the temperature change:

$$
q_1 \;=\; c_{p,s}\,\bigl(T_m - T_i\bigr)
$$

*Logic*: no phase change occurs; the temperature rises linearly (in time) with the solid's heat capacity.

**Stage 2 — Latent heat of fusion at constant T_m:**

The PCM absorbs its latent heat of fusion while the temperature is held at T_m:

$$
q_2 \;=\; L_f
$$

*Logic*: the entire enthalpy input is consumed in breaking the crystalline lattice bonds.

**Stage 3 — Sensible heating of the liquid phase (T_m → T_f):**

$$
q_3 \;=\; c_{p,l}\,\bigl(T_f - T_m\bigr)
$$

**Total energy stored per unit mass (q = Q/m):**

$$
q \;=\; c_{p,s}\,(T_m - T_i) \;+\; L_f \;+\; c_{p,l}\,(T_f - T_m)
$$

For a *constant* specific heat approximation c_{p,s} ≈ c_{p,l} = c_p, this reduces to:

$$
q \;=\; c_p\,(T_f - T_i) \;+\; L_f
$$

**Engineering interpretation**: the latent term L_f usually dominates (typically 70–90 % of q for moderate ΔT), confirming why LHS offers 5–10× the energy density of sensible storage.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stefan Number and Interface Position in a Semi-Infinite PCM Slab (Classical Stefan Problem)

**Problem Statement**: A semi-infinite slab of solid PCM initially at its melting temperature T_m occupies the region x > 0. At t = 0, the boundary x = 0 is suddenly cooled to a constant wall temperature T_s < T_m, initiating directional solidification. A planar solid–liquid interface propagates at position x = s(t).

### Governing Equation and Boundary Conditions

The temperature T(x, t) in the **liquid region** (0 < x < s(t)) satisfies the one-dimensional transient heat equation:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2} \;=\; \frac{1}{\alpha}\,\frac{\partial T}{\partial t}
$$

**Boundary and initial conditions**:

$$
T(0,t) \;=\; T_s \qquad T\bigl(s(t),t\bigr) \;=\; T_m \qquad s(0) \;=\; 0
$$

**Assumption** (quasi-steady / *Neumann* solution): the temperature profile in the liquid is treated as quasi-steady because the latent heat sink dominates the heat capacity of the thin solidified shell.

$$
T(x,t) \;\approx\; T_s \;+\; (T_m - T_s)\,\frac{x}{s(t)}
$$

### Stefan Condition (Energy Balance at the Moving Interface)

Heat conducted to the interface (from the warm liquid) equals the rate of latent heat released by the advancing solid front:

$$
\rho\,L_f\,\frac{ds}{dt} \;=\; k\,\frac{T_m - T_s}{s(t)}
$$

*Logic*: Fourier's law gives the conductive flux at x = s(t); the latent heat released per unit area per unit time is ρ·L_f·(ds/dt).

### Separation of Variables and Integration

Multiply both sides by s and rearrange:

$$
s\,\frac{ds}{dt} \;=\; \frac{k\,(T_m - T_s)}{\rho\,L_f}
$$

$$
s\,ds \;=\; \frac{k\,(T_m - T_s)}{\rho\,L_f}\,dt
$$

Integrate from 0 to s and 0 to t (using s(0) = 0):

$$
\frac{s^2}{2} \;=\; \frac{k\,(T_m - T_s)}{\rho\,L_f}\,t
$$

### Closed-Form Result for the Interface Position

$$
s(t) \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,k\,(T_m - T_s)\,t}{\rho\,L_f}}
$$

Introducing the **Stefan number** Ste = c_p·(T_m - T_s) / L_f, the expression can be rewritten in the elegant form:

$$
s(t) \;=\; 2\,\lambda\,\sqrt{\alpha\,t} \qquad\text{where}\qquad \lambda\,\exp(\lambda^2)\,\mathrm{erf}(\lambda) \;=\; \dfrac{c_p\,(T_m - T_s)}{L_f\,\sqrt{\pi}} \;=\; \dfrac{Ste}{\sqrt{\pi}}
$$

For the **small-Ste limit** (Ste ≪ 1, i.e., latent heat dominates sensible heat):

$$
\lambda \;\approx\; Ste \quad\Longrightarrow\quad s(t) \;\approx\; 2\,Ste\,\sqrt{\alpha\,t}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] The √t Law
> The interface advances as the **square root of time** — a hallmark of diffusion-controlled (and phase-change-controlled) processes. Doubling the time does NOT double the solidified thickness; it only multiplies s by **√2 ≈ 1.414**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Volumetric Storage Density and Comparison with Sensible Storage

**Definition**: Volumetric latent heat storage capacity:

$$
\rho_e \;=\; \rho \cdot L_f
$$

**Comparison** (per cubic metre of storage medium):

For water (sensible, ΔT = 20 K, c_p = 4.18 kJ/(kg·K), ρ = 1000 kg/m³):

$$
\rho_{e,\text{water}} \;=\; \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \;=\; 1000 \times 4.18 \times 20 \;=\; 83{,}600 \;\text{kJ/m}^3
$$

For a paraffin PCM (L_f = 200 kJ/kg, ρ = 800 kg/m³):

$$
\rho_{e,\text{PCM}} \;=\; 800 \times 200 \;=\; 160{,}000 \;\text{kJ/m}^3
$$

**Ratio**:

$$
\frac{\rho_{e,\text{PCM}}}{\rho_{e,\text{water}}} \;=\; \frac{160{,}000}{83{,}600} \;\approx\; 1.91
$$

For ice (ρ = 917 kg/m³, L_f = 334 kJ/kg):

$$
\rho_{e,\text{ice}} \;=\; 917 \times 334 \;\approx\; 306{,}000 \;\text{kJ/m}^3
$$

**Ratio**:

$$
\frac{\rho_{e,\text{ice}}}{\rho_{e,\text{water}}} \;\approx\; 3.66
$$

> [!NOTE] LHS volumetric energy density is typically **2× to 10× greater** than sensible water storage — a critical advantage in space-constrained applications (building walls, vehicle batteries, spacecraft).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Round-Trip Storage Efficiency

Round-trip efficiency accounts for thermal losses during the charge–hold–discharge cycle:

$$
\eta \;=\; \frac{E_{\text{discharged}}}{E_{\text{charged}}} \;\times\; 100\,\%
$$

For a hold time τ_h at temperature T_m, with an overall heat loss coefficient UA:

$$
\eta \;\approx\; 1 \;-\; \frac{UA\,(T_m - T_{\text{amb}})\,\tau_h}{m\,L_f}
$$

*Logic*: energy lost to ambient during the hold period reduces the recovered useful energy.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem)

**Question**: A PCM has melting point **T_m = 60 °C**, latent heat of fusion **L_f = 200 kJ/kg**, and specific heat **c_p = 2.5 kJ/(kg·K)** in *both* solid and liquid states. The PCM is heated from **T_i = 30 °C** to **T_f = 90 °C**. Calculate the total energy stored per kg and the percentage contribution of latent heat.

**Step 1 — Apply the three-stage formula:**

$$
q \;=\; c_{p,s}(T_m - T_i) + L_f + c_{p,l}(T_f - T_m)
$$

$$
q \;=\; 2.5 \times (60 - 30) + 200 + 2.5 \times (90 - 60)
$$

$$
q \;=\; 2.5 \times 30 + 200 + 2.5 \times 30
$$

$$
q \;=\; 75 + 200 + 75 \;=\; \boxed{350\;\text{kJ/kg}}
$$

**Step 2 — Latent heat fraction:**

$$
f_{L} \;=\; \frac{L_f}{q} \;=\; \frac{200}{350} \;\approx\; 0.571 \;=\; \boxed{57.1\,\%}
$$

**Step 3 — Sensible heat contribution (combined):**

$$
f_S \;=\; 1 - f_L \;\approx\; 42.9\,\%
$$

> [!NOTE] KTU Valuation Tip: Always quote your answer in **kJ/kg with 3 significant figures**, and explicitly state each stage's contribution — the examiner awards 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for sensible heat, 1 mark for latent heat, 1 mark for the total, 1 mark for the percentage, and 1 mark for units/dimensional check.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Latent Heat Storage — Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: A Complete LHTES System

The diagram below shows the **functional architecture** of a solar-charged LHTES system with charging, storage, and discharging modes, and the supporting auxiliary loops.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[SUN Solar Radiation Source]
    nodeB[COLL Solar Collector Flat Plate or Evacuated Tube]
    nodeC[PUMP1 Charge Pump Circulates HTF]
    nodeD[HTF1 Hot Heat Transfer Fluid]
    nodeE[HEX1 Charging Heat Exchanger Transfers Heat from HTF to PCM]
    nodeF[PCM1 Phase Change Material Storage Tank Encapsulated Spheres or Macro-Encapsulation]
    nodeG[VALVE1 Three-Way Diverting Valve for Charge Discharge Mode]
    nodeH[PUMP2 Discharge Pump]
    nodeI[HTF2 Cool Heat Transfer Fluid Returns to Collector or Bypasses]
    nodeJ[HEX2 Discharging Heat Exchanger Transfers Heat from PCM to Load]
    nodeK[LOAD Building HVAC or Domestic Hot Water or Process Heat]
    nodeL[CTRL Microprocessor Controller with T_sensors]
    nodeM[T1 Sensor at Collector Outlet]
    nodeN[T2 Sensor at PCM Tank]
    nodeO[T3 Sensor at Load Inlet]
    nodeP[AMB Ambient Surroundings Heat Loss Path]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeN
    nodeN --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeP
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeB
    nodeG --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeO
    nodeO --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeP
    nodeL --> nodeC
    nodeL --> nodeG
    nodeL --> nodeH
    nodeF -. latent heat stored .-> nodeF
```

**Reading the diagram**:
- The **CHARGING** mode (daytime): SUN → COLL → HTF1 → HEX1 → PCM (melts, stores L_f).
- The **DISCHARGING** mode (night/cloudy): PCM (solidifies, releases L_f) → HEX2 → LOAD.
- The **three-way VALVE1** routes HTF between the two heat exchangers based on demand.
- The **CTRL** block reads T1, T2, T3 sensors and modulates pumps/valves to maintain setpoint.
- Heat loss to AMB is a parasitic loss reducing round-trip efficiency η.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: PCM Encapsulation Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeQ[PCM RAW MATERIAL Paraffin or Salt Hydrate]
    nodeR[ENC1 Macro-Encapsulation PCM in Spheres Tubes or Panels]
    nodeS[ENC2 Micro-Encapsulation PCM in Polymer Shells 1 to 1000 micrometre]
    nodeT[ENC3 Shape-Stabilized PCM PCM Impregnated in HDPE or Graphite Matrix]
    nodeU[ENC4 Direct Contact PCM in Shell and Tube with HTF as Working Fluid]
    nodeV[SWH Solar Water Heater with PCM Tank]
    nodeW[BUILD PCM Wallboard and Ceiling Tiles]
    nodeX[CSPC CSP Plant with Molten Salt Eutectic]
    nodeY[ELEC Electronic Cooling Heat Sinks with Metallic PCM]
    nodeZ[TEXT Smart Textile with Microencapsulated PCM]

    nodeQ --> nodeR
    nodeQ --> nodeS
    nodeQ --> nodeT
    nodeQ --> nodeU
    nodeR --> nodeV
    nodeR --> nodeW
    nodeT --> nodeX
    nodeU --> nodeY
    nodeS --> nodeZ
    nodeS --> nodeW
```

> [!NOTE] Engineering Significance of Encapsulation
> - **Macro-encapsulation**: easiest to manufacture, used in tanks and panels; risk of leakage if shell ruptures.
> - **Micro-encapsulation**: huge surface area → high heat transfer rate; used in textiles and plaster.
> - **Shape-stabilized**: PCM is held in a solid matrix even above T_m → no leakage, mechanically robust.
> - **Direct contact**: highest heat transfer coefficient (no wall resistance) but requires HTF immiscible with PCM (e.g., silicone oil with paraffin).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Latent Heat Storage — Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`**

### Q1. Define Latent Heat Storage. List any **four** desirable thermophysical properties of a Phase Change Material (PCM).

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Definition (1 mark)*: Latent Heat Storage (LHS) is a thermal energy storage technique in which energy is stored and released during the **phase transition** of a Phase Change Material (PCM) at a **nearly constant temperature**, exploiting its high **latent heat of fusion (L_f)**.

*Any four properties (½ mark each)*:
1. High latent heat of fusion (L_f > 200 kJ/kg).
2. Suitable melting point for the operating temperature range.
3. High thermal conductivity (k > 0.5 W/m·K) for fast charge/discharge.
4. Chemical stability over many melt–freeze cycles.
5. No or negligible supercooling.
6. Non-corrosive, non-toxic, low cost.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between **Sensible Heat Storage (SHS)** and **Latent Heat Storage (LHS)**. Give **one example** of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | SHS | LHS |
|---|---|---|
| Storage mechanism | Temperature rise (ΔT) | Phase change at ≈ constant T |
| Governing equation | Q = m·c_p·ΔT | Q = m·L_f |
| Temperature during discharge | Falls continuously | Remains nearly constant at T_m |
| Energy density | Low (water: ~84 kJ/m³ per 20 K) | High (paraffin: ~160,000 kJ/m³) |
| Example | Water tank in solar heater | Paraffin wax in encapsulated PCM tank |

(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for example/diagram, 1 mark for comparison table.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Internal-Choice Pattern**: Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **classification of Phase Change Materials (PCMs)** with suitable examples. Discuss the **advantages and limitations** of organic and inorganic PCMs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PCM has melting point **T_m = 58 °C**, latent heat of fusion **L_f = 180 kJ/kg**, specific heat in solid state **c_{p,s} = 2.0 kJ/(kg·K)**, and specific heat in liquid state **c_{p,l} = 2.4 kJ/(kg·K)**. The PCM is heated from **25 °C to 95 °C**. Determine:
  (i) Total energy stored per kg.
  (ii) Percentage contribution of latent heat to the total.
  (iii) Volumetric storage density if ρ = 850 kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a) — Classification of PCMs (7 marks)

**1. Classification Tree (3 marks):**

PCMs are classified by the **type of phase transition** and **chemical nature**:

- **Solid–Liquid PCMs** (most used):
  - *Organic*: Paraffins (n-eicosane, n-docosane), fatty acids (capric, stearic), esters, alcohols.
  - *Inorganic*: Salt hydrates (Glauber's salt Na₂SO₄·10H₂O, CaCl₂·6H₂O), metallic alloys.
  - *Eutectics*: Mixtures tuned for a sharp T_m (e.g., naphthalene–benzoic acid).
- **Solid–Solid PCMs**: e.g., polyethylene glycol, layered perovskites — transition between two crystalline forms.
- **Solid–Gas / Liquid–Gas PCMs**: not used (huge volume change).

**2. Organic PCMs (2 marks):**
- *Advantages*: (i) no supercooling, (ii) chemically stable & non-corrosive, (iii) reproducible cycling, (iv) available in wide T_m range.
- *Limitations*: (i) low thermal conductivity (~0.2 W/m·K) → requires fin/encapsulation enhancement, (ii) flammable, (iii) moderate L_f (~150–250 kJ/kg).

**3. Inorganic PCMs (2 marks):**
- *Advantages*: (i) high L_f (~250–330 kJ/kg for salt hydrates), (ii) higher k (~0.5–1.0 W/m·K), (iii) low cost, (iv) non-flammable.
- *Limitations*: (i) **supercooling** (liquid cools below T_m before nucleating), (ii) **phase segregation** (heavy ions settle out, reducing L_f over cycles), (iii) **corrosive** to metals, (iv) **volume change** on melting can rupture containers.

> KTU award: 1 mark for tree, 2 marks each for organic and inorganic pros/cons.

#### Model Solution to A(b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given**: T_m = 58 °C, L_f = 180 kJ/kg, c_{p,s} = 2.0 kJ/(kg·K), c_{p,l} = 2.4 kJ/(kg·K), T_i = 25 °C, T_f = 95 °C, ρ = 850 kg/m³.

**(i) Total energy stored per kg (3 marks):**

$$
q \;=\; c_{p,s}(T_m - T_i) + L_f + c_{p,l}(T_f - T_m)
$$

$$
q \;=\; 2.0 \times (58 - 25) + 180 + 2.4 \times (95 - 58)
$$

$$
q \;=\; 2.0 \times 33 + 180 + 2.4 \times 37
$$

$$
q \;=\; 66.0 + 180 + 88.8 \;=\; \boxed{334.8\;\text{kJ/kg}}
$$

**(ii) Percentage contribution of latent heat (2 marks):**

$$
f_L \;=\; \frac{L_f}{q} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{180}{334.8} \times 100 \;\approx\; \boxed{53.8\,\%}
$$

**(iii) Volumetric storage density (2 marks):**

$$
\rho_e \;=\; \rho \cdot q \;=\; 850 \times 334.8 \;\approx\; \boxed{284{,}580\;\text{kJ/m}^3} \;\approx\; 284.6\;\text{MJ/m}^3
$$

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **position of the solid–liquid interface** in a semi-infinite PCM slab using the **Stefan analysis**. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **factors affecting the selection of a PCM** for a solar thermal energy storage application. Compare latent heat storage with sensible heat storage in terms of **energy density, cost, complexity, and life cycle**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a) — Stefan Problem Derivation (7 marks)

**1. Problem Setup (1 mark):**
Consider a semi-infinite solid PCM initially at its melting point T_m, occupying x > 0. At t = 0, the surface x = 0 is suddenly cooled to T_s < T_m. A planar solid–liquid interface propagates at x = s(t).

**2. Assumptions (1 mark):**
- 1-D heat conduction in the liquid phase.
- Constant material properties (k, ρ, c_p, L_f).
- Semi-infinite domain (no far-field effect).
- Quasi-steady temperature profile in the liquid (latent heat dominates).
- Constant wall temperature T_s (Dirichlet BC).

**3. Governing Equation and BCs (1 mark):**

$$
\frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2} \;=\; \frac{1}{\alpha}\,\frac{\partial T}{\partial t}, \quad T(0,t)=T_s,\; T(s(t),t)=T_m,\; s(0)=0
$$

**4. Quasi-steady temperature profile (1 mark):**

$$
T(x,t) \;\approx\; T_s + (T_m - T_s)\,\frac{x}{s(t)}
$$

**5. Stefan condition (1 mark):**

$$
\rho\,L_f\,\frac{ds}{dt} \;=\; k\,\frac{T_m - T_s}{s(t)}
$$

**6. Integration and final result (2 marks):**

$$
s(t) \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{2\,k\,(T_m - T_s)\,t}{\rho\,L_f}}
$$

> KTU award: 1 mark each for setup/assumptions/BCs, 1 mark for quasi-steady T profile, 1 mark for Stefan condition, 1 mark for separation of variables, 1 mark for the closed-form result, 1 mark for engineering interpretation (the √t law).

#### Model Solution to B(b) — PCM Selection Factors (7 marks)

**1. Selection Criteria (3 marks):**
A PCM for solar thermal storage must satisfy:
- *Thermophysical*: suitable T_m, high L_f, high k, high c_p, low volume change, no supercooling.
- *Kinetic*: high nucleation rate, adequate crystallization rate.
- *Chemical*: long-term stability, no chemical degradation, compatibility with container.
- *Economic*: low cost, abundant raw material.
- *Environmental*: non-toxic, non-flammable, recyclable.

**2. Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | SHS (e.g., water tank) | LHS (PCM storage) |
|---|---|---|
| Energy density (per m³) | Low (84 MJ/m³ per 20 K) | High (160–300 MJ/m³) |
| Discharge temperature | Falls with time | Nearly constant at T_m |
| Volume for same storage | Large | Compact (5–10× smaller) |
| Capital cost | Low (water + tank) | Moderate–High (PCM + encapsulation) |
| Operational complexity | Simple | Moderate (encapsulation, fin enhancement) |
| Cycle life | Very high (decades) | High (1000–5000 cycles typical) |
| Maintenance | Minimal | Periodic inspection for leakage/segregation |
| Application scale | Large (district heating) | Small to medium (BIPV, electronics) |

> KTU award: 1 mark for criteria, 1 mark each for the four comparison points (energy density, cost, complexity, life cycle), 1 mark for concluding statement.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks

> [!WARNING] Common Pitfalls in LHS Problems
> 1. **Forgetting the sensible-heat term**: Students often compute *only* Q = m·L_f and ignore the c_p·ΔT contributions. KTU explicitly asks for *total* energy stored — **always include all three stages** (solid sensible, latent, liquid sensible).
> 2. **Sign error in Stefan condition**: A classic blunder is writing ρ·L_f·(ds/dt) = -k·(∂T/∂x) instead of +k·(T_m - T_s)/s. Re-derive the direction of the heat flux and you will not slip.
> 3. **Mixing units**: c_p in kJ/(kg·K) and L_f in J/kg is a deadly combination. Convert to a common unit (kJ/kg recommended) **at the start** of every problem.
> 4. **Confusing the characteristic length L_c with the slab thickness**: For Biot number, L_c = Volume / Surface area (or half-thickness for a slab). Using the full thickness leads to a Bi value that is off by a factor of 2.
> 5. **Omitting the Stefan number definition**: Examiners award at least 1 mark for a clean, textbook definition of the Stefan number. A vague "ratio" earns 0.
> 6. **Ignoring the square-root-of-time law**: When asked "when will the interface reach 5 mm?", the **inverse** of s(t) = √(2kΔT t / ρL_f) must be applied: t = ρL_f s² / (2 k ΔT). Many students get this wrong by linearising.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT] 60-Second Rapid Revision
> - **LHS** stores heat as **latent heat of fusion** during a **phase change** (usually solid–liquid) at ≈ constant temperature.
> - The **PCM** is the working material; key metric is **L_f** (kJ/kg).
> - **Total heat stored** = solid sensible + latent + liquid sensible = **m·[c_{ps}(T_m - T_i) + L_f + c_{pl}(T_f - T_m)]**.
> - **Energy density** of LHS is **5–10× higher** than water-based SHS.
> - **Three PCM classes**: Organic (paraffins, fatty acids), Inorganic (salt hydrates, metallic alloys), Eutectics.
> - **Solid–solid PCMs** avoid leakage but have lower L_f; **solid–gas/liquid–gas** are unusable due to volume change.
> - **Stefan number** Ste = c_p·ΔT / L_f quantifies the relative importance of sensible vs latent heat.
> - **Interface position** in a semi-infinite slab: **s(t) = √(2kΔT t / ρL_f)** — a √t dependence.
> - **Fourier & Biot numbers** govern the transient heat transfer regime.
> - **Encapsulation strategies**: macro, micro, shape-stabilized, direct contact — each with distinct pros/cons.
> - **Applications**: SWH, building HVAC, CSP (molten salt eutectics at 220–565 °C), cold-chain, electronics cooling, smart textiles.
> - **Key industrial PCMs**: Solar Salt (60 % NaNO₃ + 40 % KNO₃, T_m ≈ 220 °C), Glauber's salt (T_m ≈ 32 °C), n-docosane (T_m ≈ 44 °C).
> - **Always quote units** (kJ/kg, MJ/m³, °C) and **convert** to SI before calculation.
> - **Round-trip efficiency** η = E_out / E_in, degraded mainly by ambient heat loss during the hold period.

topics.title: Thermo Chemical Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT862 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermo Chemical Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Thermochemical Storage (TCS)** is an advanced mode of thermal energy storage (TES) that harnesses the **reversible endothermic/exothermic chemical reactions** of working pairs to absorb, retain, and later release large quantities of thermal energy. Unlike sensible heat storage (which heats a medium like water or molten salt) or latent heat storage (which melts/freezes a phase change material), TCS stores heat as **latent chemical potential energy** in the form of broken or reformed molecular bonds. The energy is captured during a charging (dissociation) reaction and released during a discharging (synthesis) reaction, with the products being stored separately until energy is demanded.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus for Renewable Energy Engineering (PESFT862), TCS is positioned as the **highest energy-density class** of thermal storage, achieving volumetric and gravimetric energy densities that are typically **5–10 times greater than sensible heat storage** and **2–4 times greater than latent heat storage**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Thermochemical energy storage is fundamentally based on the equilibrium thermodynamics of reversible reactions such as metal oxide redox pairs (e.g., Fe₃O₄/FeO), hydroxide decomposition (e.g., Ca(OH)₂/CaO), carbonate looping (CaCO₃/CaO), ammonia synthesis/dissociation, and metal hydride formation/decomposition.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The Invisible Heat Battery":**
> Imagine a rechargeable battery, but instead of storing electricity, it stores **heat in invisible chemical form**.
> - **Charging phase:** You feed heat (e.g., from concentrated solar radiation or industrial waste heat) into a chemical reactor. The heat *breaks* chemical bonds in a reactant, producing two products that are separated and stored in different containers — just like splitting water into hydrogen and oxygen. The energy is now locked inside the chemicals as "potential energy of separation."
> - **Storage phase:** The two separated products can sit in insulated silos for **days, weeks, or even seasons** with virtually *zero heat loss* — because no heat is being held in the form of temperature; it is held as chemical composition.
> - **Discharging phase:** When you need heat, you simply remix the two products (often with the help of a catalyst). Their chemical bonds *re-form* and release the original heat — sometimes at a higher temperature than you stored it!

This is why TCS is sometimes called **"the heat equivalent of a long-duration battery"** and is the technology of choice for **seasonal solar thermal storage**.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Bolded Constants & Ranges)

- **Theoretical Energy Density Range:** $\mathbf{500\text{–}3000\ \text{kJ/kg}$ of reactant (gravimetric) and $\mathbf{1000\text{–}4000\ \text{kJ/L}$ (volumetric)}.
- **Storage Duration:** Practically unlimited (weeks to seasons) due to negligible self-discharge.
- **Operating Temperature Window:** $\mathbf{100^\circ\text{C}}$ to over $\mathbf{1500^\circ\text{C}}$, depending on the reaction pair chosen.
- **Round-trip Efficiency:** Typically $\mathbf{70\%\text{–}90\%}$ for high-temperature systems.
- **Reference Universal Gas Constant:** $\mathbf{R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}}$.
- **Reference Molar Volume at STP:** $\mathbf{22.414\ \text{L/mol}}$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Perspective:**
> Examiners love questions that compare the three storage modes (sensible, latent, thermochemical). Always memorize that **TCS has the highest energy density but the highest complexity and capital cost**.

## 1.4 Classification of Thermochemical Storage Systems

TCS systems are categorized based on the type of reversible reaction:

1. **Solid–Gas Reactions (most common)** — Reactant pair reacts with or releases a gas.
2. **Solid–Solid Reactions** — Two solids react to form new solids.
3. **Gas–Gas Reactions** — e.g., ammonia synthesis/dissociation.

The most studied reaction classes in KTU-relevant renewable energy contexts are:

| Reaction Class | Example Pair | Typical Temperature | Energy Carrier |
|---|---|---|---|
| Metal oxide redox | Fe₃O₄ ⇌ FeO + ½O₂ | 1300–1700 °C | O₂ gas |
| Hydroxide–oxide | Ca(OH)₂ ⇌ CaO + H₂O | 500–600 °C | Steam (H₂O) |
| Carbonate–oxide | CaCO₃ ⇌ CaO + CO₂ | 800–900 °C | CO₂ |
| Metal hydride | MgH₂ ⇌ Mg + H₂ | 250–400 °C | H₂ gas |
| Ammonia | ½N₂ + 3/2 H₂ ⇌ NH₃ | 400–600 °C | NH₃ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The complete thermochemical storage cycle operates in three distinct stages. Understanding the *why* behind each step is critical for KTU ESE answers.

### Step 1: **Charging (Endothermic Dissociation)**

- **Why:** To convert externally supplied thermal energy (typically from solar concentrators, biomass combustion, or industrial waste heat) into stored chemical potential.
- **How:** Heat is supplied to the reactor where a stable compound $AB$ absorbs energy and decomposes into two separable products $A$ and $B$.
- **Energy direction:** Heat $\rightarrow$ Chemical potential.
- **Governing reaction:** $AB + \Delta H \rightarrow A + B$ (endothermic, $\Delta H > 0$).

### Step 2: **Storage (Separation Phase)**

- **Why:** To retain energy without thermal losses.
- **How:** Products $A$ and $B$ are physically separated and held in **thermally insulated vessels**. Since energy is locked in chemical bonds, no insulation of "heat" is required — only containment of the materials.
- **Storage duration:** Days to seasons.

### Step 3: **Discharging (Exothermic Synthesis)**

- **Why:** To recover thermal energy on demand.
- **How:** Products $A$ and $B$ are re-introduced into a reactor, often over a catalyst, where they recombine and release the stored energy as usable heat.
- **Energy direction:** Chemical potential $\rightarrow$ Heat.
- **Governing reaction:** $A + B \rightarrow AB + \Delta H$ (exothermic, $\Delta H < 0$ by convention but magnitude is the same).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **examination-critical**. Memorize the symbols, units, and physical meaning of each.

### Table 2.A — Essential Equations for Thermochemical Storage

| # | Equation (LaTeX) | Quantity Computed | Typical Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta H_{r} = \Delta G^\circ + T\,\Delta S^\circ$ | Reaction enthalpy from Gibbs free energy and entropy | J/mol | Determines total energy stored per mole |
| 2 | $\Delta G^\circ = -R\,T\,\ln K_{eq}$ | Gibbs free energy from equilibrium constant | J/mol | Predicts reaction spontaneity |
| 3 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{K_{eq,2}}{K_{eq,1}}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$ | Van't Hoff integrated form | dimensionless | Predicts equilibrium shift with T |
| 4 | $\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{eq}}{P^\circ}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R\,T} + \dfrac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}$ | Van't Hoff equation (pressure form) | dimensionless | Links equilibrium pressure and T |
| 5 | $q_{TCS} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{r}}{M}$ | Gravimetric energy density | J/kg | Quantifies "heat per kg of reactant" |
| 6 | $E_{vol} = \rho \cdot q_{TCS}$ | Volumetric energy density | J/m³ | Quantifies "heat per cubic meter" |
| 7 | $\eta_{RT} = \dfrac{Q_{discharge}}{Q_{charge}} \times 100\%$ | Round-trip efficiency | % | Performance benchmark |
| 8 | $\Delta T_{lift} = T_{discharge} - T_{charge}$ | Temperature lift (for heat pumps) | K | Determines upgraded output temperature |
| 9 | $\Delta S_{r} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{r}}{T_{eq}}$ | Entropy of reaction at equilibrium | J/(mol·K) | Used in Van't Hoff plots |
| 10 | $n_{product} = \dfrac{m_{reactant}}{M_{reactant}} \cdot \nu$ | Moles of product gas released | mol | Sizes storage vessels for gas |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbol Glossary:**
> - $\Delta H_r$ = reaction enthalpy (J/mol)
> - $\Delta G^\circ$ = standard Gibbs free energy change (J/mol)
> - $\Delta S^\circ$ = standard entropy change (J/(mol·K))
> - $K_{eq}$ = equilibrium constant (dimensionless or pressure-dependent)
> - $P_{eq}$ = equilibrium partial pressure (Pa)
> - $P^\circ$ = standard pressure = **101,325 Pa**
> - $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
> - $M$ = molar mass (kg/mol)
> - $\rho$ = bulk density of reactant (kg/m³)
> - $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - $\nu$ = stoichiometric coefficient of gaseous product

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

TCS is **not just academic** — it is being deployed in:

- **Concentrated Solar Power (CSP) plants** in Spain (e.g., the Gemasolar plant uses molten salt; next-gen plants target TCS for longer-duration baseload).
- **Seasonal residential heating** in northern Europe using Ca(OH)₂/CaO pairs.
- **Industrial waste heat recovery** in cement, steel, and aluminum sectors using metal oxide loops.
- **Hydrogen economy integration** using metal hydride storage coupled with renewable power-to-heat pathways.
- **Building-integrated energy storage** for net-zero construction under the EU Energy Performance of Buildings Directive.

The engineering rationale is straightforward: as renewable penetration grows past **30–40%** grid share, **long-duration seasonal storage** becomes essential — and only TCS economically delivers the required energy density over multi-month durations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Van't Hoff Equation (Pressure Form)

The Van't Hoff equation is the **thermodynamic backbone** of thermochemical storage. It predicts the equilibrium pressure–temperature relationship that determines when a reaction pair will store or release heat. The derivation proceeds from first principles.

### Starting Point: Gibbs–Helmholtz Relationship

We begin with the Gibbs–Helmholtz equation, which links the temperature dependence of the equilibrium constant to the standard reaction enthalpy:

$$
\frac{d(\ln K_{eq})}{dT} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R\,T^{2}}
$$

**Step 1 — Assumption of temperature-independent enthalpy:**

For a moderately narrow temperature range (typical for a single TCS reaction pair), we assume $\Delta H^\circ$ is approximately constant with temperature. This is justified when the reaction involves only solids and a single ideal gas.

$$
\frac{d(\ln K_{eq})}{dT} \approx \frac{\Delta H^\circ_{const}}{R\,T^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate:**

$$
\int d(\ln K_{eq}) = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R} \int \frac{dT}{T^{2}}
$$

Evaluating both indefinite integrals:

$$
\ln K_{eq} = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R\,T} + C
$$

where $C$ is the integration constant.

**Step 3 — Determine the integration constant from standard-state thermodynamics:**

Using the fundamental relation $\Delta G^\circ = -R\,T\,\ln K_{eq}$, we have:

$$
\ln K_{eq} = -\frac{\Delta G^\circ}{R\,T}
$$

By definition, $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\,\Delta S^\circ$. Substituting:

$$
\ln K_{eq} = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ - T\,\Delta S^\circ}{R\,T} = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R\,T} + \frac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare to integration result and identify C:**

Comparing the integrated form $\ln K_{eq} = -\dfrac{\Delta H^\circ}{R\,T} + C$ with the result above, we identify the integration constant:

$$
C = \frac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}
$$

**Step 5 — Specialize to gas-phase equilibrium:**

For a reaction that produces (or consumes) an ideal gas at partial pressure $P_{eq}$, the equilibrium constant is:

$$
K_{eq} = \frac{P_{eq}}{P^\circ}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{eq}}{P^\circ}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R\,T} + \frac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}
$$

This is the celebrated **Van't Hoff equation in pressure form**. It is a linear relation when $\ln(P_{eq}/P^\circ)$ is plotted against $1/T$, with slope $-\Delta H^\circ / R$ and intercept $\Delta S^\circ / R$.

### Verification of Correctness

At equilibrium, increasing temperature shifts the reaction in the **endothermic** direction (Le Chatelier's principle). For dissociation $AB \rightarrow A + B$, an increase in $T$ raises $P_{eq}$, which is consistent with the negative slope in the Van't Hoff plot because $\Delta H^\circ > 0$ for endothermic dissociation.

## 3.2 Derivation of Gravimetric Energy Density

The gravimetric energy density expresses how much thermal energy can be stored per kilogram of reactant.

**Step 1 — Define molar stored energy:**

For a reaction $AB \rightarrow A + B$ with enthalpy $\Delta H_r$ (J/mol), each mole of $AB$ absorbs $\Delta H_r$ joules of energy when dissociated.

**Step 2 — Convert to per-mass basis:**

If $M$ is the molar mass of reactant $AB$ (in kg/mol), then one kilogram contains $1/M$ moles. Therefore, the energy stored per kilogram is:

$$
q_{TCS} = \frac{\Delta H_{r}}{M}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply to a worked numerical example (Calcium Hydroxide Pair):**

Take the reaction:

$$
\mathrm{Ca(OH)_2 \;\rightleftharpoons\; CaO + H_2O}
$$

Given:
- $\Delta H_r = 104{,}400\ \text{J/mol}$ (endothermic decomposition)
- $M_{\mathrm{Ca(OH)_2}} = 74.093 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/mol}$

Then:

$$
q_{TCS} = \frac{104{,}400\ \text{J/mol}}{74.093 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/mol}}
$$

Performing the division:

$$
q_{TCS} = 1{,}409{,}260\ \text{J/kg} \approx 1409\ \text{kJ/kg}
$$

Compare this to water sensible heat ($\approx 4.18\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$), which would require a temperature rise of **over 337 K** to match a single kilogram of Ca(OH)₂. This dramatically illustrates the energy density advantage of TCS.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Three Reaction Pairs

| Parameter | Ca(OH)₂/CaO | MgH₂/Mg | Fe₃O₄/FeO |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reaction enthalpy $\Delta H_r$ (kJ/mol) | 104.4 | 74.5 | 311.9 |
| Molar mass of reactant (g/mol) | 74.09 | 26.30 | 231.53 |
| $q_{TCS}$ (kJ/kg) | 1409 | 2833 | 1347 |
| Operating T (°C) | 500–600 | 250–400 | 1300–1700 |
| Reversible cycles (lab) | > 5000 | ~3000 | ~1000 |
| Main technical challenge | Reactor bed sintering | Slow kinetics | Containment at high T |
| Application | Building heating | Vehicle/portable | CSP / industrial |

## 3.4 Worked Example — Round-Trip Efficiency Calculation

Consider a TCS system using Ca(OH)₂/CaO with the following operational data:
- Heat input during charging: $Q_{in} = 1500\ \text{MJ}$
- Useful heat recovered during discharge: $Q_{out} = 1200\ \text{MJ}$
- Auxiliary parasitic losses (fans, pumps, controls): $Q_{para} = 100\ \text{MJ}$

**Step 1 — Compute gross round-trip efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{gross} = \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \times 100\% = \frac{1200}{1500} \times 100\% = 80.0\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compute net (parasitic-adjusted) efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{net} = \frac{Q_{out} - Q_{para}}{Q_{in}} \times 100\% = \frac{1100}{1500} \times 100\% = 73.3\%
$$

This is a realistic, industry-acceptable value for a high-temperature thermochemical storage system.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Thermochemical Storage Plant

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete functional flow of a thermochemical energy storage system, from renewable heat input through storage vessels to end-use heat delivery. All node identifiers are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `step1`) and contain no reserved keywords. Labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sourceA["RENEWABLE HEAT SOURCE: SOLAR CONCENTRATOR"]:::heatSource
    node1["PRIMARY HEAT EXCHANGER: TRANSFERS HEAT TO REACTOR"]:::heatExchanger
    reactorA["CHEMICAL REACTOR: CHARGING MODE ENDOTHERMIC DISSOCIATION"]:::reactor
    sepA["PRODUCT SEPARATION UNIT: SPLITS REACTION PRODUCTS"]:::separator
    storV1["STORAGE VESSEL A: HOLDS PRODUCT COMPONENT A"]:::storage
    storV2["STORAGE VESSEL B: HOLDS PRODUCT COMPONENT B"]:::storage
    reactorB["CHEMICAL REACTOR: DISCHARGING MODE EXOTHERMIC SYNTHESIS"]:::reactor
    node2["SECONDARY HEAT EXCHANGER: TRANSFERS HEAT TO END USER"]:::heatExchanger
    endUser["END USE: BUILDING HEATING OR INDUSTRIAL PROCESS"]:::demand
    ctrl["CONTROL SYSTEM: MONITORS TEMPERATURE PRESSURE AND FLOW"]:::control

    sourceA -->|Q_in| node1
    node1 -->|HOT FLUID| reactorA
    reactorA -->|DISCHARGED PRODUCTS| sepA
    sepA -->|PRODUCT A| storV1
    sepA -->|PRODUCT B| storV2
    storV1 -->|RECOMBINE| reactorB
    storV2 -->|RECOMBINE| reactorB
    reactorB -->|HOT FLUID| node2
    node2 -->|Q_out| endUser
    ctrl -.->|SIGNALS| reactorA
    ctrl -.->|SIGNALS| reactorB
    ctrl -.->|SIGNALS| storV1
    ctrl -.->|SIGNALS| storV2

    classDef heatSource fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000
    classDef heatExchanger fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#CD5C5C,color:#000000
    classDef reactor fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000000
    classDef separator fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef storage fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,color:#000000
    classDef demand fill:#FF6347,stroke:#B22222,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef control fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Process Sequence Diagram — Charging Versus Discharging Phases

The following Mermaid sequence diagram captures the chronological interaction between major components during a complete storage cycle, including both charging and discharging events. Node identifiers follow the alphanumeric rule.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant src as Solar Field
    participant hx1 as Charging HX
    participant rec as Reactor
    participant sep as Separator
    participant sa as Vessel A
    participant sb as Vessel B
    participant rec2 as Discharge Reactor
    participant hx2 as User HX
    participant usr as End User

    src->>hx1: Deliver Q_in
    hx1->>rec: Supply thermal energy
    rec->>rec: Endothermic dissociation
    rec->>sep: Release product mixture
    sep->>sa: Route product A
    sep->>sb: Route product B
    Note over sa,sb: Long-term storage: weeks to months
    sa->>rec2: Recombine product A
    sb->>rec2: Recombine product B
    rec2->>rec2: Exothermic synthesis
    rec2->>hx2: Release Q_out
    hx2->>usr: Deliver usable heat
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Selecting a Thermochemical Reaction Pair

The following Mermaid flowchart summarizes the engineering decision logic for selecting an appropriate reaction pair based on temperature window, application, and technical constraints.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1["STEP 1: DETERMINE OPERATING TEMPERATURE RANGE"]:::step
    q2{"TEMPERATURE BELOW 400 C?"}:::decision
    q3["STEP 2: SELECT METAL HYDRIDE PAIR SUCH AS MG H2 OR LANI5"]:::choice
    q4{"TEMPERATURE BETWEEN 400 AND 900 C?"}:::decision
    q5["STEP 3: SELECT HYDROXIDE OR CARBONATE PAIR SUCH AS CA OH2 OR CACO3"]:::choice
    q6["STEP 4: SELECT METAL OXIDE PAIR SUCH AS FE3O4 OR COO"]:::choice
    q7["STEP 5: EVALUATE REVERSIBILITY CYCLES AND KINETICS"]:::step
    q8["STEP 6: CONFIRM ENERGY DENSITY EXCEEDS 500 KJ PER KG"]:::step

    q1 --> q2
    q2 -->|YES| q3
    q2 -->|NO| q4
    q4 -->|YES| q5
    q4 -->|NO| q6
    q3 --> q7
    q5 --> q7
    q6 --> q7
    q7 --> q8

    classDef step fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#BDB76B,color:#000000
    classDef decision fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000000
    classDef choice fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000000
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Define thermochemical energy storage. List two advantages it has over sensible and latent heat storage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Thermochemical energy storage (TCS) is a thermal energy storage technique that uses **reversible endothermic and exothermic chemical reactions** to absorb, retain, and later release thermal energy in the form of chemical bond potential. The two key advantages are:

1. **Highest energy density:** TCS achieves gravimetric energy densities of 500–3000 kJ/kg, compared to roughly 100–300 kJ/kg for sensible storage and 200–600 kJ/kg for latent storage.
2. **Negligible heat loss during long-term storage:** Because energy is stored as chemical potential rather than temperature, products can be stored separately for weeks or months with virtually zero thermal loss — making seasonal storage feasible.

*(Examiner note: Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for two distinct advantages.)*

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**State the Van't Hoff equation and explain its significance in thermochemical storage system design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Van't Hoff equation (pressure form) is:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{eq}}{P^\circ}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R\,T} + \frac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}
$$

**Significance (2 marks):** This equation relates the **equilibrium pressure** of the gaseous product to the **operating temperature** of the reaction. In TCS design, it is essential for:
1. Determining the **charging temperature** required to drive the dissociation reaction forward at a given storage pressure.
2. Predicting the **discharge temperature** at which the reverse reaction will occur, enabling proper sizing of reactors and storage vessels.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **working principle of a thermochemical energy storage system** based on the calcium hydroxide / calcium oxide reversible reaction. Include the charging reaction, storage mode, and discharge reaction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **gravimetric and volumetric energy densities** of the Ca(OH)₂/CaO system, given: $\Delta H_r = 104.4\ \text{kJ/mol}$, $M_{\mathrm{Ca(OH)_2}} = 74.09\ \text{g/mol}$, bulk density of Ca(OH)₂ powder $\rho = 700\ \text{kg/m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Introduce the system (1 mark):**
The Ca(OH)₂/CaO system is one of the most studied low-to-medium temperature thermochemical storage pairs, operating between 500 °C and 600 °C.

**Step 2 — Charging (endothermic) reaction (2 marks):**
During charging, heat supplied from a renewable source (e.g., solar concentrator) decomposes calcium hydroxide into calcium oxide and steam:

$$
\mathrm{Ca(OH)_2\;(s) \;+\; \Delta H \;\longrightarrow\; CaO\;(s) \;+\; H_2O\;(g)}
$$

The endothermic enthalpy is $\Delta H = +104.4\ \text{kJ/mol}$ at standard conditions.

**Step 3 — Storage (1.5 marks):**
The solid CaO is stored in an insulated silo at ambient temperature, and the steam is condensed and stored as liquid water in a separate tank. Because energy resides in chemical bonds rather than temperature, **storage losses are essentially zero** over multi-month durations.

**Step 4 — Discharge (exothermic) reaction (1.5 marks):**
On demand, water vapor is reintroduced over the CaO bed (often at controlled pressure) and the reverse reaction releases the stored heat:

$$
\mathrm{CaO\;(s) \;+\; H_2O\;(g) \;\longrightarrow\; Ca(OH)_2\;(s) \;+\; \Delta H}
$$

The released heat can be used for space heating, domestic hot water, or low-grade industrial process heat.

**Step 5 — Key feature (1 mark):**
The reversibility of this pair has been demonstrated for **more than 5000 charge–discharge cycles** in laboratory tests, making it a leading candidate for **seasonal building heating**.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Gravimetric energy density calculation (3 marks):**

$$
q_{TCS} = \frac{\Delta H_r}{M}
$$

$$
q_{TCS} = \frac{104.4 \times 10^{3}\ \text{J/mol}}{74.09 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{kg/mol}}
$$

$$
q_{TCS} = \frac{104{,}400}{0.07409}\ \text{J/kg} \approx 1{,}409{,}231\ \text{J/kg} \approx 1409\ \text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric energy density calculation (4 marks):**

$$
E_{vol} = \rho \cdot q_{TCS} = 700\ \text{kg/m}^3 \times 1{,}409{,}231\ \text{J/kg}
$$

$$
E_{vol} = 986{,}461{,}700\ \text{J/m}^3 \approx 9.86 \times 10^{8}\ \text{J/m}^3 \approx 986\ \text{MJ/m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering interpretation (included in marks):**
For a 100 m³ storage silo, this corresponds to approximately **98.6 GJ of stored heat** — enough to heat roughly 10 typical Kerala households through an entire winter season.

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch and the Van't Hoff equation**, explain how equilibrium temperature and pressure govern the charging and discharging of a thermochemical storage system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the **Fe₃O₄/FeO thermochemical pair** used in high-temperature solar thermal storage, compute the **minimum charging temperature** required to maintain an equilibrium oxygen partial pressure of $P_{eq} = 0.21\ \text{atm}$ (atmospheric). Use the following data: $\Delta H^\circ = 311.9\ \text{kJ/mol}$ and $\Delta S^\circ = 245.0\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Define the reaction (1.5 marks):**
Consider a generic solid-gas thermochemical reaction:

$$
\mathrm{AB\;(s) \;\rightleftharpoons\; A\;(s) \;+\; B\;(g)}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the Van't Hoff equation (1.5 marks):**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{eq}}{P^\circ}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R\,T} + \frac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}
$$

**Step 3 — Explain charging operation (1.5 marks):**
During charging, the temperature is **raised above** the equilibrium temperature at the given pressure. This shifts the equilibrium toward products ($A + B$), driving the endothermic dissociation and absorbing heat. The system absorbs $\Delta H$ per mole of reactant.

**Step 4 — Explain discharging operation (1.5 marks):**
During discharge, the temperature is **lowered below** the equilibrium temperature at the storage pressure. The equilibrium shifts back toward $AB$, releasing heat. In some advanced designs, **pressure swing** is used instead of temperature swing to drive discharge — this is the principle behind **pressure-swing thermochemical reactors**.

**Step 5 — Significance (1 mark):**
The Van't Hoff equation allows engineers to **pre-compute** the exact temperature required for any chosen operating pressure, which is critical for reactor design and integration with the upstream heat source.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — State the Van't Hoff equation in pressure form (1 mark):**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{P_{eq}}{P^\circ}\right) = -\frac{\Delta H^\circ}{R\,T} + \frac{\Delta S^\circ}{R}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute given values (2 marks):**
- $P_{eq} = 0.21\ \text{atm}$
- $P^\circ = 1\ \text{atm}$
- $\Delta H^\circ = 311{,}900\ \text{J/mol}$
- $\Delta S^\circ = 245.0\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$
- $R = 8.314\ \text{J/(mol·K)}$

Left-hand side:

$$
\ln(0.21) = -1.5606
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $1/T$ (2 marks):**

$$
-1.5606 = -\frac{311{,}900}{8.314 \cdot T} + \frac{245.0}{8.314}
$$

$$
\frac{245.0}{8.314} = 29.469
$$

$$
-1.5606 - 29.469 = -\frac{311{,}900}{8.314 \cdot T}
$$

$$
-31.030 = -\frac{311{,}900}{8.314 \cdot T}
$$

$$
\frac{31.030 \cdot 8.314}{311{,}900} = \frac{1}{T}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $T$ (1.5 marks):**

$$
\frac{1}{T} = \frac{31.030 \times 8.314}{311{,}900} = \frac{257.97}{311{,}900} = 8.272 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
T = \frac{1}{8.272 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1208.9\ \text{K}
$$

Converting to Celsius:

$$
T_{^\circ\text{C}} = 1208.9 - 273.15 \approx 935.8\ ^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering comment (0.5 mark):**
The minimum charging temperature is approximately **936 °C**. In practice, CSP plants operate Fe₃O₄/FeO reactors at **1300–1700 °C** to achieve reasonable reaction kinetics and adequate driving force.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in the Van't Hoff equation:** The correct form has $-\Delta H^\circ / (R\,T)$, not $+\Delta H^\circ / (R\,T)$. This single sign error can cost **2–3 marks** in numerical problems.
> 2. **Confusing standard pressure $P^\circ$ with equilibrium pressure $P_{eq}$:** $P^\circ$ is **always** 1 atm (or 101,325 Pa). $P_{eq}$ is system-specific.
> 3. **Using Celsius instead of Kelvin in thermodynamic equations:** The gas constant $R$ and the Van't Hoff equation **require absolute temperature in Kelvin**. Using °C yields a nonsense answer and zero marks.
> 4. **Skipping the engineering interpretation step:** KTU examiners award marks for explaining *what the answer means physically*. Always conclude numerical problems with 1–2 sentences of engineering context.
> 5. **Not labeling all diagrams:** Any sketch (e.g., reactor, storage tank) must have clearly labeled streams, temperatures, and material flows. Unlabeled diagrams receive partial credit at best.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Thermochemical Storage:**

- ✅ TCS stores heat as **chemical potential** via **reversible endothermic/exothermic reactions** of working pairs.
- ✅ Three operational stages: **Charging (dissociation) → Storage (separation) → Discharging (synthesis)**.
- ✅ Energy density is **5–10× higher than sensible** and **2–4× higher than latent** storage.
- ✅ Storage duration is essentially **unlimited** with negligible losses — ideal for **seasonal storage**.
- ✅ Master the **Van't Hoff equation**: $\ln(P_{eq}/P^\circ) = -\Delta H^\circ/(RT) + \Delta S^\circ/R$.
- ✅ Reaction enthalpy $\Delta H_r$ and molar mass $M$ determine gravimetric energy density $q_{TCS} = \Delta H_r / M$.
- ✅ Volumetric energy density $E_{vol} = \rho \cdot q_{TCS}$ is critical for **storage vessel sizing**.
- ✅ Key reaction pairs to memorize: **Ca(OH)₂/CaO** (low-T), **CaCO₃/CaO** (mid-T), **Fe₃O₄/FeO** (high-T), **MgH₂/Mg** (hydrogen storage).
- ✅ Round-trip efficiency typically ranges **70–90%** for well-engineered systems.
- ✅ **Always use Kelvin** in thermodynamic equations, never Celsius.
- ✅ The standard pressure $P^\circ = 1\ \text{atm} = 101{,}325\ \text{Pa}$ is a constant you must commit to memory.
- ✅ TCS couples best with **concentrated solar power (CSP)** and **industrial waste heat recovery** applications.
- ✅ Main challenges: **reactor bed sintering, slow kinetics, gas leakage, and material degradation** over many cycles.
- ✅ Compared to batteries, TCS is best for **thermal** loads (heating, process heat), not electrical loads.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Photovoltaic System for Power Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT862 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Photovoltaic System for Power Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Photovoltaic (PV) System** is an integrated power generation arrangement that directly converts incident solar radiation (photons) into direct current (DC) electrical energy through the **photovoltaic effect** observed in semiconductor materials (typically silicon). The system comprises solar cells assembled into modules, modules wired into arrays, and balance-of-system (BOS) components such as charge controllers, inverters, batteries, and mounting structures that condition, store, and deliver usable electrical power to either a standalone load or the utility grid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PESFT862 / Module 1):**
> The 2024 scheme explicitly requires the student to explain the constructional features of a solar cell, draw and analyze its V–I characteristics, derive expressions for fill factor and efficiency, classify solar cell technologies, and describe standalone / grid-connected PV systems along with the role of MPPT.

## 1.2 The Photovoltaic Effect — The Heart of the System

The **photovoltaic effect** is the physical phenomenon in which a semiconductor material generates a voltage and hence a current when exposed to light. It was first observed by **Edmond Becquerel in 1839** and explained quantum-mechanically by **Albert Einstein in 1905** using the photoelectric effect theory.

The mechanism in brief:
- A photon with energy **E = hν ≥ E_g** (band gap energy) strikes the semiconductor.
- The energy lifts a valence electron into the conduction band, creating an **electron–hole pair (EHP)**.
- A built-in electric field at the **p–n junction** sweeps the electron toward the n-side and the hole toward the p-side.
- Connecting an external load allows the separated charges to flow as direct current.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants to Remember:**
> - Planck's Constant, **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
> - Speed of Light, **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
> - Charge of an Electron, **q = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
> - Standard Test Condition (STC) Irradiance, **G = 1000 W/m²** (AM 1.5 spectrum, cell temp 25 °C)

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **water slide at a water park**:
- The **sunlight** is the water supply at the top.
- The **photons** are individual water droplets.
- A **solar cell** is the slide — it only works for "big enough" droplets (high-energy photons).
- Tiny droplets (low-energy photons) just bounce off and do no work; this represents **photon energy below the band gap**, which is lost as heat.
- The **p–n junction** is the slope of the slide: it forces the water (charges) to flow in one direction, creating a current.
- A **battery + inverter** is the bucket and pump at the bottom: it stores the water and pushes it uphill (converts DC to AC for your home).

Once you understand this, the rest of the system is simply "plumbing" — connecting cells, modules, and arrays to collect and deliver the "water" of electricity.

## 1.4 Why PV Systems Matter

- **Direct conversion** → no moving parts, no fuel cost, no emissions during operation.
- **Modular** → systems scale from milliwatts (calculator) to gigawatts (utility farms).
- **Predictable** → solar resource data is available globally for 25+ years.
- **Long lifetime** → modern modules carry a 25-year linear power warranty.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Energy Math:** A single 400 W module measuring ~2 m² under full sun produces ~400 Wh per peak hour. Over a typical Kerala day (≈ 4.5 Peak Sun Hours), one module generates roughly **400 × 4.5 = 1.8 kWh/day** — enough to run a 60 W fan for 30 hours or a 100 W LED bulb for 18 hours.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of a Crystalline Silicon Solar Cell

A typical crystalline silicon solar cell is a thin wafer (≈ 180–200 µm thick) built as follows:

| Layer (top → bottom) | Material | Thickness | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Anti-Reflection Coating (ARC) | SiNₓ or TiO₂ | ~75 nm | Reduces reflection losses from ~30% to <5% |
| 2. Front Contact (Fingers + Busbars) | Silver (Ag) paste | ~20 µm | Collects current from n-layer |
| 3. Emitter (n⁺ layer) | Phosphorus-doped Si | ~0.3 µm | Generates minority carriers |
| 4. p–n Junction | — | — | Built-in field separates charges |
| 5. Base (p-layer) | Boron-doped Si | ~180 µm | Absorbs most photons |
| 6. Back Surface Field (BSF) | Al-doped Si⁺ | few µm | Reduces rear recombination |
| 7. Back Contact | Aluminum (Al) | — | Completes circuit |

## 2.2 The Equivalent Circuit of a Solar Cell

A real solar cell is modelled electrically as:

**One-Diode Model:**

$$I = I_{ph} - I_{D} - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

$$I_{D} = I_{0} \left[ \exp\left(\frac{q(V + I R_s)}{n k T}\right) - 1 \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this model matters:** It mathematically describes the entire V–I curve, allowing engineers to predict cell behaviour, simulate arrays, and design MPPT algorithms before manufacturing hardware.

## 2.3 Key V–I Curve Parameters (KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Typical Value (Si cell) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Short-Circuit Current | **I_sc** | Current at V = 0 | 3–8 A (per cell) |
| Open-Circuit Voltage | **V_oc** | Voltage at I = 0 | 0.55–0.72 V (per cell) |
| Maximum Power Point Current | **I_m** | Current at peak power | ≈ 0.9 × I_sc |
| Maximum Power Point Voltage | **V_m** | Voltage at peak power | ≈ 0.8 × V_oc |
| Maximum Power | **P_max** | V_m × I_m | 1.5–4 W (per cell) |
| Fill Factor | **FF** | P_max ⁄ (V_oc × I_sc) | 0.70–0.85 |
| Efficiency | **η** | P_max ⁄ (P_in × A) | 18–24% (mono c-Si) |
| Series Resistance | **R_s** | Internal ohmic loss | < 0.5 Ω |
| Shunt Resistance | **R_sh** | Leakage path | > 200 Ω |
| Ideality Factor | **n** | Diode quality | 1–2 |
| Reverse Saturation Current | **I_0** | Leakage current | 10⁻⁹ to 10⁻¹² A |

## 2.4 The V–I Characteristic Curve — Step-by-Step Reading

The V–I curve of a solar cell under STC has a distinctive "knee" shape:

- **At V = 0 (left end):** I = I_sc, the cell delivers maximum current into a short circuit.
- **At I = 0 (right end):** V = V_oc, the cell delivers maximum voltage into an open circuit.
- **The "knee" of the curve** is the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**. This is the operating point engineers force the system to track using MPPT.
- **Operating Region 1 (left of MPP):** cell behaves like a **constant current source** — current is almost independent of voltage.
- **Operating Region 2 (right of MPP):** cell behaves like a **constant voltage source** — voltage changes little, current falls sharply.

## 2.5 Fill Factor — The "Squareness" of the Curve

The **Fill Factor (FF)** quantifies how "rectangular" the V–I curve is. An ideal rectangular curve would have FF = 1. Real cells have FF between 0.70 and 0.85.

$$FF = \frac{V_m \cdot I_m}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

**Empirical (Green's) approximation for silicon cells:**

$$FF_0 = \frac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$$

where $v_{oc} = \frac{q V_{oc}}{n k T}$ is the **normalized open-circuit voltage**.

## 2.6 Conversion Efficiency of a Solar Cell

The **efficiency (η)** is the ratio of electrical power output to incident solar power:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in} \times A} = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G \times A}$$

where:
- **P_in** = incident solar power (W)
- **G** = solar irradiance (W/m²)
- **A** = cell area (m²)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Efficiency directly determines land/area requirement. A 20% efficient module needs ~5 m² per kW peak, while a 15% module needs ~6.7 m² per kW peak — critical when designing rooftop systems on limited terrace space in Kerala apartments.

## 2.7 Effect of Irradiance (G) and Temperature (T)

| Condition | Effect on I_sc | Effect on V_oc | Effect on P_max |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Irradiance (G) | Linear increase | Small logarithmic increase | Strongly increases |
| ↑ Cell Temperature (T) | Slight increase | Decreases (~2 mV/°C per cell) | Decreases |
| Partial Shading | Severe drop in one cell, others can push it into reverse bias → **hot spot** | — | Can drop to zero |

> [!WARNING]
> **Bypass Diode Necessity:** To prevent hot-spot damage from partial shading, modules include **bypass diodes** (typically 1–3 per module) that route current around shaded cell strings.

## 2.8 Classification of Solar Cells

| Generation | Type | Efficiency | Cost | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1st Gen** | Monocrystalline Silicon (mono c-Si) | 20–24% | High | Rooftop, utility |
| **1st Gen** | Polycrystalline Silicon (multi c-Si) | 16–20% | Medium | Rooftop |
| **2nd Gen** | Amorphous Silicon (a-Si) | 6–9% | Low | BIPV, calculators |
| **2nd Gen** | Cadmium Telluride (CdTe) | 18–22% | Low | Utility farms |
| **2nd Gen** | CIGS (CuInGaSe₂) | 18–21% | Medium | Flexible modules |
| **3rd Gen** | Perovskite | 25–33% (lab) | Very low | Emerging |
| **3rd Gen** | Multi-junction (III–V) | 40–47% (lab) | Very high | Space, CPV |
| **4th Gen** | Dye-Sensitized / Organic | 10–15% (lab) | Very low | Research |

## 2.9 Components of a Complete PV System

A working PV plant integrates several subsystems:

1. **PV Array** — series-parallel combination of modules to reach required V and I.
2. **Mounting Structure** — fixed-tilt, seasonal-tilt, or single/dual-axis tracker.
3. **Junction Box & DC Cabling** — combines strings; houses string fuses and SPDs.
4. **Charge Controller** — regulates battery charging (essential in off-grid systems).
5. **Battery Bank** — stores DC energy; common chemistries: Lead-acid, Li-ion, LFP.
6. **Inverter** — converts DC to AC (off-grid) or synchronizes with grid (on-grid).
7. **Maximum Power Point Tracker (MPPT)** — DC–DC stage that forces the array to operate at its MPP.
8. **Protection Devices** — DC/AC MCBs, fuses, surge protection, earthing, RCD.
9. **Monitoring System** — string monitoring, data loggers, SCADA for utility plants.

## 2.10 MPPT — Why It Is Mandatory

Because the MPP shifts continuously with **G, T, and load**, a fixed-voltage operating point wastes energy. MPPT algorithms force the array to operate at its instantaneous MPP.

**Common MPPT Algorithms (KTU-relevant):**

| Algorithm | Working Principle | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P&O (Perturb & Observe)** | Perturb V, observe ΔP; if ΔP > 0 keep perturbing, else reverse | Simple, cheap | Oscillates at MPP |
| **InCond (Incremental Conductance)** | Uses dI/dV = -I/V at MPP | Faster, less oscillation | Slightly more complex |
| **Fuzzy Logic** | Rule-based inference on ΔP, ΔV | Handles nonlinearity well | Needs expert tuning |
| **Neural Network** | Trained on (G, T, V) → V_MPP | Fastest, most accurate | Training data required |
| **Constant Voltage (V = 0.8 V_oc)** | Open-circuit sampling + lookup | Very simple | Loses 5–10% energy |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The One-Diode Model Equation

The **one-diode model** of a solar cell is constructed by applying Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the output node.

**Step 1 — Identify the three current branches at the output node:**
- A current source producing the photo-generated current **I_ph** (from sunlight).
- A diode carrying dark current **I_D** in the opposite direction.
- A shunt resistor **R_sh** carrying leakage current **I_sh**.

**Step 2 — Apply KCL (sum of currents leaving the node = 0):**

$$I + I_{D} + I_{sh} - I_{ph} = 0$$

This means: the current delivered to the external load plus the diode current plus the shunt current must equal the photo-generated current.

**Step 3 — Express the shunt current using Ohm's law:**

$$I_{sh} = \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

Because the shunt resistor sees the terminal voltage **V** plus the small voltage drop across the series resistor **I·R_s**.

**Step 4 — Express the diode current using the Shockley diode equation:**

$$I_{D} = I_{0} \left[ \exp\left(\frac{q(V + I R_s)}{n k T}\right) - 1 \right]$$

where:
- **I_0** = reverse saturation current (A)
- **n** = ideality factor (dimensionless, 1–2)
- **k** = Boltzmann constant = **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- **T** = absolute cell temperature (K)

**Step 5 — Combine all three expressions using KCL:**

$$I = I_{ph} - I_{0} \left[ \exp\left(\frac{q(V + I R_s)}{n k T}\right) - 1 \right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$

This is the **implicit, transcendental** equation describing the entire V–I curve of a real solar cell.

**Step 6 — Special cases that verify the model:**

**Case A — Short circuit (V = 0):** The diode still has a small voltage **I_sc·R_s**, but the term inside exp() is small; for an ideal cell with R_s → 0 and R_sh → ∞:

$$I \approx I_{ph} = I_{sc}$$

**Case B — Open circuit (I = 0):** All photo-current flows through the diode:

$$0 = I_{ph} - I_{0}\left[\exp\left(\frac{q V_{oc}}{n k T}\right) - 1\right]$$

Solving for V_oc:

$$V_{oc} = \frac{n k T}{q} \ln\left(\frac{I_{ph}}{I_{0}} + 1\right)$$

This is the fundamental equation showing that **V_oc depends logarithmically on light intensity** — a key result that students frequently reproduce in KTU exams.

## 3.2 Derivation: Efficiency of a Solar Cell

**Step 1 — Begin with the definition of efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Electrical Power Output}}{\text{Incident Solar Power on Cell Area}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the maximum power point power:**

$$P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF$$

where FF is the fill factor.

**Step 3 — Substitute incident solar power = irradiance × area:**

$$P_{in} = G \cdot A$$

**Step 4 — Combine to obtain the closed-form efficiency equation:**

$$\eta = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A}$$

**Step 5 — Express I_sc in terms of photo-current density (J_sc in A/m²):**

$$I_{sc} = J_{sc} \cdot A$$

Therefore:

$$\eta = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot J_{sc} \cdot FF}{G}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Engineering Insight:** This is the single most important equation for any PV system designer. Every parameter can be measured experimentally: V_oc and I_sc with a multimeter, FF from the I–V curve tracer, G with a pyranometer, and A from the cell datasheet.

## 3.3 Derivation: Fill Factor (FF) Using Green's Empirical Formula

**Step 1 — Start with the definition:**

$$FF = \frac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{V_m \cdot I_m}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

**Step 2 — Introduce normalized voltage v_oc (dimensionless):**

$$v_{oc} = \frac{q V_{oc}}{n k T}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Green's empirical correlation (valid for v_oc > 10):**

$$FF_0(v_{oc}) = \frac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$$

**Step 4 — Correct for series resistance (R_s > 0 reduces FF):**

$$FF = FF_0 \cdot (1 - r_s)$$

where $r_s = \frac{R_s}{V_{oc}/I_{sc}}$ is the normalized series resistance.

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
Assume a silicon cell with V_oc = 0.65 V, T = 300 K, n = 1.2.

$$v_{oc} = \frac{(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(0.65)}{(1.2)(1.381 \times 10^{-23})(300)}$$

$$v_{oc} = \frac{1.0413 \times 10^{-19}}{4.9716 \times 10^{-21}} = 20.94$$

$$FF_0 = \frac{20.94 - \ln(20.94 + 0.72)}{20.94 + 1} = \frac{20.94 - \ln(21.66)}{21.94}$$

$$FF_0 = \frac{20.94 - 3.076}{21.94} = \frac{17.864}{21.94} = 0.814$$

So the **ideal fill factor is ~0.81**, consistent with a high-quality silicon cell.

## 3.4 Derivation: Solar Cell Sizing for a Stand-Alone System

A classic KTU 14-mark problem asks: "Design a PV system to power a given load for X days of autonomy."

**Step 1 — Compute total daily energy demand E_L (Wh/day):**

$$E_L = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \times t_i$$

where **P_i** is the wattage of the i-th appliance and **t_i** is its daily hours of use.

**Step 2 — Account for system losses (battery, inverter, wiring, dust):**

$$E_{PV} = \frac{E_L}{\eta_{sys}} \quad \text{where} \quad \eta_{sys} = \eta_{bat} \cdot \eta_{inv} \cdot \eta_{wir} \cdot \eta_{dust}$$

Typical values: **η_bat ≈ 0.85, η_inv ≈ 0.95, η_wir ≈ 0.98, η_dust ≈ 0.95**, so η_sys ≈ **0.75**.

**Step 3 — Compute required PV array wattage:**

$$P_{PV} = \frac{E_{PV}}{PSH}$$

where **PSH (Peak Sun Hours)** is the equivalent hours of 1000 W/m² irradiance. For Kerala: **PSH ≈ 4.5 h/day** (averaged over the year).

**Step 4 — Compute battery bank capacity (Ah):**

$$C_{bat} = \frac{E_L \times N_{aut}}{V_{bat} \times DoD \times \eta_{bat}}$$

where:
- **N_aut** = days of autonomy (typically 2–3)
- **V_bat** = battery bank voltage (12 V, 24 V, or 48 V)
- **DoD** = maximum depth of discharge (Lead-acid: 0.5; Li-ion: 0.8)

**Worked Example:**
Load = 4 fans (75 W × 12 h) + 6 LED bulbs (12 W × 6 h) + 1 TV (80 W × 5 h) = 1028 Wh/day.

$$E_{PV} = \frac{1028}{0.75} = 1370 \text{ Wh/day}$$

$$P_{PV} = \frac{1370}{4.5} = 305 \text{ W}_p \quad (\text{round up to 400 W module})$$

For 24 V battery, 2 days autonomy, DoD = 0.5:

$$C_{bat} = \frac{1028 \times 2}{24 \times 0.5 \times 0.85} = \frac{2056}{10.2} = 201.6 \text{ Ah}$$

Choose a **24 V, 220 Ah** battery bank (next standard size up).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students forget the **η_sys** factor and oversize or undersize the system. Always show the efficiency step in your KTU answer sheet to score full marks.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: I–V Curve Shifts Under Changing Conditions

| Parameter Change | I_sc Effect | V_oc Effect | P_max Effect | Curve Shift |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Irradiance G doubles (1 sun → 2 suns) | Doubles | Increases ~ln(2)·n·k·T/q ≈ +18 mV | ≈ Doubles | Stretches vertically; knee shifts |
| Temperature rises 25°C → 50°C | Tiny increase (~+0.1%) | Decreases ~50 mV | Decreases ~10–15% | Shifts left; knee moves down |
| Partial shading on 1 cell in 36 | Drops to module's bypass branch | — | May drop to 1/3 | Steps appear in I–V curve |
| R_s increases (poor contact) | Almost no change at I_sc | Almost no change | Significant decrease | Curve "rounds" near V_oc |
| R_sh decreases (leakage) | Almost no change | Decreases slightly | Decreases | Curve slope increases near I_sc |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A, Q1a]**
