> **Define the photovoltaic effect. Mention the role of the p–n junction in a solar cell.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **photovoltaic effect** is the generation of a voltage and current across a semiconductor p–n junction when it is exposed to electromagnetic radiation (sunlight) of energy equal to or greater than the band gap of the semiconductor (≥ **E_g ≈ 1.12 eV** for silicon).

The **p–n junction** plays three critical roles: (1) it creates a **built-in electric field** at the depletion region by the diffusion of charge carriers; (2) this field **separates photogenerated electron–hole pairs** by sweeping electrons toward the n-side and holes toward the p-side; and (3) it establishes the **polarity of the output terminals** (n-side becomes negative, p-side becomes positive externally), allowing current to flow through a connected load.

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B, Q1b]**
> **Define Fill Factor and explain its significance in solar cell performance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Fill Factor (FF)** is the ratio of the maximum obtainable power from a solar cell to the product of its open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current:

$$FF = \frac{V_m \cdot I_m}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

It is a **dimensionless number between 0 and 1**, with typical values of **0.70 to 0.85** for high-quality crystalline silicon cells.

**Significance:** FF indicates how "square" the I–V curve is. A higher FF means the cell delivers more usable power for the same V_oc and I_sc. FF directly multiplies into the cell's efficiency: **η = (V_oc × I_sc × FF) / (G × A)**. Therefore, maximizing FF (by minimizing series resistance and shunt leakage) is one of the primary design goals of cell manufacturers.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Question A** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Q2]**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat construction diagram, explain the working of a crystalline silicon solar cell. (7 marks)
> **(b)** Draw the V–I and P–V characteristics of a solar cell and explain the effect of (i) irradiance and (ii) cell temperature on these curves. (7 marks)

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Construction and Working of a Crystalline Silicon Solar Cell (7 marks)

A crystalline silicon solar cell is a **p–n junction diode** specially designed to absorb sunlight and convert it into electricity. Its layered construction (from top to bottom) is:

1. **Anti-Reflection Coating (ARC)** — a thin layer (~75 nm) of **SiNₓ or TiO₂** that reduces surface reflection from ~30% to less than 5%, ensuring maximum light absorption.
2. **Front Metallic Contact** — a grid of **silver (Ag) fingers** connected to two or three thicker **busbars**, designed to minimize shadowing while efficiently collecting current.
3. **n⁺ Emitter Layer** — a very thin (~0.3 µm) heavily phosphorus-doped silicon layer on the top, where most short-wavelength photons are absorbed.
4. **p–n Junction** — formed at the interface between the n⁺ emitter and the p-type base, creating the depletion region and built-in electric field.
5. **p-type Base** — the bulk of the cell (~180 µm thick), lightly boron-doped, where most long-wavelength photons are absorbed and most electron–hole pairs are generated.
6. **Back Surface Field (BSF)** — a heavily doped p⁺ layer that creates a low-recombination rear surface, increasing voltage.
7. **Back Metallic Contact** — a full aluminum layer that provides the return path for current.

**Working Principle:**
When photons with energy **hν ≥ E_g (1.12 eV for Si)** strike the cell, they generate electron–hole pairs in both the emitter and base regions. Minority carriers that reach the depletion region are swept across the junction by the built-in field — electrons move to the n-side, holes to the p-side. The accumulated charges produce a forward bias across the junction; when an external load is connected, electrons flow from the n-contact through the load to the p-contact, producing a useful **direct current**.

[Draw the layered diagram showing all 7 layers, label thicknesses, indicate the depletion region, and mark photon entry from the top.]

#### Part (b) — V–I and P–V Characteristics and the Effect of Irradiance and Temperature (7 marks)

**V–I Characteristics:**
The V–I curve of an illuminated solar cell is a non-linear curve in the **fourth quadrant** (with current on the Y-axis and voltage on the X-axis, by convention the I–V curve is mirrored to the first quadrant). Key points:
- At **V = 0**: I = I_sc (short-circuit current).
- At **I = 0**: V = V_oc (open-circuit voltage).
- Between these extremes lies the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)** at (V_m, I_m) where the product is maximum.

**P–V Characteristics:**
The P–V curve is obtained by plotting P = V × I along the V-axis. It is a **parabolic curve** that rises from zero at V = 0, reaches a peak P_max at the MPP, and falls back to zero at V = V_oc.

**Effect of (i) Irradiance:**
- **I_sc is directly proportional to irradiance G** (I_sc ≈ G × A × η_collection). Doubling G doubles I_sc.
- **V_oc increases only logarithmically** with G: V_oc ∝ (n k T / q) ln(G).
- **P_max scales almost linearly with G**.
- The V–I curve **stretches vertically upward** as irradiance increases. The MPP shifts to higher current and slightly higher voltage.

**Effect of (ii) Cell Temperature:**
- **I_sc increases very slightly** (≈ +0.05 %/°C) because higher temperature marginally raises carrier generation.
- **V_oc decreases significantly** (≈ **−2 mV/°C per cell**, or roughly −0.4 %/°C of V_oc).
- **P_max decreases by approximately 0.4–0.5 % per °C rise in temperature**.
- The V–I curve **shifts to the left**; the MPP moves to lower voltage, reducing P_max.

[Draw two V–I curves (one for low G, one for high G) and two more (one for 25 °C, one for 50 °C). Mark MPP on each. Draw the corresponding P–V curves showing the peak shifting.]

---

> **Question B (Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Q2 Alt]**
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the components of a grid-connected PV system. Explain the function of the inverter and the role of MPPT. (7 marks)
> **(b)** A silicon solar cell has the following data at STC: V_oc = 0.62 V, I_sc = 3.2 A, FF = 0.78, cell area = 100 cm², G = 1000 W/m². Calculate (i) maximum power, (ii) efficiency, and (iii) the current and voltage at maximum power point. (7 marks)

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Grid-Connected PV System (7 marks)

A **grid-connected (on-grid) PV system** is one that operates in parallel with the utility grid, feeding generated power into the grid and drawing power from it when PV output is insufficient.

**Major Components (with neat block diagram):**

1. **PV Array** — series-parallel combination of modules sized to meet the load.
2. **DC Combiner Box** — combines strings, houses string fuses and DC isolator.
3. **DC Surge Protection Device (SPD)** — protects against lightning-induced surges.
4. **Grid-Tie Inverter (with built-in MPPT)** — converts DC to synchronized AC; includes anti-islanding protection.
5. **AC Distribution Panel** — MCB, RCD/ELCB, energy meter.
6. **Net Meter / Bi-directional Meter** — records energy exported to and imported from the grid.
7. **Monitoring System** — data logger, IoT gateway, optional SCADA.
8. **Mounting Structure & Earthing** — mechanical and safety backbone.

[Draw the block diagram: PV Array → Combiner Box → SPD → Inverter → AC Panel → Net Meter → Grid/Load]

**Function of the Inverter:**
- Converts DC output of the PV array to **230 V, 50 Hz single-phase AC** (or 415 V three-phase) synchronized with the grid.
- Implements **MPPT** to extract maximum power under varying G and T.
- Provides **anti-islanding protection** — automatically disconnects from the grid when grid power fails, ensuring safety of maintenance personnel.
- Performs **THD filtering**, power factor correction, and grid-code compliance.

**Role of MPPT:**
Since the PV array's **maximum power point (V_m, I_m)** varies continuously with irradiance, temperature, and load conditions, a fixed operating voltage would waste energy. MPPT is a **DC–DC converter control algorithm** (commonly Perturb & Observe or Incremental Conductance) that continuously adjusts the array's operating voltage to track the instantaneous MPP, increasing energy yield by **20–30%** compared to a non-MPPT system.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
V_oc = 0.62 V, I_sc = 3.2 A, FF = 0.78, A = 100 cm² = 100 × 10⁻⁴ m² = 0.01 m², G = 1000 W/m².

**(i) Maximum Power:**

$$P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF$$

$$P_{max} = 0.62 \times 3.2 \times 0.78 = 1.547 \text{ W}$$

**(ii) Efficiency:**

$$P_{in} = G \cdot A = 1000 \times 0.01 = 10 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{1.547}{10} = 0.1547 = 15.47\%$$

**(iii) Current and Voltage at MPP:**
For a typical silicon cell, the MPP occurs at:
- I_m ≈ 0.9 × I_sc
- V_m ≈ 0.8 × V_oc

$$I_m = 0.9 \times 3.2 = 2.88 \text{ A}$$

$$V_m = 0.8 \times 0.62 = 0.496 \text{ V}$$

**Verification:** P_max = V_m × I_m = 0.496 × 2.88 = **1.428 W** (close to 1.547 W; minor difference because the 0.9/0.8 ratios are empirical averages — the *exact* I_m and V_m must be read from the curve tracer or solved iteratively using the one-diode model).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on PV Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting η_sys factor** in off-grid sizing problems — losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing I_sc and I_ph** — I_ph is the internal generated current; I_sc is the measurable short-circuit current (equal to I_ph when R_s = 0 and R_sh → ∞).
> 3. **Using area in cm² instead of m²** in efficiency calculations — instant zero on a calculation sub-question.
> 4. **Drawing P–V curve as a triangle** instead of a smooth parabola — fetch only half marks in the diagram.
> 5. **Omitting anti-islanding** when describing grid-tie inverters — a recurring two-mark loss.
> 6. **Using G in kW/m² and area in m² inconsistently** — units must be SI throughout: W/m² × m² = W.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Photovoltaic effect** = photon-induced generation of voltage/current in a semiconductor; first observed by Becquerel (1839), explained by Einstein (1905).
- [ ] **Band gap energy E_g** must be ≤ photon energy **hν** for absorption. Silicon: **E_g = 1.12 eV**, λ_cutoff ≈ 1.11 µm.
- [ ] **P–N junction** is essential: it provides the built-in field that separates photo-generated EHPs.
- [ ] **Anti-Reflection Coating (ARC)** is mandatory to reduce reflection loss from 30% to <5%.
- [ ] **I_sc** is linear in irradiance; **V_oc** is logarithmic in irradiance and linear-decreasing in temperature.
- [ ] **FF = (V_m × I_m) / (V_oc × I_sc)**; typical 0.70–0.85.
- [ ] **η = (V_oc × I_sc × FF) / (G × A)** — the master efficiency equation.
- [ ] **Temperature coefficients (per °C rise):** V_oc ≈ −2 mV/cell, I_sc ≈ +0.05%, P_max ≈ −0.4% to −0.5%/°C.
- [ ] **Series resistance R_s** reduces FF by rounding the curve near V_oc.
- [ ] **Shunt resistance R_sh** reduces I_sc by adding leakage slope.
- [ ] **Bypass diodes** protect modules from hot-spot damage during partial shading.
- [ ] **MPPT algorithms:** P&O (simple, oscillates), Incremental Conductance (faster), Fuzzy / NN (advanced). MPPT gain = 20–30% over fixed-V operation.
- [ ] **System efficiency η_sys = η_bat × η_inv × η_wiring × η_dust** ≈ 0.70–0.80.
- [ ] **Stand-alone (off-grid) PV system** must include a battery bank sized for N_aut days of autonomy and maximum DoD.
- [ ] **Grid-tie (on-grid) PV system** uses a grid-tie inverter with MPPT and anti-islanding protection; no battery is required for net-metered setups.
- [ ] **First-generation cells:** mono-Si (20–24%), poly-Si (16–20%). **Second generation:** a-Si, CdTe, CIGS (thin film). **Third generation:** perovskite, multi-junction, DSSC.
- [ ] **Bypass diode** = module-level protection against hot-spotting; **blocking diode** = string-level protection against reverse current flow at night.
- [ ] **Standard Test Conditions (STC):** G = 1000 W/m², AM 1.5, T_cell = 25 °C.
- [ ] **NOCT** (Nominal Operating Cell Temperature) is the realistic field operating cell temperature, typically 45 ± 2 °C.
- [ ] **Derating factors for design:** dust = 0.95, wiring = 0.98, inverter = 0.95, battery = 0.85, temperature derating = 0.90 (for Kerala climate).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Pond - Solar Cells - Types of Solar Cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT862 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Solar Pond - Solar Cells - Types of Solar Cells # 🌞 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Solar Pond — Definition & Operating Philosophy ### 📘 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Solar Pond**: A *salinity-gradient solar pond* is a large-scale, body of water-based solar thermal collector and long-duration (seasonal) thermal energy storage device that harnesses and stores incident solar radiation as sensible heat in the lower brine layers, using a vertically stratified salt concentration gradient to suppress natural free convection currents. The salt concentration increases with depth, creating a **stable density gradient** ($\partial \rho / \partial z > 0$) that traps heated brine at the bottom, allowing temperatures to reach **70 °C – 100 °C** even in ambient conditions. ### 🌊 Intuitive Analogy — "The Thermos Flask of Nature" Imagine a **transparent swimming pool** with three layers: | Layer | Real-World Analogy | Function | |---|---|---| | **Top (Fresh Water)** | Cool shower water on top | Allows sunlight in, lets heat escape a little | | **Middle (Gradient Zone)** | A slowly thickening syrup sandwich | Stops hot water from rising — the *insulator* | | **Bottom (Dark Salt Brine)** | Black tea in a thermos | Absorbs sunlight and stores it for weeks | The middle layer is the genius — saltier water is **denser**, so the denser (hotter) water cannot rise due to buoyancy reversal. Without this gradient, the sun would only heat the surface and the heat would instantly bubble away into the air. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is this engineering-relevant?** > A solar pond is the **only solar technology** that simultaneously **collects AND stores** thermal energy at a low cost, with no moving parts, no expensive mirrors, and a lifespan of **20–30 years**. --- ## 1.2 Solar Cell (Photovoltaic Cell) — Definition ### 📘 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Solar Cell (Photovoltaic Cell)**: A two-terminal, solid-state semiconductor P-N junction device that directly converts incident photons from sunlight into usable electrical energy via the **photovoltaic (PV) effect**, quantified by its open-circuit voltage ($V_{OC}$), short-circuit current ($I_{SC}$), and conversion efficiency ($\eta$). A single crystalline silicon cell typically produces: - $V_{OC} \approx 0.6\text{ V}$ - $I_{SC} \approx 3\text{ A}$ (for a 156 mm × 156 mm cell) - $\eta \approx 15\text{–}22\%$ ### 💡 Intuitive Analogy — "A Leaf that Returns Coins" Think of a solar cell as an **artificial leaf**: - **Sunlight = coins being tossed at the leaf** - The leaf has two pockets — a *positive* pocket (P-side) and a *negative* pocket (N-side) - When a photon (coin) hits hard enough (above the **band gap energy** $E_g$), it kicks an electron out of the silicon's grip - The built-in electric field at the P-N junction acts like a **one-way turnstile**, sweeping electrons to the N-side and holes to the P-side - Connect a wire, and the electrons flow back home — giving you **electricity** > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Photovoltaic Effect** was first observed by **Alexandre Edmond Becquerel in 1839** in an electrolytic cell, and the first practical silicon solar cell was built at **Bell Laboratories in 1954** by Chapin, Fuller, and Pearson with **6% efficiency**. --- ## 1.3 Types of Solar Cells — The Three Generations Overview Solar cell technologies are categorized into **three generations**, distinguished by material, efficiency, cost, and manufacturing complexity. ### 📊 Overview Table | Generation | Representative Materials | Efficiency Range | Key Trait | |---|---|---|---| | **1st Gen** | Mono-crystalline Si (c-Si), Poly-crystalline Si (mc-Si) | **15% – 25%** | Mature, reliable, expensive | | **2nd Gen** | Amorphous Si (a-Si), CdTe, CIGS, GaAs | **10% – 18%** | Thin films, flexible, low cost | | **3rd Gen** | Multi-junction, Perovskite, DSSC, Organic PV | **15% – 47%** (lab) | Emerging, ultra-high efficiency potential | > [!TIP] > KTU examiners **frequently** test the classification table above. Memorize the generations, materials, and efficiency ranges. # 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Solar Pond — Theoretical Framework ### 2.1.1 The Three Zones of a Salinity-Gradient Solar Pond A solar pond is engineered in three distinct thermal-salinity strata: ``` ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ ZONE 1: UPPER CONVECTIVE ZONE (UCZ) │ ← Thin (≈ 0.2 m), low salinity │ Transparent, freely convecting │ Surface convection to air ├─────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ ZONE 2: NON-CONVECTIVE ZONE (NCZ) │ ← Thick (≈ 1.0–1.5 m), gradient │ Salt gradient insulates heat │ Stable, transparent ├─────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ ZONE 3: LOWER CONVECTIVE ZONE (LCZ) │ ← Thick (≈ 1.0–2.0 m), saturated │ Heat storage zone, black bottom │ 70 °C – 100 °C └─────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` ### 2.1.2 Stability Criterion — Why Convection is Suppressed Free convection occurs when the **adverse density gradient due to heating exceeds the stabilizing density gradient due to salinity**. The condition for stability is: $$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial z} \Big|_T \left(\frac{dT}{dz}\right) + \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial S}\Big|_T \left(\frac{dS}{dz}\right) > 0$$ Where: - $\rho$ = density of brine (kg/m³) - $T$ = temperature (K) - $S$ = salinity (% by weight) - $z$ = depth (m), positive downward If the term is **negative**, buoyancy-driven convection sets in and the pond loses its storage function. ### 2.1.3 Thermal Efficiency of Solar Pond $$\eta_{pond} = \frac{Q_{stored}}{Q_{incident}} = \frac{m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T}{I \cdot A \cdot t}$$ Where: - $m$ = mass of heated brine (kg) - $c_p$ = specific heat of brine (≈ 3,200 J/kg·K for saturated NaCl) - $\Delta T$ = temperature rise (K) - $I$ = incident solar radiation (W/m²) - $A$ = pond surface area (m²) - $t$ = duration (s) > [!IMPORTANT] > Saturated NaCl brine has $c_p \approx 3{,}200\text{ J/kg·K}$ — about **23% lower** than pure water (4,186 J/kg·K). This is a standard KTU numerical value. --- ## 2.2 Solar Cell — Semiconductor Physics Deep Dive ### 2.2.1 Photovoltaic Effect — Step-by-Step Physics A solar cell operation has **four sequential sub-processes**: 1. **Photon Absorption** — Photons with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ are absorbed in the semiconductor, generating electron-hole pairs (EHPs). 2. **Carrier Separation** — The built-in potential of the P-N junction ($V_{bi}$) sweeps electrons toward the N-side and holes toward the P-side. 3. **Carrier Collection** — Photogenerated carriers diffuse to the depletion region edges and are collected by the metal contacts. 4. **Current Extraction** — Collected carriers flow through an external load, performing useful work. > [!NOTE] > **Photons with $h\nu < E_g$** pass through the cell (transmission loss). > **Photons with $h\nu \gg E_g$** lose excess energy as heat (thermalization loss). > Only photons with $h\nu$ **just above $E_g$** are efficiently converted — the **detailed balance limit**. ### 2.2.2 The P-N Junction & Depletion Region At thermal equilibrium, electrons from the N-side diffuse into the P-side, and holes diffuse oppositely, creating a **depletion region** with a built-in electric field $\mathcal{E}$. The depletion width $W$ is given by: $$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q} \left(\frac{1}{N_A} + \frac{1}{N_D}\right)}$$ Where: - $\varepsilon_s$ = permittivity of semiconductor (F/m) - $V_{bi}$ = built-in potential (V) - $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\text{ C}$ (elementary charge) - $N_A, N_D$ = acceptor and donor doping concentrations (cm⁻³) --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > This is your **exam-day cheat sheet** — memorize every entry below. ### 🧾 MASTER FORMULA TABLE | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_g = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda}$ | Band gap energy from wavelength | $E_g$ in eV, $\lambda$ in nm | | 2 | $P_{max} = V_{mp} \times I_{mp}$ | Maximum power point | Watts (W) | | 3 | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{OC} I_{SC}}$ | Fill Factor (always < 1) | Dimensionless | | 4 | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}$ | Cell conversion efficiency | % or fraction | | 5 | $\eta_{pond} = \dfrac{m c_p \Delta T}{I A t}$ | Solar pond thermal efficiency | Dimensionless | | 6 | $V_{OC} = \dfrac{n k T}{q} \ln\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | Open-circuit voltage (ideal diode) | Volts (V) | | 7 | $I = I_L - I_0 \left[e^{\dfrac{qV}{nkT}} - 1\right]$ | Ideal solar cell I-V equation | Amps (A) | | 8 | $\tau_n = \dfrac{L_n^2}{D_n}$ | Minority carrier lifetime | Seconds (s) | | 9 | $L_D = \sqrt{D_n \tau_n}$ | Minority carrier diffusion length | Meters (m) | | 10 | $SR = \dfrac{I_{SC}}{P_{in} \cdot A}$ | Spectral response | A/W | ### 📘 Standard Constants (Bold = Must Memorize) - **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\text{ J·s}$ - **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\text{ m/s}$ - **Elementary charge:** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\text{ C}$ - **Boltzmann constant:** $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}\text{ J/K}$ - **Silicon band gap at 300 K:** $E_g = 1.12\text{ eV}$ - **GaAs band gap at 300 K:** $E_g = 1.42\text{ eV}$ - **AM1.5G reference irradiance:** $P_{in} = 1000\text{ W/m}^2$ --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Domain | Technology Used | Typical Output | |---|---|---| | Rural electrification (India) | Mono/Poly Si PV | 1–10 kWp rooftop | | Large solar farms (e.g., Bhadla, Pavagada) | Poly-Si / Mono-Si modules | 100 MW – 2 GW | | Spacecraft (ISRO satellites) | Multi-junction GaAs | 30% efficiency | | Building Integrated PV (BIPV) | Thin-film CIGS / a-Si | Flexible, semi-transparent | | Low-temperature process heat | Salt-gradient solar pond | 70–90 °C brine | | Electricity generation (Israel, USA ponds) | Salt-gradient solar pond + ORC | Up to 5 MWe | | Salt production | Shallow solar ponds | Traditional use | # 🔢 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Open-Circuit Voltage of a Solar Cell Starting from the **ideal diode equation under illumination**, we apply the boundary condition $I = 0$ (no load) to find $V_{OC}$. ### Step 1 — Write the Ideal Solar Cell Equation $$I = I_L - I_0 \left[ e^{\dfrac{qV}{nkT}} - 1 \right]$$ This is the **Schockley diode equation** with the light-generated current $I_L$ injected. ### Step 2 — Apply Open-Circuit Condition At open circuit, $I = 0$, so: $$0 = I_L - I_0 \left[ e^{\dfrac{qV_{OC}}{nkT}} - 1 \right]$$ ### Step 3 — Rearrange $$I_L = I_0 \left[ e^{\dfrac{qV_{OC}}{nkT}} - 1 \right]$$ $$\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1 = e^{\dfrac{qV_{OC}}{nkT}}$$ ### Step 4 — Take Natural Logarithm $$\ln\left( \dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1 \right) = \dfrac{qV_{OC}}{nkT}$$ ### Step 5 — Solve for $V_{OC}$ $$\boxed{V_{OC} = \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln\left( \dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1 \right)}$$ **Conversion logic**: The factor $\dfrac{kT}{q}$ is called the **thermal voltage** $V_T \approx 25.85\text{ mV}$ at $T = 300\text{ K}$. For typical cells $I_L \gg I_0$, so we can simplify: $$V_{OC} \approx \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln\left( \dfrac{I_L}{I_0} \right)$$ > [!TIP] > **Insight**: $V_{OC}$ depends **logarithmically** on light intensity. Doubling $I_L$ only increases $V_{OC}$ by a factor of $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$ times the thermal voltage — typically only **~18 mV gain**. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — Solar Cell Efficiency ### 📋 Problem Statement (KTU-Modeled) A silicon solar cell with area $A = 100\text{ cm}^2$ is illuminated with AM1.5G sunlight ($P_{in} = 1000\text{ W/m}^2$). The measured characteristics are: - $V_{OC} = 0.62\text{ V}$ - $I_{SC} = 3.20\text{ A}$ - $V_{mp} = 0.50\text{ V}$ - $I_{mp} = 2.80\text{ A}$ Calculate: **(a)** Fill Factor, **(b)** Maximum power, **(c)** Conversion efficiency. ### Part (a) — Fill Factor $$FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC}}$$ $$FF = \dfrac{0.50 \times 2.80}{0.62 \times 3.20}$$ $$FF = \dfrac{1.40}{1.984}$$ $$\boxed{FF \approx 0.7056 \text{ or } 70.56\%}$$ ### Part (b) — Maximum Power $$P_{max} = V_{mp} \times I_{mp} = 0.50 \times 2.80$$ $$\boxed{P_{max} = 1.40\text{ W}}$$ ### Part (c) — Conversion Efficiency Incident power on cell: $$P_{in,cell} = 1000\text{ W/m}^2 \times 100 \times 10^{-4}\text{ m}^2 = 10\text{ W}$$ $$\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in,cell}} = \dfrac{1.40}{10}$$ $$\boxed{\eta = 0.14 = 14\%}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Notice: A **realistic KTU numerical** integrates three sub-parts. Always convert units carefully — cm² to m² is the most common trap. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem — Solar Pond Heat Storage ### 📋 Problem Statement A salt-gradient solar pond has: - LCZ volume: $V = 2000\text{ m}^3$ - Saturated NaCl brine density: $\rho = 1{,}200\text{ kg/m}^3$ - Brine specific heat: $c_p = 3{,}200\text{ J/kg·K}$ - Temperature rise in LCZ over a sunny day: $\Delta T = 20\text{ K}$ - Pond area: $A = 2{,}500\text{ m}^2$ - Daily average solar irradiance: $I = 600\text{ W/m}^2$ for $t = 10$ hours Calculate: **(a)** Mass of heated brine, **(b)** Heat stored, **(c)** Pond thermal efficiency. ### Part (a) — Mass $$m = \rho \times V = 1200 \times 2000$$ $$\boxed{m = 2{,}400{,}000\text{ kg} = 2.4 \times 10^6\text{ kg}}$$ ### Part (b) — Heat Stored $$Q_{stored} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$ $$Q_{stored} = 2.4 \times 10^6 \times 3200 \times 20$$ $$Q_{stored} = 1.536 \times 10^{11}\text{ J}$$ $$\boxed{Q_{stored} = 153.6\text{ GJ}}$$ ### Part (c) — Thermal Efficiency $$Q_{incident} = I \cdot A \cdot t = 600 \times 2500 \times (10 \times 3600)$$ $$Q_{incident} = 600 \times 2500 \times 36000 = 5.4 \times 10^{10}\text{ J}$$ $$\eta_{pond} = \dfrac{Q_{stored}}{Q_{incident}} = \dfrac{1.536 \times 10^{11}}{5.4 \times 10^{10}}$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{pond} \approx 2.84 = 28.4\%}$$ > [!TIP] > A typical solar pond efficiency is **15–30%**. The remaining energy is lost to surface reflection, bottom/side conduction, and infrared re-radiation from the warm surface. # 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Solar Pond — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB SunLight[Sunlight I photons]:::input UCZnode[Upper Convective Zone UCZ
Low salinity 0 to 50 mm]:::layer NCZnode[Non Convective Zone NCZ
Salt Gradient 1.0 to 1.5 m]:::insulator LCZnode[Lower Convective Zone LCZ
Saturated Brine 70 to 100 C]:::storage Bottom[Dark Absorber Bottom Liner]:::layer HC[Heat Exchanger Coil]:::output Turb[Turbine or ORC Cycle]:::output Load[Electrical or Process Heat Load]:::output SunLight --> UCZnode UCZnode --> NCZnode NCZnode --> LCZnode LCZnode --> Bottom LCZnode --> HC HC --> Turb Turb --> Load classDef input fill:#ffe680,stroke:#b38f00,color:#000 classDef layer fill:#b3e0ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef insulator fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#993300,color:#000 classDef storage fill:#ff8080,stroke:#990000,color:#fff classDef output fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#006600,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Solar Cell — Cross-Sectional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Photon h nu greater than Eg]:::photon B[Front Contact Fingers]:::contact C[ARC Anti Reflection Coating]:::arc D[N type Emitter Layer]:::nlayer E[P N Junction Depletion Region]:::junction F[P type Base Layer]:::player G[Back Surface Field BSF]:::bsf H[Rear Metal Contact]:::contact I[External Load Wires]:::load A --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F F --> G G --> H H --> I B -.-> D I -.-> B classDef photon fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#cc8800,color:#000 classDef contact fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#333333,color:#000 classDef arc fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef nlayer fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 classDef junction fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#996600,color:#000 classDef player fill:#ccccff,stroke:#000099,color:#000 classDef bsf fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#330099,color:#000 classDef load fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#006600,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Solar Cell Generations — Hierarchical Classification ```mermaid flowchart TB Gen[Solar Cell Generations]:::root G1[First Generation Crystalline Silicon]:::g1 G2[Second Generation Thin Films]:::g2 G3[Third Generation Emerging]:::g3 Mono[Mono crystalline Si c Si
Eff 18 to 24 percent]:::g1a Poly[Poly crystalline Si mc Si
Eff 15 to 20 percent]:::g1b aSi[Amorphous Si a Si
Eff 6 to 10 percent]:::g2a CdTe[Cadmium Telluride CdTe
Eff 10 to 18 percent]:::g2b CIGS[Copper Indium Gallium Selenide CIGS
Eff 12 to 20 percent]:::g2c GaAs[Gallium Arsenide GaAs
Eff 20 to 30 percent]:::g2d MJ[Multi Junction Concentrator
Eff 35 to 47 percent lab]:::g3a Perov[Perovskite CH3NH3PbI3
Eff 25 to 33 percent lab]:::g3b DSSC[Dye Sensitized DSSC
Eff 11 to 15 percent]:::g3c OPV[Organic Polymer PV
Eff 10 to 18 percent lab]:::g3d Gen --> G1 Gen --> G2 Gen --> G3 G1 --> Mono G1 --> Poly G2 --> aSi G2 --> CdTe G2 --> CIGS G2 --> GaAs G3 --> MJ G3 --> Perov G3 --> DSSC G3 --> OPV classDef root fill:#ffeb99,stroke:#806000,color:#000 classDef g1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef g2 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 classDef g3 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 classDef g1a fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef g1b fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef g2a fill:#e2f0d9,stroke:#155724,color:#000 classDef g2b fill:#e2f0d9,stroke:#155724,color:#000 classDef g2c fill:#e2f0d9,stroke:#155724,color:#000 classDef g2d fill:#e2f0d9,stroke:#155724,color:#000 classDef g3a fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 classDef g3b fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 classDef g3c fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 classDef g3d fill:#f5c6cb,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Solar Cell Equivalent Circuit — Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR IL[Light Current Source IL]:::source D1[Diode D representing P N Junction]:::diode Rsh[Shunt Resistance Rsh]:::resistor Rs[Series Resistance Rs]:::resistor Vterm[Output Terminal Voltage V]:::output Iterm[Load Current I]:::output IL --- D1 D1 --- Rsh Rsh --- Rs Rs --- Vterm Vterm --- Iterm classDef source fill:#ffe680,stroke:#806000,color:#000 classDef diode fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#993300,color:#000 classDef resistor fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#333333,color:#000 classDef output fill:#b3ffb3,stroke:#006600,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > The equivalent circuit shows that real solar cells have **two parasitic resistances**: $R_s$ (series, ideally → 0) and $R_{sh}$ (shunt, ideally → ∞). The Fill Factor $FF$ is **severely degraded** by non-ideal $R_s$ and $R_{sh}$ values. # 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** **What is a salinity-gradient solar pond? List its three zones with their functions.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **salinity-gradient solar pond** is a solar collector and thermal energy storage device that uses a vertical salt concentration gradient to suppress convection and trap solar heat at the bottom. The three zones are: | Zone | Function | |---|---| | **Upper Convective Zone (UCZ)** | Thin surface layer of fresh/low-salinity water; convects freely to atmosphere | | **Non-Convective Zone (NCZ)** | Salt-gradient insulator zone; prevents convection between hot bottom and cool surface | | **Lower Convective Zone (LCZ)** | Saturated brine layer; stores solar thermal energy at 70–100 °C | --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **Define Fill Factor (FF) and open-circuit voltage ($V_{OC}$) of a solar cell.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Open-circuit voltage ($V_{OC}$):** The maximum voltage delivered by an illuminated solar cell when the external load is infinite (i.e., no current flows). For an ideal cell: $$V_{OC} = \frac{nkT}{q} \ln\left( \frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1 \right)$$ **Fill Factor (FF):** The ratio of the maximum power point power to the product $V_{OC} \times I_{SC}$. It indicates the "squareness" of the I-V curve. $$FF = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC}}$$ For a typical silicon cell, $FF \approx 0.70\text{–}0.85$. --- ## 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A — 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]** #### **(a)** Explain the construction and working of a salinity-gradient solar pond with a neat diagram. Discuss its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Construction:** A salinity-gradient solar pond is an artificial shallow pond (1.5–3 m deep) lined with **black high-density polyethylene (HDPE)** or **EPDM rubber** to prevent seepage. Salt (typically **NaCl** or **MgCl₂**) is dissolved at the bottom to create the three zones. **Working Principle:** 1. Sunlight penetrates the transparent UCZ and NCZ without much absorption. 2. Radiation reaches the **black bottom liner** and is absorbed as heat. 3. Heat warms the LCZ brine, but the salt-gradient in the NCZ prevents hot brine from rising (buoyancy is suppressed). 4. Hot brine is **drawn from the LCZ** through a heat exchanger, raising steam or pre-heating a working fluid for an **Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC)**. 5. Cooled brine is **reinjected** to maintain the gradient. **Stability condition:** The salinity-induced density gradient must overcome the thermal-induced adverse density gradient to prevent convection. **Advantages:** - ✅ Combines collection AND long-term storage in one structure - ✅ No moving parts → low O&M cost - ✅ Long lifespan (20–30 years) - ✅ Uses cheap, abundant materials (water, salt) - ✅ Can generate electricity or process heat **Limitations:** - ❌ Large land area required - ❌ Lower efficiency (15–30%) compared to PV - ❌ Risk of **gradient collapse** if mixing occurs - ❌ Salt leaching and groundwater contamination concerns - ❌ Affected by algae growth (needs periodic treatment) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Valuation Tip**: Always draw the **three-zone diagram** with salinity gradient. Marks are awarded **separately** for the diagram (2 marks), working (3 marks), and advantages/limitations (2 marks). --- #### **(b)** Explain the photovoltaic effect. Derive the expression for open-circuit voltage of an ideal solar cell. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Photovoltaic Effect Definition (3 marks):** The photovoltaic effect is the generation of a voltage and electric current in a material upon exposure to light. It is a physical and chemical phenomenon directly related to the photoelectric effect. **Four-step process:** 1. **Photon absorption** in semiconductor (requires $h\nu \geq E_g$) 2. **EHP generation** — photon energy creates an electron in the conduction band and a hole in the valence band 3. **Carrier separation** by the built-in field of the P-N junction 4. **Current collection** at external contacts **Derivation of $V_{OC}$ (4 marks):** The illuminated I-V characteristic of an ideal solar cell is: $$I = I_L - I_0 \left[ e^{\dfrac{qV}{nkT}} - 1 \right]$$ At open circuit, $I = 0$: $$0 = I_L - I_0 \left[ e^{\dfrac{qV_{OC}}{nkT}} - 1 \right]$$ Rearranging: $$e^{\dfrac{qV_{OC}}{nkT}} = \frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1$$ Taking the natural logarithm: $$\frac{qV_{OC}}{nkT} = \ln\left( \frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1 \right)$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{V_{OC} = \frac{nkT}{q} \ln\left( \frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1 \right)}$$ Since $I_L \gg I_0$: $$V_{OC} \approx \frac{nkT}{q} \ln\left( \frac{I_L}{I_0} \right)$$ --- ### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]** #### **(a)** Classify solar cells into different generations. Compare first, second, and third-generation cells with respect to materials, efficiency, cost, and applications. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Classification:** | Generation | Materials | Efficiency | Cost | Applications | |---|---|---|---|---| | **First Gen** | Mono-Si, Poly-Si | 15–24% | High | Rooftop, large PV farms | | **Second Gen** | a-Si, CdTe, CIGS, GaAs | 10–20% | Medium | BIPV, flexible modules, space | | **Third Gen** | Multi-junction, Perovskite, DSSC, OPV | 15–47% (lab) | Low (projected) | Research, emerging markets | **First Generation (Crystalline Silicon):** - Uses **mono-crystalline** (single-crystal Czochralski-grown) or **poly-crystalline** (cast multi-grain) silicon wafers (~180 µm thick) - Dominates **~95%** of current global PV market - Reliable, well-understood, but energy-intensive manufacturing **Second Generation (Thin Films):** - **Amorphous Si (a-Si):** Used in calculators, low-power electronics; suffers from **Staebler-Wronski degradation** - **CdTe:** Used by First Solar; excellent for utility-scale - **CIGS:** Highest thin-film efficiency (~22.6% record) - **GaAs:** Highest single-junction efficiency (~29.1%); expensive; used in space **Third Generation (Emerging):** - **Multi-junction concentrator cells:** Stacked sub-cells of different $E_g$ → reach **47.1%** (Fraunhofer ISE, 2024) under concentrated sunlight - **Perovskite cells:** Rapidly rising efficiency (>26%); stability issues - **DSSC (Grätzel cells):** Uses dye on TiO₂; cheap but low efficiency - **Organic PV (OPV):** Polymer-based; flexible, printable, low efficiency --- #### **(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the equivalent circuit of a solar cell and write its I-V equation. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Equivalent Circuit (3 marks):** A practical solar cell is modeled as: - A **light-generated current source** $I_L$ in parallel with - A **P-N junction diode** $D$ - A **shunt resistance** $R_{sh}$ (parallel, due to leakage) - A **series resistance** $R_s$ (in series, due to contact and bulk resistance) **I-V Equation (4 marks):** Applying Kirchhoff's Current Law: $$I = I_L - I_D - I_{sh}$$ Where: $$I_D = I_0 \left[ e^{\dfrac{q(V + I R_s)}{nkT}} - 1 \right]$$ $$I_{sh} = \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$ Therefore, the full equation is: $$\boxed{I = I_L - I_0 \left[ e^{\dfrac{q(V + I R_s)}{nkT}} - 1 \right] - \frac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}}$$ **Ideal Case:** $R_s \to 0$, $R_{sh} \to \infty$: $$I = I_L - I_0 \left[ e^{\dfrac{qV}{nkT}} - 1 \right]$$ **Key Effects:** - High $R_s$ reduces $FF$ and $P_{max}$ severely - Low $R_{sh}$ provides alternate current path, reducing $I_{SC}$ and $V_{OC}$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout** ⚠️ > Students commonly lose 2–3 marks on this question because they: > 1. **Forget the shunt resistance branch** $R_{sh}$ in the diagram > 2. **Don't include $I R_s$ inside the exponential**, losing the full expression > 3. **Don't mention the ideality factor** $n$ (typically 1 to 2) > 4. **Skip labeling** current directions — always show $I_L$ flowing inward, $I$ flowing outward through the load --- ## ⚡ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Read Before Exam** ### ✅ Solar Pond Essentials - ☐ A **salinity-gradient solar pond** has **3 zones**: UCZ, NCZ, LCZ - ☐ The **NCZ** is the heart — its gradient prevents convection - ☐ LCZ temperature reaches **70–100 °C**; applications include ORC electricity, salt production, process heat - ☐ Saturated NaCl brine: $\rho \approx 1{,}200\text{ kg/m}^3$, $c_p \approx 3{,}200\text{ J/kg·K}$ - ☐ Typical thermal efficiency: **15–30%** ### ✅ Solar Cell Physics Essentials - ☐ Photovoltaic effect: photons → EHPs → current via built-in field - ☐ **4 sequential steps**: absorption → EHP → separation → collection - ☐ $V_{OC} = \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln\left( \dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1 \right)$ - ☐ $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{OC} I_{SC}}$, ideal $FF \approx 0.89$ - ☐ $\eta = \dfrac{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC} \cdot FF}{P_{in}}$ - ☐ **Thermal voltage** at 300 K: $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85\text{ mV}$ - ☐ Silicon $E_g = 1.12\text{ eV}$, GaAs $E_g = 1.42\text{ eV}$ at 300 K ### ✅ Solar Cell Generations — Quick Recall - ☐ **1st Gen**: Mono/Poly Si — market workhorse, 15–24% - ☐ **2nd Gen**: a-Si, CdTe, CIGS, GaAs — thin-film, flexible, 10–20% - ☐ **3rd Gen**: Multi-junction (>47% lab), Perovskite (>26%), DSSC, OPV - ☐ **Record efficiency** (single-crystal Si): ~26.8% - ☐ **Record efficiency** (multi-junction, concentrated): ~47.1% ### ✅ Formula Flashcards - ☐ $E_g = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda(\text{nm})}$ — always in eV - ☐ Efficiency formula: $\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}}$ - ☐ AM1.5G standard: $1000\text{ W/m}^2$ at $25°C$ - ☐ Always convert $\text{cm}^2 \to \text{m}^2$ by multiplying by $10^{-4}$

topics.title: Principle of Working and Performance Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT862 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principle of Working and Performance Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Photovoltaic (PV) Cell — KTU 2024 Definition**
> A photovoltaic cell is a semiconductor device that converts incident solar radiation (photons) directly into electrical energy through the **photovoltaic effect**, discovered by Edmond Becquerel in 1839. The cell exhibits non-linear current-voltage (I-V) and power-voltage (P-V) characteristics whose shape depends on irradiance (G), cell temperature (T), and the semiconductor band-gap energy (E_g).

**Performance characteristics** of a PV cell/system are described by the parametric curves (I-V and P-V plots) and the extracted metrics — **Short-Circuit Current (I_sc)**, **Open-Circuit Voltage (V_oc)**, **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**, **Fill Factor (FF)**, and **Conversion Efficiency (η)** — that quantitatively define how well the device transforms sunlight into usable electricity.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"Water Wheel on a Sunny River" Analogy**
> Imagine a small water wheel placed in a flowing river. The amount of water (current) you get depends on (a) how fast the river flows (irradiance) and (b) how high you build a dam (voltage). If you completely block the river → no flow, but maximum pressure builds up (this is **V_oc**). If you remove the dam completely → maximum water flows, but at zero pressure (this is **I_sc**). The "sweet spot" where you get maximum useful work is somewhere in the middle — the **Maximum Power Point**.
> A solar cell behaves identically: photons are the "water flow," the p-n junction is the "dam," and the load resistor is the "mill wheel." Performance characteristics describe this trade-off.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|----------------|------|
| Solar Constant (AM 1.5) | G_0 | **1361 W/m²** (STC = 1000) | W/m² |
| Boltzmann Constant | k | **1.380 × 10⁻²³** | J/K |
| Electron Charge | q | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹** | C |
| Band-gap of Silicon | E_g | **1.12** | eV |
| Standard Test Conditions | STC | G = 1000 W/m², T = 25 °C | — |

### 1.4 List of Subsections within the Topic
- Principle of operation of a solar PV cell (photovoltaic effect)
- Equivalent circuit and ideal I-V equation
- Performance characteristics (I-V and P-V curves)
- Effect of irradiance and temperature
- Fill Factor and Efficiency
- Maximum Power Point Tracking (conceptual)
- Applications and real-world relevance
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principle of Working — The Photovoltaic Effect (Step-by-Step Logic)

A solar PV cell converts sunlight into electricity via a four-stage quantum mechanical process:

1. **Photon Absorption:** A photon strikes the semiconductor. If its energy *hν* ≥ band-gap energy *E_g*, it is absorbed and generates an **electron-hole pair (EHP)** in the depletion region of the p-n junction.
2. **Charge Separation:** The built-in electric field at the p-n junction (created during fabrication) immediately sweeps the electron toward the n-side and the hole toward the p-side, preventing recombination.
3. **Collection & Current Flow:** Photo-generated electrons travel through the external load via metallic contacts (grid on top, full contact at bottom), producing a direct current.
4. **Recombination Losses:** Not all EHPs survive. Bulk, surface, and Auger recombination reduce the available carriers — quantified by the quantum efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a p-n junction?** A single piece of silicon exposed to light produces charges, but they immediately recombine. The p-n junction provides the internal electric field needed to separate and collect them — without it, no usable current flows.

### 2.2 Equivalent Circuit of a Real PV Cell

A real solar cell is modeled by:
- A **photo-generated current source** (I_ph) — proportional to irradiance G
- A **diode** representing the p-n junction (dark current I_D)
- A **series resistance R_s** — accounts for contact and bulk ohmic losses
- A **shunt (parallel) resistance R_sh** — accounts for leakage currents across the junction

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Mandatory Formula Sheet for Solar PV Characteristics**

| # | Formula | Description / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---------|-----------------------------------|
| 1 | $I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left( \dfrac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT} \right) - 1 \right] - \dfrac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$ | **Implicit I-V equation of a real PV cell** (n = ideality factor, 1 ≤ n ≤ 2) |
| 2 | $I_{sc} \approx I_{ph}$ | Short-circuit approximation (valid when R_sh is large) |
| 3 | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln\!\left( \dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1 \right)$ | Open-circuit voltage (set I = 0, neglect R_s) |
| 4 | $P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$ | Maximum power (read at the "knee" of P-V curve) |
| 5 | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$ | **Fill Factor** (ideal FF ≈ 0.7–0.85 for good Si cells) |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A}$ | **Conversion Efficiency** (A = cell area in m²) |
| 7 | $V_{T} = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85 \text{ mV at 300 K}$ | Thermal voltage (commonly used approximation) |
| 8 | $I_{ph} = I_{ph,STC} \cdot \dfrac{G}{1000}$ | Photo-current scales linearly with irradiance (W/m²) |
| 9 | $\Delta V_{oc} \approx -2 \text{ mV/°C per cell}$ | Temperature coefficient of V_oc (Si cells) |
| 10 | $\Delta I_{sc} \approx +0.05\,\%/\text{°C per cell}$ | Temperature coefficient of I_sc (small positive) |

**KTU Board Tip:** Equation (1) is the *master equation* of Module 1. Memorize its derivation, simplify it for ideal cell (R_s → 0, R_sh → ∞), and be ready to draw the equivalent circuit on demand.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Sizing PV Arrays:** Efficiency η directly determines the number of panels needed for a given load.
- **MPPT Charge Controllers:** Algorithm designers rely on the P-V curve to track the MPP.
- **Site Assessment:** Irradiance G and ambient temperature are the two parameters that most affect annual energy yield.
- **Grid Integration:** V_oc sets the upper voltage limit; I_sc sets the short-circuit protection rating.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Ideal Solar Cell I-V Equation

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Starting from the diode equation and superposition, derive $I = I_{ph} - I_0\!\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$ for an **ideal** PV cell (R_s = 0, R_sh = ∞).

**Step 1 — The Diode Equation (Shockley, 1949)**
A p-n junction diode under forward bias V has current:

$$I_{D} = I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

where $I_0$ = reverse saturation current, $n$ = ideality factor, $k$ = Boltzmann constant, $T$ = absolute temperature, $q$ = electron charge.

**Step 2 — Photo-current Generation**
Light absorbed by the junction generates a current $I_{ph}$ flowing in the **reverse** direction (from n to p inside the cell, but appearing positive out of the +ve terminal to the external load).

**Step 3 — Apply Superposition**
By the principle of superposition (light-generated current adds linearly to diode current):

$$I = I_{ph} - I_{D}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Diode Equation**

$$I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange**

$$I = I_{ph} + I_0 - I_0 \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{nkT}\right)$$

This is the **ideal solar cell equation** — the foundation for all PV analysis. ✔

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Open-Circuit Voltage (V_oc)

**Step 1 — Boundary Condition**
At open circuit, the external terminals are disconnected, so $I = 0$.

**Step 2 — Substitute I = 0 into ideal equation**

$$0 = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 3 — Solve for V_oc**

$$I_{ph} = I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange the exponential term**

$$\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) = \dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1$$

**Step 5 — Take natural logarithm on both sides**

$$\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right)$$

**Step 6 — Isolate V_oc**

$$V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right) \quad \blacksquare$$

Since $I_{ph} \gg I_0$ under sunlight, we can approximate $V_{oc} \approx \dfrac{nkT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0}\right)$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Fill Factor (FF)

**Step 1 — Define the Rectangular Areas**
On the I-V curve, the rectangle formed by V_oc × I_sc represents the *ideal maximum* power. The *actual* maximum power P_max = V_mp · I_mp lies inside this rectangle.

**Step 2 — Express as Ratios**

$$FF = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

**Step 3 — Empirically Refined Expression (KTU frequently asked)**

$$FF_0 = \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$$

where the **normalized open-circuit voltage** is:

$$v_{oc} = \dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}$$

**Step 4 — Example Numerical Evaluation**
For a typical silicon cell with $V_{oc} = 0.65$ V at 300 K, with $n = 1.3$:

$$v_{oc} = \dfrac{(1.602\times10^{-19})(0.65)}{(1.3)(1.380\times10^{-23})(300)} \approx 19.21$$

Then:

$$FF_0 = \dfrac{19.21 - \ln(19.21 + 0.72)}{19.21 + 1} = \dfrac{19.21 - 2.97}{20.21} \approx 0.804$$

This matches the typical silicon FF range of **0.70 – 0.85**. ✔

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Cell Efficiency (η)

**Step 1 — Definition**

$$\eta = \dfrac{\text{Electrical Power Out}}{\text{Solar Power In}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute P_max and P_in**

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{G \cdot A}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute P_max = V_oc · I_sc · FF**

$$\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example**
A 156 mm × 156 mm mono-crystalline Si cell has V_oc = 0.65 V, I_sc = 9 A, FF = 0.78 at STC.

$$A = 0.156 \times 0.156 = 0.0243 \text{ m}^2, \quad G = 1000 \text{ W/m}^2$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{0.65 \times 9 \times 0.78}{1000 \times 0.0243} = \dfrac{4.563}{24.3} \approx 0.188 = 18.8\,\%$$

This is consistent with commercially available premium silicon PV cells.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Varying G and T

| Parameter Change | I_sc | V_oc | P_max | η |
|------------------|------|------|-------|---|
| ↑ Irradiance G | ↑ linearly | ↑ logarithmic | ↑↑ significantly | ↑ |
| ↑ Temperature T | ↑ slightly (+0.05%/°C) | ↓↓ linearly (–2 mV/°C) | ↓ | ↓ significantly |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Fact:** Solar cells *lose* about 0.4–0.5 % efficiency for every 1 °C rise in cell temperature above 25 °C. This is why panel cooling and ventilation are critical in tropical Kerala installations.

---

### 3.6 Maximum Power Point — Analytical Condition

The MPP occurs where the derivative of power with respect to voltage equals zero:

$$\dfrac{dP}{dV} = \dfrac{d(IV)}{dV} = I + V\dfrac{dI}{dV} = 0$$

For the ideal cell:

$$\dfrac{dI}{dV} = -\dfrac{q I_0}{nkT}\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{nkT}\right) = -\dfrac{q}{nkT}\!\left(I_{ph} + I_0 - I\right)$$

Setting the derivative to zero and solving iteratively yields **V_mp ≈ 0.85 V_oc** and **I_mp ≈ 0.9 I_sc** for typical Si cells — a useful rule of thumb for KTU numericals.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Equivalent Circuit of a Real Solar Cell

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Incident Photons G W per m2] --> B[Photo Current Source Iph]
    B --> C[Node A Positive Terminal]
    D[Diode Dark Current ID] --> C
    C --> E[Series Resistance Rs]
    E --> F[Node B Negative Terminal]
    G[Shunt Resistance Rsh] --> C
    G --> F
    F --> H[External Load RLoad]
    H --> C
    style B fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#003366
    style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
    style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    style H fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082
```

### 4.2 PV System Architecture — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    SOLAR[Solar Irradiance G] --> PV[PV Array of N cells in series parallel]
    PV --> DCB[DC Bus with Blocking Diode]
    DCB --> INV[MPPT Charge Controller or Inverter]
    INV --> LOAD[AC or DC Load]
    PV --> SENS[Temperature and Irradiance Sensors]
    SENS --> MPPT[MPPT Algorithm Block]
    MPPT --> INV
    INV --> GRID[Grid Tie or Battery Bank]
    style SOLAR fill:#FFD700
    style PV fill:#FFA500
    style MPPT fill:#90EE90
    style INV fill:#87CEEB
```

### 4.3 I-V and P-V Characteristic Curves (Conceptual Sketch)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph IV_PV_Region
        AX1[Voltage V axis Volts] --> BX1
        AY1[Current I or Power P axis] --> BX1
    end
    BX1[Curve Plot Region]
    BX1 --> DESC1[ISc: I intercept on Y axis when V equals 0]
    BX1 --> DESC2[VOC: V intercept on X axis when I equals 0]
    BX1 --> DESC3[Knee at MPP where P equals Vmp times Imp]
    BX1 --> DESC4[P V curve rises then falls through a maximum]
    style BX1 fill:#F0F8FF
    style DESC1 fill:#FFE4B5
    style DESC2 fill:#FFE4B5
    style DESC3 fill:#90EE90
    style DESC4 fill:#FFB6C1
```

### 4.4 Working Principle Flow — Photovoltaic Effect

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Photon h nu greater than Eg strikes p n junction] --> B[Photon absorbed in depletion region]
    B --> C[Electron hole pair EHP generated]
    C --> D[Built in electric field separates EHP]
    D --> E[Electron moves to n side and travels via front contact]
    D --> F[Hole moves to p side and travels via back contact]
    E --> G[Current flows through external load]
    F --> G
    G --> H[DC electrical power delivered P equals VI]
    style A fill:#FFD700
    style D fill:#FF6347
    style H fill:#32CD32
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*With a neat sketch, explain the principle of operation of a solar photovoltaic cell.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A solar PV cell is a semiconductor p-n junction device that converts sunlight directly into electricity using the **photovoltaic effect**. (½ mark)

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
1. When a photon with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ strikes the depletion region of the p-n junction, it is absorbed and generates an **electron-hole pair**.
2. The **built-in electric field** at the junction immediately separates the electron (swept to n-side) and the hole (swept to p-side), preventing recombination.
3. These photo-generated charge carriers are collected through metallic contacts (grid on top, full plate at bottom) and flow through the external load, producing a direct current.

The magnitude of the resulting photo-current is proportional to the incident irradiance G, while the voltage depends logarithmically on G via the diode equation. (½ mark for neat sketch showing p-n junction, depletion region, load, and incident photons)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define Fill Factor and Efficiency of a solar cell. State their typical values for a silicon cell.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Fill Factor (FF):** It is the ratio of the maximum obtainable power to the product of open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current. (1 mark)

$$FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

**Efficiency (η):** It is the ratio of electrical power output to the solar power incident on the cell. (1 mark)

$$\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A} \times 100\,\%$$

**Typical values for silicon cells:** FF ≈ **0.70 – 0.85**, η ≈ **15 – 22 %**. (1 mark)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the I-V characteristic equation of an ideal solar cell starting from the diode equation. Explain the significance of each term.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A silicon solar cell has the following parameters at STC (G = 1000 W/m², T = 300 K):
- Short-circuit current, $I_{sc} = 3.2$ A
- Open-circuit voltage, $V_{oc} = 0.62$ V
- Fill factor, $FF = 0.78$
- Cell area, $A = 100$ cm²

Calculate: (i) Maximum power output, (ii) Conversion efficiency, (iii) The photo-current and dark saturation current if $n = 1.3$ and $T = 300$ K.

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):** See **Section 3.1** above for the full step-by-step derivation. Key steps to write:
- Start with diode equation $I_D = I_0\!\left[\exp(qV/nkT) - 1\right]$ (2 marks)
- Apply superposition: light current adds to diode current in opposite sense (2 marks)
- Final ideal equation: $I = I_{ph} - I_0\!\left[\exp(qV/nkT) - 1\right]$ (1 mark)
- Significance: $I_{ph}$ ∝ G (irradiance); $I_0$ depends on material; exponential term shows non-linear V-dependence; at V=0 → I=I_sc; at I=0 → V=V_oc (2 marks)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Maximum Power Output (2 marks):**

$$P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF = 0.62 \times 3.2 \times 0.78$$

$$P_{max} = 1.5475 \text{ W} \approx 1.55 \text{ W}$$

**(ii) Conversion Efficiency (2 marks):**

Convert area: $A = 100 \text{ cm}^2 = 100 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 0.01 \text{ m}^2$

$$\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A} = \dfrac{0.62 \times 3.2 \times 0.78}{1000 \times 0.01}$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{1.5475}{10} = 0.15475 = 15.48\,\%$$

**(iii) Photo-current and Saturation Current (3 marks):**

At STC, photo-current ≈ short-circuit current: $I_{ph} \approx I_{sc} = 3.2$ A. (1 mark)

For $I_0$, use the open-circuit condition with $I_{ph} \gg I_0$:

$$V_{oc} \approx \dfrac{nkT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0}\right)$$

Thermal voltage at 300 K: $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} = \dfrac{1.380\times10^{-23}\times 300}{1.602\times10^{-19}} \approx 0.02585$ V = 25.85 mV (1 mark)

Rearrange:

$$I_0 = I_{ph}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}\right) = 3.2 \times \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-0.62}{1.3 \times 0.02585}\right)$$

$$I_0 = 3.2 \times \exp(-18.43) = 3.2 \times 1.01 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$I_0 \approx 3.23 \times 10^{-8} \text{ A} = 32.3 \text{ nA}$$ (1 mark)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the equivalent circuit of a real solar cell and explain the role of series and shunt resistances. How do they affect the I-V curve and fill factor?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the effect of (i) irradiance and (ii) temperature on the I-V and P-V characteristics of a solar cell, with suitable diagrams and equations.

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Equivalent Circuit and Resistances (7 marks):**

**Circuit Diagram (2 marks):** Draw a current source $I_{ph}$ in parallel with a diode and $R_{sh}$, the combination in series with $R_s$, connected to load terminals.

**Series Resistance R_s (2 marks):**
- Represents ohmic losses in the semiconductor bulk, metal contacts, and interconnections.
- Causes a **voltage drop** $I \cdot R_s$ that *reduces* the terminal voltage available to the load.
- Effect on I-V curve: **tilts** the curve near V_oc, *reducing FF* significantly.
- Ideally $R_s \to 0$.

**Shunt Resistance R_sh (2 marks):**
- Represents leakage current paths across the p-n junction due to manufacturing defects.
- Causes a **current loss** $(V+IR_s)/R_{sh}$ that *reduces* the terminal current.
- Effect on I-V curve: **tilts** the curve near I_sc, *reducing FF*.
- Ideally $R_{sh} \to \infty$.

**Combined Effect:** Both non-ideal resistances reduce FF and hence efficiency. A high-quality cell has $R_s < 1 \text{ Ω}$ and $R_{sh} > 1000 \text{ Ω}\cdot\text{cm}^2$. (1 mark)

**(b) Effect of Irradiance and Temperature (7 marks):**

**(i) Effect of Irradiance G (3 marks):**
- $I_{sc} \propto G$ (linear increase) — see Equation (8) in formula sheet. (1 mark)
- $V_{oc}$ increases **logarithmically** with G (Equation 3): $V_{oc} \propto \ln(G)$. (1 mark)
- **Diagrams:** Show two I-V curves at $G_1 = 1000$ W/m² and $G_2 = 500$ W/m²; the lower-G curve is "shrunk" downward and slightly leftward. The corresponding P-V curve's MPP shifts substantially down and slightly left. (1 mark)

**(ii) Effect of Temperature T (3 marks):**
- $I_{sc}$ increases very slightly with T (small positive coefficient, +0.05%/°C). (½ mark)
- $V_{oc}$ **decreases linearly** with T (negative coefficient, –2 mV/°C per cell). This is the dominant effect. (½ mark)
- $P_{max}$ and $\eta$ **decrease** with rising T — for every 1 °C rise, efficiency drops by ~0.4–0.5%. (1 mark)
- **Diagrams:** Two I-V curves at $T_1 = 25$ °C and $T_2 = 50$ °C; the higher-T curve is shifted left (lower V_oc) with slightly higher I_sc, leading to lower area under the curve and lower FF. (1 mark)

**Practical implication:** In hot climates like Kerala, panel ventilation and mounting height above rooftop are crucial design considerations. (included in marks)

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the units in FF and η calculations** — always express η as a percentage. (–½ to –1 mark)
> 2. **Mixing up area units** — the area A in the efficiency equation MUST be in m², not cm². (–1 mark)
> 3. **Incorrect signs in superposition** — students often write $I = I_{ph} + I_D$ instead of $I = I_{ph} - I_D$. (–1 mark)
> 4. **Skipping the equivalent circuit diagram** — KTU examiners *always* award 1–2 marks for a clean, labelled circuit.
> 5. **Confusing temperature coefficients** — remember: ↑T → ↓V_oc (big), ↑I_sc (tiny), ↓η.
> 6. **Not simplifying to "ideal" when asked** — when $R_s \to 0$ and $R_{sh} \to \infty$, the exponential term simplifies; many students keep the full complicated expression. (–½ mark)

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **solar PV cell** is a p-n junction device that converts photons into electricity via the **photovoltaic effect**.
- ✅ The **master I-V equation** is $I = I_{ph} - I_0\!\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{q(V+IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{V+IR_s}{R_{sh}}$.
- ✅ **Ideal cell:** set $R_s = 0$ and $R_{sh} = \infty$ — the equation reduces to $I = I_{ph} - I_0\!\left[\exp(qV/nkT) - 1\right]$.
- ✅ $I_{sc} \approx I_{ph}$ (photo-current equals short-circuit current).
- ✅ $V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1\right)$ — logarithmically depends on irradiance.
- ✅ **Fill Factor** is the "squareness" of the I-V curve: $FF = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{oc}I_{sc}}$. Typical Si: **0.70 – 0.85**.
- ✅ **Efficiency** $\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc}I_{sc}FF}{G \cdot A}$. Commercial Si: **15 – 22 %**.
- ✅ ↑ Irradiance → ↑ $I_{sc}$ linearly, ↑ $V_{oc}$ logarithmically, ↑ $P_{max}$ significantly.
- ✅ ↑ Temperature → ↓ $V_{oc}$ linearly (–2 mV/°C), ↑ $I_{sc}$ slightly (+0.05%/°C), ↓ $\eta$ by ~0.4–0.5%/°C.
- ✅ $R_s$ (series) tilts I-V near V_oc; $R_{sh}$ (shunt) tilts I-V near I_sc — both reduce FF.
- ✅ **Thermal voltage** $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K is a high-yield KTU value.
- ✅ **MPP rule of thumb:** $V_{mp} \approx 0.85 V_{oc}$, $I_{mp} \approx 0.9 I_{sc}$ for typical Si cells.
- ✅ Real-world implication for Kerala: tropical climate demands **panel ventilation**, **elevated mounting**, and **MPPT controllers** to compensate for temperature losses.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production Process - Block Diagram Only - Applications: Solar Space Heating and Cooling of Buildings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT862 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Production Process - Block Diagram Only - Applications: Solar Space Heating and Cooling of Buildings ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Production Process — Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Solar Thermal Energy Engineering**, the **Production Process** refers to the sequential engineering stages by which incident **solar radiation** (insolation) is captured, converted into **usable thermal energy** (heat), stored when required, and finally distributed to meet a defined energy demand such as **building space heating** or **building cooling**. Because KTU specifies *"Block Diagram Only"* for this module, the engineering emphasis is placed on recognizing **functional blocks** and **energy flow paths** rather than on detailed component-level fabrication. The canonical production chain for a generic solar thermal system comprises **five functional blocks**: 1. **Collector Subsystem** — intercepts solar irradiance. 2. **Heat Transfer / Transport Subsystem** — carries the working fluid. 3. **Storage Subsystem** — buffers time-gap between supply and demand. 4. **Distribution / End-Use Subsystem** — delivers heat/cold to the load. 5. **Auxiliary / Backup Subsystem** — guarantees reliability during low-irradiance periods. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The phrase *"Block Diagram Only"* in your syllabus is a deliberate instruction. Do **not** write collector-by-collector manufacturing steps. Instead, draw and label the **functional flow blocks**, name the energy carrier at each stage, and identify the **thermodynamic transformation** occurring inside each block. ### 1.2 Applications — Formal Definition The two principal **building-sector applications** of solar thermal production are: - **Solar Space Heating:** Use of solar-derived heat to maintain indoor air temperature above a comfort set-point (typically **18 °C – 22 °C** as per IS 3362 / ASHRAE 55). - **Solar Cooling of Buildings:** Use of solar-derived heat (as the *primary energy input*) to drive thermally-activated cooling cycles such as **absorption refrigeration**, **desiccant evaporation**, or **ejector cooling**, producing chilled water or conditioned air (typically **12 °C – 18 °C** supply air temperature). ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy — The Solar Tea Kettle System:** > > Imagine you are trying to make tea using only sunlight. > > 1. You place a **black-painted metal pot** in the sun — this is your **collector** (it absorbs photons and gets hot). > 2. Water inside the pot boils — this is the **heat-transfer fluid** carrying energy. > 3. You pour the hot water into a **thermos flask** to keep it hot till evening — this is your **thermal storage**. > 4. You use the hot water to make tea for guests — this is the **distribution to load**. > 5. On a cloudy day, you light a gas stove — this is the **auxiliary/backup**. > > The *exact same logic* powers a building. The "pot" becomes a flat-plate collector, the "water" becomes a glycol mixture, the "thermos" becomes a water tank, and the "guests" become the occupants of the building. The **block diagram** of a solar thermal system is simply the engineering drawing of this kitchen workflow. ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Magnitude | |---|---|---| | **Solar Constant** | G_sc | **1367 W/m²** | | Peak insolation at surface (AM 1.5) | I | **1000 W/m²** | | Standard collector absorptivity | α | **0.92 – 0.97** | | Standard collector emissivity | ε | **0.05 – 0.15** | | Clear-day daily radiation (horizontal) | H | **5 – 7 kWh/m²/day** (tropical Kerala) | | Mean ground temperature | T_g | **≈ 20 °C – 25 °C** | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of the Production Process (Block-Wise) #### Block 1 — Solar Collector (Energy Capture Stage) - **Why?** The sun's irradiance is diffuse and low-grade (~1000 W/m²). To use it, we need a surface that absorbs *short-wave* solar radiation and emits *very little long-wave* radiation. This is achieved by **selective coatings** (e.g., black chrome, titanium nitride-oxynitride, or cermet). - **How?** Photons strike the absorber plate → kinetic energy of lattice vibrations → rise in plate temperature → conduction to fluid tubes → convective transport by working fluid (water, air, glycol mixture, or therminol). - **Engineering Output:** Useful heat gain $Q_u$ [W] and thermal efficiency $\eta_c$. **The governing energy balance on the absorber plate:** $$Q_{absorbed} = Q_{useful} + Q_{losses}$$ $$\alpha \tau_s \, I \, A_c = \dot{m} \, c_p \, (T_o - T_i) + U_L \, A_c \, (T_p - T_a)$$ where: - $\alpha$ = absorptivity of selective surface, - $\tau_s$ = transmittance of glass cover, - $I$ = incident solar irradiance [W/m²], - $A_c$ = collector aperture area [m²], - $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate of fluid [kg/s], - $c_p$ = specific heat of fluid [J/kg·K], - $T_o, T_i$ = outlet and inlet fluid temperatures [K], - $U_L$ = overall heat-loss coefficient [W/m²·K], - $T_p$ = mean absorber plate temperature [K], - $T_a$ = ambient air temperature [K]. #### Block 2 — Heat Transfer / Transport Subsystem - **Why?** Energy captured at the roof must reach the storage tank or load without excessive losses. - **How?** A pump (for liquid systems) or a fan/blower (for air systems) drives the working fluid through insulated pipes/ducts. - **Engineering Output:** Pumping power $W_p = \dot{V} \Delta P / \eta_{pump}$. #### Block 3 — Thermal Storage Subsystem - **Why?** Solar supply peaks at noon; building heating demand peaks at night. A mismatch of ~6–10 hours exists. Storage bridges this gap. - **How?** Three primary storage modes: 1. **Sensible storage** (water tanks, pebble beds, concrete). 2. **Latent storage** (Phase Change Materials — PCMs like Glauber's salt, paraffin). 3. **Thermochemical storage** (reversible reactions, e.g., $\text{CaO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \leftrightarrow \text{Ca(OH)}_2$). - **Engineering Output:** Storage capacity $Q_{st} = (m \, c_p \, \Delta T)$ for sensible systems, or $Q_{st} = m \, h_{fg}$ for latent systems. #### Block 4 — Distribution / End-Use Subsystem - **Why?** Deliver useful heat (or cooling effect) at the required temperature and rate. - **How?** Radiators, underfloor loops, fan-coil units, air-handling units (AHUs), chilled ceilings, or radiant panels. - **Engineering Output:** Heat delivered to conditioned space $Q_{load}$. #### Block 5 — Auxiliary / Backup Subsystem - **Why?** Solar is intermittent (cloud, monsoon, night). The system must guarantee uninterrupted comfort. - **How?** Electrical heater, gas boiler, biomass stove, or heat-pump integration. - **Engineering Output:** Auxiliary energy input $E_{aux}$ such that $Q_{solar} + E_{aux} = Q_{load}$ always. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize this table.** Every equation here has appeared (or is statistically likely to appear) in KTU model examinations for PESFT862. | # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Use in Exam | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\eta_c = \dfrac{Q_u}{I \cdot A_c}$ | Collector thermal efficiency | Direct numerical / descriptive | | 2 | $\eta_c = \alpha \tau - \dfrac{U_L (T_i - T_a)}{I}$ | **Hottel–Whillier–Bliss (HWB) equation** | Most frequent 14-mark derivation | | 3 | $Q_u = A_c \, F_R \left[ (\alpha \tau) I - U_L (T_{in} - T_a) \right]$ | HWB with heat-removal factor $F_R$ | Full derivation problem | | 4 | $Q_{st} = m \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | Sensible storage capacity | Storage subsystem question | | 5 | $Q_{st} = m \, h_{fg}$ | Latent (PCM) storage capacity | PCM problems | | 6 | $f = \dfrac{Q_{solar}}{Q_{load}}$ | **Solar Fraction** (annual) | System sizing problem | | 7 | $\text{COP}_{abs} = \dfrac{Q_{evap}}{Q_{gen} + W_p}$ | Coefficient of Performance (absorption chiller) | Cooling application | | 8 | $\text{COP}_{Carnot,heat} = \dfrac{T_c}{T_h - T_c}$ | Theoretical Carnot COP for cooling | Thermodynamic limit question | | 9 | $Q_{load} = UA \, \Delta T_{design}$ | Building heat-loss rate | Space heating design | | 10 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{Q_u}{c_p (T_o - T_i)}$ | Required flow rate | Pump/pipe sizing | | 11 | $I = I_{sc} \left( \dfrac{r_o}{r} \right)^2 \cos\theta_z$ | Direct beam irradiance on tilted surface | Geometry/angle question | | 12 | $\tau_d = \tau^d$ (or empirical Angstrom) | Diffuse transmission through glazing | Cover-loss estimation | | 13 | $\dfrac{Q_u}{A_c} = F_R(\alpha \tau) I - F_R U_L (T_{in} - T_a)$ | Linear HWB performance line | Plotting efficiency curves | | 14 | $\eta_{overall} = \dfrac{Q_{useful,delivered}}{I \cdot A_c \cdot t}$ | System (daily) efficiency | Energy audit problems | > **Note:** Use $\vert$ for absolute-value bars in exams (e.g., $\vert T_i - T_a \vert$) instead of $\mid$ — both are accepted by examiners. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Solar space heating** is deployed in **passive form** in Ladakh, Spiti, and **Ladakhi-style** buildings worldwide; **active form** is commercial in Germany, China, Israel, and parts of **Northern Kerala** (Wayanad hill stations). - **Solar cooling** is the most research-active area because **cooling demand peaks when solar resource peaks** (perfect *temporal synergy*). - Both applications reduce **grid peak-load**, **CO₂ emissions**, and dependence on **LPG / diesel** — directly aligned with India's **MNRE Solar Thermal Mission** and **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code) 2017** norms. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Hottel–Whillier–Bliss (HWB) Collector Equation The HWB equation is the **most important derivation** in this entire module. Below is the full, step-by-step derivation suitable for a KTU 14-mark answer. #### Step 1 — Energy balance on the absorber plate (steady state) Sunlight passes through the glass cover (transmittance $\tau_s$), strikes the absorber (absorptivity $\alpha$), and is converted to heat. In steady state: $$\alpha \tau_s \, I \, A_c = h_w \, A_c \, (T_p - T_f) + U_L \, A_c \, (T_p - T_a)$$ where $T_f$ is the *mean fluid temperature* and $h_w$ is the *internal heat-transfer coefficient* between plate and fluid (often combined into a plate efficiency factor $F'$). #### Step 2 — Introduce the Collector Efficiency Factor $F'$ Define $F'$ as the ratio of *actual useful gain* to *useful gain if the entire absorber were at fluid temperature*: $$F' = \dfrac{\dot{m} c_p (T_o - T_i)}{A_c \left[ \alpha \tau_s I - U_L (T_p - T_a) \right]}$$ After rearrangement (detailed algebra omitted here, but reproducible in exam by expressing $T_p$ from Step 1): $$Q_u = A_c \, F' \left[ \alpha \tau_s I - U_L (T_{pm} - T_a) \right]$$ #### Step 3 — Convert plate-mean temperature to inlet-fluid temperature using $F_R$ Define the **Heat Removal Factor** $F_R$: $$F_R = \dfrac{\dot{m} c_p (T_o - T_i)}{A_c \left[ \alpha \tau_s I - U_L (T_{in} - T_a) \right]}$$ Substituting and rearranging: $$\boxed{Q_u = A_c \, F_R \left[ (\alpha \tau) I - U_L (T_{in} - T_a) \right]}$$ #### Step 4 — Divide by $I \cdot A_c$ to obtain the efficiency form $$\boxed{\eta_c = F_R (\alpha \tau) - F_R \, U_L \cdot \dfrac{(T_{in} - T_a)}{I}}$$ This is the famous **straight-line performance equation**. When you plot $\eta_c$ vs $\dfrac{(T_{in} - T_a)}{I}$, you get a straight line whose slope is $-F_R U_L$ and intercept is $F_R (\alpha \tau)$. **Physical interpretation:** - Intercept → maximum possible efficiency (when fluid ≈ ambient). - Slope → rate of efficiency drop as fluid temperature rises. #### Step 5 — Numerical Illustration (Worked Example) > A flat-plate collector has $\alpha \tau = 0.80$, $F_R U_L = 4.5 \,\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$, $F_R(\alpha\tau) = 0.76$. Incident radiation $I = 800 \,\text{W/m}^2$, inlet temperature $T_{in} = 60\,°\text{C}$, ambient $T_a = 30\,°\text{C}$. Find efficiency. $$\eta_c = 0.76 - 4.5 \cdot \dfrac{60 - 30}{800}$$ $$\eta_c = 0.76 - 4.5 \cdot 0.0375$$ $$\eta_c = 0.76 - 0.169$$ $$\boxed{\eta_c = 0.591 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 59.1\%}$$ **Inference:** At high fluid temperatures, efficiency drops sharply — this is why solar cooling applications require **concentrating collectors** (parabolic trough, CPC) instead of flat-plate collectors. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Solar Space Heating vs. Solar Cooling | Engineering Attribute | Solar Space Heating | Solar Cooling | |---|---|---| | **Primary Energy Input** | Solar thermal (low-grade) | Solar thermal (medium-to-high grade, 80–200 °C) | | **Thermodynamic Cycle** | Direct heat transfer / heat pump | Absorption / desiccant / ejector | | **Useful Output Temperature** | 30 °C – 60 °C | 7 °C – 18 °C chilled fluid | | **Storage Mode** | Sensible water tank (most common) | Chilled-water tank, PCM cold storage | | **Typical COP / Efficiency** | $\eta_{system} \approx 30\%-50\%$ | $\text{COP}_{abs} \approx 0.6–0.8$ | | **Auxiliary Energy** | Electrical heater or boiler | Pump + small fan (no compressor!) | | **Solar Resource Match** | Needs winter sun (low in Kerala!) | Excellent — peak demand matches peak supply | | **Collector Type** | Flat-plate air or liquid | Flat-plate + single-effect absorption, or CPC | | **KTU Exam Favourite Topic** | Yes (Part B very common) | Yes (numerical on COP) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Master Block Diagram — Generic Solar Thermal Production Process ```mermaid flowchart LR A["SUN RADIATION
I W per m2"] --> B["COLLECTOR
Absorber Plate
Selective Coating"] B --> C["HEAT TRANSFER UNIT
Pipes / Ducts
Pump or Fan"] C --> D["THERMAL STORAGE
Water Tank or PCM"] D --> E["DISTRIBUTION
Radiator / AHU / Chilled Ceiling"] E --> F["CONDITIONED SPACE
Building Load"] A -.Backup.-> G["AUXILIARY HEATER
Electric / Gas / Biomass"] G --> F F -.Return Fluid.-> C ``` **Reading the diagram:** - Solid arrows = primary solar energy path. - Dashed arrow = auxiliary (backup) energy injection when solar is insufficient. - Return loop (bottom dashed) represents the cold-fluid return to be re-heated. ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Solar Space Heating Subsystem (Active Liquid System) ```mermaid flowchart TB S["SOLAR INSOLATION
I W per m2"] --> C1["FLAT PLATE
COLLECTOR ARRAY"] C1 --> V1["3 WAY VALVE
Flow Diversion"] V1 --> ST["INSULATED STORAGE
TANK"] ST --> HX["HEAT EXCHANGER
or DIRECT COIL"] HX --> R["RADIATOR /
UNDERFLOOR LOOP"] R --> RM["CONDITIONED ROOM"] RM --> P["CIRCULATION
PUMP"] P --> C1 AUX["ELECTRICAL
IMMERSION HEATER"] -.-> ST ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Solar Absorption Cooling Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart LR SR["SOLAR
RADIATION"] --> SC["COLLECTOR
80 to 180 C"] SC --> GEN["GENERATOR
LiBr Water or NH3 H2O"] GEN --> COND["CONDENSER
Heat Rejected"] COND --> EVAP["EVAPORATOR
Chilled Water 7 to 12 C"] EVAP --> ABS["ABSORBER
Recombines Refrigerant"] ABS --> GEN EVAP --> AHU["AHU FAN COIL
Cooling Coil"] AHU --> ROOM["CONDITIONED
ROOM SPACE"] COND --> CT["COOLING TOWER
or DRY COOLER"] AUX["AUX BOILER
or BACKUP"] -.-> GEN ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key insight:** In absorption cooling, the **generator** is the block that consumes solar heat.
