The **cooling tower / dry cooler** rejects heat from the **condenser** and **absorber** — this is *why absorption chillers need a heat-rejection path even while producing cold.* ### 4.4 Block Diagram — Solar Passive Space Heating (Direct Gain Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR S["SUN"] --> G["SOUTH FACING
GLAZING"] G --> TM["THERMAL MASS
Floor / Wall"] TM --> R["ROOM AIR"] R --> NL["NATURAL
VENTILATION"] R --> IN["INSULATION
Walls Roof"] TM -.Night.-> R ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Pattern]** **Q1. With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the production process of solar thermal energy.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The production process of solar thermal energy involves five functional blocks connected in series: 1. **Collector** — captures solar radiation using a selective absorber. 2. **Heat transfer / transport** — pumps the heated fluid to storage. 3. **Thermal storage** — stores excess heat for use during non-sunny hours. 4. **Distribution** — delivers heat to the load (room, water, process). 5. **Auxiliary backup** — supplies heat when solar is inadequate. Energy flow: Sun → Collector → Fluid → Storage → Load. A neat block diagram (as shown in Section 4.1) carrying all five blocks with labelled energy carriers earns **full 3 marks**. --- **Q2. Distinguish between solar space heating and solar cooling of buildings in three points.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Solar Space Heating | Solar Cooling | |---|---|---| | Required temperature | Low (30–60 °C) | Medium-high (80–200 °C) | | Cycle used | Direct heat transfer / heat pump | Absorption / desiccant | | Energy output | Warm air / hot water | Chilled water (7–12 °C) | --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Module 1 Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern] **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the production process of solar thermal energy. List the functional blocks and describe the role of each. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** *(Draw the 5-block diagram as in Section 4.1 — full marks for diagram, labelling and description.)* **Block 1 — Collector:** Intercepts solar irradiance and converts it to thermal energy. Flat-plate collectors dominate space heating; evacuated-tube and concentrating collectors are used for high-temperature applications. **Block 2 — Heat Transport:** Pump (liquid) or fan (air) circulates the working fluid through insulated pipes to minimize losses. Working fluids: water, water-glycol mixture, therminol VP-1 (high temp), or air. **Block 3 — Thermal Storage:** Buffers time-mismatch. Sensible water storage (cheapest), PCM latent storage (compact), or thermochemical storage (long-duration). **Block 4 — Distribution:** Delivers heat to radiators, underfloor loops, AHU coils, or process equipment. **Block 5 — Auxiliary / Backup:** Electric immersion heater, gas boiler, biomass unit. Engages only when storage temperature falls below set-point. **(b)** A flat-plate solar collector has the following specifications: - Aperture area: $A_c = 2.5 \text{ m}^2$ - $\alpha \tau = 0.78$ - $F_R U_L = 5.0 \text{ W/m}^2\cdot\text{K}$ - Incident radiation: $I = 900 \text{ W/m}^2$ - Inlet temperature: $T_{in} = 65\,°\text{C}$ - Ambient temperature: $T_a = 28\,°\text{C}$ **Compute:** (i) The collector efficiency. **(3 marks)** (ii) The useful heat gain per hour. **(4 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step (i):** Apply the HWB efficiency equation: $$\eta_c = F_R(\alpha\tau) - F_R U_L \cdot \dfrac{(T_{in} - T_a)}{I}$$ Substituting: $$\eta_c = 0.78 - 5.0 \cdot \dfrac{65 - 28}{900}$$ $$\eta_c = 0.78 - 5.0 \cdot \dfrac{37}{900}$$ $$\eta_c = 0.78 - 5.0 \cdot 0.04111$$ $$\eta_c = 0.78 - 0.2056$$ $$\boxed{\eta_c = 0.5744 \;\;\text{or}\;\; 57.44\%}$$ **Step (ii):** Useful heat gain per second: $$Q_u = \eta_c \cdot I \cdot A_c$$ $$Q_u = 0.5744 \times 900 \times 2.5$$ $$Q_u = 1292.4 \text{ W}$$ Per hour: $$Q_{u,hour} = 1292.4 \times 3600 \text{ J}$$ $$\boxed{Q_{u,hour} = 4.652 \times 10^6 \text{ J} = 4.652 \text{ MJ} = 1.292 \text{ kWh}}$$ --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Pattern] **(a)** Explain with a block diagram the working of a **solar absorption cooling system** for buildings. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** *(Draw the block diagram as in Section 4.3.)* A solar absorption cooling system uses solar heat as its primary energy input to drive an absorption refrigeration cycle. The four main blocks are: 1. **Generator** — receives hot fluid from solar collector (80–180 °C). Heat drives refrigerant (e.g., ammonia) out of solution. 2. **Condenser** — high-pressure refrigerant vapor rejects heat to atmosphere/cooling tower and condenses. 3. **Evaporator** — low-pressure liquid refrigerant evaporates by absorbing heat from building-return chilled-water loop → produces chilled water at 7–12 °C. 4. **Absorber** — refrigerant vapor is re-absorbed into the solution (e.g., water in LiBr–H₂O system). The **COP** typically lies between 0.5 and 0.8. An **auxiliary boiler** backs up the solar generator during low-irradiance periods. **(b)** A LiBr–H₂O single-effect absorption chiller is driven by solar heat. The generator temperature is $T_{gen} = 90\,°\text{C} = 363 \text{ K}$, and the evaporator produces chilled water at $T_{evap} = 8\,°\text{C} = 281 \text{ K}$. Compute: **(7 marks)** (i) The Carnot COP for the cooling cycle. **(3 marks)** (ii) If the actual COP is 60% of Carnot and the cooling load is 50 kW, estimate the required solar heat input to the generator. **(4 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step (i):** The Carnot COP for a refrigeration/cooling cycle operating between a high temperature $T_h$ (heat rejection) and low temperature $T_c$ (cooling) is: $$\text{COP}_{Carnot} = \dfrac{T_c}{T_h - T_c}$$ Taking $T_h = T_{gen} = 363 \text{ K}$ and $T_c = T_{evap} = 281 \text{ K}$: $$\text{COP}_{Carnot} = \dfrac{281}{363 - 281}$$ $$\text{COP}_{Carnot} = \dfrac{281}{82}$$ $$\boxed{\text{COP}_{Carnot} = 3.43}$$ **Step (ii):** Actual COP: $$\text{COP}_{actual} = 0.60 \times 3.43 = 2.06$$ By definition: $$\text{COP}_{actual} = \dfrac{Q_{evap}}{Q_{gen} + W_{pump}}$$ Neglecting pump work $W_{pump} \approx 0$: $$Q_{gen} = \dfrac{Q_{evap}}{\text{COP}_{actual}}$$ $$Q_{gen} = \dfrac{50}{2.06}$$ $$\boxed{Q_{gen} \approx 24.27 \text{ kW}}$$ **Inference:** The solar collector array must be sized to deliver ~24.3 kW of thermal power to the generator, plus system losses. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Read Carefully!** > > 1. **Block Diagram with no labels = 0 marks for the figure.** Always write "Solar Radiation → Collector → Fluid → Storage → Load" with arrows and label each block with the energy carrier. > 2. **Confusing "production process" with "manufacturing process."** The question is about *energy conversion*, NOT about how solar panels are manufactured. > 3. **HWB equation — sign error on $\Delta T$.** Many students write $(T_a - T_{in})$ instead of $(T_{in} - T_a)$. This gives *positive* slope → wrong physics, lose 2 marks. > 4. **Absorption cooling — forgetting cooling tower.** A solar absorption chiller still needs a heat-rejection path; examiners specifically deduct 1 mark if the cooling tower / condenser path is missing from the block diagram. > 5. **Solar Fraction definition:** Students often write $f = Q_{solar}/Q_{aux}$ instead of $f = Q_{solar}/Q_{load}$. Memorize the correct one. > 6. **Unit confusion:** Always quote $Q_u$ in **W or kW** (rate), and $Q_{u,hour}$ in **kWh or MJ** (energy). Mixing units = 1 mark deducted. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist - ☐ **Production Process** = capture → transfer → store → distribute → backup. - ☐ **Block Diagram Only** = functional flow diagram, not manufacturing flow. - ☐ **5 Functional Blocks**: Collector, Transport, Storage, Distribution, Auxiliary. - ☐ **HWB Equation** $\eta_c = F_R(\alpha\tau) - F_R U_L \cdot (T_{in} - T_a)/I$ — must memorize. - ☐ **Solar Fraction** $f = Q_{solar}/Q_{load}$ — annual metric. - ☐ **Sensible storage** $Q_{st} = m c_p \Delta T$; **Latent (PCM)** $Q_{st} = m h_{fg}$. - ☐ **Solar Space Heating** — direct low-temperature heat delivery; flat-plate collectors suffice. - ☐ **Solar Cooling** — needs *higher temperature* input; uses absorption/desiccant cycles. - ☐ **Absorption cooling cycle** has 4 blocks: Generator → Condenser → Evaporator → Absorber + Cooling Tower. - ☐ **Carnot COP** $\text{COP}_{Carnot} = T_c/(T_h - T_c)$ — use **absolute Kelvin** temperatures. - ☐ **Typical actual COP** of single-effect LiBr chiller ≈ **0.6 – 0.8**. - ☐ **Energy conservation on absorber plate:** absorbed = useful + losses. - ☐ **Selective coating** — high $\alpha$, low $\varepsilon$ → enables high-temperature operation. - ☐ **Storage solves** the time-gap problem between supply (noon) and demand (evening/night). - ☐ **Backup is mandatory** for solar systems in real installations.

topics.title: Solar Pumping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT862 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solar Pumping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SOLAR PUMPING — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Solar Pumping (Photovoltaic Water Pumping)** is the process of converting incident **solar irradiance** into electrical energy through a **photovoltaic (PV) array**, and then using that electricity to drive an electric motor coupled to a **hydraulic pump** that lifts or moves water from a source (well, river, reservoir) to a delivery point (tank, field, livestock trough).

A complete solar pumping system is formally defined (as per **IS 14665 / IEC 62253** aligned standards) as an integrated assembly of:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PESFT862 Module 1):**
> *"Solar pumping is the application of solar photovoltaic technology to lift and deliver water for irrigation, drinking water supply, and allied rural/agricultural uses, where the PV generator directly or through power-conditioning electronics drives a centrifugal, positive-displacement, or submersible pump."*

The five canonical subsystems are:
1. **PV Array** (DC source) — converts sunlight → DC electricity.
2. **Power Conditioning Unit (PCU) / MPPT Controller** — tracks the maximum power point and provides motor-compatible voltage/frequency.
3. **Electric Motor** — DC, brushless DC (BLDC), or AC (via VFD).
4. **Pump** — surface (centrifugal) or submersible (borehole).
5. **Hydraulic Circuit** — suction pipe, delivery pipe, foot valve, non-return valve, and storage tank.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Sun as a Diesel Tanker"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Think of it this way):**
> Imagine you are a farmer in Wayanad, Kerala, and every morning a **diesel tanker** arrives to fill a tank that runs your irrigation pump for 8 hours. The diesel represents stored *chemical energy* that powers a generator. A **solar pump** is essentially replacing that diesel tanker with the **sun itself** — sunlight falls on your roof "free of charge," and the PV panels convert it directly into electricity that runs the pump. The catch? The sun "tanker" only shows up during the day, and its delivery rate (irradiance) changes with cloud cover, season, and time. Therefore, the entire engineering challenge of solar pumping is **matching a variable energy source to a variable water demand**, with high efficiency.

## 1.3 Why Solar Pumping? (The Engineering Motivation)

- **Decentralized** — no grid extension required for remote/rural areas.
- **Zero fuel cost** — operating cost ≈ **zero** (only periodic cleaning).
- **Low maintenance** — typically **2,000–5,000 hours** between service intervals.
- **Environment-friendly** — no CO₂ emissions during operation.
- **Diesel displacement** — one 5 HP solar pump can displace **~1,500 litres of diesel/year**.

| Parameter | Diesel Pump | Solar Pump |
|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Diesel (paid daily) | Sun (free) |
| Operating cost (per hour) | ₹40–80 | ₹0 |
| CO₂ emissions | ~2.7 kg/L of diesel | 0 |
| Maintenance frequency | High (oil, filters) | Low (panel cleaning) |
| Lifespan | ~5 years | ~20 years (panels) |
| Noise | High | Negligible |

## 1.4 Classification of Solar Pumps

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Classification Mnemonic — "3D 3P 3M":**
> - **3D** = **D**irect DC, **D**C via battery, **D**C via MPPT
> - **3P** = **P**ositive displacement, **P**ump type — submersible, **P**ump type — surface
> - **3M** = **M**onoblock, **M**ulti-stage, **M**ixed flow

**(a) Based on Electrical Architecture:**
- **Direct DC Solar Pump** — PV array → DC motor → pump. No inverter, no battery. Simple, cheap, but pump runs only in sunshine.
- **DC Solar Pump with Battery** — PV → Battery → DC motor. Provides evening operation, but battery adds cost & maintenance.
- **AC Solar Pump with VFD** — PV → MPPT + Inverter (VFD) → AC motor → pump. Modern, efficient, supports three-phase submersibles.

**(b) Based on Hydraulic Construction:**
- **Surface Pumps** — installed at ground level; suited for suction heads < 7 m and low-to-medium total heads.
- **Submersible Pumps** — lowered into the borehole; designed for deep wells (up to 200 m+).

**(c) Based on Pump Mechanism:**
- **Centrifugal (Volute)** — best for high flow, low head.
- **Positive Displacement (Helical / Diaphragm)** — best for low flow, high head.
- **Mixed Flow / Multi-stage** — for medium-head, medium-flow applications.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Energy Conversion Chain (The "Why" Behind Each Stage)

The solar-to-water energy chain is a **four-stage cascade**, and total system efficiency is the **product** of each stage's efficiency (not the sum). This is the single most important concept in solar pump design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Efficiency Cascade Principle:**
> Total conversion efficiency = η_PV × η_MPPT × η_motor × η_pump
> Since each η < 1, the **multiplicative cascade** means even a small loss at any stage drastically reduces the water delivered. This is why *every* link in the chain must be carefully designed.

**Stage 1 — Photovoltaic Conversion (η_PV ≈ 15–22%):**
- Silicon cells convert photons to DC electricity.
- Output depends on **irradiance G (W/m²)**, **cell temperature T_c (°C)**, and **solar spectrum**.

**Stage 2 — MPPT Tracking (η_MPPT ≈ 95–99%):**
- Continuously adjusts the operating point of the PV array to harvest maximum available power under varying G and T.
- Algorithms: **P&O (Perturb & Observe)**, **Incremental Conductance**, **Fuzzy Logic**.

**Stage 3 — Motor Conversion (η_motor ≈ 75–92%):**
- DC motors: simple but brush wear; **BLDC motors**: 90%+ efficient, becoming standard.
- AC induction motors with VFD: 85–92% efficient, robust, three-phase availability.

**Stage 4 — Pump Hydraulic Efficiency (η_pump ≈ 30–70%):**
- Depends on the operating point relative to the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)**.
- Pumps run off-design as the sun varies → average η is **lower** than the rated peak.

## 2.2 Fundamental Hydraulic Relations

The water power (hydraulic energy) delivered by a pump is governed by the basic energy equation:

$$P_{hyd} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H_{total}$$

where:
- $P_{hyd}$ = hydraulic power (Watts)
- $\rho$ = density of water = **1000 kg/m³**
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)
- $H_{total}$ = total dynamic head (m) = static head + friction head + velocity head

In convenient units (with Q in **litres per second** and H in **metres**):

$$P_{hyd} \; (\text{W}) = 9.81 \times Q_{(L/s)} \times H_{(m)}$$

The **Daily Water Output** is simply:

$$V_{daily} = \int_0^{day} Q(t) \, dt \approx \bar{Q} \times T_{sun}$$

## 2.3 Affinity Laws for Pumps

When pump speed (n) or impeller diameter (D) changes, the pump performance parameters transform according to the **Affinity Laws** (also called the **Similarity Laws**). These are *critical* for solar pump analysis because solar pumps operate over a wide range of speeds as insolation changes.

$$Q \propto n, \quad H \propto n^2, \quad P \propto n^3$$

For a variable frequency drive (VFD) controlled AC pump running at fractional speed $f$:

$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{n_2}{n_1} = \frac{f_2}{f_1}, \qquad \frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{f_2}{f_1}\right)^2, \qquad \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)^3 = \left(\frac{f_2}{f_1}\right)^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for solar pumps:**
> When cloud cover reduces PV output by 30%, motor speed drops by ~30%, but the **hydraulic power drops by 1 - 0.7³ ≈ 65%**. This is why solar pumps cannot be "oversized by 30%" to maintain flow — the cubic relationship is brutal.

## 2.4 PV Array Sizing for Solar Pumps

The PV array must be sized to deliver enough daily energy to lift the required water volume:

$$E_{PV,\, daily} = \frac{V_{req} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot H_{total}}{\eta_{system}}$$

The number of PV modules in parallel/series is then:

$$N_{modules} = \frac{P_{pump,\,peak}}{P_{module,\, STC}} \times SF$$

where $SF$ (sizing factor) typically = **1.2 to 1.5** to account for soiling, temperature, and low-irradiance derating.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{hyd} = \rho g Q H$ | Hydraulic power | W | Direct power calculation |
| 2 | $P_{shaft} = P_{hyd} / \eta_{pump}$ | Shaft power required at pump input | W | Motor sizing |
| 3 | $P_{PV} = P_{shaft} / (\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{MPPT})$ | Required PV array output | W | PV array sizing |
| 4 | $Q \propto n$, $H \propto n^2$, $P \propto n^3$ | Affinity laws | — | Variable-speed analysis |
| 5 | $H_{total} = H_{static} + h_f + h_v$ | Total dynamic head | m | Head-loss problems |
| 6 | $h_f = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{V^2}{2g}$ | Darcy–Weisbach friction loss | m | Pipe sizing |
| 7 | $V_{daily} = \bar{Q} \times T_{psh}$ | Daily water volume | m³ | Output estimation |
| 8 | $N_{modules} = P_{pump,peak} \cdot SF / P_{mod}$ | Module count | — | Array sizing |
| 9 | $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{PV} \cdot \eta_{MPPT} \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{pump}$ | Total system efficiency | — | Performance analysis |
| 10 | $I_{mp} = I_{sc,STC} \cdot (G/G_{STC})$ | Short-circuit current scaling | A | Irradiance correction |
| 11 | $V_{oc,cell} \downarrow$ by ~2 mV/°C rise | Temperature derating | V/cell | Hot-climate design |
| 12 | $Q_{match} = \frac{P_{PV}}{9.81 \cdot H \cdot \eta_{sys}}$ | Flow achievable for given PV input | L/s | Output prediction |

**Standard Reference Values (must memorize):**
- Standard Test Conditions: $G_{STC}$ = **1000 W/m²**, AM 1.5, T_cell = **25 °C**
- Water density: $\rho$ = **1000 kg/m³**
- Gravitational acceleration: g = **9.81 m/s²**
- Peak Sun Hours (Kerala): **4.5–5.5 h/day**

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Solar pumping is the **single largest deployment** of off-grid solar PV worldwide — India alone has installed **over 4 lakh solar pumps** under the **PM-KUSUM scheme**. It is a flagship technology for:
- **Agricultural irrigation** in arid zones (Rajasthan, Saurashtra).
- **Drinking water supply** in tribal/rural hamlets (Kerala, Odisha).
- **Drip & sprinkler integration** for high-value horticulture.
- **Livestock watering** on pasture lands.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Hydraulic Power (P_hyd = ρgQH)

**Statement:** *The mechanical (hydraulic) power required to lift a volume of water to a height H at a flow rate Q is the rate at which potential energy is imparted to the water.*

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Define mass flow rate.**
The volumetric flow rate is $Q$ (m³/s). The mass of water flowing per second is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$$

**Step 2 — Define potential energy of that mass per unit weight.**
The work done per unit weight of water lifted through a head H is simply H (metres of water column). Therefore, the **weight** flow rate is:

$$\dot{W} = \dot{m} \cdot g = \rho \cdot Q \cdot g$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by head to get power.**
Power is work per unit time, so we multiply the weight flow rate by the head H:

$$P_{hyd} = \dot{W} \cdot H = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$$

**Step 4 — Substituting values.**
With $\rho$ = 1000 kg/m³ and g = 9.81 m/s², in the practical unit system where Q is in **L/s** and H is in **m**:

$$P_{hyd} \, (W) = 1000 \times 9.81 \times \frac{Q_{(L/s)}}{1000} \times H_{(m)} = 9.81 \cdot Q_{(L/s)} \cdot H_{(m)}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Affinity Laws

**Statement:** *For a geometrically similar pump running at two different speeds n₁ and n₂, the flow, head, and power scale with the ratios of speed as 1, 2, and 3 respectively.*

**Derivation (from dimensional similarity):**

**Step 1 — Geometric similarity requires:**
All linear dimensions scale by the same ratio. For two pumps of identical shape but different speed or impeller diameter, the velocity triangle of the impeller scales linearly with the peripheral speed:

$$\frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{n_2}{n_1} \cdot \frac{D_2}{D_1}$$

**Step 2 — Flow rate is volumetric throughput.**
Volumetric flow Q = velocity × cross-sectional area. Since velocity ∝ n·D and area ∝ D²:

$$Q \propto n \cdot D^3 \Rightarrow \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{n_2}{n_1} \cdot \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^3$$

**Step 3 — Head is proportional to (velocity)².**
Head H ∝ V²/2g, and V ∝ n·D, so:

$$H \propto n^2 \cdot D^2 \Rightarrow \frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)^2 \cdot \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2$$

**Step 4 — Power is proportional to (velocity)³.**
Power P = ρgQH, so combining the above:

$$P \propto n^3 \cdot D^5 \Rightarrow \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)^3 \cdot \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^5$$

**Step 5 — Special case for variable speed (constant D).**
Setting D₂ = D₁ reduces the relations to the simple form used in VFD-driven solar pumps:

$$Q \propto n, \quad H \propto n^2, \quad P \propto n^3$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Sizing a Solar Pump for a Kerala Farm

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU-style question):**
A farmer in Palakkad needs to irrigate 1 hectare of paddy. The total dynamic head is 12 m, the required daily water volume is 80 m³, the average system efficiency is 35%, and the Peak Sun Hours in Palakkad = 5.0 h/day. Size the PV array and the pump.

**Given:**
- $V_{daily}$ = 80 m³ = 80,000 L
- $H_{total}$ = 12 m
- $\eta_{system}$ = 0.35
- $T_{psh}$ = 5.0 h
- $g$ = 9.81 m/s²

**Step 1 — Hydraulic energy required per day.**

$$E_{hyd} = V_{daily} \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot H = 80{,}000 \times 9.81 \times 12$$

$$E_{hyd} = 9{,}417{,}600 \, \text{J/day} = 9.418 \, \text{MJ/day}$$

**Step 2 — Required average hydraulic power (over T_psh).**

$$P_{hyd,avg} = \frac{E_{hyd}}{T_{psh}} = \frac{9.418 \times 10^6}{5.0 \times 3600} = 523.2 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 3 — Required pump shaft power (assuming average η_pump = 0.45).**

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{pump}} = \frac{523.2}{0.45} = 1162.7 \, \text{W} \approx 1.16 \, \text{kW}$$

**Step 4 — Required electrical input to motor (η_motor = 0.88).**

$$P_{motor} = \frac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_{motor}} = \frac{1162.7}{0.88} = 1321.3 \, \text{W} \approx 1.32 \, \text{kW}$$

**Step 5 — Required PV array DC output (η_MPPT = 0.97).**

$$P_{PV} = \frac{P_{motor}}{\eta_{MPPT}} = \frac{1321.3}{0.97} = 1362.2 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 6 — Number of modules.**
Using 330 Wp mono-PERC modules and a sizing factor of 1.3:

$$N_{modules} = \frac{1362.2 \times 1.3}{330} = \frac{1770.9}{330} = 5.37$$

$$\boxed{N_{modules} = 6 \text{ modules (rounded up)}}$$

**Step 7 — Required flow rate check.**
The instantaneous flow at peak sun:

$$Q = \frac{P_{PV}}{9.81 \cdot H \cdot \eta_{sys}} = \frac{1362.2}{9.81 \times 12 \times 0.35} = 33.0 \, L/s$$

**Step 8 — Verify daily water delivered.**

$$V_{daily} = Q \times T_{psh} = 33.0 \times 5 \times 3600 / 1000 = 594 \, \text{m}^3/day \gg 80 \, \text{m}^3$$

This shows the array is well-sized; the deliverable volume is much greater than the requirement, providing margin for cloudy days.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Marking Note:** Always show the chain of efficiency multiplications explicitly. Marks are awarded at *each division* step — skipping a step loses 1–2 marks.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Pump Types

| Feature | Surface Centrifugal | Submersible (Centrifugal) | Positive Displacement |
|---|---|---|---|
| Max head (m) | 30–50 | 200+ | 100–250 |
| Max flow (L/s) | 50+ | 20+ | 1–5 |
| Suction head capability | ≤ 7 m (with foot valve) | N/A (submerged) | ≤ 7 m |
| PV array compatibility | DC, AC | Mostly AC (3-phase) | DC (preferred) |
| Efficiency at BEP | 60–70% | 65–75% | 50–65% |
| Cost (relative) | Low | High | Medium |
| Best use case | Open well, river, pond | Borewell, deep well | Slow flow, high head |
| Maintenance | Easy (accessible) | Difficult (pull-out needed) | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Solar Pumping System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Sun[Sunlight Irradiance G W per m2] --> PVA[PV Array String MPPT Tracking]
    PVA --> MPPT[MPPT Controller DC DC Stage]
    MPPT --> INVVFD[Inverter or VFD DC to AC Conversion]
    MPPT --> DCM[DC Motor or BLDC Motor]
    INVVFD --> ACM[Three Phase AC Induction Motor]
    DCM --> PUMP[Centrifugal or PD Pump]
    ACM --> PUMP
    PUMP --> HYD[Hydraulic Circuit Pipes Valves]
    HYD --> WTR[Water Delivered to Tank or Field]
    SENS[Level Sensor Tank Float] --> MPPT
    SENS --> INVVFD
    TANK[Storage Tank Optional Battery] --> WTR
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **PV Array** produces DC power proportional to irradiance G.
- The **MPPT controller** continuously optimizes the operating voltage to harvest peak power.
- For **DC pumps**, the MPPT output directly drives a **DC or BLDC motor**.
- For **AC pumps** (3-phase submersible), a **VFD/Inverter** converts the MPPT output to variable-frequency AC.
- A **level sensor** in the storage tank provides a feedback signal to shut down the pump when the tank is full (dry-run protection).
- Hydraulic losses in pipes, valves, and fittings reduce the net water delivered.

## 4.2 Subsystem Interconnection Map (Detailed View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PV[PV GENERATION SIDE]
        A1[Module 1 330 Wp Mono PERC] --> A2[Module 2 330 Wp]
        A2 --> A3[Module 3 330 Wp]
        A3 --> A4[Module N 330 Wp]
    end
    PV --> B[Combiner Box DC Isolator MCB SPD]
    B --> C[MPPT Charge Controller]
    C --> D[Inverter VFD Variable Frequency Drive]
    D --> E[Three Phase Submersible Motor 1 HP to 10 HP]
    E --> F[Coupling or Direct Drive]
    F --> G[Multi Stage Centrifugal Pump]
    G --> H[HDPE Riser Pipe DN 50]
    H --> I[Non Return Valve NRV]
    I --> J[Delivery Pipe to Field or Tank]
    J --> K[Storage Tank or Drip Network]
    K --> L[FLOAT SWITCH Level Sensor]
    L -.feedback.-> C
    L -.feedback.-> D
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow — Sizing a Solar Pump

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Start Determine Daily Water Need V daily in m3] --> Q2[Measure Total Dynamic Head H total in metres]
    Q2 --> Q3[Compute Hydraulic Energy E hyd = V rho g H]
    Q3 --> Q4[Divide by System Efficiency eta sys for PV Energy]
    Q4 --> Q5[Convert to PV Array Wattage using Peak Sun Hours]
    Q5 --> Q6{Select Pump Type}
    Q6 -->|Low head high flow| Q7[Surface Centrifugal Pump]
    Q6 -->|Deep well| Q8[Submersible Multi Stage Pump]
    Q6 -->|Slow flow high head| Q9[Positive Displacement Pump]
    Q7 --> Q10[Choose Motor DC BLDC or AC VFD]
    Q8 --> Q10
    Q9 --> Q10
    Q10 --> Q11[Final Selection and MPPT Sizing]
    Q11 --> Q12[Installation and Commissioning]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define solar pumping. List any four advantages of solar water pumping systems over conventional diesel pumping systems.**

**Model Answer:**

**Solar pumping** is the technique of using photovoltaic solar energy to drive an electric motor coupled to a water pump, lifting water from a source (well, river, reservoir) to a delivery point (tank, field) for irrigation, drinking, or livestock use. The system consists of a PV array, MPPT controller, motor-pump set, and hydraulic circuit.

**Four advantages over diesel pumping:**

1. **Zero recurring fuel cost** — solar energy is free; diesel cost escalates with global oil prices.
2. **Lower maintenance** — solar pumps have no oil change, no fuel filter, and minimal moving parts (typical service interval 3–5 years vs. 3–6 months for diesel).
3. **Environment-friendly** — zero on-site CO₂ emissions; no spill risk of diesel/lubricants contaminating groundwater.
4. **Longer asset life** — PV modules last 20–25 years versus 5–7 years for diesel engines.
5. *(Bonus)* Silent operation; ideal for residential and wildlife zones.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part A:** Do **not** write generic "renewable energy" advantages. Be **specific** to *pumping*. Marks are awarded only for system-level reasoning, not vague platitudes.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Explain the role of an MPPT controller in a solar pumping system. Why is it essential when using a centrifugal pump?**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of MPPT Controller:**

The **Maximum Power Point Tracker (MPPT)** is a power-electronics interface between the PV array and the motor. Its role is to:

1. Continuously vary the operating voltage of the PV array to maintain the **maximum power point (MPP)** under changing irradiance (G) and cell temperature (T_c).
2. Match the PV array's V-I characteristic to the motor's voltage/speed requirement.
3. Provide **soft-start** and **dry-run protection** for the pump.

**Why essential for centrifugal pumps:**

A centrifugal pump's power consumption follows the **Affinity Law**: P ∝ n³. Therefore, if the PV array is directly connected to a DC motor without MPPT, the pump will operate far from its maximum-power transfer point, especially during low-irradiance periods (morning, evening, cloudy). The result is **very poor water delivery and possible stalling** of the motor.

> For example, at 50% of rated irradiance, an unconditioned system delivers only ~10% of rated power (cubic relation), but with MPPT the array is forced to operate at its local MPP at that irradiance, recovering a major portion of the available power.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Part A:** Do **not** confuse MPPT with a simple charge controller. MPPT implies *active* tracking algorithms (P&O, Incremental Conductance), not just voltage regulation.

---

## 📗 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

> **[INTERNAL CHOICE: Answer ANY ONE — Question A OR Question B. Each carries 14 marks.]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**With a neat block diagram, explain the main components of a solar photovoltaic water pumping system. State the function of each component.**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (also see Section 4.1):**
`Sun → PV Array → MPPT Controller → Inverter (VFD) → AC Motor → Pump → Hydraulic Circuit → Storage Tank → Field`

**Function of each component:**

1. **PV Array** — converts solar irradiance (W/m²) into DC electricity using the photovoltaic effect. Output: 0.5 V per cell × N cells in series. Multiple modules connected in series-parallel achieve the voltage and current needed by the motor.

2. **MPPT Controller** — continuously adjusts the array's operating point to harvest maximum available power under variable G and T_c. Uses algorithms such as **Perturb & Observe (P&O)** or **Incremental Conductance (IncCond)**.

3. **Inverter / VFD** — converts DC to variable-frequency, variable-voltage AC. Allows the 3-phase induction motor to start and run efficiently even at low PV power output.

4. **Motor** — converts electrical energy to mechanical (rotational) energy. AC induction motors (IE3/IE4 class) are standard for > 2 HP systems; BLDC motors for sub-2 HP DC systems.

5. **Pump** — converts mechanical rotation into hydraulic energy (pressure + flow). Types: centrifugal, positive-displacement, submersible, surface.

6. **Hydraulic circuit** — pipes, foot valve, non-return valve (NRV), and control valves. Carries water from source to delivery point with minimal friction loss.

7. **Storage tank** — provides water availability beyond sunshine hours; allows pump to be sized smaller than peak instantaneous demand.

8. **Float switch / level sensors** — provide feedback to shut off the pump when the tank is full or when the well water level drops (dry-run protection).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**A solar pump is required to deliver 50 m³ of water per day from a depth of 25 m (total dynamic head). The PV site has an average of 5 peak sun hours/day and the overall system efficiency is 30%. Calculate: (i) the hydraulic energy required per day, (ii) the PV array power rating required, (iii) the recommended pump-motor rating.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $V_{daily}$ = 50 m³ = 50,000 L; $H_{total}$ = 25 m; $T_{psh}$ = 5 h; $\eta_{sys}$ = 0.30; $\rho$ = 1000 kg/m³; g = 9.81 m/s².

**(i) Hydraulic energy per day:**

$$E_{hyd} = V \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot H = 50{,}000 \times 9.81 \times 25 = 12{,}262{,}500 \, \text{J/day}$$

$$E_{hyd} = 12.26 \, \text{MJ/day} = \frac{12.26 \times 10^6}{3.6 \times 10^6} \, \text{kWh} = 3.41 \, \text{kWh/day}$$

**(ii) PV Array Power Rating:**

$$P_{PV} = \frac{E_{hyd}}{T_{psh} \cdot \eta_{sys}} = \frac{3410 \, \text{Wh}}{5 \times 0.30} = \frac{3410}{1.5} = 2273 \, \text{Wp}$$

**Apply sizing factor (1.3):** $P_{PV,design} = 2273 \times 1.3 = 2955 \, \text{Wp} \approx 3.0 \, \text{kWp}$

**Number of 330 Wp modules:** $N = 3000 / 330 = 9.1 \Rightarrow \mathbf{10 \text{ modules}}$

**(iii) Pump-Motor Rating:**

Required average hydraulic power: $P_{hyd,avg} = 2273 \times 0.30 = 682 \, \text{W}$

Pumping over 5 h, peak motor output: $P_{motor,peak} = 682 / (\eta_{motor} \cdot \eta_{pump})$. Assuming combined motor-pump efficiency 0.55:

$$P_{motor,peak} = \frac{682}{0.55} = 1240 \, \text{W} \approx 1.5 \, \text{HP}$$

**Recommended rating: 1.5 HP (1.1 kW) 3-phase submersible pump with VFD and 3 kWp PV array.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Derive the Affinity Laws for a centrifugal pump. Using these laws, explain how the operating point of a solar pump varies with the available solar irradiance.**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation** (refer to Section 3.2):

For a geometrically similar pump running at two speeds n₁ and n₂, the velocity triangle scales with the peripheral speed. The volumetric flow Q ∝ (velocity) × (area):

$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

Head is proportional to velocity squared:

$$\frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)^2$$

Power is the product of Q, H, and ρg, hence:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)^3$$

**Application to Solar Pumping:**

As solar irradiance G falls (cloudy, morning, evening), the PV array output voltage and current both fall. The MPPT + VFD reduces the motor frequency proportionally. Therefore, motor speed n ∝ G, and the resulting operating point of the pump is:

| Irradiance (% of peak) | Speed (% of rated) | Flow (% of rated) | Head (% of rated) | Power (% of rated) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 100% | 100% | 100% | 100% | 100% |
| 75% | 75% | 75% | 56% | 42% |
| 50% | 50% | 50% | 25% | 12.5% |
| 25% | 25% | 25% | 6.25% | 1.6% |

**Key Insight:** A reduction in irradiance of 50% reduces the available power to only **12.5%**. This is why solar pumps can pump very little water during low-light periods, and why storage tanks are essential.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**A 2 HP solar pump operates for 6 peak sun hours per day with an overall system efficiency of 32%. The total dynamic head is 18 m. Calculate the daily water output in litres and the volume of water saved per month (30 days) compared to a diesel pump of equivalent capacity that consumes 1.2 litres of diesel per hour and emits 2.68 kg CO₂ per litre of diesel.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $P_{motor}$ = 2 HP = 2 × 746 = **1492 W**; $\eta_{sys}$ = 0.32; $H_{total}$ = 18 m; $T_{psh}$ = 6 h; diesel rate = 1.2 L/h; $CO_2$ = 2.68 kg/L.

**Step 1 — Hydraulic power delivered by the pump (input − losses):**

$$P_{hyd} = P_{motor} \times \eta_{sys} = 1492 \times 0.32 = 477.4 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 2 — Instantaneous flow rate:**

$$Q = \frac{P_{hyd}}{9.81 \times H} = \frac{477.4}{9.81 \times 18} = \frac{477.4}{176.58} = 2.703 \, \text{L/s}$$

**Step 3 — Daily water output:**

$$V_{daily} = Q \times T_{psh} = 2.703 \times 6 \times 3600 = 58{,}389 \, \text{L/day} \approx 58.4 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$$

**Step 4 — Monthly diesel displaced (assuming equivalent operating time of 6 h/day):**

$$\text{Diesel saved/month} = 1.2 \, \text{L/h} \times 6 \, \text{h/day} \times 30 \, \text{day} = 216 \, \text{L/month}$$

**Step 5 — Monthly CO₂ avoided:**

$$CO_2 \, \text{avoided} = 216 \times 2.68 = 578.9 \, \text{kg/month} \approx 0.58 \, \text{tonnes/month}$$

**Step 6 — Annual CO₂ avoided (for context):**

$$CO_2 \, \text{avoided/year} = 0.58 \times 12 = 6.95 \, \text{tonnes/year}$$

**Summary:**
- Daily water output = **58,400 L**
- Monthly diesel saved = **216 L**
- Annual CO₂ avoided = **~6.95 tonnes**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Common Mark-Loss Zones in Part B:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — forgetting that 1 HP = 746 W (not 750 W or 736 W). KTUs are strict: 1 HP = **745.7 W** (mechanical).
> 2. **Confusing η_sys with η_pump** — η_sys is the *total* chain efficiency, not the pump alone.
> 3. **Skipping the sizing factor** in array sizing — always multiply by 1.2–1.3 for design margin.
> 4. **Not including static + friction head** as the *total* head — students often use only the static head.
> 5. **Forgetting the affinity-law cubic relationship** when explaining off-design performance.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU ESE):**

- ✅ Solar pumping = **PV array → MPPT → Motor → Pump → Hydraulic circuit → Water**.
- ✅ **Hydraulic power equation:** $P_{hyd} = \rho g Q H = 9.81 \cdot Q_{(L/s)} \cdot H_{(m)}$ (Watts).
- ✅ **Efficiency cascade:** $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{PV} \times \eta_{MPPT} \times \eta_{motor} \times \eta_{pump}$.
- ✅ **Affinity Laws:** $Q \propto n$, $H \propto n^2$, **$P \propto n^3$** (the cubic is *the* most-asked concept).
- ✅ **Standard constants:** $\rho$ = 1000 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s², $G_{STC}$ = 1000 W/m², 1 HP = 745.7 W.
- ✅ **Sizing factor** for PV array = 1.2 to 1.5 (always include in array sizing).
- ✅ **Total head** = static + friction + velocity head (never use just static).
- ✅ **Daily water output** = average flow × peak sun hours.
- ✅ **MPPT is essential** for centrifugal pumps (P ∝ n³ relationship).
- ✅ **Surface pumps** = low head, easy maintenance; **Submersible pumps** = deep wells, high head.
- ✅ **Positive Displacement pumps** = low flow, very high head (best for low-irradiance operation).
- ✅ Kerala's peak sun hours = **4.5 to 5.5 h/day** (regional data point often used in KTU questions).
- ✅ **1 HP solar pump displaces ~1500 L diesel/year** (key comparative metric).
- ✅ Always write the **sizing chain** as: $E_{hyd} \rightarrow P_{hyd,avg} \rightarrow P_{shaft} \rightarrow P_{motor} \rightarrow P_{PV}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Cooker
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT862 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solar Cooker

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Solar Cooker**: A device that harnesses **solar radiant energy** and converts it into **thermal energy** to cook, pasteurise, or heat foodstuffs without consuming conventional fuels (LPG, firewood, kerosene, or electricity). It operates on the principle of the **greenhouse effect** combined with **selective absorption** of incident solar radiation on a darkened absorber plate enclosed within a transparent medium.

A solar cooker is broadly classified under the family of **solar thermal devices** and serves as one of the simplest, most affordable, and most sustainable applications of solar energy — particularly suited for **decentralised domestic cooking** in tropical and sub-tropical regions like Kerala, where global horizontal irradiance (GHI) typically ranges between **4.5 – 5.5 kWh/m²/day**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: The student must be able to classify solar cookers, explain the constructional details of at least the **box-type** and the **parabolic dish-type** cookers, and evaluate their **thermal performance** using standard IS 13429 / MNES test procedures.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine leaving your car parked under the hot afternoon sun with all the windows closed. After a couple of hours, the interior becomes unbearably hot — almost oven-like. Three things are happening simultaneously inside the car:

1. **Shortwave sunlight** penetrates the glass windows (just like the glass cover of a solar cooker).
2. The dark upholstery **absorbs** this radiation and heats up.
3. The heated surface tries to **re-radiate** longwave (infrared) energy, but the glass is opaque to it — so heat gets **trapped**.

This is the **greenhouse effect**, and this is precisely how a **box-type solar cooker** functions. The dish-type cooker, on the other hand, behaves like a **magnifying glass** used by schoolchildren to focus sunlight onto a single point and burn paper — only industrialised.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Solar Cookers

Solar cookers are broadly divided into **three principal categories**:

| Sl. No. | Type | Temperature Range | Tracking Required |
|:-------:|:-----|:-----------------:|:-----------------:|
| 1 | Box-Type Solar Cooker (Direct/Indirect) | 100 °C – 150 °C | No |
| 2 | Concentrating / Parabolic Dish Type | 250 °C – 400 °C | Yes (2-axis) |
| 3 | Community / Institutional Cooker | 150 °C – 300 °C | Yes (1 or 2 axis) |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Hook**: *"Box cooks rice, Dish fries spice."* — Box cookers are slow and used for boiling/steaming; dish cookers reach high temperatures and can fry, roast, or bake.

---

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Test Metrics

The following values are essential for solving any numerical problem on solar cookers:

- **Solar Constant (Gsc)** = **1361 W/m²** (extraterrestrial irradiance at mean Earth–Sun distance)
- **Peak Sun Hours** assumed in tests ≈ **700 W/m²** to **1000 W/m²**
- **Standard Testing Insolation** in India (MNES) = **750 W/m²** ambient conditions
- **Ambient Temperature (Ta)** during standard test ≈ **30 °C – 40 °C**
- **Stagnation Temperature (T_stg)** = Maximum achievable absorber temperature with no load

> [!WARNING]
> Always state the **insolation value** (in W/m²) along with the **ambient temperature** (in °C) when quoting solar cooker efficiency — efficiency is meaningless without these operating conditions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Box-Type Solar Cooker — Construction & Working

### 2.1.1 Components (Inside-Out Construction)

1. **Outer Box (Casing)**: Made of galvanised iron (GI) sheet, fibre-reinforced plastic (FRP), or aluminium. Provides mechanical rigidity.
2. **Thermal Insulation Layer**: 5 – 8 cm thick layer of **glass wool**, **rock wool**, **paddy husk**, or **thermocol**. Minimises conductive/convective heat loss to ambient.
3. **Inner Tray (Reflector Base)**: A blackened aluminium or copper tray acting as the absorber.
4. **Absorber Plate**: A **matte-black painted** (or selective-coated) metallic surface with high **absorptivity (α ≈ 0.92 – 0.95)** and low **emissivity (ε ≈ 0.10 – 0.20)** for shortwave radiation.
5. **Transparent Cover (Glazing)**: A single or double layer of **toughened glass** (3 – 4 mm thick) that admits shortwave solar radiation but traps longwave re-radiation.
6. **Reflector Mirrors (Top/Side)**: Plane mirrors fitted at 30°–45° tilt on the lid and sides to augment incident radiation by 30%–50%.

### 2.1.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1 — Transmission (τ)**: Solar beam and diffuse radiation pass through the glass cover. Typical glass transmittance $\tau \approx 0.88$.
- **Step 2 — Absorption (α)**: The blackened absorber plate absorbs nearly all incident radiation ($\alpha \approx 0.95$). Plate temperature rises.
- **Step 3 — Greenhouse Trapping**: The heated plate re-emits **longwave infrared** radiation. Glass is nearly opaque to IR ($\tau_{IR} \approx 0$), so this energy gets reflected back to the plate.
- **Step 4 — Convective Suppression**: The enclosed air pocket between glass and plate suppresses buoyancy-driven convection.
- **Step 5 — Useful Cooking**: Cooking pots (preferably painted black on the outer side) placed on the absorber receive heat via **conduction, convection, and radiation**.
- **Step 6 — Heat Loss Reduction**: Insulation below the absorber minimises downward conductive loss.

> **Engineering Utility**: Box-type solar cookers are deployed extensively in **mid-day meal (MDM) schemes** of Indian government schools, **tribal welfare programmes**, and disaster-relief camps where fuel logistics are difficult.

---

## 2.2 Parabolic Dish-Type Solar Cooker

### 2.2.1 Construction

- **Reflector**: A parabolic concave dish made of **aluminium sheet, acrylic mirror, or silvered glass mosaic**. Aperture diameter typically **1.0 – 2.0 m**.
- **Focal Point**: All parallel solar rays converge at the **geometric focus** of the paraboloid.
- **Receiver / Cooking Vessel**: A blackened pot suspended at the focal point.
- **Tracking Mechanism**: A **manual or motorized two-axis sun-tracking system** (azimuth + altitude) is mandatory because the concentration ratio falls sharply with off-axis errors.
- **Support Frame**: Mounted on a sturdy metallic tripod.

### 2.2.2 Working Principle

The parabolic dish acts as a **point-focus concentrator**. The geometric concentration ratio (CR) is given by:

$$CR = \frac{A_{aperture}}{A_{receiver}} = \frac{\pi D^{2}/4}{\pi d^{2}/4} = \left(\frac{D}{d}\right)^{2}$$

where $D$ is the aperture diameter and $d$ is the receiver (pot) diameter. Typical values of CR for domestic dish cookers lie between **300 and 1500**.

### 2.2.3 Optical Errors

Real dishes suffer from:
- **Slope error** (surface deviations)
- **Sun shape error** (angular subtense ≈ 0.53°)
- **Tracking error**

These reduce the actual intercepted flux at the focus, and the **intercept factor (γ)** is the ratio of energy actually intercepted by the receiver to the total reflected energy.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula repository** for any KTU 2024 question on solar cookers. Memorise it thoroughly.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:------|
| 1 | $Q_{in} = A \cdot I \cdot \tau \cdot \alpha$ | Solar energy absorbed by plate | W |
| 2 | $Q_{loss} = U_{L} \cdot A \cdot (T_{p} - T_{a})$ | Top + bottom + edge heat loss | W |
| 3 | $T_{p} = T_{a} + \dfrac{\alpha \cdot \tau \cdot I}{U_{L}}$ | Stagnation plate temperature (steady) | °C or K |
| 4 | $\eta_{thermal} = \dfrac{m \cdot c_{p} \cdot \Delta T}{A \cdot I \cdot t}$ | Cooking thermal efficiency | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\eta_{optical} = \tau \cdot \alpha \cdot \rho \cdot \gamma$ | Optical efficiency of concentrator | dimensionless |
| 6 | $CR = \left(\dfrac{D}{d}\right)^{2}$ | Geometrical concentration ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $F_{R} = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot c_{p}}{A \cdot U_{L}}\left[1 - \exp\left(-\dfrac{A \cdot U_{L} \cdot F'}{\dot{m} \cdot c_{p}}\right)\right]$ | Heat removal factor | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\eta = F_{R}\left[(\tau \alpha) - \dfrac{U_{L}(T_{i}-T_{a})}{I}\right]$ | Hottel–Whillier–Bliss equation | dimensionless |
| 9 | $Q_{useful} = \dot{m} \cdot c_{p} \cdot (T_{f} - T_{i})$ | Rate of useful heat gain | W |
| 10 | $t_{cook} = \dfrac{m \cdot c_{p} \cdot \Delta T}{\eta \cdot A \cdot I}$ | Cooking time (water boiling test) | s |

**Symbol Glossary (used in table):**

$A$ = absorber/aperture area (m²); $I$ = solar insolation (W/m²); $\tau$ = transmittance; $\alpha$ = absorptance; $\rho$ = reflectance; $\gamma$ = intercept factor; $U_L$ = overall heat loss coefficient (W/m²·K); $T_p$, $T_a$, $T_i$, $T_f$ = plate, ambient, initial water, final water temperature (°C); $m$ = mass of water (kg); $c_p$ = specific heat (≈ 4186 J/kg·K for water); $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate (kg/s); $F'$ = collector efficiency factor; $t$ = time (s); $D$, $d$ = aperture and receiver diameter (m).

> [!NOTE]
> The symbol $\vert$ (vertical bar) used in derivatives like $\Delta T \vert$ must always be written as `\vert` or `\mid` inside LaTeX, **never** as a raw ASCII pipe `\|`, to avoid breaking Markdown tables.

---

## 2.4 Heat Loss Mechanisms in a Box Cooker

The **overall top loss coefficient** for a single-glazed box cooker is:

$$U_{t} = \left[\dfrac{1}{h_{w,i} + h_{r,pc}} + \dfrac{L_{g}}{k_{g}}\right]^{-1} + h_{r,pg} + h_{c,pg}$$

where:
- $h_{w,i}$ = combined radiative + convective coefficient between plate and glass (≈ 5–8 W/m²·K)
- $h_{r,pc}$ = radiative transfer plate-cover
- $L_g, k_g$ = glass thickness and thermal conductivity
- $h_{r,pg}$ = radiative transfer cover-sky
- $h_{c,pg}$ = convective loss from cover to ambient

> **Engineering Insight**: Adding a **second glass cover** can reduce $U_t$ by nearly 35–45%, raising stagnation temperature by **15 °C – 25 °C**. This is why double-glazed box cookers outperform single-glazed ones but cost more and add weight.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stagnation Temperature of the Absorber Plate

At **steady-state**, with **no cooking load**, all absorbed solar energy equals the heat lost to ambient. We start with the energy balance:

**Step 1** — Write the absorbed energy flux on the plate:

$$q_{in} = \alpha \cdot \tau \cdot I$$

*Explanation*: Only the fraction $\tau$ of the incident radiation passes through the glass; only the fraction $\alpha$ of what reaches the plate is absorbed.

**Step 2** — Write the heat lost from the plate to the surroundings through the overall loss coefficient:

$$q_{loss} = U_{L}(T_{p} - T_{a})$$

*Explanation*: All heat losses (top convection/radiation, bottom conduction, side edges) are lumped into a single overall coefficient $U_L$ (W/m²·K).

**Step 3** — Equate absorbed and lost fluxes at steady-state:

$$\alpha \cdot \tau \cdot I = U_{L}(T_{p} - T_{a})$$

**Step 4** — Solve for the plate (stagnation) temperature:

$$\boxed{T_{p} = T_{a} + \dfrac{\alpha \cdot \tau \cdot I}{U_{L}}}$$

*Explanation*: Higher insolation $I$, higher absorptance–transmittance product, and lower $U_L$ push stagnation temperature higher.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thermal Efficiency of a Box-Type Solar Cooker (Water Boiling Test)

The MNES / IS 13429 test procedure involves boiling a known mass of water and recording the time-temperature curve. We derive the **first-hour and second-hour efficiency**.

**Step 1** — Useful thermal energy gained by water in time interval $t$:

$$Q_{u} = m \cdot c_{p} \cdot (T_{f} - T_{i})$$

*Explanation*: $T_i$ is the initial water temperature at start of test interval; $T_f$ is the temperature at the end.

**Step 2** — Solar energy incident on the absorber area $A$ during the same interval $t$ (in seconds):

$$Q_{s} = A \cdot I \cdot t$$

*Explanation*: Multiply average insolation by area and duration.

**Step 3** — Define thermal efficiency as the ratio of useful heat to incident solar energy:

$$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{Q_{u}}{Q_{s}}$$

**Step 4** — Substitute:

$$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{m \cdot c_{p} \cdot (T_{f} - T_{i})}{A \cdot I \cdot t}$$

**Step 5** — Substitute the canonical MNES test values: $m = 1$ kg, $c_p = 4186$ J/kg·K, $A \approx 0.25$ m² (typical 50 cm × 50 cm plate), and standard insolation $I$ in W/m²:

$$\eta_{th} = \dfrac{1 \cdot 4186 \cdot \Delta T}{0.25 \cdot I \cdot t} = \dfrac{16744 \cdot \Delta T}{I \cdot t}$$

**Step 6** — For **standard first-hour efficiency** (water temperature rises from 30 °C to ~95 °C in one hour under 750 W/m²):

$$\eta_{1} = \dfrac{16744 \cdot (95 - 30)}{750 \cdot 3600} = \dfrac{16744 \cdot 65}{2700000} \approx 0.403$$

So a well-designed box-type cooker gives roughly **40 % – 50 %** first-hour thermal efficiency.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Optical Efficiency of a Parabolic Dish

**Step 1** — Fraction of incident solar radiation transmitted through the glass cover of the receiver (if any):

$$\eta_{1} = \tau_{cov} \approx 0.92$$

**Step 2** — Fraction reflected by the dish surface:

$$\eta_{2} = \rho_{dish} \approx 0.80 \text{ (for aluminium)} \text{ to } 0.92 \text{ (silvered glass)}$$

**Step 3** — Fraction of reflected rays intercepted by the receiver (intercept factor):

$$\eta_{3} = \gamma \approx 0.85 - 0.95$$

**Step 4** — Fraction absorbed by the blackened receiver:

$$\eta_{4} = \alpha_{rec} \approx 0.92 - 0.96$$

**Step 5** — Multiply all independent optical factors:

$$\boxed{\eta_{optical} = \tau_{cov} \cdot \rho_{dish} \cdot \gamma \cdot \alpha_{rec}}$$

**Step 6** — Plug in representative numbers for a domestic dish:

$$\eta_{optical} = 0.92 \cdot 0.85 \cdot 0.90 \cdot 0.94 \approx 0.661$$

Thus only ~**66 %** of incident solar radiation is absorbed by the cooking pot even before any thermal losses occur — the rest is spillage (intercept losses), reflection losses, or transmission losses.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Box-Type vs Dish-Type Cookers

| Parameter | Box-Type | Parabolic Dish |
|:----------|:--------:|:--------------:|
| Temperature Range | 100 – 150 °C | 250 – 400 °C |
| Concentration Ratio | ≈ 1 (no concentration) | 300 – 1500 |
| Sun Tracking | Not required | Mandatory (2-axis) |
| Cooking Time (1 L water) | 2.5 – 3.5 hours | 15 – 25 minutes |
| Best Suited For | Boiling, steaming, slow-cooking | Frying, roasting, baking |
| Typical Efficiency | 35 % – 55 % | 50 % – 65 % |
| Cost (Domestic, India) | ₹ 2,000 – ₹ 4,500 | ₹ 7,000 – ₹ 15,000 |
| Maintenance | Low | Moderate (tracking alignment) |
| Portability | High (≈ 8 – 12 kg) | Low (tripod mounted) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **figure of merit (FOM)** for a solar cooker is defined as $FOM = \eta \times T_{stg}$, where $\eta$ is in % and $T_{stg}$ in °C. A high FOM means the device is efficient *and* hot — desirable for fast cooking.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Box-Type Solar Cooker Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SUN(("☀️ Sun"))
    SUN -->|Solar Beam I W/m²| GLASS["Transparent Glass Glazing tau ≈ 0.88"]
    GLASS -->|Transmitted Radiation| AIR["Enclosed Air Pocket Convection Suppressed"]
    AIR -->|Incident on Plate| PLATE["Blackened Absorber Plate alpha ≈ 0.95"]
    PLATE -->|Conduction and Convection| POT["Cooking Vessel Painted Black"]
    POT -->|Useful Heat Q_u| FOOD["Food / Water Boils"]
    PLATE -->|Downward Loss Q_cond| INS["Glass Wool Insulation k ≈ 0.04 W/mK"]
    INS -->|Negligible Loss| OUTBOX["Outer GI Casing"]
    GLASS -->|Longwave IR Reflected Back| PLATE
    PLATE -.->|Re-radiation Trapped| GLASS
    REF["Top Plane Reflector Mirror tilt 30 to 45 deg"] -.->|Augments I by 30 to 50 percent| AIR
```

```mermaid
    SUN2 -->|Incident Beam I| DISH["Parabolic Reflector Dish Aperture D"]
    DISH -->|Reflected Concentrated Rays| FOCUS[("Geometric Focus Point")]
    FOCUS -->|Concentrated Flux CR times I| REC["Receiver Cooking Pot Blackened"]
    REC -->|Useful Heat| FOOD2["Food Cooked at High Temperature"]

    TRACK -->|Manual or Motorized| OPER[("Operator / Motor")]
```
