Wind Energy (Time) | $E = P \cdot t \cdot CF$ | kWh | CF = capacity factor | | Annual Energy Output | $AEO = P_{rated} \cdot 8760 \cdot CF$ | kWh/year | CF = 0.25–0.45 typical | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Where These Equations Are Used in Industry:** > > 1. **Wind Resource Assessment:** Engineers use the $V^{3}$ relationship to extrapolate short-term mast measurements to long-term hub-height wind speeds (essential for bankable feasibility studies). > 2. **Turbine Sizing:** The swept area $A$ relationship determines the optimal rotor diameter for a given rated power. > 3. **Site Comparison:** Wind Power Density (WPD) classification lets engineers compare sites independent of turbine technology. > 4. **Performance Curve Generation:** Manufacturers publish power curves $P(V)$ based on $C_{P}(\lambda,\theta_{pitch})$ look-up tables. > 5. **Yield Estimation:** Annual Energy Production (AEO) calculations for project financing use Weibull-distributed wind speeds integrated with the $V^{3}$ relationship. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Maximum Power Extraction (Betz Limit) > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU examinations on wind energy. Every step must be reproducible in the answer script. **Given:** An ideal actuator disk of area $A$ intercepts a uniform wind stream of velocity $V_{1}$. **Assumption:** The disk is frictionless, the flow is incompressible, steady, and one-dimensional with no rotational component. **Step 1 — Mass Conservation Across the Disk:** The mass flow rate must be equal at all cross-sections (continuity equation): $$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A_{1} \cdot V_{1} = \rho \cdot A \cdot V = \rho \cdot A_{2} \cdot V_{2}$$ This implies that since $V_{1} > V > V_{2}$, the stream tube must expand: $A_{1} < A < A_{2}$. **Step 2 — Bernoulli's Equation Application:** Applying Bernoulli's equation between the upstream region (far from disk) and the disk plane: $$P_{1} + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_{1}^{2} = P_{d} + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^{2}$$ And between the disk plane and the far downstream region: $$P_{d} + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^{2} = P_{2} + \frac{1}{2}\rho V_{2}^{2}$$ **Step 3 — Thrust Force on the Disk:** The net force on the disk equals the difference in pressure forces plus momentum change. By integrating pressure over the disk and applying the momentum theorem: $$F = (P_{1} - P_{2}) \cdot A = \rho \cdot A \cdot V \cdot (V_{1} - V_{2})$$ **Step 4 — Power Extracted by the Disk:** The power transferred to the rotor equals force multiplied by velocity at the disk plane: $$P = F \cdot V = \rho \cdot A \cdot V^{2} \cdot (V_{1} - V_{2})$$ **Step 5 — Introduce the Induction Factor:** Define $a$ such that the velocity at the disk is reduced by the fraction $a$ from the free-stream velocity: $$V = V_{1}(1 - a)$$ From the momentum equation, the wake velocity reduces by twice this factor: $$V_{2} = V_{1}(1 - 2a)$$ **Step 6 — Express Power in Terms of $a$ and $V_{1}$:** $$P = \rho \cdot A \cdot [V_{1}(1-a)]^{2} \cdot [V_{1} - V_{1}(1-2a)]$$ $$P = \rho \cdot A \cdot V_{1}^{2}(1-a)^{2} \cdot 2a V_{1}$$ $$\boxed{P = 2 \rho A V_{1}^{3} \cdot a(1-a)^{2}}$$ **Step 7 — Express Available Wind Power:** The total power available in the undisturbed wind through the same area $A$ is: $$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V_{1}^{3}$$ **Step 8 — Form the Power Coefficient:** $$C_{P} = \frac{P}{P_{wind}} = \frac{2 \rho A V_{1}^{3} a(1-a)^{2}}{\frac{1}{2} \rho A V_{1}^{3}}$$ $$\boxed{C_{P} = 4a(1-a)^{2}}$$ **Step 9 — Maximize $C_{P}$ by Differentiation:** $$\frac{dC_{P}}{da} = \frac{d}{da}\left[4a(1-a)^{2}\right] = 0$$ Apply the product rule: $$\frac{dC_{P}}{da} = 4\left[(1-a)^{2} + a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1)\right] = 0$$ $$\frac{dC_{P}}{da} = 4(1-a)\left[(1-a) - 2a\right] = 4(1-a)(1 - 3a) = 0$$ Either $(1-a) = 0$ (trivial, $a=1$ gives $C_{P}=0$) or $(1-3a) = 0$. **Therefore, $a = \frac{1}{3}$** **Step 10 — Compute Maximum $C_{P}$:** $$C_{P,max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27}$$ $$\boxed{C_{P,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \approx 59.3\%}$$ **Step 11 — Corresponding Velocities at Maximum Extraction:** $$V = V_{1}\left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{2}{3}V_{1}$$ $$V_{2} = V_{1}\left(1 - \frac{2}{3}\right) = \frac{1}{3}V_{1}$$ This confirms the elegant 3:2:1 velocity ratio for the Betz optimum. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Wind Power and Energy Calculation > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 80 m. The hub-height wind speed is 12 m/s and air density is 1.20 kg/m³. Assume a power coefficient of 0.40 (typical for modern HAWT). Calculate: (a) Swept area, (b) Available wind power, (c) Actual power output, (d) Maximum theoretical (Betz) power, (e) Annual energy production assuming 30% capacity factor. **Step (a) — Swept Area:** $$A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot (80)^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 6400}{4} = 1600\pi \approx 5026.55 \ m^{2}$$ **Step (b) — Available Wind Power:** $$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1.20 \cdot 5026.55 \cdot (12)^{3}$$ $$P_{wind} = 0.6 \cdot 5026.55 \cdot 1728 = 0.6 \cdot 8{,}685{,}878.4$$ $$\boxed{P_{wind} \approx 5{,}211{,}527 \ W \approx 5.21 \ MW}$$ **Step (c) — Actual Power Output:** $$P_{actual} = C_{P} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3} = 0.40 \cdot 5{,}211{,}527$$ $$\boxed{P_{actual} \approx 2{,}084{,}611 \ W \approx 2.08 \ MW}$$ **Step (d) — Maximum Theoretical (Betz) Power:** $$P_{Betz} = \frac{16}{27} \cdot P_{wind} = 0.5926 \cdot 5{,}211{,}527$$ $$\boxed{P_{Betz} \approx 3{,}088{,}363 \ W \approx 3.09 \ MW}$$ **Step (e) — Annual Energy Production (AEP):** $$AEP = P_{actual} \cdot 8760 \cdot CF = 2.08 \times 10^{6} \cdot 8760 \cdot 0.30$$ $$AEP = 2.08 \times 10^{6} \cdot 2628 \ W \cdot h$$ $$\boxed{AEP \approx 5.466 \times 10^{9} \ Wh = 5.47 \ GWh/year}$$ ### 3.3 Wind Speed Extrapolation Using the Power Law **Given:** Reference wind speed $V_{ref} = 8$ m/s at height $h_{ref} = 10$ m. Find wind speed at hub height $h = 80$ m. Use terrain coefficient $\alpha = 0.143$ (open farmland, IEC Class II). **Step 1 — Apply the Power Law:** $$V_{h} = V_{ref} \cdot \left(\frac{h}{h_{ref}}\right)^{\alpha}$$ $$V_{80} = 8 \cdot \left(\frac{80}{10}\right)^{0.143}$$ $$V_{80} = 8 \cdot (8)^{0.143}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the Exponent:** $$\ln(8) = 2.0794$$ $$8^{0.143} = e^{0.143 \cdot 2.0794} = e^{0.2974} \approx 1.346$$ **Step 3 — Final Value:** $$\boxed{V_{80} = 8 \cdot 1.346 \approx 10.77 \ m/s}$$ This 34.6% increase in wind speed at hub height translates to a power density increase of $1.346^{3} \approx 2.44$, or **144% more available power** — a powerful illustration of why hub height matters. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Wind Turbine Classification | **Parameter** | **High-Speed HAWT** | **Low-Speed HAWT (Dutch)** | **Savonius (VAWT)** | **Darrieus (VAWT)** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Tip Speed Ratio (λ) | 6–8 | 3–5 | 0.9–1.2 | 4–6 | | Solidity (σ) | 0.02–0.05 | 0.10–0.30 | 0.8–1.5 | 0.30–0.60 | | $C_{P}$ Practical | 0.40–0.48 | 0.30–0.40 | 0.15–0.25 | 0.30–0.38 | | Starting Torque | Very Low | Moderate | **Very High** | Zero (requires push) | | Yaw Control | Required | Required | Not Required | Not Required | | Cut-in Speed | 3–4 m/s | 2.5–3.5 m/s | 2 m/s | 3–4 m/s | | Application | Utility-scale | Medium-scale | Pumping, off-grid | Urban, off-grid | ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **Modeled after KTU University Exam pattern — direct, conceptual, definition-based questions.** --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** Define the term **Power Coefficient ($C_{P}$)** in wind energy systems. What is its theoretical maximum value and what does it signify? **Model Answer:** The power coefficient $C_{P}$ is defined as the ratio of the actual mechanical power extracted by a wind turbine rotor to the total kinetic power available in the wind passing through the rotor's swept area. $$C_{P} = \frac{P_{extracted}}{P_{wind}} = \frac{P}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}}$$ The theoretical maximum value of $C_{P}$ is the **Betz Limit**, equal to $\frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ (or 59.3%). This value was derived by Albert Betz in 1919 and represents the absolute maximum fraction of wind kinetic energy that any turbine (ideal, frictionless) can extract. Practical real-world turbines achieve $C_{P}$ values of 0.35–0.45 due to mechanical, electrical, and aerodynamic losses. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** State the **Betz Limit** for wind energy conversion. Briefly explain why 100% extraction of wind energy is physically impossible. **Model Answer:** The **Betz Limit** states that the maximum fraction of kinetic energy extractable from the wind by an ideal turbine is $C_{P,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 59.3\%$. 100% extraction is impossible because: 1. If all kinetic energy were removed, the wind behind the turbine would have zero velocity ($V_{2} = 0$). 2. This would create a "plug" of stationary air behind the disk, with no air mass able to enter and replace it. 3. Mass conservation requires continuous flow; hence the wake must maintain some velocity ($V_{2} > 0$). 4. The Betz optimum occurs when $V_{2} = \frac{1}{3}V_{1}$ and velocity at the disk is $V = \frac{2}{3}V_{1}$. ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** When answering Betz Limit derivations, students commonly lose marks by: > 1. Forgetting to state the **assumptions** of actuator disk theory (incompressible, frictionless, 1-D flow). > 2. Not properly defining the **induction factor $a$** before using it. > 3. Skipping the **differentiation step** — examiners expect to see $\frac{dC_{P}}{da} = 0$ explicitly written. > 4. Failing to compute the corresponding velocities ($V$, $V_{2}$) at the optimum — these "free marks" are often missed. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Betz Limit** using actuator disk theory. Show clearly all steps including the assumption of induction factor $a$, the derivation of the power coefficient, and the maximization condition. **(b) [7 Marks)** A horizontal axis wind turbine has the following specifications: - Rotor diameter $D = 60$ m - Hub height wind speed $V = 14$ m/s - Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ - Power coefficient $C_{P} = 0.42$ Calculate: (i) The swept area and the available wind power. (ii) The actual mechanical power output of the rotor. (iii) The maximum theoretical power (Betz Limit) at this wind speed. (iv) How much annual energy (in MWh) is generated if the capacity factor is 32%? --- **Model Solution to A(a):** [See complete derivation in Section 3.1 above. Key checkpoints for full marks:] | **Step** | **Mark Allocation** | |---|---| | Define actuator disk and state assumptions | 1 | | Write mass conservation equation | 1 | | Apply Bernoulli & momentum equation to derive thrust | 1.5 | | Introduce induction factor $a = (V_1-V)/V_1$ | 1 | | Derive $C_P = 4a(1-a)^2$ | 1.5 | | Differentiate and solve $a = 1/3$ | 0.5 | | Compute $C_{P,max} = 16/27$ | 0.5 | **Model Solution to A(b):** **(i) Swept Area and Available Wind Power:** $$A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 60^{2}}{4} = 900\pi \approx 2827.43 \ m^{2}$$ $$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot 2827.43 \cdot 14^{3}$$ $$P_{wind} = 0.6125 \cdot 2827.43 \cdot 2744 = 0.6125 \cdot 7{,}758{,}488.9$$ $$\boxed{P_{wind} \approx 4{,}752{,}074 \ W \approx 4.75 \ MW}$$ **(ii) Actual Mechanical Power Output:** $$P_{mech} = C_{P} \cdot P_{wind} = 0.42 \cdot 4{,}752{,}074$$ $$\boxed{P_{mech} \approx 1{,}995{,}871 \ W \approx 2.00 \ MW}$$ **(iii) Betz Limit Power:** $$P_{Betz} = \frac{16}{27} \cdot P_{wind} = 0.5926 \cdot 4{,}752{,}074$$ $$\boxed{P_{Betz} \approx 2{,}815{,}948 \ W \approx 2.82 \ MW}$$ **(iv) Annual Energy Generated:** $$E = P_{mech} \cdot 8760 \cdot CF = 1{,}995{,}871 \cdot 8760 \cdot 0.32$$ $$E = 1{,}995{,}871 \cdot 2803.2$$ $$\boxed{E \approx 5.594 \times 10^{9} \ Wh \approx 5594 \ MWh/year}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model — Alternative Choice] **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **aerodynamic principle of lift-based wind turbine blade design**. Define **Tip Speed Ratio (TSR)** and explain its significance in determining turbine performance with reference to different turbine types (high-speed HAWT vs. low-speed HAWT). **(b) [7 Marks]** A small wind energy conversion system is to be installed at a coastal location with the following data: - Mean wind speed at 10 m height: 6.5 m/s - Required hub height: 50 m - Terrain coefficient: $\alpha = 0.10$ (smooth, flat coastal terrain) - Air density at hub: 1.225 kg/m³ - Rotor radius: 4 m - Generator efficiency: 0.92, Gearbox efficiency: 0.95 Calculate: (i) The wind speed at hub height using the power law, (ii) The available wind power at the hub, (iii) The actual electrical power output assuming $C_{P} = 0.38$, (iv) Comment on whether this site is suitable for power generation. --- **Model Solution to B(a):** **Aerodynamic Principle (Lift-Based Design):** Modern wind turbine blades are designed as **airfoils** (similar to aircraft wings). As wind flows over the curved upper surface and flatter lower surface of the blade, the air on the upper surface travels faster (creating lower pressure via Bernoulli's principle), while the air on the lower surface moves slower (higher pressure). This pressure differential generates a net upward force component called **lift**, perpendicular to the relative wind direction. A smaller **drag** force acts parallel to the relative wind. The lift force per unit span is: $$L = \frac{1}{2}\rho V_{rel}^{2} c \cdot C_{L}$$ The component of lift in the rotational direction provides the **torque** that spins the rotor. **Tip Speed Ratio Definition:** $$\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V} = \frac{\pi D n}{V}$$ **Significance for Different Turbine Types:** | **Parameter** | **High-Speed HAWT** | **Low-Speed HAWT** | |---|---|---| | Tip Speed Ratio | $\lambda$ = 6–8 | $\lambda$ = 3–5 | | Rotational Speed | High | Moderate | | Torque | Low | High | | Solidity | Low (2–3 blades) | High (multi-blade) | | $C_P$ Achievable | 0.40–0.48 | 0.30–0.40 | | Application | Electricity generation | Mechanical pumping | High-$\lambda$ turbines extract maximum power from a given swept area but require gearing. Low-$\lambda$ turbines have high starting torque, suitable for direct mechanical drive applications. **Model Solution to B(b):** **(i) Wind Speed at Hub Height (Power Law):** $$V_{50} = V_{10} \cdot \left(\frac{50}{10}\right)^{0.10} = 6.5 \cdot (5)^{0.10}$$ $$\ln(5) = 1.6094$$ $$5^{0.10} = e^{0.10 \cdot 1.6094} = e^{0.16094} \approx 1.1746$$ $$\boxed{V_{50} = 6.5 \cdot 1.1746 \approx 7.635 \ m/s}$$ **(ii) Available Wind Power at Hub:** $$A = \pi R^{2} = \pi \cdot (4)^{2} = 16\pi \approx 50.27 \ m^{2}$$ $$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1.225 \cdot 50.27 \cdot (7.635)^{3}$$ $$P_{wind} = 0.6125 \cdot 50.27 \cdot 445.18$$ $$\boxed{P_{wind} \approx 13{,}704 \ W \approx 13.70 \ kW}$$ **(iii) Actual Electrical Power Output:** $$P_{mech} = C_{P} \cdot P_{wind} = 0.38 \cdot 13{,}704 = 5{,}207.5 \ W$$ $$P_{elec} = P_{mech} \cdot \eta_{gearbox} \cdot \eta_{gen} = 5{,}207.5 \cdot 0.95 \cdot 0.92$$ $$\boxed{P_{elec} \approx 4{,}549 \ W \approx 4.55 \ kW}$$ **(iv) Site Suitability Comment:** The hub-height wind speed of 7.635 m/s is **marginal** — it falls in IEC Class III territory (5.0–6.5 m/s for power, 6.5–7.5 m/s for Class II). The site is **acceptable for small-scale, battery-backed hybrid systems** but is not ideal for a standalone utility-scale installation. Recommended actions: (a) Install anemometer mast for 1-year measurement campaign, (b) Consider a taller tower (80 m) to capture higher-speed winds aloft, (c) Pair with solar PV for a hybrid system to improve overall capacity factor. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Energy in Wind:** **Core Equations:** - ☑ Wind Power: $P = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^{3}$ — the cubic law is fundamental - ☑ Power Coefficient: $C_P = 4a(1-a)^2$ — derived from actuator disk theory - ☑ **Betz Limit: $C_{P,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ at $a = 1/3$** — most-tested formula - ☑ Velocity ratios at optimum: $V_1 : V : V_2 = 3 : 2 : 1$ - ☑ Tip Speed Ratio: $\lambda = \omega R / V = \pi D n / V$ - ☑ Solidity: $\sigma = Nc / (\pi R)$ - ☑ Wind speed extrapolation (power law): $V_h = V_{ref}(h/h_{ref})^\alpha$ - ☑ Air density at altitude: $\rho(h) = \rho_0 e^{-h/8500}$ - ☑ Annual Energy: $E = P_{rated} \cdot 8760 \cdot CF$ **Conceptual Points:** - ☑ Wind energy is **kinetic** energy of moving air - ☑ Betz Limit arises from mass conservation + momentum conservation - ☑ 100% extraction impossible → wake must maintain velocity ($V_2 > 0$) - ☑ Practical $C_P$ (0.35–0.45) << Betz limit (0.5926) due to mechanical & electrical losses - ☑ Wind power density used for site comparison (independent of turbine size) - ☑ Tip speed ratio determines turbine classification: High $\lambda$ → HAWT; Low $\lambda$ → drag-type - ☑ Solidity inversely related to $\lambda$ for optimum aerodynamic performance - ☑ Wind speed cubed → small increases in wind speed yield large increases in power - ☑ Hub-height wind speed > ground-level wind speed (vertical wind shear) - ☑ Capacity factor (0.25–0.45 onshore) reflects intermittent nature of wind **Numerical Tricks:** - ☑ Always check the **units** of $V$ (must be m/s) and $\rho$ (must be kg/m³) before substituting - ☑ For diameter-based formulas: $A = \pi D^2/4$; for radius-based: $A = \pi R^2$ - ☑ **Sanity check**: For $V = 12$ m/s, $D = 80$ m, $C_P = 0.4$, output should be in single-digit MW range - ☑ When answering Betz limit derivation, **always include the assumptions list** for full marks **Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid:** - ⚠ Confusing $C_P$ (rotor power coefficient) with overall turbine efficiency - ⚠ Using $\frac{1}{27}$ or $\frac{16}{81}$ instead of $\frac{16}{27}$ - ⚠ Forgetting the factor of 4 when writing $C_P = 4a(1-a)^2$ - ⚠ Not justifying why $a = 1/3$ is the maximum (not minimum) - ⚠ Mixing up swept area formula $\pi D^2/4$ vs $\pi R^2$ (both valid but stay consistent)

topics.title: Calculation of Energy Content
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT862 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Calculation of Energy Content

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Energy Content** (also termed **Calorific Value (CV)** or **Heat of Combustion**) is defined as the total amount of heat liberated when a unit mass (for solid and liquid fuels) or unit volume (for gaseous fuels) of a fuel is completely burned in the presence of oxygen under standard conditions of **298 K** and **101.325 kPa**, with the products of combustion being cooled back to the initial temperature.

In the context of Renewable Energy Engineering, energy content determines the **thermochemical viability** of a biofuel or biomass feedstock — i.e., how much usable thermal energy can be extracted per kilogram (or cubic metre) of the resource, which directly governs its suitability as a substitute for fossil fuels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> The energy content of a renewable fuel is the single most important parameter for designing **combustion systems**, **gasifiers**, **biomass boilers**, and **biodiesel engines**. It is reported in **MJ/kg** for solids/liquids and **MJ/Nm³** for gases.

The energy content is broadly classified into two types:

- **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** — also called **Gross Calorific Value (GCV)**. Heat released when the water vapour produced during combustion is fully condensed back to liquid, so the latent heat of condensation of water is *recovered*.
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** — also called **Net Calorific Value (NCV)**. Heat released when the water of combustion remains in the vapour state. The latent heat of vaporisation is *not* recovered.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference:** In Europe, energy statistics and engine performance are reported on the **LHV** basis, whereas in the **USA and India (coal trading)**, the **HHV** basis is conventional.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are burning a log of wood in two scenarios:

- **Scenario A (HHV):** You carefully collect all the steam that rises from the burning log and condense it back to water. The total heat released includes the "hidden" heat trapped in that steam.
- **Scenario B (LHV):** You let the steam escape into the air. You only measure the heat in the flame and hot gases.

Both are the *same log*, the *same combustion*, but the *recovered heat* differs. **HHV > LHV always**, because LHV subtracts the latent heat lost with the water vapour.

> A simple analogy: Think of HHV as a *full glass of water* poured on a thirsty person, while LHV is the water that evaporates before they can drink it — the original amount is identical, but what is *usefully captured* differs.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- Standard Combustion Temperature: **T = 298 K (25 °C)**
- Standard Pressure: **P = 101.325 kPa (1 atm)**
- Latent heat of vaporisation of water at 25 °C: **h_fg = 2,442 kJ/kg (≈ 44 kJ/mol)**
- Molar mass of water: **M_H₂O = 18 g/mol**
- Molar mass of hydrogen: **M_H₂ = 2 g/mol**
- Energy conversion factor: **1 kcal = 4.186 kJ ; 1 Btu = 1.055 kJ**

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Conversion Memory Trick:** For hydrogen-bound water, **every 1% hydrogen in the fuel ≈ 0.09 MJ/kg reduction** from HHV to LHV (because 9 kg of water forms per kg of H₂, multiplied by 2.442 MJ/tonne of water).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Why Energy Content Must Be Calculated

The energy content of a renewable fuel (biomass, biogas, biodiesel, producer gas, hydrogen, etc.) **cannot be measured by direct combustion in every lab** for every sample, because:

1. **Variability** — Biomass composition (cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin, extractives) varies by species, season, region.
2. **Moisture fluctuations** — Energy content is highly sensitive to the bound and free water content.
3. **Ash and inert content** — Reduces the combustible fraction.
4. **Cost & time** — Bomb calorimetry is accurate but expensive.

Hence, **analytical/empirical formulas** based on the **ultimate analysis** (C, H, O, N, S, ash, moisture) and **proximate analysis** (volatile matter, fixed carbon, ash, moisture) are widely used.

---

### 2.2 Classification of Fuels and Energy Basis

| Fuel Type | State | Energy Basis | Typical Unit |
|-----------|-------|--------------|--------------|
| Wood, bagasse, agro-residue | Solid | Per mass | **MJ/kg** |
| Biodiesel, ethanol, methanol | Liquid | Per mass | **MJ/kg** |
| Biogas, producer gas, H₂ | Gas | Per normal volume | **MJ/Nm³** |
| Hydrogen (cryogenic) | Gas/Liquid | Per mass or volume | **MJ/kg** or **MJ/Nm³** |

---

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

**Step 1 — Identify the fuel state.** Solid/liquid → use mass basis (kg). Gaseous → use volumetric basis (Nm³ at STP).

**Step 2 — Determine composition.** Conduct **ultimate analysis** (% of C, H, O, N, S by mass) and **proximate analysis** (% of moisture, volatile matter, fixed carbon, ash).

**Step 3 — Apply the appropriate empirical formula.** For solid biomass → Dulong's equation; for liquid biofuels → stoichiometric calculation from molecular formula; for gaseous fuels → volumetric summation.

**Step 4 — Convert HHV to LHV (if needed).** Subtract the latent heat carried away by the water vapour formed during combustion.

**Step 5 — Cross-check with experimental bomb calorimeter** (optional but recommended for KTU lab viva).

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Equation Number |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $\text{HHV} = 33.83\,C + 144.2\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 9.28\,S$ | **Dulong's Formula** for solid fuels (in MJ/kg); C, H, O, S in mass % | (2.1) |
| 2 | $\text{LHV} = \text{HHV} - 9\,h_fg\,H/100$ | Conversion of HHV → LHV for solid fuel (MJ/kg) | (2.2) |
| 3 | $\text{LHV} = \text{HHV} - 2.442\!\left(\dfrac{M}{100} + 9\,\dfrac{H}{100}\right)$ | LHV with moisture correction (MJ/kg) | (2.3) |
| 4 | $\text{GCV} = 0.3536\,FC + 0.1559\,VM - 0.0078\,A$ | **Channiwala & Parikh (CP)** empirical correlation for biomass (MJ/kg); FC, VM, A in dry mass % | (2.4) |
| 5 | $\text{HHV}_{\text{gas}} = \sum_i x_i\,C_{v,i}$ | Volumetric summation for gaseous fuels (MJ/Nm³); $x_i$ = mole fraction, $C_{v,i}$ = CV of component i | (2.5) |
| 6 | $E = m \cdot \text{HHV}$ | Total energy from burning mass $m$ of fuel (MJ) | (2.6) |
| 7 | $Q_{H_2O} = 9 \cdot \dfrac{H}{100} \cdot h_{fg}$ | Heat lost as latent heat of water vapour per kg of fuel (MJ/kg) | (2.7) |
| 8 | $\text{HHV}_{\text{CH}_4} = 55.53 \;\text{MJ/kg} = 37.04 \;\text{MJ/Nm}^3$ | Reference values for pure methane | (2.8) |
| 9 | $\text{HHV}_{\text{H}_2} = 141.86 \;\text{MJ/kg} = 12.74 \;\text{MJ/Nm}^3$ | Reference values for pure hydrogen | (2.9) |
| 10 | $\text{HHV}_{\text{CO}} = 10.10 \;\text{MJ/kg} = 12.63 \;\text{MJ/Nm}^3$ | Reference values for carbon monoxide | (2.10) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb for Biomass (commonly tested in KTU):**
> - Wood: ~16–20 MJ/kg (dry basis)
> - Bagasse: ~17–19 MJ/kg
> - Rice husk: ~13–15 MJ/kg (high ash)
> - Cow dung cake: ~12–15 MJ/kg
> - Biodiesel: ~37–40 MJ/kg
> - Ethanol: ~26–30 MJ/kg
> - Methanol: ~20 MJ/kg

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Biomass power plant design:** HHV determines the boiler size; LHV determines actual heat available to steam at the exhaust.
- **Internal Combustion Engines:** LHV is used because exhaust gases leave hot and dry (water does not condense in the cylinder).
- **Pellet quality grading:** European ENplus A1 requires **HHV ≥ 16.5 MJ/kg**, moisture ≤ 10%.
- **Biodiesel blending decisions:** Energy content of B20 (20% biodiesel + 80% petrodiesel) is ~95% of pure diesel — affects mileage calculations.
- **Biogas plant sizing:** Knowing calorific value of CH₄ (55.53 MJ/kg) allows sizing of gensets from digester volume.
- **Hydrogen economy:** Energy per kg of H₂ is 3× that of gasoline — fundamental for fuel cell design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Dulong's Formula (Empirical Foundation)

The Dulong formula arises from treating each combustible element as contributing independently to the heat released, **corrected** for the oxygen already bound in the fuel.

**Step 1 — Carbon combustion reaction:**
$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5 \;\text{MJ/kmol}$$

**Step 2 — Hydrogen combustion reaction:**
$$H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O_{(\ell)} \quad \Delta H = -285.8 \;\text{MJ/kmol}$$

**Step 3 — Sulphur combustion reaction:**
$$S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2 \quad \Delta H = -296.8 \;\text{MJ/kmol}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to per-kg contribution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Contribution from C} &= \frac{393.5\;\text{MJ}}{12\;\text{kg}} = 32.79\;\text{MJ/kg of C} \\[4pt]
\text{Contribution from H} &= \frac{285.8 \times 2}{2\;\text{kg}} = 285.8\;\text{MJ/kg of H}_2 \\
&\Rightarrow \text{per kg of H (in fuel)} = 285.8 \times \frac{2}{2 \times 1} = 285.8\;\text{MJ/kg of H} \\[4pt]
\text{Contribution from S} &= \frac{296.8}{32} = 9.275\;\text{MJ/kg of S}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Oxygen correction:**
The fuel contains O₂ already bound (from carbohydrates, etc.). This oxygen is assumed to be tied to hydrogen as water, so it consumes some of the available hydrogen, preventing it from combusting externally. The fraction of H₂ thus neutralised is:
$$\text{H}_2 \;\text{consumed internally} = \frac{O/32}{2} = \frac{O}{32} \times 2 \;\text{(in kmol terms)} = \frac{O}{8}\;\text{kg per kg of fuel}$$
*(Explanation: 32 kg O₂ reacts with 4 kg H₂ to form 36 kg H₂O; hence 1 kg O₂ neutralises 4/32 = 1/8 kg of H₂.)*

So the **effective hydrogen** that actually burns is:
$$H_{\text{eff}} = H - \frac{O}{8}\quad\text{(in mass %)}$$

**Step 6 — Combine all contributions into one master equation:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{HHV} &= 32.79\,C + 285.8\!\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 9.275\,S \quad\text{(kJ per kg of fuel, mass%)} \\[4pt]
\text{HHV (MJ/kg)} &= 0.3279\,C + 2.858\!\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 0.09275\,S
\end{aligned}$$

Multiplying numerator/denominator to get the **commonly used Dulong constants** (in MJ/kg with C, H, O, S in wt %):
$$\boxed{\;\text{HHV} = 33.83\,C + 144.2\!\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 9.28\,S \quad\text{MJ/kg}\;}$$
*(Dulong's Formula — Eq. 2.1)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the constants differ slightly:** The constants 33.83 and 144.2 are derived by adjusting 0.3279×100 and 2.858×100 respectively, after minor empirical tuning to match actual bomb calorimeter readings. The same formula is widely attributed to **Dulong (1820s)**, with a similar form independently re-derived by **Steuer**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Solid Biomass (Wood)

**Given:** A sample of air-dried *Acacia nilotica* wood has the following ultimate analysis (dry basis):

| C | H | O | N | S | Ash | Moisture |
|---|---|---|---|---|-----|----------|
| 49.0% | 6.0% | 43.0% | 0.5% | 0.1% | 1.4% | 10% (air-dried) |

**Find:** HHV (dry basis), HHV (as-received basis), LHV (as-received basis), and total energy per tonne of as-received wood.

**Step A — HHV on dry basis using Dulong:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{HHV}_{\text{dry}} &= 33.83 \times 49 + 144.2 \times \left(6 - \frac{43}{8}\right) + 9.28 \times 0.1 \\[4pt]
&= 1657.67 + 144.2 \times (6 - 5.375) + 0.928 \\[4pt]
&= 1657.67 + 144.2 \times 0.625 + 0.928 \\[4pt]
&= 1657.67 + 90.125 + 0.928 \\[4pt]
&= 1748.72\;\text{kcal/kg} \quad (\text{note: Dulong gives kcal/kg!})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step B — Convert kcal/kg to MJ/kg:** multiply by 4.186 × 10⁻³
$$\text{HHV}_{\text{dry}} = 1748.72 \times 4.186 \times 10^{-3} = 7.32\;\text{kJ/kg} \;(\text{ERROR — Dulong gives kcal/kg})$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Mistake:** The Dulong coefficients 33.83, 144.2, 9.28 are in **kcal/kg**, not MJ/kg! Many textbooks present it ambiguously. You MUST confirm. For exam safety, divide by 1000 to convert kcal/kg → MJ/kg directly:
> $$\text{HHV (MJ/kg)} = 0.03383\,C + 0.1442(H - O/8) + 0.00928\,S$$

**Step B (Corrected) — HHV dry basis in MJ/kg:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{HHV}_{\text{dry}} &= \frac{33.83 \times 49 + 144.2 \times 0.625 + 9.28 \times 0.1}{1000} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1657.67 + 90.125 + 0.928}{1000} = \frac{1748.72}{1000} \\[4pt]
&= 1.7487\;\text{kJ/kg — NO!}
\end{aligned}$$

Let us reconcile carefully. The coefficients are derived as follows:
$$\frac{393.5\;\text{MJ}}{12\;\text{kg}} = 32.79\;\text{MJ/kg of C}$$

So if C = 49 kg per 100 kg fuel:
$$32.79 \times \frac{49}{100} = 16.07\;\text{MJ/kg of fuel from carbon}$$

The way Dulong's formula is written — `33.83 C` — assumes C is in **weight percent** (i.e., 49 not 0.49), and the answer comes out in **kcal/kg**, then needs to be divided by **1000** for kcal → MJ:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{HHV (kcal/kg)} &= 33.83 \times C_{\%} + 144.2 \times (H_{\%} - O_{\%}/8) + 9.28 \times S_{\%} \\[4pt]
&= 33.83 \times 49 + 144.2 \times 0.625 + 9.28 \times 0.1 \\[4pt]
&= 1657.67 + 90.125 + 0.928 = 1748.72\;\text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\text{HHV}_{\text{dry}} = \frac{1748.72}{1000} \times 4.186\;\text{MJ/kg} = 7.32\;\text{MJ/kg}$$

> [!WARNING]
> This is **far too low** for wood (real wood HHV = ~18 MJ/kg). The error is that the formula gives **kcal/kg**, but students sometimes treat it as **kcal/kg of element × 100** — let us re-check.

The Dulong formula, in **kcal/kg of fuel**, is indeed:
$$\boxed{\;\text{HHV} = 81\,C + 342.5(H - O/8) + 22.5\,S \quad \text{kcal/kg}\;}$$
when C, H, O, S are in **mass fractions (not percent)**.

If we use mass fractions:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{HHV}_{\text{dry}} &= 81 \times 0.49 + 342.5 \times (0.06 - 0.43/8) + 22.5 \times 0.001 \\[4pt]
&= 39.69 + 342.5 \times 0.00625 + 0.0225 \\[4pt]
&= 39.69 + 2.141 + 0.0225 = 41.85\;\text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

This is **still too low** — clearly there is a unit discrepancy in the textbook presentation. The KTU-friendly version used in Indian engineering textbooks (e.g., **Sukhatme, Nag**) is:

$$\boxed{\;\text{HHV} = \frac{1}{100}\left[8080\,C + 34500\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S\right]\;\text{kcal/kg}\;}$$

Using this:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{HHV}_{\text{dry}} &= \frac{1}{100}\!\left[8080 \times 49 + 34500 \times 0.625 + 2240 \times 0.1\right] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{100}\!\left[395920 + 21562.5 + 224\right] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{417706.5}{100} = 4177.07\;\text{kcal/kg} \\[4pt]
&= 4.177\;\text{kcal/g} = 17.48\;\text{MJ/kg} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}$$

This matches the expected **HHV ≈ 17–18 MJ/kg** for wood. **Always use the kcal/kg form with C, H, O, S in wt %, then divide by 1000 to convert to kcal/g, then multiply by 4.186 × 10⁻³ to get MJ/kg.** The "33.83" version in many references is a decimal-shifted shortcut that should be used cautiously.

**Final reconciliation — using the simpler numerical form:**
$$\text{HHV (MJ/kg)} = 0.3491\,C + 1.1783\,H - 0.1034\,O - 0.0211\,A$$
*(S and N are often neglected for biomass.)*

For our wood:
$$\text{HHV}_{\text{dry}} = 0.3491 \times 49 + 1.1783 \times 6 - 0.1034 \times 43 = 17.10 + 7.07 - 4.45 = 19.72\;\text{MJ/kg}$$

**Step C — HHV on as-received basis (10% moisture):**
$$\text{HHV}_{\text{ar}} = \text{HHV}_{\text{dry}} \times (1 - M) = 19.72 \times 0.90 = 17.75\;\text{MJ/kg}$$

**Step D — LHV on as-received basis:**
Water from combustion of H: $9 \times H/100 = 9 \times 0.06 \times 0.9 = 0.486$ kg water per kg fuel (combustion origin)
Plus free moisture: 0.10 kg water per kg fuel.
Total water = 0.586 kg/kg fuel
$$\text{LHV}_{\text{ar}} = 17.75 - 0.586 \times 2.442 = 17.75 - 1.43 = 16.32\;\text{MJ/kg}$$

**Step E — Total energy per tonne:**
$$E = 1000 \times 17.75 = 17{,}750\;\text{MJ/tonne}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Gaseous Biofuel (Biogas)

**Given:** Biogas composition (by volume): CH₄ = 60%, CO₂ = 35%, H₂S = 1%, N₂ = 4%.

**Find:** HHV per Nm³.

**Step 1 — Reference calorific values (per Nm³):**
| Gas | HHV (MJ/Nm³) |
|-----|--------------|
| CH₄ | 37.04 |
| H₂S | 23.86 |
| CO₂ | 0 (inert) |
| N₂ | 0 (inert) |

**Step 2 — Apply Eq. 2.5:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{HHV} &= x_{CH_4} \times C_{v,CH_4} + x_{H_2S} \times C_{v,H_2S} + x_{CO_2}(0) + x_{N_2}(0) \\[4pt]
&= 0.60 \times 37.04 + 0.01 \times 23.86 + 0 + 0 \\[4pt]
&= 22.224 + 0.2386 = 22.46\;\text{MJ/Nm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Note:** LHV of biogas ≈ HHV − 1.06 MJ/Nm³ (water from CH₄ combustion only).

$$\text{LHV} \approx 22.46 - 1.06 = 21.40\;\text{MJ/Nm}^3$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Liquid Biofuel (Biodiesel from Waste Cooking Oil)

**Given:** Methyl ester of oleic acid (C₁₉H₃₆O₂). Molecular weight = 296.5 g/mol.
Combustion: C₁₉H₃₆O₂ + 27 O₂ → 19 CO₂ + 18 H₂O

**Find:** HHV in MJ/kg using stoichiometric heats of formation.

**Step 1 — Standard heats of formation (ΔH_f° in MJ/kmol):**
- C₁₉H₃₆O₂(l) = **−884 kJ/mol = −884,000 kJ/kmol** (methyl oleate)
- O₂(g) = 0
- CO₂(g) = −393.5 MJ/kmol
- H₂O(l) = −285.8 MJ/kmol

**Step 2 — Heat of combustion:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta H_c &= \sum \Delta H_f^\circ (\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H_f^\circ (\text{reactants}) \\[4pt]
&= [19 \times (-393.5) + 18 \times (-285.8)] - [(-884) + 27 \times 0] \\[4pt]
&= [-7476.5 - 5144.4] - [-884] \\[4pt]
&= -12620.9 + 884 = -11736.9\;\text{MJ/kmol}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Per kg:**
$$\text{HHV} = \frac{11736.9}{296.5} = 39.58\;\text{MJ/kg}$$

This matches the experimental HHV of biodiesel (37–40 MJ/kg). ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Energy Content Determination Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Sample Selection] --> B{Identify State}
    B -->|Solid Biomass| C[Ultimate + Proximate Analysis]
    B -->|Liquid Biofuel| D[Molecular Formula Determination]
    B -->|Gaseous Fuel| E[Volumetric Gas Chromatography]
    C --> F[Apply Dulong Formula]
    D --> G[Apply Stoichiometric Combustion]
    E --> H[Apply Volumetric Summation]
    F --> I[HHV dry basis MJ per kg]
    G --> I
    H --> J[HHV MJ per Nm3]
    I --> K[Apply Moisture Correction]
    K --> L[HHV as received basis]
    L --> M[Subtract Water Vapour Latent Heat]
    M --> N[LHV final]
    J --> O[Subtract Water Vapour from CH4 only]
    O --> N
    N --> P[Cross verify with Bomb Calorimeter]
    P --> Q[Final Energy Content Report]
```

### 4.2 Decision Tree — Which Formula to Apply

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Fuel Sample] --> T{Available Data?}
    T -->|Ultimate Analysis C H O S| U[Dulong Equation Eq 2.1]
    T -->|Proximate Analysis FC VM Ash| V[Channiwala Parikh Eq 2.4]
    T -->|Molecular Formula| W[Stoichiometric Combustion Energy]
    T -->|Volumetric Composition| X[Gas Mixture Summation Eq 2.5]
    U --> Y[Apply Eq 2.3 for LHV]
    V --> Y
    W --> Y
    X --> Y
    Y --> Z[Final LHV HHV Report MJ per kg or MJ per Nm3]
```

### 4.3 Phase Diagram — Solid Biomass Energy Content Zones

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Moisture 0 to 10 percent] --> P2[High HHV 18 to 20 MJ per kg]
    P3[Moisture 10 to 30 percent] --> P4[Medium HHV 14 to 17 MJ per kg]
    P5[Moisture 30 to 60 percent] --> P6[Low HHV 8 to 13 MJ per kg]
    P7[Moisture greater than 60 percent] --> P8[Not viable for direct combustion]
    P1 -.-> P3 -.-> P5 -.-> P7
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Block Diagrams:** Each block represents an *analytical step* and each arrow represents a *data flow*. The moisture zone chart illustrates why wet biomass like bagasse (40–50% moisture) is typically pre-dried before combustion.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
Define **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** and **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** of a fuel. Explain why HHV is always greater than LHV with the help of a numerical illustration.

**Model Answer:**
- **HHV:** Total heat released when unit mass (or volume) of fuel is completely combusted and the products of combustion are cooled back to 25 °C, such that water formed is condensed to liquid.
- **LHV:** Total heat released when unit fuel is combusted and the products leave at 25 °C with water in the *vapour* state.
- **Reason for HHV > LHV:** LHV excludes the latent heat of condensation (≈ 2,442 kJ/kg) carried away by water vapour. For every 1 kg of H₂ burnt, 9 kg of water is formed, so **Δ = 9 × 2,442 = 21,978 kJ/kg of H₂**, equivalent to ~0.220 MJ per percent of H₂ in fuel.
- **Numerical illustration:** For a fuel with 6% H, LHV ≈ HHV − 0.06 × 21.978 ≈ HHV − 1.32 MJ/kg.

**(3 marks: 1 for each HHV definition, LHV definition, and numerical illustration)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
State and explain **Dulong's formula** for the calorific value of solid fuels. What are its limitations?

**Model Answer:**
- **Dulong's Formula:**
$$\text{HHV} = \frac{1}{100}\left[8080\,C + 34500\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S\right]\;\text{kcal/kg}$$
where C, H, O, S are mass percentages on dry basis.
- **Explanation:** It assumes each combustible element contributes additively. The $(H - O/8)$ term corrects for oxygen already bound as water in the fuel.
- **Limitations:**
  1. Does not account for ash moisture.
  2. Inaccurate for high-ash fuels (ash > 15%).
  3. Ignores nitrogen contribution.
  4. Deviates for fuels with high fixed carbon and low volatile matter.
  5. Empirical — coefficients are tuned for coals, less accurate for biomass.

**(3 marks: 1.5 for formula, 1.5 for limitations)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> For the 14-mark questions, students routinely lose marks by:
> 1. **Forgetting the unit conversion** from kcal/kg to MJ/kg (factor 4.186 × 10⁻³) — losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Neglecting the $(H - O/8)$ correction** in Dulong's formula — losing 3 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up HHV and LHV** in the final answer without clearly stating which basis is being reported — losing 1 mark.
> 4. **Not converting from dry basis to as-received basis** when moisture is given — losing 2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the units** in each intermediate step — losing 1 mark.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** `Energy Content of Solid Biomass` *(CHOSEN)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Re-attempt]**

A sample of rice husk is analysed in the lab. The ultimate analysis (dry basis, mass %) is:

| C | H | O | N | S | Ash |
|---|---|---|---|---|-----|
| 38.5 | 4.7 | 35.6 | 0.4 | 0.05 | 20.75 |

The as-received moisture content is 9 %.

**(a)** Calculate the **HHV** on (i) dry basis and (ii) as-received basis using Dulong's formula. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the **LHV** on as-received basis and the **total energy content per tonne** of as-received rice husk. Comment on the suitability of rice husk as a fuel. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

Using Dulong's formula in kcal/kg:
$$\text{HHV}_{\text{dry}} = \frac{1}{100}\left[8080\,C + 34500\!\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S\right]\;\text{kcal/kg}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute values:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{HHV}_{\text{dry}} &= \frac{1}{100}\!\left[8080 \times 38.5 + 34500 \times \left(4.7 - \frac{35.6}{8}\right) + 2240 \times 0.05\right] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{100}\!\left[311080 + 34500 \times (4.7 - 4.45) + 112\right] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{100}\!\left[311080 + 34500 \times 0.25 + 112\right] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{100}\!\left[311080 + 8625 + 112\right] \\[4pt]
&= \frac{319817}{100} = 3198.17\;\text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to MJ/kg:**
$$\text{HHV}_{\text{dry}} = 3198.17 \times 4.186 \times 10^{-3} = 13.39\;\text{MJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — HHV as-received:**
$$\text{HHV}_{\text{ar}} = \text{HHV}_{\text{dry}} \times (1 - M) = 13.39 \times (1 - 0.09) = 13.39 \times 0.91 = 12.18\;\text{MJ/kg}$$

**Answer (a):**
- (i) HHV dry basis = **13.39 MJ/kg**
- (ii) HHV as-received = **12.18 MJ/kg**

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Total water formed per kg of as-received fuel:**

Water from hydrogen combustion: $9 \times \frac{H_{\text{ar}}}{100} = 9 \times \frac{4.7 \times 0.91}{100} = 9 \times 0.0428 = 0.385$ kg water/kg fuel
Plus free moisture: 0.09 kg/kg fuel
**Total water** = 0.385 + 0.09 = **0.475 kg water per kg fuel**

**Step 2 — Latent heat loss:**
$$Q_{\text{latent}} = 0.475 \times 2.442 = 1.16\;\text{MJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — LHV:**
$$\text{LHV}_{\text{ar}} = \text{HHV}_{\text{ar}} - Q_{\text{latent}} = 12.18 - 1.16 = 11.02\;\text{MJ/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Total energy per tonne (1000 kg):**
$$E = 1000 \times 11.02 = 11{,}020\;\text{MJ/tonne}$$

**Step 5 — Suitability comment:**
Rice husk with LHV = 11 MJ/kg is a **marginal fuel** for direct combustion. Suitable for:
- ✅ Cogeneration in sugar mills (using bagasse + husk)
- ✅ Gasification (downdraft gasifier) with efficiency gains
- ❌ Not ideal for high-efficiency boilers (need >14 MJ/kg)

**(7 marks: 2 for water formation, 2 for LHV, 1 for total energy, 2 for suitability comment with engineering judgement)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** `Energy Content of Gaseous Biofuel` *(ALTERNATIVE)*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Re-attempt]**

A biogas plant produces gas with the following volumetric composition:

| CH₄ | CO₂ | H₂S | H₂ | N₂ |
|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| 58% | 35% | 2% | 3% | 2% |

**(a)** Calculate the **HHV** and **LHV** of the biogas in **MJ/Nm³**. Use the table of standard calorific values given below. **(7 marks)**

| Component | HHV (MJ/Nm³) | LHV (MJ/Nm³) |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|
| CH₄ | 37.04 | 31.70 |
| H₂S | 23.86 | 21.84 |
| H₂ | 12.74 | 10.79 |
| CO₂, N₂ | 0 | 0 |

**(b)** If the biogas plant produces **500 Nm³/day** of this gas, calculate the **daily energy output** in kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ). Comment on how upgrading this biogas to biomethane (98% CH₄) would change its energy density and economic viability. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1 — HHV calculation (Eq. 2.5):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{HHV}_{\text{biogas}} &= 0.58 \times 37.04 + 0.02 \times 23.86 + 0.03 \times 12.74 + 0.35 \times 0 + 0.02 \times 0 \\[4pt]
&= 21.483 + 0.477 + 0.382 + 0 + 0 \\[4pt]
&= 22.34\;\text{MJ/Nm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — LHV calculation:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{LHV}_{\text{biogas}} &= 0.58 \times 31.70 + 0.02 \times 21.84 + 0.03 \times 10.79 \\[4pt]
&= 18.386 + 0.437 + 0.324 \\[4pt]
&= 19.15\;\text{MJ/Nm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer (a):** HHV = **22.34 MJ/Nm³**, LHV = **19.15 MJ/Nm³**

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Daily energy output:**
$$E_{\text{daily}} = 500 \times 19.15 = 9575\;\text{MJ/day}$$

Convert to kWh:
$$E_{\text{daily}} = \frac{9575}{3.6} = 2659.7\;\text{kWh/day} \approx 2660\;\text{kWh/day}$$

**Step 2 — Energy density comparison:**

For upgraded biomethane (98% CH₄):
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{HHV}_{\text{upgraded}} &= 0.98 \times 37.04 = 36.30\;\text{MJ/Nm}^3 \\[4pt]
\text{LHV}_{\text{upgraded}} &= 0.98 \times 31.70 = 31.07\;\text{MJ/Nm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Percentage increase:**
$$\frac{31.07 - 19.15}{19.15} \times 100 = 62.2\%\;\text{increase in energy density}$$

**Step 3 — Economic and engineering implications:**
- ✅ Biomethane can be injected into natural gas grid (same calorific value, drop-in fuel).
- ✅ Suitable for CNG vehicles (compressed biogas → CBG scheme of Govt. of India).
- ✅ Cost of upgrading (CO₂ removal via PSA/water scrubbing): ₹ 4–6/Nm³.
- ✅ Market price of CBG: ₹ 35–46/kg vs PNG at ₹ 25/kg — economically attractive.
- ❌ Need purification to <2% CO₂, <4 mg/Nm³ H₂S, <1 ppm siloxanes.

**(7 marks: 2 for daily energy, 2 for upgraded CV, 1.5 for economic comment, 1.5 for engineering comment)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print this before the exam!**

- ☐ **HHV (GCV)** = heat released with water in **liquid** state. **LHV (NCV)** = heat released with water in **vapour** state. **HHV > LHV always.**
- ☐ Dulong's Formula (in kcal/kg with C, H, O, S in wt%):
  $$\text{HHV} = \frac{1}{100}\left[8080\,C + 34500\!\left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S\right]$$
- ☐ The term $\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right)$ is the **effective hydrogen** that actually combusts.
- ☐ **Multiply kcal/kg by 4.186 × 10⁻³** to get MJ/kg.
- ☐ **As-received basis:** Multiply dry-basis HHV by $(1 - M)$, where $M$ = moisture mass fraction.
- ☐ **LHV = HHV − 2.442 × (M + 9H)** in MJ/kg, where M and H are mass fractions (as-received).
- ☐ For **gaseous fuels**: HHV (MJ/Nm³) = $\sum x_i \cdot C_{v,i}^{HHV}$.
- ☐ **Reference values to memorise:**
  - CH₄: 37.04 MJ/Nm³
  - H₂: 12.74 MJ/Nm³
  - CO: 12.63 MJ/Nm³
  - Biodiesel: ~40 MJ/kg
  - Ethanol: ~29.7 MJ/kg
  - Wood (dry): ~18–20 MJ/kg
- ☐ **Channiwala–Parikh** is used when only proximate analysis (FC, VM, A) is available.
- ☐ **Bomb calorimeter** is the experimental reference; Dulong is the empirical shortcut.
- ☐ Water from hydrogen combustion = $9 \times H/100$ kg per kg of fuel.
- ☐ **1 kcal/kg × 4.186 × 10⁻³ = MJ/kg** (unit conversion is the most common error!).
- ☐ For KTU exams, **always state the basis** (dry, as-received, HHV, LHV) in the answer.
- ☐ **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ** (energy unit conversion for power calculations).
- ☐ Moisture > 60% makes biomass **not viable** for direct combustion (autothermal point).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Coefficients
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT862 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Power Coefficients

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Power Coefficient ($C_P$)** of a wind turbine is defined as the dimensionless ratio of the actual mechanical power extracted by the turbine rotor to the total kinetic power available in the undisturbed wind stream passing through the rotor's swept area. It quantifies the aerodynamic efficiency of the wind energy conversion process and is the most fundamental performance parameter in wind turbine analysis.

Mathematically expressed as:

$$C_P = \frac{P_{turbine}}{P_{wind}}$$

where $P_{turbine}$ is the actual power captured by the rotor blades and $P_{wind}$ is the gross kinetic power present in the wind. The maximum theoretical value that any ideal wind turbine can achieve is governed by **Betz's Law**, giving $C_{P,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ (commonly rounded to **59.3%**).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain English Explanation using a Real-World Analogy**

Imagine you are standing in a flowing river holding a large circular sieve (a basket with holes). You cannot capture 100% of the water because:
1. Some water must flow through the holes (energy leaves the system un-extracted).
2. If you block the sieve completely, water will pile up and spill around it (the flow stalls).
3. There exists a *sweet spot* — partially blocking the flow — that extracts the maximum possible energy without causing the river to divert entirely around your sieve.

The **Power Coefficient** is exactly this "blocking efficiency" of a wind turbine. The wind stream behaves like the river; the rotor acts like the sieve. No matter how perfectly designed the blades are, you can NEVER extract 100% of the wind's kinetic energy because the air *must* continue moving away on the downstream side (otherwise the incoming wind has no path to enter the rotor). **Albert Betz** proved in 1919 that the absolute physical ceiling is **59.3%** of the available wind power.

### Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Maximum theoretical $C_P$ (Betz Limit):** $\frac{16}{27} = 0.5926 \approx 59.3\%$
- **Air density (sea level, standard):** $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (bold for emphasis as standard reference)
- **Swept area of rotor:** $A = \pi R^2$ where $R$ is rotor radius in meters
- **Typical modern utility-scale $C_P$:** $0.35$ to $0.45$ (real turbines operate well below Betz limit due to aerodynamic, mechanical, and control losses)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The concept of power coefficient forms the analytical foundation for Module 2 of PESFT862. Questions frequently appear linking $C_P$ with Tip Speed Ratio ($\lambda$) and pitch angle ($\beta$), requiring students to interpret $C_P$-$\lambda$ performance curves.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Fundamental Operating Principles (Structured Logic Steps)

The performance of any horizontal-axis wind turbine is governed by **three interconnected aerodynamic coefficients**. Understanding their derivation pathways and interdependence is essential for KTU examination success.

- **Step 1 — Power Available in the Wind ($P_{wind}$):** Wind possesses kinetic energy by virtue of its mass and velocity. The volumetric flow rate through a swept area $A$ at velocity $V$ carries a kinetic power equal to $\frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$. This is the *gross* resource before any turbine interaction.

- **Step 2 — Power Extraction Model (Actuator Disc Theory):** Rankine–Froude treats the rotor as an infinitely thin porous disc that extracts energy by creating a pressure drop. The air slows from upstream velocity $V_1$ to far-wake velocity $V_4$, passing through the disc plane at $V_d$ (where $V_d = \frac{V_1 + V_4}{2}$). The induction factor $a$ relates these: $V_d = V_1(1-a)$.

- **Step 3 — Derivation of the Power Coefficient:** By applying Bernoulli's equation across the disc and equating thrust, the extracted power is found to be:
  $$P_{extracted} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^3 \cdot 4a(1-a)^2$$
  Therefore: $C_P = 4a(1-a)^2$

- **Step 4 — Optimization to Find Betz Limit:** Differentiating $C_P$ with respect to $a$ and setting $\frac{dC_P}{da} = 0$ yields the optimal induction factor $a = \frac{1}{3}$. Substituting back gives:
  $$C_{P,max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$$

- **Step 5 — Tip Speed Ratio ($\lambda$):** The blade tip speed relative to wind speed critically determines $C_P$. It is defined as:
  $$\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V} = \frac{\pi R n}{30 V}$$
  where $n$ is rotational speed in rpm.

- **Step 6 — Thrust and Torque Coefficients:** The aerodynamic forces and moments on the rotor are also normalized:
  $$C_T = \frac{T}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V^2} \quad ; \quad C_Q = \frac{Q}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A R V^2}$$

- **Step 7 — Fundamental Coupling Identity:** Since $P = \omega Q$ and $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$, algebraic manipulation yields the elegant identity:
  $$C_P = \lambda \cdot C_Q$$
  This is a frequent KTU exam question — students must derive or state this relationship.

- **Step 8 — Why Real Turbines Fall Short of Betz Limit:** Real rotors suffer from blade tip losses, finite number of blades, hub drag, wake rotation, airfoil drag, and structural constraints. Modern three-bladed turbines typically achieve $C_P \approx 0.45$ to $0.50$.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Master Table** — Memorize all entries for ESE preparation.

| Quantity | Formula | Symbol Definitions | Typical Units |
|----------|---------|-------------------|---------------|
| Wind Power (Available) | $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$ | $\rho$ = air density, $A$ = swept area, $V$ = wind speed | Watts (W) |
| Swept Area | $A = \pi R^2$ | $R$ = rotor radius | m² |
| Extracted Power | $P_{turbine} = C_P \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$ | $C_P$ = power coefficient | W |
| Power Coefficient (actuator disc) | $C_P = 4a(1-a)^2$ | $a$ = axial induction factor | dimensionless |
| Betz Limit | $C_{P,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | No variables | dimensionless |
| Optimal Induction Factor | $a_{opt} = \frac{1}{3}$ | At Betz condition | dimensionless |
| Tip Speed Ratio | $\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V} = \frac{\pi R n}{30 V}$ | $\omega$ = angular velocity, $n$ = rpm | dimensionless |
| Thrust Coefficient | $C_T = \frac{T}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V^2}$ | $T$ = thrust force | dimensionless |
| Torque Coefficient | $C_Q = \frac{Q}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A R V^2}$ | $Q$ = torque | dimensionless |
| Fundamental Coupling | $C_P = \lambda \cdot C_Q$ | Connects power and torque coefficients | dimensionless |
| Wind Energy per Year | $E = P_{wind} \cdot 8760 \cdot CF$ | $CF$ = capacity factor | Wh or kWh |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The power coefficient is the central design metric used by wind turbine manufacturers (Vestas, Siemens-Gamesa, Suzlon) to rate aerodynamic performance. It directly determines Annual Energy Production (AEP) and Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE). Control algorithms (pitch control, torque control, yaw control) are all designed to maintain operation at or near maximum $C_P$ across varying wind conditions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Power Available in the Wind

Consider a wind stream of cross-sectional area $A$ (m²) and velocity $V$ (m/s) flowing perpendicularly through an imaginary surface.

**Step 1 — Mass Flow Rate:** The mass of air passing through area $A$ per unit time is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho A V$$

**Step 2 — Kinetic Energy per Unit Mass:** Each kilogram of moving air carries kinetic energy:

$$KE_{per\ kg} = \frac{1}{2}V^2$$

**Step 3 — Power Definition:** Power is the rate of energy transfer (energy per second). Multiplying mass flow rate by specific kinetic energy:

$$P_{wind} = \dot{m} \times KE_{per\ kg} = (\rho A V) \times \left(\frac{1}{2}V^2\right)$$

**Step 4 — Final Simplified Form:**

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This is the *gross* available power. Notice the cubic dependence on $V$ — this is why wind speed measurement accuracy is the single most important parameter in wind energy estimation.

---

### Derivation 2: Betz Limit (Maximum Power Coefficient)

**Starting Assumption (Actuator Disc):** A wind turbine rotor is modeled as an ideal porous disc that extracts energy uniformly across its area. Let:
- $V_1$ = upstream (free stream) wind velocity
- $V_d$ = velocity at the disc plane
- $V_4$ = far-wake (downstream) velocity
- $a$ = axial induction factor, defined by $V_d = V_1(1-a)$

**Step 1 — Mass Conservation:** Mass flow rate must be constant through any streamtube cross-section:

$$\rho A_1 V_1 = \rho A_d V_d = \rho A_4 V_4$$

**Step 2 — Velocity Continuity Assumption (Froude's Hypothesis):** For an idealized disc:

$$V_d = \frac{V_1 + V_4}{2}$$

Solving for $V_4$ in terms of $V_1$ and $a$:

$$V_4 = V_1(1-2a)$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation:** Apply Bernoulli along a streamline from far upstream ($\infty$) to far downstream ($\infty$) on either side of the disc. The pressure jump across the disc is:

$$\Delta p = p_d^+ - p_d^- = \frac{1}{2}\rho (V_1^2 - V_4^2)$$

Substituting $V_4 = V_1(1-2a)$:

$$\Delta p = \frac{1}{2}\rho \left[V_1^2 - V_1^2(1-2a)^2\right] = \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 \left[1 - (1-2a)^2\right]$$

Expanding the square:

$$\Delta p = \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 \left[1 - (1 - 4a + 4a^2)\right] = \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 (4a - 4a^2) = 2\rho V_1^2 a(1-a)$$

**Step 4 — Thrust on the Disc:** The thrust force equals the pressure drop times the disc area $A$:

$$T = \Delta p \cdot A = 2\rho A V_1^2 a(1-a)$$

**Step 5 — Power Extracted (Alternative Method via Momentum):** The power extracted equals thrust times the velocity at the disc:

$$P_{extracted} = T \cdot V_d = \left[2\rho A V_1^2 a(1-a)\right] \times V_1(1-a)$$

$$P_{extracted} = 2\rho A V_1^3 a(1-a)^2$$

**Step 6 — Form the Power Coefficient:**

$$C_P = \frac{P_{extracted}}{P_{wind}} = \frac{2\rho A V_1^3 a(1-a)^2}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^3}$$

$$C_P = 4a(1-a)^2$$

**Step 7 — Maximize $C_P$ with respect to $a$:** Take the derivative and set it to zero:

$$\frac{dC_P}{da} = 4\left[(1-a)^2 + a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1)\right] = 4(1-a)\left[(1-a) - 2a\right] = 4(1-a)(1-3a)$$

Setting $\frac{dC_P}{da} = 0$ gives two roots: $a = 1$ (trivial, no power) and:

$$a_{opt} = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute $a_{opt}$ into $C_P$ expression:**

$$C_{P,max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27}$$

$$\boxed{C_{P,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \text{ (Betz Limit)}}$$

---

### Derivation 3: Relationship $C_P = \lambda \cdot C_Q$

**Step 1 — Start from Definitions:**

$$P_{turbine} = C_P \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3 \quad \text{and} \quad Q = C_Q \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A R V^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Mechanical Power Identity:** Power equals torque times angular velocity:

$$P_{turbine} = \omega Q$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Both Expressions:**

$$C_P \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3 = \omega \cdot C_Q \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A R V^2$$

**Step 4 — Cancel Common Factors** ($\frac{1}{2}\rho A V^2$ from both sides):

$$C_P \cdot V = \omega \cdot C_Q \cdot R$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to Isolate $C_P$:**

$$C_P = C_Q \cdot \frac{\omega R}{V}$$

**Step 6 — Recognize the Tip Speed Ratio:** By definition $\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V}$:

$$\boxed{C_P = \lambda \cdot C_Q}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This identity is incredibly useful in KTU numerical problems. If the problem gives you $\lambda$ and $C_P$, you can directly compute $C_Q$ without additional aerodynamic data.

---

### Numerical Example (Typical KTU-Style Problem)

> **Problem:** A wind turbine has rotor diameter $D = 80$ m, operates at wind speed $V = 12$ m/s, rotates at $n = 18$ rpm, and has measured power output $P = 1.8$ MW. Air density $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. Calculate $C_P$ and $C_Q$.

**Solution Steps:**

**Step 1 — Calculate Swept Area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (80)^2}{4} = 5026.55 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Available Wind Power:**

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times (12)^3$$

$$P_{wind} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times 1728 = 5.319 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Power Coefficient:**

$$C_P = \frac{P_{turbine}}{P_{wind}} = \frac{1.8}{5.319} = 0.338$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Tip Speed Ratio:**

$$\lambda = \frac{\pi R n}{30 V} = \frac{\pi \times 40 \times 18}{30 \times 12} = \frac{2261.95}{360} = 6.283$$

**Step 5 — Calculate Torque Coefficient Using $C_P = \lambda C_Q$:**

$$C_Q = \frac{C_P}{\lambda} = \frac{0.338}{6.283} = 0.0538$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Wind Turbine Power Flow Architecture (Actuator Disc Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Free Stream Wind V1] --> B[Actuator Disc Rotor]
    B --> C[Far Wake V4]
    B --> D[Pressure Drop Delta P]
    D --> E[Thrust Force T]
    B --> F[Extracted Power Pext]
    F --> G[Power Coefficient CP]
    G --> H[Betz Limit Check]
    H --> I{Is CP Less Than 16 By 27}
    I -->|Yes| J[Physically Realistic]
    I -->|No| K[Violation - Reject]
    A --> L[Power in Wind Pwind]
    L --> G
```

### Diagram 2: Cp versus Lambda Performance Curve Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Analysis] --> B[Define Tip Speed Ratio Lambda]
    B --> C[Measure Rotational Speed Omega]
    C --> D[Compute Lambda equals Omega R divided by V]
    D --> E{Compare Lambda with Lambda Opt}
    E -->|Lambda Less Than Lambda Opt| F[Suboptimal Region - CP Rising]
    E -->|Lambda Equals Lambda Opt| G[Maximum CP Region - Betz Approached]
    E -->|Lambda Greater Than Lambda Opt| H[Stall Region - CP Declining]
    F --> I[Adjust Generator Torque to Increase Omega]
    G --> J[Lock Operating Point at Peak CP]
    H --> K[Reduce Rotational Speed via Pitch Control]
    I --> D
    J --> L[Optimal Power Extraction Achieved]
    K --> D
```

### Diagram 3: Coefficient Interrelationship Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Wind Velocity V] --> B[Swept Area A]
    B --> C[Wind Power Pwind]
    A --> D[Tip Speed Ratio Lambda]
    D --> E[Power Coefficient CP]
    C --> E
    E --> F[Turbine Power Pturbine]
    A --> G[Thrust Force T]
    G --> H[Thrust Coefficient CT]
    B --> H
    C --> H
    A --> I[Angular Velocity Omega]
    I --> J[Mechanical Torque Q]
    J --> K[Torque Coefficient CQ]
    B --> K
    D --> K
    E --> L[Identity Check CP equals Lambda times CQ]
    K --> L
    D --> L
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** Diagram 1 illustrates the actuator disc control volume and the relationship between upstream velocity, pressure drop, and power extraction. Diagram 2 maps the operational control strategy used in MPPT (Maximum Power Point Tracking) algorithms. Diagram 3 unifies the three coefficients ($C_P$, $C_T$, $C_Q$) into a single framework for problem solving — students should be able to traverse these connections rapidly during exams.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1:** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
*Define the term "Power Coefficient" of a wind turbine. State the Betz limit value and explain its physical significance.*

**Model Answer:**
The Power Coefficient ($C_P$) of a wind turbine is the ratio of actual mechanical power extracted by the rotor to the kinetic power available in the undisturbed wind stream:
$$C_P = \frac{P_{turbine}}{P_{wind}} = \frac{P_{turbine}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3}$$
It is dimensionless. The **Betz limit** states that no ideal wind turbine can extract more than $C_{P,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ (i.e., **59.3%**) of the wind's kinetic energy. Physically, this limit exists because the air must continue moving downstream after passing through the rotor — if all kinetic energy were extracted, the air would stagnate and block further inflow. This represents the absolute theoretical ceiling of wind energy conversion efficiency.

---

**Question 2:** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
*Define Tip Speed Ratio ($\lambda$) and explain how it affects the power coefficient of a wind turbine.*

**Model Answer:**
The Tip Speed Ratio ($\lambda$) is the ratio of the linear speed of the blade tip to the free-stream wind velocity:
$$\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V} = \frac{\pi R n}{30 V}$$
The power coefficient $C_P$ is strongly dependent on $\lambda$. For a given turbine design, $C_P$ increases with $\lambda$ up to an optimal value $\lambda_{opt}$ where $C_P$ reaches its maximum, and then decreases for higher $\lambda$ values. This behavior is depicted in the standard $C_P$-$\lambda$ curve. Therefore, for maximum energy capture, wind turbine controllers continuously adjust rotor speed to maintain operation at or near $\lambda_{opt}$ across varying wind conditions.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the power coefficient $C_P$ in terms of the axial induction factor $a$ using the actuator disc theory. Show that the maximum theoretical value of $C_P$ is $\frac{16}{27}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Model Setup:** The rotor is modeled as an infinitely thin actuator disc. Let $V_1$ = upstream velocity, $V_d$ = velocity at disc, $V_4$ = far-wake velocity, and axial induction factor $a$ defined by $V_d = V_1(1-a)$.

**Step 2 — Froude's Rankine Hypothesis:** $V_d = \frac{V_1 + V_4}{2}$, giving $V_4 = V_1(1-2a)$.

**Step 3 — Bernoulli's Equation Application:** The pressure jump across the disc:
$$\Delta p = \frac{1}{2}\rho (V_1^2 - V_4^2) = \frac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 \left[1 - (1-2a)^2\right] = 2\rho V_1^2 a(1-a)$$

**Step 4 — Thrust Calculation:** $T = \Delta p \cdot A = 2\rho A V_1^2 a(1-a)$.

**Step 5 — Extracted Power:** $P_{ext} = T \cdot V_d = 2\rho A V_1^3 a(1-a)^2$.

**Step 6 — Form $C_P$:**
$$C_P = \frac{P_{ext}}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V_1^3} = 4a(1-a)^2$$

**Step 7 — Maximize:** $\frac{dC_P}{da} = 4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0$ gives $a = \frac{1}{3}$.

**Step 8 — Substitute:** $C_{P,max} = 4 \times \frac{1}{3} \times \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$. **(Betz Limit)**

---

**(b)** A horizontal-axis wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 60 m and operates at a wind speed of 10 m/s with a rotational speed of 22 rpm. The power output measured is 850 kW. Take air density as 1.225 kg/m³. Calculate the swept area, available wind power, power coefficient, tip speed ratio, and torque coefficient. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Swept Area:**
$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (60)^2}{4} = 2827.43 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Available Wind Power:**
$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 2827.43 \times (10)^3 = 1.732 \text{ MW} = 1731.8 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Power Coefficient:**
$$C_P = \frac{P_{turbine}}{P_{wind}} = \frac{850}{1731.8} = 0.491$$

**Step 4 — Tip Speed Ratio:**
$$\lambda = \frac{\pi R n}{30 V} = \frac{\pi \times 30 \times 22}{30 \times 10} = \frac{2073.45}{300} = 6.91$$

**Step 5 — Torque Coefficient:** Using $C_P = \lambda C_Q$:
$$C_Q = \frac{C_P}{\lambda} = \frac{0.491}{6.91} = 0.0711$$

**Step 6 — Verification:** $0.491 < 0.593$ (Betz limit) ✓ — physically realistic result.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the concept of thrust coefficient and torque coefficient of a wind turbine. Derive the relationship $C_P = \lambda \cdot C_Q$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definitions:** The thrust coefficient normalizes the aerodynamic thrust force experienced by the rotor:
$$C_T = \frac{T}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V^2}$$
The torque coefficient normalizes the mechanical torque output:
$$C_Q = \frac{Q}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A R V^2}$$

**Step 2 — Mechanical Power Identity:** The mechanical power output of a rotating system equals torque multiplied by angular velocity:
$$P_{turbine} = \omega Q$$

**Step 3 — Express Power Using $C_P$ and Torque Using $C_Q$:**
$$C_P \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3 = \omega \cdot C_Q \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho A R V^2$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:** Cancel $\frac{1}{2}\rho A V^2$ from both sides:
$$C_P \cdot V = \omega \cdot C_Q \cdot R$$

**Step 5 — Identify TSR:** Since $\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V}$:
$$C_P = C_Q \cdot \lambda \implies \boxed{C_P = \lambda C_Q}$$

---

**(b)** A wind farm uses turbines with rotor diameter 90 m, rated power 2.5 MW, and rated wind speed 14 m/s. The Betz limit is 0.593 and the actual operating power coefficient at rated conditions is 0.45. Calculate: (i) the maximum theoretical power extractable, (ii) the actual power output, (iii) the ratio of actual to maximum theoretical power. Assume $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Swept Area:**
$$A = \frac{\pi (90)^2}{4} = 6361.73 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Total Available Wind Power:**
$$P_{wind} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 6361.73 \times (14)^3 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 6361.73 \times 2744 = 10.694 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Theoretical Power (Betz Limit):**
$$P_{Betz} = C_{P,max} \times P_{wind} = 0.593 \times 10.694 = 6.342 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 4 — Actual Power Output:**
$$P_{actual} = C_{P,actual} \times P_{wind} = 0.45 \times 10.694 = 4.812 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 5 — Ratio:**
$$\eta_{ratio} = \frac{P_{actual}}{P_{Betz}} = \frac{4.812}{6.342} = 0.759 \text{ or } 75.9\%$$

This shows the real turbine achieves **75.9%** of the theoretical maximum — typical of well-designed utility-scale machines.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout**
>
> - **Pitfall 1 — Confusing $\frac{16}{27}$ with the Betz number:** Students often write $0.59$ or $59\%$ instead of the exact fraction. Always state $C_{P,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ and the corresponding induction factor $a = \frac{1}{3}$.
> - **Pitfall 2 — Forgetting units in $P_{wind}$:** The $V^3$ term must be in (m/s)³. A common error is using km/h without conversion, which inflates the answer by a factor of $(3.6)^3 \approx 46.7$.
> - **Pitfall 3 — Misapplying $\lambda$ formula:** KTU accepts both $\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V}$ and $\lambda = \frac{\pi R n}{30 V}$. Use whichever is convenient; mixing up $n$ (rpm) and $\omega$ (rad/s) without conversion causes 7-mark deductions.
> - **Pitfall 4 — Skipping the differentiation step:** In Betz limit derivations, the step $\frac{dC_P}{da} = 4(1-a)(1-3a)$ must be shown explicitly. A bare statement of the answer $C_P = 16/27$ without derivation attracts 0 marks in ESE Part B.
> - **Pitfall 5 — Numerical value of $C_P$ exceeding Betz limit:** If your final answer gives $C_P > 0.593$, immediately recheck the input data — physically impossible. State the verification explicitly to earn full marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** $C_P = \frac{P_{turbine}}{P_{wind}}$ — dimensionless, value always between 0 and $\frac{16}{27}$.
- **Betz Limit:** $C_{P,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ at optimal induction factor $a = \frac{1}{3}$.
- **Actuator Disc Result:** $C_P = 4a(1-a)^2$ — the *fundamental equation* in wind turbine aerodynamics.
- **Wind Power Cubic Law:** $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$ — doubling wind speed gives **8× power**.
- **Tip Speed Ratio:** $\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V} = \frac{\pi R n}{30 V}$ — controls $C_P$ via blade aerodynamics.
- **Optimal $\lambda$:** Modern three-bladed turbines have $\lambda_{opt} \approx 6$ to $8$.
- **Thrust Coefficient:** $C_T = \frac{T}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A V^2}$ — important for structural load design.
- **Torque Coefficient:** $C_Q = \frac{Q}{\frac{1}{2}\rho A R V^2}$.
- **Master Identity:** $C_P = \lambda \cdot C_Q$ — frequently tested.
- **Pitch Angle $\beta$:** Increasing $\beta$ (feathering blades) reduces $C_P$ — used for power limiting above rated wind speed.
- **Real-World $C_P$:** Modern utility turbines operate at $C_P \approx 0.35$ to $0.50$ due to wake rotation, tip losses, hub drag, and airfoil drag.
- **Physical Constants to Memorize:** $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ (sea level), $\pi = 3.1416$, $\frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$.
- **Operational Strategy:** Variable-speed turbines use MPPT to track $\lambda_{opt}$ and thus maximize $C_P$.
- **Above Rated Wind:** $C_P$ is intentionally *reduced* by pitch control to prevent overloading the generator.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Betz Limit Theory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT862 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Betz Limit Theory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Betz Limit Theory

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Betz Limit** (also known as the **Betz–Lanchester Limit** or the **Betz Coefficient**) is the theoretical maximum coefficient of power ($C_{P,\max}$) that can be extracted by an **ideal, frictionless wind turbine** operating in free-flowing air. It was first derived by the German physicist **Albert Betz** in 1919.

Mathematically, the Betz Limit states that no wind energy conversion device can convert more than:

$$C_{P,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 59.3\%$$

of the **kinetic energy** present in a moving air mass into useful mechanical work. The remaining $\approx 40.7\%$ of the energy must be retained in the wake to maintain continuous mass flow through the rotor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The value $C_P = 16/27 = 0.59259...$ is the absolute theoretical ceiling. Real wind turbines achieve $C_P$ values between **0.35 and 0.45** (i.e., 75% of Betz Limit) due to rotational, tip-loss, and drag inefficiencies.

### 1.2 The Actuator Disc Concept (Foundation of Betz Theory)

To analyse energy extraction, Betz modelled the rotor as a porous **"actuator disc"** — an imaginary, frictionless disc through which the air mass must pass. The disc:

1. Extracts energy from the air by reducing its velocity from $V_1$ (upstream) to $V_2$ (downstream/wake).
2. Creates a pressure difference ($P^+ - P^-$) across it.
3. Does not rotate and has no thickness in the strictest idealised form.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why an "actuator" disc?** The real rotor is a complex rotating surface; the actuator disc is a mathematical simplification that allows application of **1-D momentum, continuity, and Bernoulli's equations** across a control volume. This converts a complex aerodynamic problem into a tractable engineering analysis.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing in a flowing river holding a large sieve. The water entering the sieve is fast ($V_1$), but water exiting the other side is slower ($V_2$) because the sieve absorbed some kinetic energy. 

- If you used a **solid wall** (100% blockage), $V_2 = 0$, and the river would back up and divert around the wall — almost **no energy is captured** (0% efficiency).
- If you used a **wide-open sieve** (0% blockage), $V_2 \approx V_1$, and again **no energy is captured** (0% efficiency).
- The **sweet spot** lies between these extremes — when the exit velocity is exactly **one-third (1/3)** of the inlet velocity. At this magic ratio, the sieve extracts the **maximum possible kinetic energy** from the river.

This "magic ratio" of 1/3 is the essence of the Betz Limit, and the **maximum extraction efficiency** is **16/27 ≈ 59.3%**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Viva:** *"Betz says — keep one-third of the wind's speed in the wake; that's how you harvest the most power."*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Foundational Assumptions of Betz Theory

For the derivation to hold, Betz postulated a perfectly idealised system. The **assumptions** are critical for KTU theory questions:

| # | Assumption | Engineering Meaning |
|---|------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Homogeneous, incompressible, steady flow** | Air density $\rho$ is constant; no pulsations. |
| 2 | **Infinite number of blades** (or no rotational wake) | The disc is a smooth actuator with uniform pressure. |
| 3 | **No frictional drag** | Only pressure forces act on the air mass. |
| 4 | **Uniform velocity across the disc area** | The disc area $A$ equals the swept area of the rotor. |
| 5 | **Non-rotating wake** | The wake does not spin (avoids swirl losses). |

### 2.2 Why the Wake Cannot Be Stopped Completely

This is the **physical "Why"** behind the Betz Limit that examiners love to test:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Physical Insight:** If the turbine extracted 100% of the wind's kinetic energy, the air behind the disc would have $V_2 = 0$. This would create a **complete stagnation** downstream, meaning no new air could enter the disc. Therefore, *some* kinetic energy must remain in the wake to allow continuous mass flow. Betz proved that the optimum trade-off occurs at $V_2 = V_1/3$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Betz Limit

The following table consolidates **every equation** you must memorise for the End Semester Examination (ESE). Pay special attention to the substitution rules to avoid common algebraic errors.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Legend | Units (SI) |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|------------|
| 1 | Power in undisturbed wind | $P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V_1^3$ | $\rho$ = air density (**1.225 kg/m³**), $A$ = swept area, $V_1$ = upstream wind speed | W (Watts) |
| 2 | Mass flow rate through disc | $\dot{m} = \rho A V_d$ | $V_d$ = velocity at disc plane | kg/s |
| 3 | Velocity at disc (continuity) | $V_d = \frac{V_1 + V_2}{2}$ | $V_2$ = downstream wake velocity | m/s |
| 4 | Thrust force on disc | $F_T = \dot{m}(V_1 - V_2) = \frac{1}{2} \rho A (V_1^2 - V_2^2)$ | By momentum equation | N (Newtons) |
| 5 | Power extracted by disc | $P = F_T \cdot V_d = \frac{1}{4} \rho A (V_1^2 - V_2^2)(V_1 + V_2)$ | Work done per second | W |
| 6 | Power coefficient | $C_P = \dfrac{P}{P_{wind}} = \dfrac{(1+x)^2 (1-x)}{2}$ where $x = \dfrac{V_2}{V_1}$ | Dimensionless ratio | — |
| 7 | Induction factor (axial) | $a = \dfrac{V_1 - V_d}{V_1} = \dfrac{1 - x}{2}$ | Fractional speed drop at disc | — |
| 8 | **Betz Limit value** | $C_{P,\max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926$ | **Universal constant — independent of size** | — |
| 9 | Optimal wake ratio | $\left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)_{opt} = \dfrac{1}{3}$ | At maximum power point | — |
| 10 | Optimal disc velocity | $(V_d)_{opt} = \dfrac{2}{3} V_1$ | At Betz optimum | m/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse $V_d$ (velocity at the disc) with $V_1$ (upstream). $V_d$ is the **arithmetic mean** of upstream and downstream velocities ONLY when the disc is modelled as a porous plane. For real rotors with rotational wake, this changes.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Betz Limit is the **design benchmark** for every commercial wind turbine manufactured today. Its applications include:

- **Blade design:** Engineers use the Betz optimum ($V_2 = V_1/3$) to compute target rotor tip-speed ratios ($\lambda \approx 7$–$8$ for modern 3-blade designs).
- **Capacity estimation:** A wind farm's maximum theoretical annual energy is computed by integrating $C_{P,\max} \cdot P_{wind}$ over the wind speed distribution (Weibull).
