<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Horizontal Axis Wind Turbines (HAWT) and Vertical Axis Wind Turbines (VAWT) with respect to axis orientation, yaw mechanism, and wind direction sensitivity.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | HAWT | VAWT |
|---|---|---|
| Axis orientation | Horizontal (parallel to ground) | Vertical (perpendicular to ground) |
| Yaw mechanism | Required to align rotor with wind | Not required (omnidirectional) |
| Wind direction sensitivity | High (needs alignment) | Independent of direction |

HAWTs achieve higher aerodynamic efficiency (Cₚ up to 0.48), while VAWTs offer simpler installation, lower maintenance cost, and operate in turbulent flows.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State and explain the Betz Limit. Why is its value 16/27?**

**Model Answer:**
The Betz Limit is the maximum fraction of kinetic energy of wind that an ideal wind turbine can extract.

$$
C_{P,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593 \quad \text{i.e., } 59.3\%
$$

It is derived from 1-D momentum theory by maximizing the axial induction factor `a = 1/3`, yielding the theoretical limit that no turbine — regardless of blade design — can extract more than 59.3 % of wind power. The remainder (40.7 %) remains in the wake as residual kinetic energy.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** HAWT vs VAWT sub-classifications (Savonius vs Darrieus) — many students write only one type in detail. Always cover **both** subtypes.
> 2. In derivation questions, **show every algebraic step**. Skipping the substitution of axial induction factor **a** costs 3–4 marks immediately.
> 3. Numerical questions on tip-speed ratio (λ) demand correct **unit consistency** (rad/s vs rpm). Wrong conversion = direct 2-mark penalty.
> 4. In classification tables, examiners look for **at least 6 comparative parameters**; restricting to 3 parameters may cap marks at 7.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Classify Wind Energy Conversion Systems based on (i) axis of rotation, (ii) power control mechanism, and (iii) rotational speed. Explain each category with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A three-blade HAWT has a rotor radius of **40 m**. The wind speed is **12 m/s**, rotational speed is **20 rpm**, air density is **1.225 kg/m³**, and the power coefficient is **0.40**. Calculate: (i) swept area, (ii) tip speed ratio, (iii) available wind power, and (iv) actual turbine power output. **(7 Marks)**

### **Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Classification

**(i) Based on Axis of Rotation:**
- **HAWT (Horizontal Axis):** Axis parallel to ground. Subtypes: Upwind (rotor in front of tower), Downwind (rotor behind tower). Examples: Vestas V164, GE Cypress.
- **VAWT (Vertical Axis):** Axis perpendicular to ground. Subtypes: Savonius (drag-based), Darrieus (lift-based), Giromill (H-rotor, variable pitch).

**(ii) Based on Power Control:**
- **Stall-Regulated (Passive Stall):** Blade aerodynamically stalls at high wind speeds; no moving parts. Used in older designs.
- **Pitch-Regulated (Active Pitch):** Blades rotate about their long axis to reduce angle of attack at high winds; industry standard for modern MW turbines.
- **Active Stall:** Combines pitching with stall at very high wind for smoother power output.

**(iii) Based on Rotational Speed:**
- **Constant Speed (Type A/B):** SCIG directly connected to grid via soft starter.
- **Limited Variable Speed (Type C):** DFIG with rotor-side converter (partial rating ~30 %).
- **Full Variable Speed (Type D):** PMSG / SCIG with full-rated back-to-back converter; highest energy yield.

#### Part (b) — Numerical

Given: R = 40 m, V = 12 m/s, n = 20 rpm, ρ = 1.225 kg/m³, Cₚ = 0.40.

**(i) Swept Area:**

$$
A = \pi R^{2} = \pi \cdot (40)^{2} = 1600\pi \approx 5026.55 \ \text{m}^{2}
$$

**(ii) Tip Speed Ratio:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{\pi R n}{30 V} = \frac{\pi \cdot 40 \cdot 20}{30 \cdot 12} = \frac{800\pi}{360} \approx 6.98
$$

**(iii) Available Wind Power:**

$$
P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3} = 0.5 \cdot 1.225 \cdot 5026.55 \cdot (12)^{3}
$$

$$
P_{wind} = 0.6125 \cdot 5026.55 \cdot 1728 \approx 5.319 \ \text{MW}
$$

**(iv) Actual Turbine Power Output:**

$$
P_{turbine} = C_{p} \cdot P_{wind} = 0.40 \cdot 5.319 \approx 2.128 \ \text{MW}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the construction and working principle of **Savonius** and **Darrieus** VAWTs. Compare their starting torque and efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Betz Limit starting from 1-D momentum theory. Show that the maximum power coefficient is **16/27** and the corresponding axial induction factor is **1/3**. **(7 Marks)**

### **Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) — VAWT Construction & Working

**Savonius Rotor:**
- Constructed from **two or three half-cylinders (S-shaped buckets)** mounted on a vertical shaft.
- **Working:** Wind striking the concave side experiences higher drag than the convex side → net torque rotates the rotor.
- **Starting torque:** Very high (drag-type machine starts at any wind speed).
- **Efficiency:** Low Cₚ ≈ 0.20 due to high drag losses.

**Darrieus Rotor:**
- Constructed from **two or three curved (troposkein-shaped) blades** rotating about a vertical axis.
- **Working:** Aerodynamic lift on the curved blades (like an aircraft wing) provides torque at high tip speeds.
- **Starting torque:** Poor (no self-starting capability; requires external motor or Savonius-assisted start).
- **Efficiency:** Higher Cₚ ≈ 0.35, approaching HAWT values.

**Comparison:**

| Parameter | Savonius | Darrieus |
|---|---|---|
| Working principle | Drag | Lift |
| Self-starting | Yes | No |
| Cₚ | ~0.20 | ~0.35 |
| Tip speed ratio | Low (≈ 1) | High (≈ 5–6) |
| Application | Water pumping, ventilation | Electricity generation |

#### Part (b) — Derivation of Betz Limit

The derivation is fully presented in **Section 3.1, Steps 1–5** above.

**Summary of Key Equations:**

$$
C_{P} = 4a(1-a)^{2}
$$

Differentiating and equating to zero:

$$
\frac{dC_{P}}{da} = 4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0 \implies a = \frac{1}{3}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
C_{P,max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593
$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** Practical turbines achieve 75–80 % of the Betz limit (i.e., Cₚ ≈ 0.45–0.48). The shortfall arises from rotational wake effects, tip losses, viscous drag, and non-ideal axial momentum distribution.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- [ ] **WECS** = Wind Energy Conversion System; converts kinetic wind energy → mechanical → electrical.
- [ ] **Primary classification:** HAWT (high Cₚ, needs yaw) vs VAWT (omnidirectional, simpler).
- [ ] **HAWT subtypes:** Upwind (preferred) vs Downwind (passive yaw).
- [ ] **VAWT subtypes:** Savonius (drag, self-starting, low Cₚ), Darrieus (lift, no self-start, high Cₚ), Giromill (variable pitch H-rotor).
- [ ] **Power control:** Stall (passive, cheap) vs Pitch (active, modern) vs Active Stall (combined).
- [ ] **Speed types:** Type A/B constant; Type C limited variable (DFIG); Type D full variable (PMSG).
- [ ] **Betz Limit = 16/27 ≈ 0.593** is the absolute theoretical ceiling.
- [ ] **Tip Speed Ratio λ = ωR/V = πRn/(30V)**; high λ → fast turbines (HAWT), low λ → slow turbines (multi-blade pumpers).
- [ ] **Wind Power Equation:** $P = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^{3} C_{p}$ — cubic dependence on wind speed is critical.
- [ ] **Wind shear law:** $V = V_{0} (D/D_{0})^{\alpha}$ with α from 0.10 (sea) to 0.40 (urban).
- [ ] **Swept Area for HAWT:** $A = \pi R^{2}$; for VAWT: $A = D \times H$.
- [ ] Real-world Cₚ is 70–80 % of Betz limit due to losses (rotational, tip, viscous).
- [ ] Global wind market is dominated (>90 %) by **3-blade upwind HAWT** with **pitch regulation** and **DFIG/PMSG variable-speed** drivetrains.
- [ ] Always show **units and numerical substitutions** in KTU derivations to earn full marks.
- [ ] Always convert **rpm to rad/s** correctly: ω = πn/30.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of OTEC System - Methods of OTEC Power Generation: Open Cycle (Claude Cycle), Closed Cycle (Anderson Cycle) and Hybrid Cycle - Block Diagram Description of OTEC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT862 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principle of OTEC System - Methods of OTEC Power Generation: Open Cycle (Claude Cycle), Closed Cycle (Anderson Cycle) and Hybrid Cycle - Block Diagram Description of OTEC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC)** is a renewable energy conversion technology that exploits the **natural thermal gradient** existing between the **warm upper layer** of a tropical ocean and the **cold deep water** to drive a heat engine and generate **baseload electrical power**. The minimum useful surface-to-deep temperature differential is **~20 °C** (typically 20–25 °C in equatorial belts between 20°N and 20°S latitude).

The conversion principle is governed by the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**: a heat engine operating between a **hot reservoir (warm surface seawater at $T_H$)** and a **cold reservoir (deep seawater at $T_C$)** can convert part of the supplied heat into **shaft work (turbine output)**, with the theoretical upper limit given by the **Carnot efficiency**.

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$

For an OTEC plant operating between, say, **$T_H = 298\text{ K}$ (25 °C)** and **$T_C = 278\text{ K}$ (5 °C)**:

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{278}{298} \approx 0.067 = 6.7\%$$

This small-but-continuous value is the **engineering trade-off** that defines every OTEC design choice.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Atmospheric Pressure Cooker"**:
> Think of the ocean surface as a giant solar collector absorbing tropical sunlight all day. The top few metres become a **warm bath** (25–28 °C), while water below 1000 m remains a **chilly, untouched reservoir** (~4–6 °C). OTEC essentially installs a **"pressure cooker"** at the surface, where the warm water "boils" a working fluid to spin a turbine, and then uses the **deep cold water as an ice-cube condenser** to re-liquefy the vapour. The cycle repeats — continuously, day and night, since the ocean stores the solar energy.

**Why this matters:** Unlike solar PV or wind, OTEC is a **24 × 7 (baseload)** renewable source because the ocean's thermal mass is enormous. However, the **low efficiency** (≈ 3–4 % in practice) demands **enormous volumetric flows** of seawater, making **pump power consumption** a critical design parameter.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Reference Numbers (memorise for KTU exams):**
> - **Minimum $\Delta T$** for viable operation: **20 °C**
> - **Typical Carnot efficiency**: **6 – 8 %**
> - **Practical thermal efficiency**: **3 – 4 %**
> - **Required depth** for cold water intake: **≥ 1000 m**
> - **Working fluid in Closed Cycle**: **Ammonia (NH₃)** — preferred for high vapour pressure at low temperatures
> - **Required warm surface temperature**: **≥ 25 °C** (limits deployment to tropical belt)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Power-Generation Methods — Conceptual Breakdown

OTEC systems are classified by the **physical state of the working fluid** and the **loop topology**. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly mandates the following three.

### 2.1.1 Open Cycle (Claude Cycle)

- **Working fluid:** Warm **surface seawater itself** (no separate fluid).
- **Phase change:** Flash evaporation under vacuum (pressure ~ 0.03 bar at 25 °C).
- **Turbine driver:** Low-pressure steam turbine.
- **Condenser:** Direct contact with cold deep seawater (the vapour is condensed by the cold water).
- **By-product:** **Desalinated fresh water** — a valuable co-product that improves project economics.
- **Disadvantage:** Very large turbine size due to low steam density; high vacuum pump work.

### 2.1.2 Closed Cycle (Anderson Cycle)

- **Working fluid:** A **volatile refrigerant** (typically **ammonia, NH₃**, or R-134a) circulating in a **sealed loop**.
- **Heat addition:** Warm seawater boils the ammonia in an **evaporator (heat exchanger)**.
- **Heat rejection:** Cold deep seawater condenses the ammonia in a **condenser (heat exchanger)**.
- **No direct contact** between seawater and working fluid — eliminates biofouling of the turbine.
- **Advantage:** Compact, high-pressure ammonia vapour → smaller, cheaper turbine.
- **Inventor:** Originally proposed by **Jacques-Arsène d'Arsonval** (1881) and first practically demonstrated by **Harold T. Anderson** in the 1960s.

### 2.1.3 Hybrid Cycle

- **Combines** a **Closed Cycle (primary power loop)** with a **flashing Open Cycle (secondary flash loop)**.
- The warm seawater is first used to **boil ammonia** (closed loop), then partially flashed to **generate low-pressure steam** that drives a **secondary turbine** before being discharged.
- **Benefit:** Higher overall thermal efficiency, **desalinated water** as a co-product, and better utilisation of the thermal resource.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All key equations for OTEC problem-solving (KTU Exam Ready):**

| # | Formula | Description / Engineering Use |
|---|---------|------------------------------|
| 1 | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}$ | Theoretical maximum thermal efficiency; use **absolute temperatures (K)**. |
| 2 | $\eta_{\text{actual}} = \eta_{\text{Carnot}} \times \eta_{\text{rel}} \times \eta_{\text{mech}}$ | Product of relative (turbine/condenser) and mechanical efficiencies. |
| 3 | $W_{\text{net}} = Q_H \times \eta_{\text{net}}$ | Net work output from supplied heat. |
| 4 | $Q_H = \dot{m}_{w} \, c_{p,w} \, (T_H - T_{\text{evap}})$ | Heat extracted from warm seawater per unit time. |
| 5 | $Q_C = \dot{m}_{c} \, c_{p,c} \, (T_{\text{cond}} - T_C)$ | Heat rejected to cold deep seawater per unit time. |
| 6 | $Q_{\text{evap}} = \dot{m}_{f} \, h_{fg}$ | Heat transferred in evaporator (closed cycle), where $\dot{m}_{f}$ is the working fluid mass flow. |
| 7 | $\dot{m}_{f} = \dfrac{Q_H}{h_{fg,\text{NH}_3}}$ | Mass flow rate of working fluid (ammonia). |
| 8 | $\text{COP}_{\text{OTEC}} = \eta_{\text{Carnot}} \approx 0.06\text{–}0.08$ | Not a true COP, but the Carnot figure of merit. |
| 9 | $P_{\text{net}} = P_{\text{turbine}} - P_{\text{pump,cold}} - P_{\text{pump,warm}}$ | Net electrical output (gross minus parasitic loads). |
| 10 | $\Delta T_{\text{min}} = 20\text{ °C}$ | Empirical minimum for commercial viability. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant values used in numericals:**
> - $c_{p,\text{seawater}} \approx 4.0\text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
> - $h_{fg,\text{water at 25 °C}} \approx 2442\text{ kJ/kg}$
> - $h_{fg,\text{NH}_3 \text{ at 25 °C}} \approx 1167\text{ kJ/kg}$ (much lower → smaller mass flow)

## 2.3 Real-World Utility & Engineering Significance

- **Tropical island nations** (Maldives, Hawaii, Indonesia, Réunion, Japan — Okinawa) are the prime beneficiaries since they have **no indigenous fossil fuels** but year-round warm seas.
- **Largest operational plant historically:** **NH3 Closed-Cycle, 100 kW, Kona, Hawaii (Natural Energy Laboratory of Hawaii Authority — NELHA, 1979)**.
- **Japan (Saga University)** built a 50 kW closed-cycle demonstration plant in 1980s.
- **Co-products** that improve economic viability: **fresh water (desalination)**, **chilled nutrient-rich deep water for aquaculture**, and **cold air conditioning** (using cold deep water directly).
- **Strategic application:** Producing **liquid hydrogen (LH₂)** or **ammonia (NH₃) fuel** from the desalinated water and surplus OTEC electricity — a true "green fuel" pathway.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Net Power Output of a Generic OTEC Plant

We will rigorously derive the **net electrical power** from a generic closed-cycle OTEC plant as a function of the warm/cold water mass flows and the working-fluid loop.

### Given (Generic Design Problem)

- Warm surface seawater: $T_H = 28\text{ °C} = 301\text{ K}$
- Cold deep seawater: $T_C = 5\text{ °C} = 278\text{ K}$
- Mass flow of warm water: $\dot{m}_w = 1000\text{ kg/s}$
- Mass flow of cold water: $\dot{m}_c = 1000\text{ kg/s}$
- Specific heat of seawater: $c_{p,w} = 4.0\text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- Evaporator outlet temperature of working fluid: $T_{\text{evap}} = 25\text{ °C}$
- Condenser inlet temperature: $T_{\text{cond}} = 10\text{ °C}$
- Working fluid (ammonia) latent heat: $h_{fg} = 1167\text{ kJ/kg}$
- Turbine isentropic efficiency: $\eta_t = 0.85$
- Generator efficiency: $\eta_g = 0.95$
- Cold-water pump efficiency: $\eta_p = 0.80$

### Step 1 — Heat Supplied to the Evaporator (from warm seawater)

The warm seawater cools from $T_H$ down to the evaporator temperature $T_{\text{evap}}$. The available heat rate is:

$$
\dot{Q}_H = \dot{m}_w \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot (T_H - T_{\text{evap}})
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
\dot{Q}_H = 1000 \times 4.0 \times (28 - 25) = 1000 \times 4.0 \times 3
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_H = 12{,}000\text{ kW} = 12\text{ MW}_{\text{th}}
$$

> **Engineering meaning:** A million litres of warm seawater per hour releases 12 MW of thermal energy to boil the ammonia.

### Step 2 — Mass Flow Rate of Working Fluid (Ammonia)

The heat required to evaporate the ammonia equals $\dot{Q}_H$. Hence:

$$
\dot{m}_{NH_3} = \frac{\dot{Q}_H}{h_{fg,\text{NH}_3}} = \frac{12{,}000}{1167}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{NH_3} \approx 10.28\text{ kg/s}
$$

> **Engineering meaning:** Only ~10 kg/s of ammonia is required — a 100× reduction compared to a steam cycle, thanks to ammonia's favourable $h_{fg}$.

### Step 3 — Ideal (Carnot) Turbine Work

For the temperature limits $T_{\text{evap}} = 298\text{ K}$ and $T_{\text{cond}} = 283\text{ K}$:

$$
\dot{W}_{\text{Carnot}} = \dot{Q}_H \times \eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 12{,}000 \times \left(1 - \frac{283}{298}\right)
$$

$$
\dot{W}_{\text{Carnot}} = 12{,}000 \times (1 - 0.9497) = 12{,}000 \times 0.0503
$$

$$
\dot{W}_{\text{Carnot}} \approx 604\text{ kW}_{\text{mech}}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Real Efficiencies (Turbine + Generator)

$$
\dot{W}_{\text{electrical}} = \dot{W}_{\text{Carnot}} \times \eta_t \times \eta_g
$$

$$
\dot{W}_{\text{electrical}} = 604 \times 0.85 \times 0.95
$$

$$
\dot{W}_{\text{electrical}} \approx 487.7\text{ kW}_{\text{e}}
$$

### Step 5 — Parasitic Pump Power (Cold Water Lift to 1000 m)

The hydraulic power required to lift cold seawater is approximated by the **gauge pressure** of a 1000 m water column (≈ 100 bar or 10 MPa). For a volumetric flow $\dot{V}_c$:

$$
P_{\text{pump,hyd}} = \dot{V}_c \times \Delta p
$$

With $\dot{m}_c = 1000\text{ kg/s}$ and seawater density $\rho \approx 1025\text{ kg/m}^3$:

$$
\dot{V}_c = \frac{1000}{1025} \approx 0.976\text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

For a 1000 m lift, $\Delta p = \rho g h = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 1000 \approx 10.05\text{ MPa} = 10{,}050\text{ kPa}$.

$$
P_{\text{pump,hyd}} = 0.976 \times 10{,}050 \approx 9810\text{ kW} \approx 9.81\text{ MW}
$$

Accounting for pump efficiency:

$$
P_{\text{pump,shaft}} = \frac{9810}{0.80} \approx 12{,}263\text{ kW}
$$

> **⚠ Critical Insight:** This parasitic load is **~25× the net electrical output** we computed! In real OTEC design, this is solved by using a **gravity-fed (downward sloping) cold-water pipe (CWP)** so the *head* itself provides the pressure, with pumps only providing trim/flow control.

### Step 6 — Net Electrical Output (with assisted CWP)

If pumps are only sized for friction losses (assume 5 % of the hydraulic power):

$$
P_{\text{pump,real}} = 0.05 \times 9810 \approx 490\text{ kW}
$$

$$
\dot{W}_{\text{net}} = \dot{W}_{\text{electrical}} - P_{\text{pump,real}}
$$

$$
\dot{W}_{\text{net}} = 487.7 - 490 \approx -2.3\text{ kW}
$$

> This shows the design is **on the borderline of viability**. In practice, $\dot{m}_w$ and $\dot{m}_c$ must be **two-to-three times larger** to achieve positive net output — confirming the **scale-sensitive** nature of OTEC.

### Step 7 — Re-compute Net Output with Realistic $\dot{m}_w = 3000\text{ kg/s}$

$$
\dot{Q}_H = 3000 \times 4.0 \times 3 = 36{,}000\text{ kW}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{NH_3} = \frac{36{,}000}{1167} = 30.85\text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
\dot{W}_{\text{Carnot}} = 36{,}000 \times 0.0503 = 1810.8\text{ kW}
$$

$$
\dot{W}_{\text{electrical}} = 1810.8 \times 0.85 \times 0.95 \approx 1462\text{ kW}_{\text{e}}
$$

With $P_{\text{pump}} \approx 1500\text{ kW}$ (3× the warm water flow but with assisted CWP), we get **near-zero or marginal net output** — illustrating the **fundamental challenge** of OTEC.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Method Comparison

| Parameter | Open Cycle (Claude) | Closed Cycle (Anderson) | Hybrid Cycle |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------------------|--------------|
| **Working fluid** | Warm seawater itself | Ammonia (sealed loop) | NH₃ + flashed seawater |
| **Pressure at turbine inlet** | Very low (~0.03 bar) | Moderate (~10 bar) | Mixed |
| **Turbine size** | Very large | Compact | Multiple turbines |
| **Heat exchangers** | Not needed (direct) | Required (evaporator + condenser) | Required |
| **Fresh water by-product** | **Yes** (from condensed vapour) | No (unless added) | **Yes** |
| **Vacuum pump needed** | **Yes** (large) | No | Partial |
| **Biofouling of turbine** | Possible | None (closed) | Minimal |
| **Start-up time** | Slow (vacuum build-up) | Fast | Moderate |
| **Thermal efficiency** | ~2–3 % | ~3–4 % | ~4–5 % |
| **Land footprint** | Small | Moderate | Large |
| **KTU 2024 priority** | **High** | **High** | Moderate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Open Cycle (Claude Cycle) OTEC Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["WARM SEAWATER INTAKE\nat surface, ~25 to 28 C"] --> A2["FLASH EVAPORATOR\nlow pressure chamber"]
    A2 -->|low pressure steam| A3["TURBINE\nlow pressure steam turbine"]
    A3 -->|shaft work| A4["GENERATOR\nelectrical output"]
    A3 -->|spent steam| A5["CONDENSER\ndirect contact with cold water"]
    A6["COLD DEEP SEAWATER PIPE\n1000 m depth, ~4 to 6 C"] --> A5
    A5 -->|condensate| A7["DESALINATED WATER\nfresh water output"]
    A5 -->|waste brine| A8["WASTE DISCHARGE\nto ocean"]
    A2 -.vacuum pump.-> A9["VACUUM PUMP\nmaintains low pressure"]
```

**Reading guide:** Warm surface water enters the flash chamber; the vacuum causes it to boil. The steam drives the turbine, then meets cold deep water in the condenser — yielding **desalinated water as a valuable co-product**.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Closed Cycle (Anderson Cycle) OTEC Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1["WARM SEAWATER INTAKE\nsurface, ~25 to 28 C"] --> B2["EVAPORATOR\nheat exchanger, NH3 boils"]
    B2 -->|high pressure NH3 vapour| B3["TURBINE\ncompact ammonia turbine"]
    B3 -->|shaft work| B4["GENERATOR\nelectrical output"]
    B3 -->|low pressure NH3 vapour| B5["CONDENSER\nheat exchanger with cold water"]
    B6["COLD DEEP SEAWATER PIPE\n1000 m depth, ~4 to 6 C"] --> B5
    B5 -->|liquid NH3| B7["PUMP\ncirculation pump"]
    B7 -->|high pressure liquid NH3| B2
    B5 -->|warmed seawater| B8["WASTE DISCHARGE\nto ocean"]
```

**Reading guide:** Ammonia circulates in a **sealed loop**. It is boiled by warm water in the **evaporator**, expands through the **turbine**, condensed back to liquid by cold deep water, and re-pressurised by the **pump** — completing a **Rankine cycle** identical in principle to a conventional power plant, but at very low temperature.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Hybrid Cycle OTEC Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1["WARM SEAWATER INTAKE\nsurface, ~25 to 28 C"] --> C2["EVAPORATOR\nboils NH3 in closed loop"]
    C2 -->|NH3 vapour| C3["NH3 TURBINE\nprimary power"]
    C3 -->|shaft work| C4["GENERATOR 1\nprimary electrical output"]
    C2 -->|warmed seawater| C5["FLASH CHAMBER\nsecondary open cycle"]
    C5 -->|low pressure steam| C6["STEAM TURBINE\nsecondary power"]
    C6 -->|shaft work| C7["GENERATOR 2\nsecondary electrical output"]
    C6 -->|spent steam| C8["CONDENSER\ncold water contact"]
    C9["COLD DEEP SEAWATER PIPE\n1000 m depth, ~4 to 6 C"] --> C2
    C9 --> C8
    C8 -->|fresh water| C10["DESALINATED WATER\nfresh water output"]
    C8 -->|brine| C11["WASTE DISCHARGE\nto ocean"]
```

**Reading guide:** A single warm-water stream serves **two cycles in series** — first boiling ammonia (high-grade heat → efficient conversion), then flashing steam (lower-grade heat → still useful). The result is **higher overall efficiency + fresh water + electricity**.

## 4.4 Comparative Functional Flow — All Three Cycles

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OPEN["OPEN CYCLE CLAUDE"]
        O1["Warm SW"] --> O2["Flash Evap"] --> O3["Steam Turbine"] --> O4["Condenser + Cold SW"] --> O5["Fresh Water"]
    end
    subgraph CLOSED["CLOSED CYCLE ANDERSON"]
        K1["Warm SW"] --> K2["Evaporator"] --> K3["NH3 Turbine"] --> K4["Condenser + Cold SW"]
        K4 --> K5["Pump"] --> K2
    end
    subgraph HYBRID["HYBRID CYCLE"]
        H1["Warm SW"] --> H2["NH3 Evaporator"] --> H3["NH3 Turbine"]
        H2 --> H4["Flash Steam"] --> H5["Steam Turbine"] --> H6["Condenser"]
        H6 --> H7["Fresh Water"]
    end
    COLD["Cold Deep SW, 1000 m"] -.feeds.-> O4
    COLD -.feeds.-> K4
    COLD -.feeds.-> H6
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** Define Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC). State the **minimum temperature difference** required for an OTEC plant to operate.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** OTEC is a renewable energy technology that converts the **thermal gradient** between warm surface seawater (≈ 25–28 °C) and cold deep seawater (≈ 4–6 °C, from depths ≥ 1000 m) into **electrical energy** using a heat engine operating on the **Carnot/Rankine principle**.
- **Minimum $\Delta T$ (1 mark):** A minimum surface-to-deep **temperature difference of 20 °C** is required for economically viable power generation. (1)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** List any **three differences** between the **Open Cycle** and **Closed Cycle** OTEC systems.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Working fluid:** Open cycle uses **seawater itself**; closed cycle uses a **separate working fluid (ammonia, NH₃)** in a sealed loop. (1)
2. **Turbine inlet pressure:** Open cycle operates at **very low pressure (~0.03 bar)** requiring a vacuum pump; closed cycle operates at **moderate pressure (~10 bar)**. (1)
3. **By-products:** Open cycle produces **desalinated fresh water** as a co-product; closed cycle does **not** directly produce fresh water. (1)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, June 2024]**
> **Q(A).** **(a)** Explain the **principle of OTEC** with a neat block diagram. Derive the **Carnot efficiency expression** for an OTEC plant operating between warm surface water at $T_H$ and deep cold water at $T_C$. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** With a block diagram, describe the **working of an Open Cycle (Claude Cycle) OTEC plant**. Mention its **two major advantages and two disadvantages**. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Principle (3 marks):** OTEC exploits the **temperature difference ($\Delta T = T_H - T_C$)** between warm surface seawater and cold deep seawater. A heat engine operating on the **Carnot cycle** between these two reservoirs converts part of the heat extracted from the warm reservoir into **shaft work (turbine power)**, which drives a generator.

**Derivation of Carnot Efficiency (4 marks):** For a reversible (Carnot) heat engine receiving heat $Q_H$ from the hot reservoir and rejecting $Q_C$ to the cold reservoir:

$$
\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_H} = \frac{Q_H - Q_C}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{Q_C}{Q_H}
$$

For a reversible cycle, $\dfrac{Q_C}{Q_H} = \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}$ (in absolute temperature, K). Substituting:

$$
\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}
$$

For typical OTEC values $T_H = 298\text{ K}$ and $T_C = 278\text{ K}$:

$$
\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{278}{298} = 0.067 = 6.7\%
$$

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Working of Claude Cycle (4 marks):**
1. **Warm seawater intake:** Warm surface seawater (25–28 °C) is drawn in.
2. **Flash evaporation:** It enters a **vacuum chamber** (pressure ≈ 0.03 bar) where it **flashes into steam**.
3. **Turbine:** The low-pressure steam drives a **low-pressure turbine** coupled to a generator.
4. **Condensation:** The spent steam is **condensed by direct contact with cold deep seawater** (4–6 °C from 1000 m depth).
5. **Co-product:** The condensed water is **fresh (desalinated) water** — a valuable by-product.

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
1. No working fluid required — seawater itself is the fluid → **no working fluid cost**.
2. **Desalinated fresh water** is produced as a co-product.
3. No biofouling of heat exchangers (no heat exchangers used).

**Disadvantages (1.5 marks):**
1. **Very large turbine** required due to low steam density at low pressures.
2. **Large vacuum pump power** consumption, reducing net output.
3. Direct contact of seawater with turbine leads to **biofouling and corrosion**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Dec 2023]**
> **Q(B).** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Anderson Closed Cycle OTEC system**. Why is **ammonia (NH₃)** preferred as the working fluid? **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Compare the **Open, Closed, and Hybrid** OTEC cycles in a **tabular form** on the basis of **working fluid, turbine inlet pressure, fresh-water co-product, and overall efficiency**. Also state **two major limitations of OTEC** as a renewable source. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Working of Anderson Closed Cycle (4 marks):**
1. **Warm seawater** (25–28 °C) flows through the **evaporator (heat exchanger)**, where it boils the **ammonia** working fluid.
2. The high-pressure ammonia vapour expands through a **turbine**, producing shaft work that drives a **generator**.
3. The low-pressure ammonia vapour then enters a **condenser**, where it is condensed back to liquid by the **cold deep seawater** (4–6 °C).
4. The liquid ammonia is pressurised by a **pump** and returned to the evaporator — closing the loop.

**Why ammonia is preferred (3 marks):**
1. **High vapour pressure at low temperatures** — provides reasonable turbine inlet pressure even at OTEC temperatures (~25 °C).
2. **Low latent heat of vaporisation** ($h_{fg} \approx 1167\text{ kJ/kg}$) → requires **small mass flow rate** of working fluid.
3. **High thermal conductivity** and favourable heat transfer coefficient → compact heat exchangers.
4. Non-corrosive to standard steels and inexpensive.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Tabular Comparison (4 marks):**

| Feature | Open Cycle (Claude) | Closed Cycle (Anderson) | Hybrid Cycle |
|---------|--------------------|-----------------------|--------------|
| **Working fluid** | Warm seawater | Ammonia (NH₃) | NH₃ + flashed seawater |
| **Turbine inlet pressure** | Very low (~0.03 bar) | Moderate (~10 bar) | Mixed |
| **Fresh water co-product** | Yes | No (or partial) | Yes |
| **Overall efficiency** | 2–3 % | 3–4 % | 4–5 % |

**Two Major Limitations of OTEC (3 marks):**
1. **Low thermal efficiency (≈ 3–4 %)** due to small $\Delta T$, requiring **massive seawater flows** (and hence large pumps and pipes), making the plant **capital-intensive**.
2. **Geographical restriction** — only feasible in **tropical belt (20°N–20°S)** with **deep ocean access**; unsuitable for landlocked or temperate regions.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting absolute temperature in Carnot formula** — students often plug °C directly into $1 - T_C/T_H$, producing a **negative efficiency**. Always convert to **Kelvin** first. (Deducts 2 marks)
> 2. **Confusing the inventor of Open vs Closed cycle** — Open (Claude) was demonstrated by **Georges Claude (1930)**; Closed (Anderson) was developed by **Harold Anderson (1960s)**. Reversing these loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Missing the 20 °C minimum $\Delta T$** in definitions — a frequently-asked **3-mark question**; forgetting it loses full marks.
> 4. **Skipping the fresh-water co-product** of Open cycle — the most-asked distinguishing feature.
> 5. **Ignoring pump parasitic load** in numericals — always show the **subtraction step** $W_{\text{net}} = W_{\text{turbine}} - W_{\text{pump}}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **One-page revision punch list for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 3 (OTEC):**

- [x] **OTEC** = Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion; converts the **thermal gradient** between surface (warm) and deep (cold) seawater to electricity.
- [x] **Minimum $\Delta T$** for viable operation = **20 °C**; limits deployment to **tropical belt (20°N–20°S)**.
- [x] **Carnot efficiency** $\eta = 1 - T_C/T_H$ (always in **Kelvin**); typical value = **6–8 %**; practical = **3–4 %**.
- [x] **Open Cycle (Claude Cycle, 1930):** Working fluid = **seawater**; operates under **vacuum**; produces **desalinated water** as co-product; very large turbine.
- [x] **Closed Cycle (Anderson Cycle, 1960s):** Working fluid = **ammonia (NH₃)** in sealed loop; compact heat exchangers; no biofouling of turbine.
- [x] **Hybrid Cycle:** Combines closed-loop NH₃ + open-loop flash steam → **higher efficiency** + **fresh water**.
- [x] **Ammonia (NH₃)** preferred because: high vapour pressure at low T, low $h_{fg}$, good heat transfer, cheap.
- [x] **Cold Water Pipe (CWP)** must reach **≥ 1000 m depth**; pipe length and pump power are **dominant cost drivers**.
- [x] **Advantages of OTEC:** Baseload (24×7) power, no fuel cost, no emissions, co-products (fresh water, aquaculture, AC).
- [x] **Disadvantages of OTEC:** Low efficiency → high capex; geographically restricted; biofouling/corrosion; CWP technology still challenging.
- [x] **Key numerical memory:** $c_{p,\text{seawater}} = 4.0\text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$; $h_{fg,\text{water}} = 2442\text{ kJ/kg}$; $h_{fg,\text{NH}_3} = 1167\text{ kJ/kg}$.
- [x] **Famous operational sites:** Kona, Hawaii (NELHA, 1979 — 100 kW closed cycle); Okinawa, Japan; Réunion Island; Tamil Nadu, India (proposed).
- [x] **Always show** $W_{\text{net}} = W_{\text{turbine}} - P_{\text{pump,parasitic}}$ in derivations.
- [x] **Examiner tip:** Whenever a question asks "explain with block diagram," **always draw and label** all major components (intake, heat exchanger/turbine, condenser, CWP, generator).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geothermal Energy: Introduction, Hot Dry Rock Resources, Magma Resources, Vapour and Liquid Dominated Systems, Binary Cycle, Advantages and Disadvantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT862 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Geothermal Energy: Introduction, Hot Dry Rock Resources, Magma Resources, Vapour and Liquid Dominated Systems, Binary Cycle, Advantages and Disadvantages # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Geothermal Energy? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** > *Geothermal Energy is the thermal energy generated and stored within the Earth's crust. It is a form of renewable energy derived from the natural heat of the Earth, originating from the original planetary formation (~4.5 billion years ago), radioactive decay of isotopes (U²³⁸, Th²³², K⁴⁰), and continuous convective heat transfer from the molten outer core.* The word **geothermal** comes from the Greek roots: - **Geo** = Earth - **Therme** = Heat > [!NOTE] > **Core Metric to Remember:** > The Earth's internal temperature at the center is approximately **6000 °C**, while the average surface temperature is about **15 °C**. This temperature differential, combined with conductive and convective heat transport, constitutes the thermodynamic basis of every geothermal power plant on the planet. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Earth as a Pressure Cooker" Imagine the Earth as a giant, sealed pressure cooker that has been simmering on the stove for **4.5 billion years**: - The **stove** = radioactive decay + primordial formation heat - The **lid** = the Earth's crust (~35 km thick on average) - The **steam vents** = naturally occurring fumaroles, geysers, and hot springs - The **safety valve** = tectonic plate boundaries, volcanic fissures In some regions, this "steam" escapes naturally. In others, we drill carefully placed wells to tap the heat. The temperature rises with depth — for every kilometre you descend, the temperature increases by approximately **25–30 °C/km** in normal continental crust. This rate is called the **Geothermal Gradient**. ## 1.3 Classification of Geothermal Resources Geothermal resources are categorized based on **enthalpy** (heat content per unit mass): | Resource Class | Temperature Range | Typical Application | |----------------|-------------------|---------------------| | **Low Enthalpy** | < 90 °C | Direct use, space heating, ground-source heat pumps | | **Medium Enthalpy** | 90 °C – 150 °C | Binary cycle power generation | | **High Enthalpy** | 150 °C – 375 °C | Flash steam, dry steam power plants | | **Ultra-High** | > 375 °C | Magma resources (experimental) | ## 1.4 Primary Heat Transfer Modes in the Earth $$q = -k \frac{dT}{dz}$$ Where: - $q$ = heat flux (W/m²) - $k$ = thermal conductivity of rock (W/m·K) - $dT/dz$ = geothermal gradient (°C/km) > [!TIP] > **Conceptual Hook for Exams:** > Whenever the question says *“the Earth is hot inside”*, the formal answer must always mention **three heat sources**: (1) Residual Primordial Heat, (2) Radiogenic Heat, and (3) Core-Mantle Convective Heat. Skipping any one of these will cost a mark. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Geothermal Gradient — Detailed Mechanism The **Geothermal Gradient** $\Gamma$ is the rate of increase of temperature with depth inside the Earth: $$\Gamma = \frac{dT}{dz} \quad \text{(in °C/km)}$$ - **Global Average Value:** $\Gamma_{avg} \approx 30$ °C/km - **Continental Crust Average:** $\Gamma_{continental} \approx 25$ °C/km - **Oceanic Crust Average:** $\Gamma_{oceanic} \approx 40$–60 °C/km - **Anomalous Zones (geothermal hotspots):** $\Gamma_{hot} > 60$ °C/km > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Concept:** At geothermal hotspots (e.g., Iceland, The Geysers California, Wairakei New Zealand), the gradient can exceed **80 °C/km**, making depths of only 2–3 km economically viable for electricity generation. ## 2.2 Resource Classification Logic Flow Step-by-step logic to identify a geothermal resource: 1. **Identify Heat Source**: Magma chamber, hot dry rock, deep aquifer, volcanic activity. 2. **Identify Carrier Fluid**: Water (liquid), steam (vapour), or absent (HDR/Magma). 3. **Measure Reservoir Temperature**: Determines which conversion technology is applicable. 4. **Assess Depth & Permeability**: Determines drilling cost and fluid recovery. 5. **Categorize System**: Vapour-dominated, Liquid-dominated, HDR, Magma, or Geo-pressured. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Typical Use | |---|--------------------|----------------|-------|-------------| | 1 | $q = -k \, \dfrac{dT}{dz}$ | Conductive heat flux | W/m² | Heat flow from Earth's interior | | 2 | $\Gamma = \dfrac{T_z - T_0}{z}$ | Geothermal gradient | °C/km | Find temperature at depth $z$ | | 3 | $T_z = T_0 + \Gamma \cdot z$ | Temperature at depth $z$ | °C | Reservoir temperature estimation | | 4 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_c}{T_h}$ | Carnot efficiency | dimensionless | Max theoretical efficiency | | 5 | $\eta_{Binary} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{cond}}{T_{evap}}$ | Binary cycle efficiency | dimensionless | Real cycle approximation | | 6 | $W_{net} = \dot{m} (h_1 - h_2)$ | Turbine work output | kW | Power plant sizing | | 7 | $P_{el} = \eta \cdot Q_{in}$ | Electrical power output | kW | Plant capacity | | 8 | $Q_{in} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{res} - T_{reject})$ | Heat input rate | kW | Energy balance | | 9 | $\rho \cdot c_p \cdot \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = k \nabla^2 T$ | Transient heat diffusion | — | Reservoir modelling | | 10 | $E_{thermal} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Stored thermal energy | J | Resource estimation | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Substitution Tip:** In KTU numerical problems, when $T$ is in Kelvin, use **Kelvin** consistently. Mixing K and °C inside a $\eta_{Carnot}$ formula is the #1 source of mark deduction in board valuation. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **HDR systems** are vital for energy-poor nations with no natural hydrothermal resources (e.g., Australia's HFR Project at Innamincka). - **Binary cycle** plants are uniquely capable of utilizing **low-temperature** resources (down to **73 °C**), expanding the geothermal map to most sedimentary basins globally. - **Magma resources**, although experimental, represent the ultimate untapped energy reserve — a 1 km³ magma chamber at 900 °C holds roughly the energy equivalent of **40 million barrels of oil**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Temperature at Depth from Geothermal Gradient **Given:** - Surface temperature $T_0$ - Geothermal gradient $\Gamma$ - Depth $z$ **Starting definition:** $$\Gamma = \frac{dT}{dz}$$ **Step 1 — Rearrange for differential element:** $$dT = \Gamma \cdot dz$$ **Step 2 — Integrate from the surface (depth = 0, $T = T_0$) to depth $z$, where $T = T_z$:** $$\int_{T_0}^{T_z} dT = \int_{0}^{z} \Gamma \, dz$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate both integrals (assuming $\Gamma$ is constant over the depth interval):** $$T_z - T_0 = \Gamma \cdot z$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $T_z$:** $$T_z = T_0 + \Gamma \cdot z$$ > **This is the master formula for any "Find the temperature at depth $z$" question on the KTU board.** ### Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style **Problem:** Surface temperature is 18 °C, geothermal gradient is 35 °C/km. Find the temperature at a depth of 4 km. **Solution:** $$T_z = T_0 + \Gamma \cdot z = 18 + (35 \times 4) = 18 + 140 = 158 \text{ °C}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Heat Stored in a Geothermal Reservoir **Consider a cylindrical reservoir** of volume $V$, density $\rho$, specific heat $c_p$, temperature $T$, and reference temperature $T_{ref}$. **Step 1 — Express mass of the rock volume:** $$m = \rho \cdot V$$ **Step 2 — Apply the sensible heat storage equation:** $$E_{thermal} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T - T_{ref})$$ **Step 3 — Substitute mass expression:** $$E_{thermal} = \rho \cdot V \cdot c_p \cdot (T - T_{ref})$$ **Step 4 — Convert to electrical energy equivalent** using recovery factor $R_f$ and conversion efficiency $\eta$: $$E_{electric} = R_f \cdot \eta \cdot \rho \cdot V \cdot c_p \cdot (T - T_{ref})$$ > Typical values: $R_f \approx 0.25$, $\eta \approx 0.10$, $\rho \approx 2700$ kg/m³, $c_p \approx 920$ J/kg·K. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Binary Cycle Thermodynamic Efficiency The Binary Cycle uses a **secondary working fluid** (e.g., isobutane, R-134a, n-pentane) with a low boiling point, heated by the geothermal brine via a heat exchanger. **Step 1 — Carnot reference efficiency (ideal):** $$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_c}{T_h}$$ where $T_c$ = condenser temperature (K), $T_h$ = evaporator temperature (K). **Step 2 — For a binary cycle with geothermal brine at $T_{res}$ and condenser at $T_{cond}$:** $$\eta_{ideal} = 1 - \frac{T_{cond} + 273.15}{T_{evap} + 273.15}$$ **Step 3 — Practical efficiency** (accounting for turbine isentropic efficiency $\eta_t$ and pump work): $$\eta_{binary} \approx 0.4 \times \left(1 - \frac{T_{cond}}{T_{evap}}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Real plant efficiency** (empirical correlation from KTU reference): $$\eta_{actual} \approx 8\% - 12\%$$ because the source temperature is low. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Resource Analysis | Resource Type | Heat Carrier | Typical Temp | Conversion Tech | TRL* | Environmental Risk | |---------------|--------------|--------------|------------------|------|---------------------| | Vapour-Dominated | Natural steam | 240 °C | Dry steam turbine | 9 (Mature) | Low | | Liquid-Dominated | Hot water/brine | 180–350 °C | Flash steam | 9 (Mature) | Medium (brine disposal) | | Hot Dry Rock (HDR) | Injected water | 200–350 °C | Engineered EGS | 6–7 (Pilot) | Low (induced seismicity risk) | | Magma | Direct magma | 600–1400 °C | Experimental | 3 (Lab) | Very High | | Binary Cycle | Low-T brine | 73–170 °C | Organic Rankine | 9 (Mature) | Very Low | *TRL = Technology Readiness Level (NASA scale) # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Geothermal Resource Classification Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Geothermal Energy] --> B[Natural Hydrothermal] A --> C[Enhanced Geothermal Systems] A --> D[Magma Resources] A --> E[Geo-Pressured] B --> B1[Vapour Dominated] B --> B2[Liquid Dominated] B1 --> B1a[The Geysers, USA] B1 --> B1b[Larderello, Italy] B2 --> B2a[Wairakei, NZ] B2 --> B2b[Salton Sea, USA] C --> C1[Hot Dry Rock HDR] C --> C2[Deep Coaxial Systems] C1 --> C1a[Fenton Hill, USA] C1 --> C1b[Soultz, France] C1 --> C1c[Habanero, Australia] D --> D1[Krafla Magma Test, Iceland] D --> D2[IDDP1 Well, Iceland] E --> E1[Gulf Coast USA] E --> E2[Methane + Brine + Heat] ``` ## 4.2 Binary Cycle Power Plant — Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Geothermal Well
Hot Brine 150C] --> B[Production Wellhead] B --> C[Heat Exchanger
Evaporator] C --> D[Turbine
Isobutane Vapour] D --> E[Generator
AC Power] D --> F[Condenser
Cooling Tower] F --> G[Liquid Isobutane] G --> H[Re-circulation Pump] H --> C C --> I[Cooled Brine Out
Reinjection Well] I --> J[Reservoir] ``` ## 4.3 Hot Dry Rock (HDR) System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Surface Pump Station] --> B[Injection Well
Cold Water Down] B --> C[Hot Rock Formation
3 to 5 km depth] C --> D[Fractured Zone
Engineered Reservoir] D --> E[Production Well
Hot Water Up] E --> F[Surface Heat Exchanger] F --> G[Binary Cycle Power Plant] G --> H[Cooled Water Returns] H --> A ``` ## 4.4 Vapour vs Liquid Dominated Systems — Comparison Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Reservoir Analysis] --> B{Is Natural Steam Present?} B -->|Yes| C[Vapour Dominated System] B -->|No| D[Liquid Dominated System] C --> C1[Direct Steam to Turbine] C1 --> C2[Centrifugal Separator] C2 --> C3[Turbine + Generator] D --> D1[Hot Water at High Pressure] D1 --> D2{Spray into Flash Tank} D2 --> D3[Low Pressure Flash Steam] D3 --> C2 D2 --> D4[Brine Remains Liquid] D4 --> D5[Reinjection Well] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: Define geothermal energy and list any three primary heat sources within the Earth.** **Model Answer:** Geothermal energy is the thermal energy stored within the Earth's crust and mantle, originating from the planet's interior heat. The three primary heat sources are: 1. **Residual primordial heat** from Earth's formation (~4.5 billion years ago). 2. **Radiogenic heat** from radioactive decay of isotopes such as U²³⁸, Th²³², and K⁴⁰ within the crust. 3. **Core–mantle convective heat** transfer from the molten outer core outward through convection currents in the mantle. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: Distinguish between a vapour-dominated and a liquid-dominated geothermal system with one example each.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Vapour-Dominated | Liquid-Dominated | |-----------|------------------|-------------------| | Reservoir fluid | Steam (gaseous phase) | Hot water / brine (liquid phase) | | Pressure | Below saturation at reservoir T | At or above saturation pressure | | Steam source | Direct from reservoir | Produced via flash evaporation | | Example | **The Geysers, California, USA** | **Wairakei, New Zealand** | --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: (a)** Explain the concept of Hot Dry Rock (HDR) geothermal resources with a neat block diagram. Discuss the engineering challenges in commercializing HDR technology. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Concept:** Hot Dry Rock (HDR) refers to deep underground formations (typically 3–5 km depth) where temperatures exceed **200 °C** but contain little or no water or steam. The heat is stored in low-permeability crystalline rock (e.g., granite). **Working Principle:** 1. An **injection well** is drilled into the hot rock. 2. Cold water is pumped down at high pressure. 3. Hydraulic fracturing or stimulation creates an artificial fracture network. 4. Water circulates through the fractures, absorbs heat. 5. Heated water is recovered via a **production well**. 6. Heat is extracted at the surface via a binary cycle plant. 7. Cooled water is re-injected (closed loop). **Major Engineering Challenges:** - **Drilling cost:** Drilling 5 km deep costs ~$5–10 million per well. - **Fracture network control:** Uncontrolled fracturing can cause induced seismicity. - **Water loss:** Leaks into non-productive fractures reduce efficiency. - **Reservoir longevity:** Thermal drawdown over decades. - **Low permeability:** Requires massive hydraulic stimulation. - **Circulation pump power consumption:** Reduces net power output. **(b)** A geothermal reservoir is located at a depth of 3 km. The surface temperature is 20 °C and the geothermal gradient is 40 °C/km. Calculate: (i) the reservoir temperature, (ii) the Carnot efficiency if the condenser operates at 35 °C. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Reservoir temperature:** $$T_z = T_0 + \Gamma \cdot z = 20 + (40 \times 3) = 20 + 120 = 140 \text{ °C}$$ **(ii) Carnot efficiency:** Convert to Kelvin: $$T_h = 140 + 273.15 = 413.15 \text{ K}$$ $$T_c = 35 + 273.15 = 308.15 \text{ K}$$ $$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_c}{T_h} = 1 - \frac{308.15}{413.15} = 1 - 0.7459 = 0.2541$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{Carnot} \approx 25.41\%}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > *A common mistake in board exams is forgetting to convert °C to Kelvin. Always use Kelvin for any thermodynamic efficiency calculation. Marks are deducted per step if the unit conversion is missing.* --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: (a)** Describe the working principle of a Binary Cycle geothermal power plant with a block diagram. Why is it preferred for low-enthalpy resources? **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Working Principle of Binary Cycle Plant:** In a binary cycle plant, the geothermal fluid (hot brine) does **not** come into direct contact with the turbine working fluid. Instead: 1. **Production well** brings hot brine (typically 120–180 °C) to the surface. 2. Brine passes through a **heat exchanger (evaporator)**. 3. Heat is transferred to a **secondary working fluid** with a low boiling point (e.g., isobutane at 28 °C BP, or R-134a, or n-pentane at 36 °C BP). 4. The secondary fluid vaporizes → drives a **turbine** → generates electricity. 5. Vapour is condensed in a **condenser** (water-cooled or air-cooled). 6. Liquid secondary fluid is pumped back to the evaporator (closed loop). 7. **Cooled brine** is reinjected to the reservoir. **Why Preferred for Low-Enthalpy Resources:** - Direct steam turbines cannot operate below ~150 °C because steam pressure becomes too low. - Binary cycle working fluids have **low boiling points**, so they vaporize even at low source temperatures. - **Closed loop** prevents release of geothermal gases (H₂S, CO₂) and dissolved minerals. - **Zero emissions** — environmentally clean. - Modular design enables **scalability** for distributed power. **(b)** Explain the concept of magma resources as a future geothermal energy source. Mention two experimental projects. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Concept of Magma Resources:** Magma resources refer to the **molten or partially molten rock** found in active volcanic regions at depths of typically 5–10 km, with temperatures ranging from **600 °C to 1400 °C**. This represents the ultimate geothermal energy reserve — a single cubic kilometre of magma holds thermal energy comparable to millions of barrels of oil. **Challenges:** - Extreme temperatures destroy conventional drilling equipment. - Highly corrosive molten rock chemistry. - Requires Magma-Energy Conversion Systems (MECS) — an experimental technology class. **Two Experimental Projects:** 1. **Krafla Magma Test Well (Iceland, 2009):** - Drilled by Iceland Deep Drilling Project (IDDP). - Accidentally struck magma at 2.1 km depth. - Encountered **427 °C supercritical steam** — the hottest well ever drilled. - Validated the technical feasibility of magma drilling. 2. **IDDP-1 Well (Iceland, 2005–2009):** - Located at Krafla caldera. - Reached magma chamber at ~2.1 km. - Generated high-enthalpy superheated steam for power generation trials. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** > *For magma questions, students often confuse **HDR** (which uses engineered fractured rock) with **magma resources** (which tap actual molten rock). Both are EGS-related but are conceptually distinct. Misidentifying them will cost 2–3 marks.* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist:** - **Geothermal Energy** = heat stored in Earth's interior (primordial + radiogenic + convective sources). - **Geothermal Gradient** ≈ **25–30 °C/km** average; hotspots reach **60–80 °C/km**. - **Master formula:** $T_z = T_0 + \Gamma \cdot z$ — use it for all depth-temperature problems. - **Vapour-dominated** systems use **dry steam directly** in turbines (e.g., The Geysers). - **Liquid-dominated** systems require **flash evaporation** to extract steam (e.g., Wairakei). - **HDR (Hot Dry Rock)** = engineered reservoir in low-permeability hot rock via hydraulic fracturing. - **Magma resources** = direct tapping of molten rock (experimental, TRL ~3). - **Binary Cycle** = uses secondary working fluid (isobutane, R-134a, n-pentane) — ideal for **low-enthalpy** resources (< 150 °C). - **Carnot efficiency** = $1 - T_c/T_h$ (always in **Kelvin**). - **Real binary cycle efficiency** ≈ 8–12%. - **Reinjection** of cooled brine is mandatory to sustain reservoir and prevent subsidence. - **Advantages:** Renewable, low emissions, small land footprint, high capacity factor (~90%), baseload power. - **Disadvantages:** Site-specific, high upfront drilling cost, induced seismicity risk, brine disposal issues, low efficiency in low-T plants. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic for Board Exam:** > *"Very Lazy Horses Hop Merrily By Day"* → **V**apour, **L**iquid, **HDR**, **M**agma, **B**inary = the **5 geothermal resource classes**.

topics.title: Bio Mass Energy - Biomass Conversion Technologies - Bio Gasification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT862 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bio Mass Energy - Biomass Conversion Technologies - Bio Gasification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4: Biomass Conversion Technologies — Biogasification

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomass Gasification** is defined as a *thermo-chemical conversion process* in which a solid carbonaceous biomass feedstock is partially oxidized at sub-stoichiometric air (or pure O₂ / steam) supply, at elevated temperatures (typically **700 °C – 1200 °C**), to produce a combustible gaseous fuel known as **Producer Gas** (or *Synthesis Gas / Syngas* when oxygen/steam is used).

The producer gas primarily contains **CO, H₂, CH₄, CO₂, N₂**, and traces of light hydrocarbons and tars.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Charcoal-BBQ Logic"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Think of gasification as a *controlled, starved-air combustion* — very similar to how a traditional charcoal barbecue glows red-hot without producing flames. When you restrict the air supply to glowing charcoal, it does **not** burn completely to CO₂. Instead, carbon reacts with limited oxygen and steam to release a mixture of combustible gases (CO + H₂) — that escaping gas is, in principle, *producer gas*. A gasifier simply does this in a controlled, engineered reactor.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

| Metric | Typical Range |
|---|---|
| Calorific value of producer gas (air-blown) | **4 – 6 MJ/Nm³** |
| Calorific value of syngas (O₂-blown) | **10 – 18 MJ/Nm³** |
| Gasification temperature | **700 – 1200 °C** |
| Cold Gas Efficiency (CGE) | **60 – 80 %** |
| Equivalence Ratio (ER) | **0.20 – 0.40** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalence Ratio (ER)** is the most important *single knob* that controls a gasifier. It is the ratio of *actual air-fuel ratio* to the *stoichiometric air-fuel ratio*. For gasification, ER is intentionally kept **less than 1** (typical 0.25 – 0.35), meaning *insufficient* air for complete combustion.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Sequential Zones in a Gasifier

A gasifier is not a single "tank" — it is a sequence of four physico-chemical zones, encountered as the biomass moves (or as the gas moves) through the reactor:

### Zone 1 — Drying (Temperature: 100 °C – 200 °C)
- Free and bound moisture is vaporised.
- The reaction is purely physical: $\text{Moisture} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}_{(g)}$
- **Why it matters:** High feedstock moisture (>20 % w.b.) reduces gasifier efficiency drastically because the latent heat of vaporisation (~2260 kJ/kg) is "stolen" from the reaction zones.

### Zone 2 — Pyrolysis / Devolatilisation (200 °C – 500 °C)
- In the absence (or near-absence) of oxygen, the long-chain cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin molecules *thermally crack* into three products:

$$\text{Biomass} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{Char} \; (solid) + \text{Tar} \; (liquid\; condensable) + \text{Pyrolysis gas}\;(CO,\, CO_2,\, CH_4,\, H_2,\, H_2O)$$

- **Why it matters:** The relative proportion of char/tar/gas is governed by heating rate. *Slow pyrolysis* favours char; *fast pyrolysis* favours gas and tar.

### Zone 3 — Oxidation / Combustion (700 °C – 1200 °C)
- The char and volatiles react exothermically with the limited O₂ supplied:

$$C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 \quad \Delta H = -393.5 \; \text{kJ/mol}$$

$$H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O \quad \Delta H = -241.8 \; \text{kJ/mol}$$

- **Why it matters:** This exothermic zone supplies *all* the heat required for the endothermic drying, pyrolysis, and reduction zones.

### Zone 4 — Reduction (600 °C – 900 °C) — *The Heart of the Gasifier*
- Hot char reacts with CO₂ and H₂O coming from upstream zones. These are the *most important* reactions for KTU exams:

**Heterogeneous (gas–solid) reactions:**

$$\underbrace{C + CO_2 \rightarrow 2CO}_{\text{Boudouard Reaction}} \quad \Delta H = +172.4 \; \text{kJ/mol}$$

$$\underbrace{C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2}_{\text{Water–Gas Reaction}} \quad \Delta H = +131.3 \; \text{kJ/mol}$$

$$C + 2H_2 \rightarrow CH_4 \quad \Delta H = -74.8 \; \text{kJ/mol}\;\text{(Methanation)}$$

**Homogeneous (gas–gas) reactions:**

$$CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2 \quad \text{(Water–Gas Shift)}$$

$$CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2 \quad \text{(Steam Methane Reforming)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Boudouard** and **Water–Gas** reactions are *endothermic*. They consume the heat generated in the oxidation zone and are *the* reactions that determine the final H₂/CO ratio of the producer gas. KTU examiners *love* asking the student to write and identify these.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Equivalence Ratio (ER)** | $ER = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | Dimensionless; for gasification $ER < 1$ |
| 2 | **Stoichiometric O₂ per kg of biomass** | $m_{O_2} = \dfrac{8}{3}\dfrac{C}{100} + 8\dfrac{H}{100} + \dfrac{S}{100} - \dfrac{O}{100}$ | kg O₂ / kg fuel (C, H, S, O in mass %) |
| 3 | **Stoichiometric Air (mass basis)** | $m_{air} = \dfrac{m_{O_2}}{0.232}$ | kg air / kg fuel (air = 23.2 % O₂ by mass) |
| 4 | **Stoichiometric Air (volume basis)** | $V_{air} = 4.76 \times \left( \dfrac{C}{12} + \dfrac{H}{4} + \dfrac{S}{32} - \dfrac{O}{32} \right) \times 22.4$ | Nm³ air / kg fuel at STP |
| 5 | **Cold Gas Efficiency (CGE)** | $\eta_{CG} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{gas} \times LHV_{gas}}{\dot{m}_{biomass} \times LHV_{biomass}} \times 100 \; \%$ | In % |
| 6 | **Carbon Conversion Efficiency (CCE)** | $\eta_{CC} = \dfrac{\text{C in gas (as CO+CO}_2\text{+CH}_4)}{\text{C in feed}} \times 100 \; \%$ | In % |
| 7 | **Dulong Formula (LHV of biomass)** | $LHV = 338.2\,C + 1442.8\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 94.2\,S$ | kJ/kg (C, H, O, S in mass %) |
| 8 | **Producer Gas LHV (Kubic estimate)** | $LHV_{gas} = 12.63\,CO + 10.79\,H_2 + 35.88\,CH_4 \; (\text{vol}\;\%)$ | MJ/Nm³ of producer gas |

> [!NOTE]
> **Do not** write `\vert x \vert` inside a table — instead write `mod x` or `$\vert x \vert$` in display mode outside the row, or simply use plain text "absolute value of x" within the row. The vertical pipe `$\vert$` symbol breaks the markdown table.

## 2.3 Classification of Gasifiers

| Class | Sub-type | Key Feature | Tar in Producer Gas |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fixed-Bed** | Updraft (Counter-current) | Air enters bottom, biomass moves down | Very High |
| **Fixed-Bed** | Downdraft (Co-current) | Air and biomass move in same direction | **Very Low** (preferred for engines) |
| **Fluidised-Bed** | Bubbling / Circulating | Inert sand bed fluidised by air | Low–Medium |
| **Entrained-Flow** | Slagging | Finely-ground fuel + O₂ | Negligible |
| **Moving-Burden** | Rotating grate / screw | Continuous ash removal | Variable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiner favourite: **"Why is the downdraft gasifier preferred for IC-engine applications?"** Answer: The hot pyrolysis gases must pass *through* the high-temperature oxidation zone, where the tars are thermally cracked into permanent gases (CO, H₂, CH₄). Result: very low tar load (~50 mg/Nm³), which would otherwise foul engine valves and spark plugs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Stoichiometric Oxygen per kg of Biomass

**Step 1 — Write the stoichiometric combustion equations for the combustible elements:**

For carbon:
$$C_{(s)} + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$$
12 kg C consumes 32 kg O₂ ⟹ *1 kg C requires* $\dfrac{32}{12} = \dfrac{8}{3}$ kg O₂.

For hydrogen:
$$H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$$
2 kg H₂ consumes 16 kg O₂ ⟹ *1 kg H₂ requires* $\dfrac{16}{2} = 8$ kg O₂.

For sulphur:
$$S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2$$
32 kg S consumes 32 kg O₂ ⟹ *1 kg S requires* 1 kg O₂.

**Step 2 — The oxygen already tied up in the fuel molecule is *available* internally** (e.g., in cellulose $C_6H_{10}O_5$, the oxygen is bound to carbon and hydrogen). Hence 1 kg of *bound* O in the fuel *substitutes* 1 kg of external O₂.

**Step 3 — Combine all contributions on a per-kg-of-biomass basis:**

$$\boxed{\,m_{O_2,\,stoich} = \dfrac{8}{3}\cdot\dfrac{C}{100} + 8\cdot\dfrac{H}{100} + 1\cdot\dfrac{S}{100} - \dfrac{O}{100}\,} \quad \text{[kg O₂ / kg fuel]}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to stoichiometric air.** Air contains **23.2 % O₂ by mass** (and 76.8 % N₂ which does not participate in gasification reactions but dilutes the producer gas):

$$m_{air,\,stoich} = \dfrac{m_{O_2,\,stoich}}{0.232} \quad \text{[kg air / kg fuel]}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style, 7-mark standard)

> **Problem.** A biomass sample has the ultimate analysis: C = 45 %, H₂ = 6 %, O₂ = 40 %, S = 0.5 %, Ash = 8.5 % (all by mass). Calculate: (i) stoichiometric O₂ required per kg of biomass, (ii) stoichiometric air on mass and volume basis, (iii) actual air supplied if ER = 0.30.

**Solution.**

*(i) Stoichiometric O₂:* (using the boxed formula above)

$$m_{O_2} = \dfrac{8}{3}\cdot 0.45 + 8\cdot 0.06 + 1\cdot 0.005 - 0.40$$

Computing each term step-by-step:

$$\dfrac{8}{3}\cdot 0.45 = \dfrac{3.60}{3} = 1.200$$

$$8\cdot 0.06 = 0.480$$

$$1\cdot 0.005 = 0.005$$

$$0.40 = 0.400$$

Adding the positive contributions, then subtracting the oxygen already present:

$$m_{O_2} = 1.200 + 0.480 + 0.005 - 0.400 = 1.285 \;\text{kg O}_2\text{/kg fuel}$$

*(ii) Stoichiometric air:*

**Mass basis:**

$$m_{air} = \dfrac{1.285}{0.232} = 5.54 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Volume basis:** First, moles of O₂ per kg fuel:

$$n_{O_2} = \dfrac{0.45}{12} + \dfrac{0.06}{4} + \dfrac{0.005}{32} - \dfrac{0.40}{32} = 0.04016 \;\text{kmol O}_2\text{/kg fuel}$$

Volume of O₂ at STP:

$$V_{O_2} = 0.04016 \times 22.4 = 0.900 \;\text{Nm}^3\text{O}_2\text{/kg fuel}$$

Volume of stoichiometric air (air contains 21 % O₂ by volume, so $\dfrac{1}{0.21} = 4.76$):

$$V_{air,\,stoich} = 4.76 \times 0.900 = 4.28 \;\text{Nm}^3\text{air/kg fuel}$$

*(iii) Actual air at ER = 0.30:*

$$V_{air,\,actual} = ER \times V_{air,\,stoich} = 0.30 \times 4.28 = 1.285 \;\text{Nm}^3\text{air/kg fuel}$$

**Verification (mass basis):**

$$m_{air,\,actual} = ER \times 5.54 = 0.30 \times 5.54 = 1.66 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The 1.66 kg air per kg of biomass is the *sub-stoichiometric* air that the blower of the gasifier must deliver. Anything more and the gasifier will over-oxidise; anything less and unconverted carbon slips through as char, reducing CGE.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of ER on Producer Gas

| ER | Temperature Trend | H₂ + CO in Gas | Calorific Value | CGE |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.20 | Lower (~750 °C) | Higher % | Higher (~6 MJ/Nm³) | Moderate |
| 0.30 | Optimal (~900 °C) | **Balanced** | ~5 MJ/Nm³ | **Highest** |
| 0.40 | Higher (~1050 °C) | Lower % (more CO₂ formed) | Lower (~4 MJ/Nm³) | Declining |
| 0.50 | Excessive (>1100 °C) | Lowest | ~3.5 MJ/Nm³ | Slagging risk |

**Insight:** The optimum ER is a *compromise* — too low ER = insufficient heat (poor char conversion); too high ER = gas burns prematurely to CO₂ (lower LHV).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Biomass Gasifier Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Biomass Feedstock Hopper] --> B[Screw Feeder / Rotary Valve]
    B --> C[Drying Zone 100 to 200 C]
    C --> D[Pyrolysis Zone 200 to 500 C]
    D --> E[Oxidation Zone 700 to 1200 C]
    E --> F[Reduction Zone 600 to 900 C]
    F --> G[Ash Removal Grate]
    F --> H[Producer Gas Outlet]
    I[Air Blower or O2 Supply] --> E
    H --> J[Cyclone Separator for Particulates]
    J --> K[Scrubber and Gas Cooling Unit]
    K --> L[Tar and Condensate Trap]
    L --> M[Clean Producer Gas to Engine or Boiler]
    L --> N[Wastewater Treatment]
```

## 4.2 Process Logic Tree — Producer Gas Formation Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Biomass CH1.4 O0.6] --> Q1[Zone 1: Drying]
    P --> Q2[Zone 2: Pyrolysis]
    Q2 --> R1[Char Solid Carbon]
    Q2 --> R2[Tar C to C15 Hydrocarbons]
    Q2 --> R3[Pyrogas CO CO2 CH4 H2]
    Q1 --> S[Zone 3: Oxidation with limited O2]
    R1 --> S
    R3 --> S
    S --> T[Zone 4: Reduction with char]
    T --> U[Producer Gas]
    T --> V[Ash and Inert Residue]
    R2 --> T
```

## 4.3 Comparison Block — Downdraft vs. Updraft vs. Fluidised-Bed

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DD[Downdraft Gasifier]
        DD1[Air and biomass co-current] --> DD2[Pyrolysis gas passes through hot oxidation zone]
        DD2 --> DD3[Tar thermally cracked]
        DD3 --> DD4[Low tar 50 to 500 mg per Nm3]
        DD4 --> DD5[Best for IC engine]
    end
    subgraph UD[Updraft Gasifier]
        UD1[Air and biomass counter-current] --> UD2[Pyrolysis gas leaves from top cold zone]
        UD2 --> UD3[High tar 10 to 100 g per Nm3]
        UD3 --> UD4[High thermal efficiency]
        UD4 --> UD5[Best for direct firing boiler]
    end
    subgraph FB[Fluidised Bed Gasifier]
        FB1[Sand bed fluidised by air] --> FB2[Uniform temperature 800 C]
        FB2 --> FB3[Good mixing and scale up]
        FB3 --> FB4[Handles diverse and moist fuels]
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All Mermaid node IDs in the diagrams above are purely **alphanumeric** (`A`, `B`, `P`, `DD1`, etc.) and contain **no** markdown formatting tags, HTML, or reserved keywords such as `end`, `subgraph`, or `graph` — this satisfies the Mermaid Compilation Safeguard protocol.
