# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define biomass gasification. How is it different from combustion? `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Biomass gasification is a thermo-chemical conversion process in which solid biomass is converted into a combustible gas (producer gas) by partial oxidation at sub-stoichiometric air supply (ER < 1) and at temperatures of 700 – 1200 °C.

**Key differences from combustion:**

| Aspect | Combustion | Gasification |
|---|---|---|
| Air supply | Stoichiometric / excess (ER ≥ 1) | Sub-stoichiometric (ER = 0.2 – 0.4) |
| Product | Heat, CO₂, H₂O (flue gas) | Producer gas (CO + H₂ + CH₄) |
| Goal | Direct heat generation | Gaseous fuel for downstream use |
| O₂ in product | Negligible | Trace (unconverted) |

### Q2. What is Equivalence Ratio (ER)? State its typical range for gasification. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Equivalence Ratio is the ratio of the actual air–fuel ratio to the stoichiometric air–fuel ratio:

$$ER = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$$

- For *complete combustion*: ER ≥ 1.
- For *gasification*: ER is maintained in the range **0.20 – 0.40** (commonly ~0.30).
- Lower ER produces gas of *higher calorific value* but at the cost of incomplete carbon conversion; higher ER burns the producer gas prematurely, reducing LHV.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### ❖ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a downdraft gasifier. Why is it preferred for IC-engine applications? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A downdraft gasifier has **four co-current zones** arranged vertically: drying, pyrolysis, oxidation, and reduction. Biomass is fed from the top; air is admitted at the oxidation zone through a tuyere. Producer gas exits from the bottom.

**Step-by-step flow:**

1. *Drying Zone:* Moisture is driven off at 100 – 200 °C.
2. *Pyrolysis Zone:* Volatiles and char are released at 200 – 500 °C.
3. *Oxidation Zone:* Limited O₂ burns part of the char at 1000 – 1200 °C, releasing the heat needed for other zones.
4. *Reduction Zone:* Hot char reacts with CO₂ and H₂O (from the pyrolysis products) to form CO + H₂ at 600 – 900 °C. The producer gas is then drawn out from below the grate.

**Why preferred for IC engines:**

- The hot pyrolysis vapours must pass *through* the high-temperature oxidation zone, where they are thermally cracked to permanent gases. This gives a **very low tar content (~50 – 500 mg/Nm³)** — critical because tars foul spark plugs and engine valves.
- Producer gas exits the reactor warm and relatively clean of condensable hydrocarbons.
- Stable operation across a wide range of feedstock moisture (≤ 20 %).

**(b) A biomass has the following ultimate analysis: C = 42 %, H = 5.5 %, O = 38 %, S = 0.5 %, Ash = 14 %. Calculate: (i) stoichiometric O₂ required per kg of fuel, (ii) stoichiometric air in Nm³/kg fuel, and (iii) the actual air at ER = 0.28. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Stoichiometric O₂:**

$$m_{O_2} = \dfrac{8}{3}\cdot 0.42 + 8\cdot 0.055 + 1\cdot 0.005 - 0.38$$

Computing each term:

$$\dfrac{8}{3}\cdot 0.42 = 1.120 \;\text{kg}$$

$$8\cdot 0.055 = 0.440 \;\text{kg}$$

$$1\cdot 0.005 = 0.005 \;\text{kg}$$

$$0.380 \;\text{kg (oxygen in fuel)}$$

Therefore:

$$m_{O_2} = 1.120 + 0.440 + 0.005 - 0.380 = 1.185 \;\text{kg O}_2\text{/kg fuel}$$

**(ii) Stoichiometric air (volume basis):**

Moles of O₂ per kg fuel:

$$n_{O_2} = \dfrac{0.42}{12} + \dfrac{0.055}{4} + \dfrac{0.005}{32} - \dfrac{0.38}{32}$$

Computing each fraction:

$$\dfrac{0.42}{12} = 0.03500$$

$$\dfrac{0.055}{4} = 0.01375$$

$$\dfrac{0.005}{32} = 0.000156$$

$$\dfrac{0.38}{32} = 0.011875$$

Adding positive contributions, then subtracting the O-in-fuel:

$$n_{O_2} = 0.03500 + 0.01375 + 0.000156 - 0.011875 = 0.03703 \;\text{kmol O}_2\text{/kg fuel}$$

Volume of O₂ at STP:

$$V_{O_2} = 0.03703 \times 22.4 = 0.829 \;\text{Nm}^3\text{O}_2\text{/kg fuel}$$

Stoichiometric air:

$$V_{air} = 4.76 \times 0.829 = 3.95 \;\text{Nm}^3\text{air/kg fuel}$$

**(iii) Actual air at ER = 0.28:**

$$V_{air,\,actual} = 0.28 \times 3.95 = 1.106 \;\text{Nm}^3\text{air/kg fuel}$$

---

### ❖ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a) Explain the principal chemical reactions occurring in the reduction zone of a biomass gasifier. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The reduction zone (600 – 900 °C) is *the heart* of a gasifier. The principal reactions are:

**Heterogeneous (gas–solid) reactions:**

1. **Boudouard Reaction:**
$$C + CO_2 \rightarrow 2CO \quad \Delta H = +172.4 \;\text{kJ/mol (endothermic)}$$

2. **Water–Gas Reaction:**
$$C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2 \quad \Delta H = +131.3 \;\text{kJ/mol (endothermic)}$$

3. **Methanation:**
$$C + 2H_2 \rightarrow CH_4 \quad \Delta H = -74.8 \;\text{kJ/mol (exothermic)}$$

**Homogeneous (gas–gas) reactions:**

4. **Water–Gas Shift:**
$$CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2 \quad \text{(mildly exothermic)}$$

5. **Steam Methane Reforming:**
$$CH_4 + H_2O \rightarrow CO + 3H_2 \quad \Delta H = +206 \;\text{kJ/mol}$$

**Significance:**
- Reactions (1) and (2) are the *principal* producers of CO and H₂.
- They are endothermic and consume the heat generated in the oxidation zone.
- The H₂/CO ratio in the producer gas can be *engineered* by controlling temperature, pressure, and steam-to-carbon ratio.
- Catalyst poisons such as tars, ammonia, and H₂S can be minimised by maintaining a high reduction-zone temperature (>800 °C).

**(b) Compare fixed-bed and fluidised-bed gasifiers. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Fixed-Bed (Downdraft) | Fluidised-Bed |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedstock size** | Uniform, 20 – 100 mm | Crushed, 0 – 6 mm |
| **Operating temperature** | 700 – 1200 °C (gradient) | Uniform 800 – 900 °C |
| **Tar in gas** | Low (50 – 500 mg/Nm³) | Low–Medium |
| **Pressure drop** | Low | Higher (due to bed support) |
| **Scale-up** | Limited (up to ~1 MW) | Easy (up to 25 MW commercial) |
| **Turndown ratio** | Poor | Excellent |
| **Carbon conversion** | 85 – 95 % | 90 – 98 % |
| **Feedstock flexibility** | Limited (wood, pellets) | High (wood, agro-residue, RDF) |
| **Ash handling** | Simple grate | Needs cyclone, ash cooler |
| **Capital cost per kW** | Low | Moderate to High |
| **Typical application** | Rural IC engines | Large-scale power, CHP |

**Key takeaway:** For *small-scale rural engine* applications, the **downdraft** gasifier is preferred due to low tar and simple construction. For *commercial-scale* plants processing diverse feedstocks, the **fluidised-bed** gasifier is superior because of uniform temperature, better gas–solid contact, and higher throughput.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes reported in KTU valuation camps:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract the bound O₂ in the fuel** when computing stoichiometric air. This is the *single largest* arithmetic error — it inflates the air requirement by 30 – 40 %. Always write down the formula with the "- O/100" term explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing mass % O₂ in air (23.2 %) with volume % O₂ in air (21 %).** Use 0.232 for mass-based air calculations and 4.76 (= 1/0.21) for volume-based calculations.
> 3. **Writing "ER > 1 for gasification."** This is *wrong*. Gasification ALWAYS operates at ER < 1; ER > 1 is combustion.
> 4. **Mixing up the Boudouard and Water–Gas reactions** — they are both endothermic but the stoichiometry differs: $C + CO_2 \rightarrow 2CO$ vs. $C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2$.
> 5. **Calling producer gas "biogas."** Biogas is the *anaerobic digestion* product (mainly CH₄ + CO₂). Producer gas is the *gasification* product (mainly CO + H₂ + N₂). Examiners will deduct marks.
> 6. **Reporting ash content as part of the gasification stoichiometry** — ash is inert and must be excluded from air calculations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **10-Point Final Checklist before the exam:**
> 1. ✅ Gasification = *sub-stoichiometric* oxidation at **700 – 1200 °C** with **ER = 0.20 – 0.40**.
> 2. ✅ Four zones: **Drying → Pyrolysis → Oxidation → Reduction** (in that order along biomass path).
> 3. ✅ Two *star* reactions in the reduction zone: **Boudouard** ($C + CO_2 \rightarrow 2CO$) and **Water–Gas** ($C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2$).
> 4. ✅ **Downdraft gasifier** = co-current = **low tar** = best for **IC engines**.
> 5. ✅ **Updraft gasifier** = counter-current = **high tar** = best for **direct-heat boilers**.
> 6. ✅ **Fluidised-bed gasifier** = uniform temperature + feedstock flexibility = best for **large-scale CHP**.
> 7. ✅ Stoichiometric O₂ formula: $\frac{8}{3}\frac{C}{100} + 8\frac{H}{100} + \frac{S}{100} - \frac{O}{100}$ (kg O₂/kg fuel).
> 8. ✅ Stoichiometric air (volume): multiply O₂-volume by **4.76**; (mass): divide O₂-mass by **0.232**.
> 9. ✅ Producer gas composition (air-blown): ~20 % CO, ~15 % H₂, ~3 % CH₄, ~10 % CO₂, ~52 % N₂; LHV ≈ 4 – 6 MJ/Nm³.
> 10. ✅ **Cold Gas Efficiency** = $\dfrac{m_{gas} \cdot LHV_{gas}}{m_{biomass} \cdot LHV_{biomass}} \times 100 \; \%$ — typical **60 – 80 %**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bio Ethanol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT862 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bio Ethanol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bioethanol** (also called **ethyl alcohol**, **grain alcohol**, or **bio-ethanol**) is a **biofuel** produced through the **fermentation of sugars, starches, or cellulosic biomass** derived from agricultural crops, forestry residues, or organic municipal waste. Chemically, it is represented by the molecular formula **C₂H₅OH** (ethanol, CH₃-CH₂-OH) and is produced via the **biochemical conversion route** using microorganisms (primarily *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* yeast) or via the **catalytic hydration of bio-ethylene**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Bioethanol is defined as **"ethanol produced from biomass feedstocks through fermentation, distillation, and dehydration processes, used as a transportation fuel either in pure form (E100) or blended with petrol (E5, E10, E20, E85)."**

## 1.2 Classification by Generation

| Generation | Feedstock Type | Examples | Lignocellulose |
|------------|---------------|----------|----------------|
| **1st Gen (Conventional)** | Sugar/Starch crops | Sugarcane, corn, wheat, beet | No |
| **2nd Gen (Advanced)** | Lignocellulosic biomass | Switchgrass, wood waste, agricultural residues | Yes |
| **3rd Gen (Algal)** | Algal biomass | Microalgae, macroalgae | Variable |
| **4th Gen** | Engineered organisms | Genetically modified algae/cyanobacteria | Yes |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Brewery for Fuel" Analogy:**
> Think of bioethanol production like a giant industrial brewery. Just as yeast converts the sugars in fruit/ grain into alcohol in beer or wine, industrial bioethanol plants use the **same microbial principle** but at massive scale — tonnes of biomass are fermented in huge stainless-steel tanks to produce **hydrous ethanol (≈ 95% v/v)**, which is then distilled and dehydrated into **anhydrous ethanol (99.5%+)**, the fuel-grade product. The "mash" is the biomass slurry, the "fermenter" is the giant tank, and the "distillation column" is the same technology used in whiskey production.

## 1.4 Key Physical & Chemical Properties (Engine-Grade Anhydrous Bioethanol)

| Property | Value | Unit |
|----------|-------|------|
| Molecular Formula | C₂H₅OH | — |
| Molecular Weight | **46.07** | g/mol |
| Density (at 20°C) | **0.789** | kg/L |
| Boiling Point | **78.37** | °C |
| Octane Number (RON) | **≈ 108** | — |
| Lower Heating Value (LHV) | **26.9** | MJ/kg |
| Higher Heating Value (HHV) | **29.7** | MJ/kg |
| Flash Point | **16.6** | °C |
| Auto-Ignition Temperature | **363** | °C |
| Stoichiometric AFR | **9.0** | kg air/kg fuel |
| Latent Heat of Vaporization | **0.904** | MJ/kg |
| Water Solubility | **Fully Miscible** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Octane Number Matters:** Petrol has a Research Octane Number (RON) of 91–98. Bioethanol's RON of ~108 means it **suppresses engine knock** in modern high-compression engines, allowing greater thermodynamic efficiency.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Production Pathways

### Pathway A: Sugar/Starch Fermentation (1st Generation)

This is the **dominant industrial route** used by Brazil (sugarcane) and the USA (corn).

**Operational Steps:**

1. **Feedstock Preparation** — Sugarcane is crushed for juice; corn is milled and liquefied.
2. **Saccharification** — Starch is hydrolyzed to fermentable sugars using enzymes (amylases).
3. **Fermentation** — *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* yeast converts hexose sugars to ethanol + CO₂ in anaerobic conditions.
4. **Distillation** — Multistage column separates ethanol (95.6% v/v azeotrope with water) from the wash.
5. **Dehydration** — Molecular sieves (zeolite 3Å) or azeotropic distillation removes residual water to achieve fuel grade (≥ 99.5%).
6. **Denaturation** — Small amount of petrol or denatonium benzoate is added to make it unfit for drinking.

### Pathway B: Cellulosic/Lignocellulosic (2nd Generation)

- **Pretreatment** (steam explosion, dilute acid, AFEX) to break down lignin.
- **Enzymatic Hydrolysis** (cellulase enzymes) to release C5 and C6 sugars.
- **Fermentation** of both pentoses and hexoses (using genetically modified yeasts like *Zymomonas mobilis*).
- **Distillation + Dehydration** — same as Pathway A.

### Pathway C: Catalytic Hydration of Bio-Ethylene

$$C_2H_4 \, (g) + H_2O \, (g) \xrightarrow{H_3PO_4 \text{ on } SiO_2, \, 300°C, \, 60-70 \text{ atm}} C_2H_5OH \, (g)$$

This **synthetic route** uses bio-ethylene derived from bioethanol dehydration.

## 2.2 Core Chemical Reaction: Fermentation Stoichiometry

For a hexose sugar (C₆H₁₂O₆), the **Gay-Lussac equation** governs fermentation:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Yeast, anaerobic}} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 + Heat$$

**Theoretical Mass Yield:**

$$Y_{ethanol} = \frac{2 \times M_{C_2H_5OH}}{M_{C_6H_{12}O_6}} = \frac{2 \times 46}{180} = 0.511 \text{ kg ethanol/kg glucose}$$

**In practice, the yield is 90–95% of theoretical** due to biomass formation (yeast growth) and by-products (glycerol, organic acids).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|--------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2C_2H_5OH + 2CO_2$ | Fermentation stoichiometry | — |
| 2 | $Y_{ethanol} = 0.511 \times \eta_{process}$ | Ethanol yield (η = 0.90–0.95) | kg/kg |
| 3 | $C_{2}H_{5}OH + 3O_{2} \rightarrow 2CO_{2} + 3H_{2}O$ | Combustion of ethanol | — |
| 4 | $LHV_{blend} = x \cdot LHV_{eth} + (1-x) \cdot LHV_{pet}$ | Heating value of E-x blend | MJ/kg |
| 5 | $\rho_{blend} = x \cdot \rho_{eth} + (1-x) \cdot \rho_{pet}$ | Density of E-x blend | kg/L |
| 6 | $\eta_{carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_{exhaust}}{T_{combustion}}$ | Otto cycle efficiency | — |
| 7 | $AFR_{stoich} = \frac{3 \times 32}{46} = 2.087$ | Stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (mass) | kg/kg |
| 8 | $E_{out} = m_{eth} \times LHV_{eth} \times \eta_{engine}$ | Useful engine work | MJ |
| 9 | $CO_2 \, reduction = 1 - \frac{ fossil \, CO_2}{bio \, CO_2 + fossil}$ | Well-to-wheel GHG reduction | fraction |
| 10 | $P_{ethanol} = V_{wash} \times ABV \times 0.789$ | Ethanol mass from wash | kg |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where the `|` symbol is used in formula descriptions (such as "magnitude" or "divides"), always render using `\vert` in LaTeX to keep markdown tables intact.**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Transportation Sector** — E10 (10% ethanol in petrol) is mandated in India (2018 NITI Aayog Ethanol Blending Programme target: **E20 by 2025**).
- **Power Generation** — Ethanol fuel cells (SOFC) and microturbines.
- **Chemical Feedstock** — For bio-ethylene, ethyl tertiary butyl ether (ETBE), and ethyl acetate.
- **Green Chemistry** — Solvent replacement in pharmaceutical and cosmetic industries.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Ethanol Yield from Glucose

We start with the balanced Gay-Lussac fermentation equation:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$$

**Step 1 — Identify Molecular Weights**

From the periodic table:
- Carbon (C) = 12 g/mol
- Hydrogen (H) = 1 g/mol
- Oxygen (O) = 16 g/mol

Therefore:
- $M_{C_6H_{12}O_6} = (6 \times 12) + (12 \times 1) + (6 \times 16) = 72 + 12 + 96 = 180 \text{ g/mol}$
- $M_{C_2H_5OH} = (2 \times 12) + (6 \times 1) + (1 \times 16) = 24 + 6 + 16 = 46 \text{ g/mol}$
- $M_{CO_2} = 12 + (2 \times 16) = 44 \text{ g/mol}$

**Step 2 — Mass Ratio from Stoichiometry**

From the balanced equation, **1 mole of glucose** produces **2 moles of ethanol**:

$$\text{Mass of ethanol} = 2 \times M_{C_2H_5OH} = 2 \times 46 = 92 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical Yield**

$$Y_{theoretical} = \frac{m_{ethanol}}{m_{glucose}} = \frac{92}{180} = 0.5111 \text{ kg ethanol/kg glucose}$$

**Step 4 — Practical Yield Correction**

Accounting for yeast metabolism, by-product formation, and distillation losses ($\eta_{process} = 0.90$):

$$Y_{practical} = 0.5111 \times 0.90 = 0.460 \text{ kg ethanol/kg glucose}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Content of an E10 Blend

**Given:** 1 Litre of E10 blend contains 10% v/v bioethanol + 90% v/v petrol.

**Step 1 — Densities**

- $\rho_{ethanol} = 0.789 \text{ kg/L}$
- $\rho_{petrol} = 0.745 \text{ kg/L}$ (typical)

**Step 2 — Mass Fractions in 1 Litre of Blend**

Volume of ethanol = $0.10 \times 1 = 0.10 \text{ L}$

Volume of petrol = $0.90 \times 1 = 0.90 \text{ L}$

Mass of ethanol = $0.10 \times 0.789 = 0.0789 \text{ kg}$

Mass of petrol = $0.90 \times 0.745 = 0.6705 \text{ kg}$

Total mass of blend = $0.0789 + 0.6705 = 0.7494 \text{ kg}$

**Step 3 — Lower Heating Values**

- $LHV_{ethanol} = 26.9 \text{ MJ/kg}$
- $LHV_{petrol} = 44.0 \text{ MJ/kg}$

**Step 4 — Weighted LHV of the Blend**

$$LHV_{blend} = \frac{(m_{eth} \times LHV_{eth}) + (m_{pet} \times LHV_{pet})}{m_{total}}$$

Substituting:

$$LHV_{blend} = \frac{(0.0789 \times 26.9) + (0.6705 \times 44.0)}{0.7494}$$

$$LHV_{blend} = \frac{2.1224 + 29.502}{0.7494} = \frac{31.624}{0.7494}$$

$$\boxed{LHV_{blend} \approx 42.21 \text{ MJ/kg}}$$

This represents a **~4% energy density reduction** vs pure petrol — a critical parameter for fuel economy.

## 3.3 Derivation: Mass Balance on a Fermenter

**Given:** 10,000 kg/day of glucose solution (15% w/w) fed to a continuous stirred-tank fermenter. Yeast converts glucose to ethanol with **90% of theoretical yield**.

**Step 1 — Glucose Input**

$$m_{glucose} = 10{,}000 \times 0.15 = 1500 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical Maximum Ethanol**

$$m_{ethanol,theo} = 1500 \times 0.5111 = 766.65 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 3 — Actual Ethanol Produced**

$$m_{ethanol,actual} = 766.65 \times 0.90 = 690.0 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 4 — CO₂ By-product**

From the stoichiometry, 180 g glucose produces 88 g CO₂. Therefore:

$$m_{CO_2} = 1500 \times \frac{88}{180} = 733.3 \text{ kg/day}$$

(assuming complete conversion of glucose; the actual CO₂ is slightly less due to biomass).

**Step 5 — Material Balance Check**

Inputs = Outputs + Accumulation

- Inputs: 10,000 kg (feed)
- Outputs: 10,000 - 690.0 (ethanol) - 733.3 (CO₂) ≈ 8576.7 kg/day (spent wash, water, residual sugars, yeast cells)

This is the **spent mash**, also called **stillage**, which is processed into **DDGS** (Dried Distillers Grains with Solubles) for animal feed.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Ethanol Generation Routes

| Parameter | 1st Gen (Sugarcane) | 1st Gen (Corn) | 2nd Gen (Cellulosic) |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------|----------------------|
| **Yield (L/ha)** | 6000–8000 | 3000–4000 | 4000–6000 |
| **Energy Ratio (Eout/Ein)** | 8–10 | 1.3–1.8 | 2–6 |
| **GHG Reduction vs Petrol** | 70–90% | 20–40% | 60–90% |
| **Feedstock Cost (% of total)** | 40–60% | 60–70% | 30–50% |
| **Water Footprint (L/L ethanol)** | 1500–3000 | 2500–5000 | 3000–8000 |
| **Land Use Concern** | High | Very High | Low–Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Bioethanol Production Process (1st Generation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Biomass Feedstock: Sugarcane/Corn/Sugar Beet] --> B[Feedstock Preparation: Crushing / Milling]
    B --> C[Hydrolysis / Saccharification: Starch to Sugar using Amylase]
    C --> D[Fermenter: Yeast Saccharomyces cerevisiae in Anaerobic Conditions]
    D --> E[Beer / Wash: 8-12% v/v Ethanol]
    E --> F[Distillation Column: Multistage to 95.6% Azeotrope]
    F --> G[Dehydration Unit: Molecular Sieves 3A Zeolite]
    G --> H[Anhydrous Ethanol: 99.5 percent v/v Fuel Grade]
    H --> I[Denaturation: Addition of Petrol 2-5 percent]
    I --> J[Final Product: Fuel Grade Bioethanol E100]
    D --> K[Carbon Dioxide By-product: Captured for Carbonated Drinks / Dry Ice]
    E --> L[Spent Mash / Stillage: Distillers Grains]
    L --> M[DDGS: Dried Distillers Grains with Solubles: Animal Feed]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Cellulosic Bioethanol (2nd Generation) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Pretreatment
        A1[Lignocellulosic Biomass: Switchgrass / Bagasse / Wood] --> A2[Mechanical Size Reduction]
        A2 --> A3[Steam Explosion / Dilute Acid Pretreatment at 180-220 C]
    end
    subgraph Hydrolysis
        A3 --> B1[Cellulase Enzyme Cocktail]
        B1 --> B2[C6 Glucose + C5 Xylose Sugar Hydrolysate]
    end
    subgraph Fermentation
        B2 --> C1[Co-Fermentation: Recombinant Yeast Zymomonas mobilis]
        C1 --> C2[Fermented Broth: 4-6 percent Ethanol]
    end
    subgraph Downstream
        C2 --> D1[Beer Column Distillation]
        D1 --> D2[Rectification Column]
        D2 --> D3[ Molecular Sieve Dehydration]
        D3 --> D4[Anhydrous Cellulosic Ethanol E100]
    end
    A3 --> E1[Lignin Residue: Combustion for Process Steam and Power]
    E1 --> F1[CHP: Combined Heat and Power]
    F1 --> C1
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Bioethanol as Transportation Fuel — Value Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Photosynthesis: CO2 + H2O + Sunlight] --> B[Biomass Growth: Sugarcane / Corn / Cellulose]
    B --> C[Harvesting and Logistics]
    C --> D[Bioethanol Plant: Fermentation + Distillation]
    D --> E[Distribution: Pipelines / Tankers / Rail]
    E --> F[Blending Terminal: E5 / E10 / E20 / E85]
    F --> G[Retail Fuel Station]
    G --> H[Internal Combustion Engine: SI or HCCI]
    H --> I[Combustion: CO2 + H2O + Energy]
    I --> J[Exhaust: Lower CO / NOx / Particulates]
    I --> A
    D --> K[By-products: CO2 to Industry, DDGS to Feed, Vinasse to Fertilizer]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** The carbon loop in Diagram 4.3 illustrates the **closed carbon cycle** of bioethanol — a primary reason it is classified as a **carbon-neutral fuel** under the Kyoto Protocol and EU Renewable Energy Directive (RED II).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]

**Define bioethanol. List any three physical properties that make it suitable as a transportation fuel.**

**Model Answer:**

Bioethanol is a **renewable liquid biofuel** with the molecular formula **C₂H₅OH**, produced by the **fermentation of sugars, starches, or cellulosic biomass** using yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) or via catalytic hydration of bio-ethylene.

Three physical properties suitable as a transportation fuel:
1. **High Octane Number (RON ≈ 108)** — allows higher compression ratios without knock, improving engine efficiency.
2. **High Heat of Vaporization (0.904 MJ/kg)** — provides **volumetric efficiency** and cools the intake charge, enabling higher power output.
3. **Miscibility with petrol** — allows it to be **blended in any ratio** (E5, E10, E20, E85, E100) without phase separation issues (above 0°C).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]

**Write the stoichiometric equation for the fermentation of glucose. Calculate the theoretical yield of ethanol.**

**Model Answer:**

**Balanced equation:**

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Yeast, anaerobic}} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 + \text{Heat}$$

**Theoretical Yield Calculation:**

Molecular weight of glucose = **180 g/mol**
Molecular weight of ethanol = **46 g/mol**

From stoichiometry: 1 mole glucose → 2 moles ethanol

$$Y_{ethanol} = \frac{2 \times 46}{180} = \frac{92}{180} = 0.511 \text{ kg ethanol/kg glucose}$$

**Theoretical yield = 0.511 kg ethanol per kg of glucose, or 51.1% by mass.**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Set A]

**Q. (a)** Explain the production of bioethanol from sugarcane in detail with a neat flow diagram. List the major by-products and their applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sugar mill produces 100 tonnes/day of sugarcane. The juice contains 15% w/w sucrose. After fermentation with 90% efficiency and distillation losses of 5%, calculate the volume of anhydrous bioethanol produced per day. Take the density of anhydrous ethanol as 0.789 kg/L. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1 — Sugarcane Crushing and Juice Extraction:**
Sugarcane is passed through roller mills, yielding **sucrose-rich juice (~15–17% w/w sucrose)** and bagasse (fibrous residue).

**Step 2 — Juice Treatment:**
The juice is filtered, lime-treated (Ca(OH)₂) to remove impurities, and pH-adjusted to 6.0–6.5.

**Step 3 — Fermentation:**
The clarified juice is cooled to 30°C and inoculated with **Saccharomyces cerevisiae** yeast in large fermenters. The reaction:

$$C_{12}H_{22}O_{11} + H_2O \rightarrow 2\,C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 4\,C_2H_5OH + 4\,CO_2$$

Fermentation is complete in **6–10 hours**, producing "wine" with **8–12% v/v ethanol**.

**Step 4 — Distillation:**
The wine is fed to a **beer column** (strips ethanol from wash) followed by a **rectification column** producing **95.6% v/v hydrous ethanol** (the ethanol-water azeotrope).

**Step 5 — Dehydration:**
Molecular sieves (3Å zeolite) selectively adsorb water, producing **anhydrous ethanol (≥ 99.5% v/v)** — the fuel-grade product.

**Step 6 — Denaturation and Storage:**
Small amounts of petrol (2–5%) or denatonium benzoate are added; the fuel is stored in sealed tanks to prevent moisture pickup.

**Major By-products and Applications:**
- **Bagasse** → burned in boilers for process steam and electricity (CHP)
- **CO₂** → captured for carbonated beverages, dry ice, or greenhouses
- **Vinasse (stillage)** → liquid fertilizer (rich in K and N)
- **Yeast cream** → animal feed supplement

**Flow Diagram:**

Sugarcane → Milling → Juice → Lime Treatment → Fermenter (30°C, yeast) → Beer → Distillation → Azeotrope (95.6%) → Molecular Sieve → Anhydrous Ethanol → Denaturation → E100

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given Data:**
- Sugarcane input: 100 tonnes/day = 100,000 kg/day
- Sucrose concentration in juice: 15% w/w
- Fermentation efficiency: 90%
- Distillation loss: 5%
- Density of ethanol: 0.789 kg/L

**Step 1 — Mass of Sucrose in Juice**

Assuming juice yield = 70% of cane (typical), mass of juice = 100,000 × 0.70 = 70,000 kg/day

But using direct sucrose mass given in problem (simpler approach):

Mass of sucrose = 100,000 × 0.15 = **15,000 kg/day**

**Step 2 — Theoretical Ethanol Yield from Sucrose**

Molecular weight of sucrose (C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁) = 342 g/mol

From stoichiometry: 1 mole sucrose → 4 moles ethanol

$$Y_{sucrose} = \frac{4 \times 46}{342} = 0.538 \text{ kg ethanol/kg sucrose}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical Ethanol Mass**

$$m_{ethanol,theo} = 15{,}000 \times 0.538 = 8{,}070 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 4 — Apply 90% Fermentation Efficiency**

$$m_{ethanol,fermented} = 8{,}070 \times 0.90 = 7{,}263 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 5 — Apply 5% Distillation Loss**

$$m_{ethanol,final} = 7{,}263 \times (1 - 0.05) = 7{,}263 \times 0.95 = 6{,}900 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 6 — Convert Mass to Volume**

$$V_{ethanol} = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{6{,}900}{0.789} = 8{,}745 \text{ L/day}$$

$$\boxed{V_{ethanol} \approx 8.745 \text{ kL/day} \text{ or } 8{,}745 \text{ litres/day}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Set B]

**Q. (a)** Compare 1st generation and 2nd generation bioethanol production processes in terms of feedstock, conversion technology, yield, and sustainability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An E20 fuel blend (20% v/v ethanol + 80% v/v petrol) is used in a vehicle. Calculate: (i) the mass fraction of each component in 1 litre of blend, and (ii) the lower heating value (LHV) of the blend. Given: ρ_ethanol = 0.789 kg/L, ρ_petrol = 0.745 kg/L, LHV_ethanol = 26.9 MJ/kg, LHV_petrol = 44.0 MJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

| Parameter | 1st Generation | 2nd Generation |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|
| **Feedstock** | Sugar/starch crops (sugarcane, corn, wheat) | Lignocellulosic biomass (bagasse, switchgrass, wood waste) |
| **Pre-treatment** | Simple crushing/milling | Steam explosion, dilute acid, AFEX |
| **Hydrolysis** | Simple acid/enzyme hydrolysis of starch | Costly enzymatic hydrolysis of cellulose + hemicellulose |
| **Sugars** | Mostly C6 glucose | Both C6 and C5 (xylose) — needs co-fermentation |
| **Technology** | Mature, commercial | Under development, demonstration plants |
| **Yield (L/ha)** | 3000–8000 | 4000–6000 |
| **Cost of feedstock** | High (competes with food) | Low (agricultural waste) |
| **Energy Ratio (Eout/Ein)** | 1.3–10 | 2–6 |
| **GHG reduction vs petrol** | 20–90% | 60–90% |
| **Food vs fuel debate** | Highly debated | Resolved (waste-based) |
| **Sustainability** | Questionable | High |

**Conclusion:** 2nd generation bioethanol addresses the **food vs fuel conflict** and offers better sustainability, but **2nd Gen is currently more expensive** due to enzymatic hydrolysis costs and pretreatment capital.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Volume of Each Component in 1 L of E20**

- Volume of ethanol = 0.20 × 1 = 0.20 L
- Volume of petrol = 0.80 × 1 = 0.80 L

**Step 2 — Mass of Each Component**

- Mass of ethanol = 0.20 × 0.789 = **0.1578 kg**
- Mass of petrol = 0.80 × 0.745 = **0.5960 kg**
- Total mass of blend = 0.1578 + 0.5960 = **0.7538 kg**

**Step 3 — Mass Fractions**

- $w_{ethanol} = 0.1578 / 0.7538 = 0.2094$ (or 20.94%)
- $w_{petrol} = 0.5960 / 0.7538 = 0.7906$ (or 79.06%)

**Step 4 — LHV of the Blend (Mass-Weighted)**

$$LHV_{blend} = w_{eth} \times LHV_{eth} + w_{pet} \times LHV_{pet}$$

$$LHV_{blend} = (0.2094 \times 26.9) + (0.7906 \times 44.0)$$

$$LHV_{blend} = 5.633 + 34.786 = 40.42 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

**Step 5 — LHV per Litre of Blend**

$$LHV_{volumetric} = LHV_{blend} \times \rho_{blend} = 40.42 \times 0.7538 = 30.47 \text{ MJ/L}$$

$$\boxed{LHV_{blend} = 40.42 \text{ MJ/kg} \text{ (mass basis)} \quad \text{or} \quad 30.47 \text{ MJ/L (volumetric basis)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark Losers):**
> 1. **Confusing volume % with mass %** — E20 is 20% *by volume* of ethanol, but mass fraction is ~21% because ethanol is denser. Use correct density conversion.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply both 90% fermentation efficiency AND distillation loss** separately in mass balance problems — these are multiplicative factors.
> 3. **Units inconsistency** — Always report LHV in **both MJ/kg and MJ/L**; examiners award bonus marks for completeness.
> 4. **Sucrose vs glucose stoichiometry** — Students often incorrectly use glucose M.W. (180) for sucrose (M.W. = 342). **Sucrose yields 4 moles ethanol, not 2**.
> 5. **Theoretical yield of 0.511** is for **glucose only**. For sucrose, the figure is **0.538 kg/kg**. This is a **1-mark difference** in 7-mark problems.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Bioethanol = C₂H₅OH**, produced by **fermentation of biomass** using yeast.
- [x] **Three generations:** 1st (sugar/starch), 2nd (cellulosic), 3rd/4th (algal/engineered).
- [x] **Key Reaction:** C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2 C₂H₅OH + 2 CO₂ (Gay-Lussac equation).
- [x] **Theoretical Yield:**
  - From glucose: **0.511 kg/kg** (51.1% mass)
  - From sucrose: **0.538 kg/kg** (53.8% mass)
- [x] **Practical yield** ≈ 90–95% of theoretical.
- [x] **Physical Properties:** RON ≈ 108, LHV = 26.9 MJ/kg, density 0.789 kg/L.
- [x] **Azeotrope** at 95.6% v/v with water — must be broken via **molecular sieve** (3Å zeolite).
- [x] **By-products of fermentation:** CO₂ (food/beverage), DDGS (animal feed), bagasse (CHP), vinasse (fertilizer).
- [x] **India Policy:** **E20 blending target by 2025** under the National Policy on Biofuels 2018.
- [x] **Carbon-neutral cycle:** CO₂ released in combustion is reabsorbed by next biomass crop → closed carbon loop.
- [x] **Major Producers:** Brazil (sugarcane) and USA (corn) — together produce ~85% of world bioethanol.
- [x] **Energy Ratio (Eout/Ein):** Sugarcane ethanol ≈ 8–10; corn ethanol ≈ 1.3–1.8; cellulosic ≈ 2–6.
- [x] **Advantages:** Octane booster, oxygenate (replaces MTBE), reduces CO, particulate, knock.
- [x] **Challenges:** Lower energy density vs petrol, hygroscopic nature, water contamination, food vs fuel debate.
- [x] **For KTU numerical problems:** Always state assumptions, show all intermediate steps, and include **units in every line**.
- [x] **The combustion reaction:** C₂H₅OH + 3O₂ → 2CO₂ + 3H₂O.
- [x] **For E-blend calculations:** Use **LHV_blend = Σ wᵢ × LHVᵢ** where wᵢ = mass fractions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bio Diesel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT862 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bio Diesel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🟢 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Biodiesel** is formally defined as a **mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids** derived from a renewable lipid feedstock such as vegetable oils, animal fats, or recycled cooking grease. The most common form is **Fatty Acid Methyl Ester (FAME)**, produced by reacting the triglyceride molecule with a short-chain alcohol (typically methanol) in the presence of a catalyst — a chemical transformation known as **transesterification**.

The simplified global reaction is:

$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{Catalyst}} 3\,\text{FAME} + \text{Glycerol}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Biodiesel is **NOT** raw vegetable oil pressed into a fuel tank. Raw vegetable oil has a viscosity roughly **10 to 15 times** higher than petrodiesel, which causes injector coking, incomplete combustion, and engine failure. Transesterification is the chemical "key" that unlocks diesel-like viscosity while preserving the renewable carbon backbone.

> [!NOTE]
> **ASTM Standard Reference:** ASTM D6751 (USA) and EN 14214 (Europe) are the two governing international standards that legally define what qualifies as "biodiesel" for commercial blending and resale.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Soap-Making Cousin" Intuition

Imagine you are making **soap at home** by boiling animal fat with lye (NaOH) and water. What actually happens at the molecular level is that the fat molecule is being **cut into smaller pieces**, with glycerol falling off and the remaining fatty acids grabbing onto the sodium from the lye. Biodiesel manufacturing is essentially the **same chemistry** — except we use methanol instead of water, and we keep the fatty portion (now as methyl ester) as our fuel rather than throwing it away as soap.

Think of the triglyceride as a **three-armed robot** holding three fatty acid "hands." Transesterification is like a methanol "scissor tool" that snips off each arm one by one, leaving three independent, lightweight biodiesel molecules and one discarded glycerol "core."

## 1.3 Key Physical & Chemical Constants (Bolded)

| Parameter | Biodiesel (FAME) | Petro-diesel |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular Weight (approx.) | **~292 g/mol** | ~200–250 g/mol |
| Density at 15 °C | **~880 kg/m³** | ~850 kg/m³ |
| Cetane Number | **45–65** | 40–55 |
| Oxygen Content (wt %) | **~10–12 %** | 0 % |
| Calorific Value | **~37 MJ/kg** | ~43 MJ/kg |

---

## 1.4 Historical & Geographical Context (KTU Board Context)

Biodiesel is **not new** — **Rudolf Diesel** himself demonstrated his original 1895 engine running on **peanut oil** at the World Exhibition in Paris. The modern biodiesel industry only truly scaled up after the 1970s oil crises and especially post-1990s, when the EU and the USA mandated blending targets to reduce dependence on fossil fuels.

In the Indian context, the **National Policy on Biofuels (2018)** targets **5 % biodiesel blending (B5)** by 2030, and Kuttanad (Kerala) and other states have active jatropha and waste cooking oil programs aligned with KTU's regional energy engineering context.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🟡 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Transesterification — The Heart of Biodiesel Chemistry

Transesterification (also called **alcoholysis**) is the displacement of the alcohol moiety of an ester by another alcohol. In the biodiesel context, the alcohol moiety of the triglyceride (the glycerol backbone) is replaced by methanol, producing FAME and free glycerol.

### Step-by-Step Logic of the Reaction Mechanism

1. **Deprotonation of methanol:** The base catalyst (e.g., NaOH or KOH) abstracts a proton from methanol, generating a **methoxide anion (CH₃O⁻)**, which is the actual reactive nucleophile.
2. **Nucleophilic attack:** The methoxide attacks the carbonyl carbon (C=O) of the triglyceride.
3. **Tetrahedral intermediate formation:** A temporary 4-bonded carbon intermediate is formed.
4. **Collapse of intermediate:** The alkoxide leaving group (diglyceride) departs, regenerating the C=O double bond, producing one FAME molecule and a diglyceride.
5. **Repetition:** Steps 2–4 repeat twice more on the diglyceride and monoglyceride intermediates, ultimately yielding **3 FAME molecules + 1 glycerol molecule**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Stoichiometric Insight:** Although the balanced equation suggests a **3:1 molar ratio** of methanol to oil, in industrial practice a **6:1 to 12:1 molar ratio** is used to drive the equilibrium forward (Le Chatelier's principle) and ensure complete conversion (> 98 %).

## 2.2 Feedstock Classification (Generational Approach)

| Generation | Source | Examples | Yield (L/ha) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1st Gen** | Edible vegetable oils | Soybean, sunflower, rapeseed, palm | 500–2,500 |
| **2nd Gen** | Non-edible / waste | Jatropha, Karanja, waste cooking oil | 600–2,000 |
| **3rd Gen** | Microalgae | Chlorella, Spirulina | 5,000–15,000 |
| **4th Gen** | Engineered / synthetic | Genetically modified organisms | Theoretical only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2018 Scheme & Beyond — Kerala Context:** Kerala's major biodiesel research focus is on **2nd generation feedstocks**, particularly **waste cooking oil (WCO)** and **jatropha curcas** grown on wastelands — avoiding the food-vs-fuel debate.

## 2.3 Production Process Flow (Industrial Overview)

A typical industrial biodiesel plant follows these stages:

1. **Feedstock Pretreatment** — Degumming (removing phospholipids with H₃PO₄), dewaxing, dehydration.
2. **Acid Esterification** — For high free fatty acid (FFA > 2 %) feedstocks, H₂SO₄ pretreatment converts FFAs to FAME first (otherwise soap forms).
3. **Base-Catalyzed Transesterification** — Main reaction at 60–65 °C with NaOH/KOH.
4. **Phase Separation** — Gravity settling separates biodiesel (top, lighter) from glycerol (bottom, denser).
5. **Purification** — Washing with warm water to remove residual catalyst and methanol; vacuum drying.
6. **Quality Testing** — Checks against ASTM D6751 / EN 14214 specs (cetane, viscosity, acid value, water content).

## 2.4 Catalysts Used in Transesterification

| Type | Example | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Homogeneous Base** | NaOH, KOH | Fast, low temp, cheap | Soap formation with FFA |
| **Homogeneous Acid** | H₂SO₄, HCl | Tolerates FFA | Slow, corrosive |
| **Heterogeneous** | CaO, MgO, zeolites | Reusable, clean separation | Slower kinetics |
| **Enzymatic** | Lipase (Candida, Pseudomonas) | Mild, no soap | Expensive, slow |
| **Supercritical** | None (uses sc-CO₂ + methanol) | No catalyst, no soap | High pressure (≥ 80 bar), energy intensive |

## 2.5 Comparison: Biodiesel vs Petro-diesel vs Raw Vegetable Oil

| Property | Petro-diesel | Raw Vegetable Oil | Biodiesel (FAME) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Viscosity (mm²/s @ 40 °C) | 2.0–4.5 | 30–50 | 3.5–5.0 |
| Flash Point (°C) | 55–65 | > 200 | 120–170 |
| Cetane Number | 40–55 | 35–45 | 45–65 |
| Sulfur Content | High (50 ppm) | None | None |
| Energy Density (MJ/kg) | ~43 | ~38 | ~37 |
| Biodegradability | Slow | Fast | **Very fast (98 % in 28 days)** |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Rule:** All vertical bars in formulas below use `\vert` (not `|`) to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n_{\text{oil}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{oil}}}{MW_{\text{oil}}}$ | Moles of triglyceride | mol |
| 2 | $V_{\text{MeOH}} = 3\,R \cdot \dfrac{MW_{\text{MeOH}}}{\rho_{\text{MeOH}}} \cdot n_{\text{oil}}$ | Volume of methanol required (R = molar ratio) | L |
| 3 | $m_{\text{cat}} = 0.005 \cdot m_{\text{oil}}$ to $0.01 \cdot m_{\text{oil}}$ | Catalyst mass (0.5–1 wt % of oil) | kg |
| 4 | $\eta_{\text{conv}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{FAME,actual}}}{m_{\text{FAME,theoretical}}} \times 100$ | Conversion efficiency | % |
| 5 | $\text{SV} = \dfrac{56.1 \cdot N_{\text{KOH}} \cdot V_{\text{KOH}}}{m_{\text{oil}}}$ | Saponification Value | mg KOH/g |
| 6 | $\text{IV} = \dfrac{126.9 \cdot N_{\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3} \cdot (V_{\text{blank}} - V_{\text{sample}})}{m_{\text{oil}}}$ | Iodine Value (unsaturation) | g I₂/100 g |
| 7 | $\text{AV} = \dfrac{56.1 \cdot N_{\text{KOH}} \cdot V_{\text{KOH}}}{m_{\text{sample}}}$ | Acid Value (FFA indicator) | mg KOH/g |
| 8 | $\rho_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{w_1}{\rho_1} + \dfrac{w_2}{\rho_2}}$ | Density of biodiesel blend | kg/m³ |
| 9 | $Y_{\text{FAME}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{FAME}}}{m_{\text{oil}}} \times 100$ | Mass yield of biodiesel | % |
| 10 | $\text{StdVol} = V_{15} \cdot \left[1 - \alpha \cdot (T - 15)\right]$ | Volume correction to 15 °C (α ≈ 0.00065 /°C for biodiesel) | L |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Transport Sector:** B20 blend is approved for most heavy-duty diesel engines without modification; B100 requires minor elastomer replacement.
- **Power Generation:** Standalone biodiesel gensets in rural Kerala (especially in Lakshadweep and Wayanad) for off-grid electrification.
- **Carbon Accounting:** Biodiesel lifecycle CO₂ is **~78 % lower** than petrodiesel (closed-loop CO₂ from atmospheric photosynthesis).
- **Lubricity Additive:** Even at 1–2 % blend, biodiesel drastically improves diesel engine lubricity — a major reason ULSD (Ultra-Low Sulfur Diesel) requires lubricity additives, while B2 does not.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔵 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Methanol Requirement for a Given Oil Batch

**Problem Statement:** Calculate the volume of methanol (density **792 kg/m³**, molecular weight **32.04 g/mol**) required to fully transesterify **1000 kg of soybean oil** (MW ≈ **885 g/mol**, FFA < 1 %) using a **6:1 molar ratio** and **1 wt % NaOH catalyst**.

### Step 1 — Determine Moles of Triglyceride

The mass of oil is $m_{\text{oil}} = 1000\,\text{kg} = 1{,}000{,}000\,\text{g}$.

$$
n_{\text{oil}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{oil}}}{MW_{\text{oil}}} = \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000\,\text{g}}{885\,\text{g/mol}}
$$

$$
n_{\text{oil}} = 1129.94\,\text{mol} \approx 1130\,\text{mol}
$$

*Logic:* Dividing mass by molecular weight gives the number of independent triglyceride molecules available to react.

### Step 2 — Determine Moles of Methanol Required

The molar ratio $R = 6$ means 6 mol of methanol per 1 mol of triglyceride (the stoichiometric 3:1 is doubled to push equilibrium forward).

$$
n_{\text{MeOH}} = R \times 3 \times n_{\text{oil}}
$$

$$
n_{\text{MeOH}} = 6 \times 1129.94 = 6779.6\,\text{mol}
$$

*Logic:* Each triglyceride has 3 ester linkages; we provide 6 mol methanol per triglyceride to ensure all 3 linkages are cleaved with surplus.

### Step 3 — Determine Mass of Methanol

$$
m_{\text{MeOH}} = n_{\text{MeOH}} \times MW_{\text{MeOH}}
$$

$$
m_{\text{MeOH}} = 6779.6 \times 32.04 = 217{,}219\,\text{g} \approx 217.22\,\text{kg}
$$

*Logic:* Multiply moles by molecular weight to convert to mass.

### Step 4 — Determine Volume of Methanol

$$
V_{\text{MeOH}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{MeOH}}}{\rho_{\text{MeOH}}} = \dfrac{217{,}220\,\text{g}}{0.792\,\text{g/cm}^3}
$$

$$
V_{\text{MeOH}} = 274{,}270\,\text{cm}^3 = 274.27\,\text{L}
$$

*Logic:* Volume = mass ÷ density; 1 cm³ = 1 mL.

### Step 5 — Determine Catalyst Mass

$$
m_{\text{NaOH}} = 0.01 \times m_{\text{oil}} = 0.01 \times 1000 = 10\,\text{kg}
$$

*Logic:* 1 wt % catalyst of oil mass is industry standard for clean, low-FFA feedstocks.

### ✅ Final Boxed Answer

$$
\boxed{V_{\text{MeOH}} \approx 274.3\,\text{L}, \quad m_{\text{NaOH}} = 10\,\text{kg}, \quad \text{Theoretical FAME} \approx 1005.5\,\text{kg}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Biodiesel Yield from a Real Batch

**Problem Statement:** 1000 kg of oil reacted as above yields 940 kg of purified FAME (after washing losses). Compute: (a) mass yield, (b) theoretical glycerol mass, (c) actual conversion efficiency.

### (a) Mass Yield of FAME

$$
Y_{\text{FAME}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{FAME,actual}}}{m_{\text{oil}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{940}{1000} \times 100 = 94\,\%
$$

*Logic:* Yield = product mass ÷ input oil mass; 94 % is excellent industrial performance (typically 90–97 %).

### (b) Theoretical Glycerol Mass

For every 1 mol triglyceride (885 g), 1 mol glycerol (92.09 g) is produced.

$$
m_{\text{gly,ideal}} = n_{\text{oil}} \times MW_{\text{glycerol}}
$$

$$
m_{\text{gly,ideal}} = 1129.94 \times 92.09 = 104{,}053\,\text{g} \approx 104.05\,\text{kg}
$$

*Logic:* One glycerol per triglyceride (the "core" leftover); multiplying moles by glycerol's MW gives mass.

### (c) Conversion Efficiency (Assuming 5 % FAME lost in wash water)

$$
\eta_{\text{conv}} = \dfrac{940 + 47}{(940 + 47 + 104.05 + 5)} \times 100 \approx 91.7\,\%
$$

*Logic:* Mass-balance closure — total inputs must equal total outputs + losses; efficiency = (FAME recovered ÷ theoretical FAME) × 100.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Blend Density (B20 Example)

**Problem Statement:** B20 = 20 % biodiesel (ρ_B = 880 kg/m³) + 80 % petro-diesel (ρ_D = 850 kg/m³). Compute blend density assuming volume additivity.

Mass fractions first (assume 100 L blend → 20 L biodiesel + 80 L diesel):

$$
m_B = 20 \times 0.880 = 17.6\,\text{kg}
$$

$$
m_D = 80 \times 0.850 = 68.0\,\text{kg}
$$

$$
\rho_{\text{B20}} = \dfrac{17.6 + 68.0}{100} = 855.6\,\text{kg/m}^3
$$

$$
\boxed{\rho_{\text{B20}} = 855.6\,\text{kg/m}^3}
$$

*Logic:* Sum of component masses divided by total volume gives the bulk blend density; this value must lie in ASTM D975 limits for diesel (~820–860 kg/m³).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Feedstocks Numerically

| Feedstock | MW (g/mol) | FFA (%) | SV (mg KOH/g) | IV (g I₂/100 g) | Suitable Catalyst |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Soybean Oil | ~885 | 0.5–1.5 | 188–195 | 120–140 | Base (NaOH) |
| Jatropha Oil | ~872 | 2.0–8.0 | 188–198 | 95–110 | Two-step (Acid + Base) |
| Waste Cooking Oil | ~870 | 5.0–15 | 180–195 | 90–130 | Acid pretreatment → Base |
| Pongamia (Karanja) | ~880 | 3.0–8.0 | 184–192 | 80–95 | Two-step |
| Tallow (Animal Fat) | ~850 | 1.0–5.0 | 192–200 | 40–60 | Base |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:** If FFA > 2 %, **always** perform acid esterification (H₂SO₄ + methanol) first, or you will form **soap (saponification)** instead of biodiesel — wasting your oil and fouling your catalyst.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Cetane Number Estimation (Forin Equation — Conceptual)

The cetane number (CN) is a measure of ignition quality. While ASTM D613 is the gold-standard test, a **simplified empirical correlation** (not lab-grade) using iodine value (IV) and saponification value (SV) is sometimes used:

$$
\text{CN}_{\text{est}} \approx 46.3 + \dfrac{5458}{\text{SV}} - 0.225 \cdot \text{IV}
$$

**Worked example (Soybean, SV = 190, IV = 130):**

$$
\text{CN}_{\text{est}} = 46.3 + \dfrac{5458}{190} - 0.225 \times 130
$$

$$
\text{CN}_{\text{est}} = 46.3 + 28.73 - 29.25 = 45.78
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{CN}_{\text{est}} \approx 45.8}
$$

*Logic:* Higher unsaturation (IV) lowers CN; longer/larger fatty acids (higher SV denominator effect) raise CN. This matches lab data for soy biodiesel.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🟣 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Biodiesel Production Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Feedstock Receiving] --> A1[Oil Storage Tank]
    A1 --> A2[Degumming with H3PO4]
    A2 --> A3[FFA Test Lab Check]
    A3 -->|FFA less than 2 percent| A4[Direct Base Transesterification Reactor 60 C NaOH Catalyst]
    A3 -->|FFA greater than 2 percent| A4b[Acid Esterification Step H2SO4 plus Methanol]
    A4b --> A4
    A4 --> A5[Gravity Settling Decanter 8 hours]
    A5 --> A6[Biodiesel Layer Top]
    A5 --> A7[Crude Glycerol Layer Bottom]
    A6 --> A8[Warm Water Washing 3 stages]
    A8 --> A9[Vacuum Drying 110 C 5 kPa]
    A9 --> A10[Quality Lab ASTM D6751 Tests]
    A10 -->|Pass| A11[Biodiesel Storage Tank]
    A10 -->|Fail| A12[Rework Refining]
    A11 --> A13[Blending Station B5 B20 B100]
    A13 --> A14[Dispatch to Pumps Gensets Vehicles]
    A7 --> A15[Glycerol Purification Vacuum Distillation]
    A15 --> A16[Pharma Grade Glycerol Sales]
```

**Reading Guide:** The flow follows feedstock → pretreatment → reaction → separation → purification → testing → dispatch. The decision diamond (FFA check) is the industrial fork between single-step and two-step processes.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Transesterification Reaction Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R0[Triglyceride plus 3 Methanol] --> R1[Step 1 Nucleophilic Attack]
    R1 --> R2[Tetrahedral Intermediate]
    R2 --> R3[1 FAME plus Diglyceride]
    R3 --> R4[Step 2 Attack on Diglyceride]
    R4 --> R5[1 FAME plus Monoglyceride]
    R5 --> R6[Step 3 Attack on Monoglyceride]
    R6 --> R7[1 FAME plus Glycerol]
    R7 --> R8[Total 3 FAME plus 1 Glycerol]
```

**Reading Guide:** Three sequential substitutions on the same glycerol backbone, each releasing one FAME molecule, until the glycerol "core" is fully liberated.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Feedstock Generations Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    G0[Biodiesel Feedstock Generations] --> G1[Gen 1 Edible Oils]
    G0 --> G2[Gen 2 Non Edible and Waste]
    G0 --> G3[Gen 3 Microalgae]
    G0 --> G4[Gen 4 Engineered Synthetic]
    G1 --> G1a[Soybean Sunflower Palm Rapeseed]
    G2 --> G2a[Jatropha Karanja Waste Cooking Oil Animal Tallow]
    G3 --> G3a[Chlorella Spirulina Nannochloropsis]
    G4 --> G4a[Genetically Modified Microbes Synthetic Biology Pathways]
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic — ASTM D6751 Critical Quality Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q0[Biodiesel Quality Control ASTM D6751] --> Q1[Physical Tests]
    Q0 --> Q2[Chemical Tests]
    Q0 --> Q3[Performance Tests]
    Q1 --> Q1a[Viscosity 1.9 to 6.0 cSt]
    Q1 --> Q1b[Density 860 to 900 kg per m3]
    Q1 --> Q1c[Flash Point greater than 130 C]
    Q2 --> Q2a[Acid Value less than 0.5 mg KOH per g]
    Q2 --> Q2b[Water Content less than 0.05 percent vol]
    Q2 --> Q2c[Cetane Number greater than 47]
    Q3 --> Q3a[Oxidation Stability greater than 3 hours]
    Q3 --> Q3b[Sulfated Ash less than 0.02 percent]
    Q3 --> Q3c[Free Glycerol less than 0.02 percent]
```

# 🔴 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model]
**Define biodiesel. Why is raw vegetable oil not directly used as a fuel in diesel engines?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Biodiesel is a mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids, typically produced by the **transesterification** of vegetable oils or animal fats with a short-chain alcohol like methanol, in the presence of a catalyst (1 mark). It is chemically defined as **Fatty Acid Methyl Ester (FAME)** per ASTM D6751 standards (1 mark).

Raw vegetable oil is not directly usable because its **kinematic viscosity (30–50 mm²/s)** is **10–15 times higher** than petrodiesel, causing poor atomization, incomplete combustion, carbon deposits in injectors, and eventual engine seizure (1 mark). Transesterification breaks the bulky triglyceride into three small FAME molecules, drastically reducing viscosity to the diesel-compatible range of **3.5–5.0 mm²/s**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model]
**List any four advantages of biodiesel over petrodiesel.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Renewable & Biodegradable** — Derived from vegetable oils/algal lipids; ~98 % biodegradable in 28 days (1 mark).
2. **Lower Emissions** — Reduces CO, HC, PM, and SOx; lifecycle CO₂ ~78 % lower (1 mark).
3. **Higher Cetane & Lubricity** — Improves engine ignition quality and protects fuel injection pumps, especially in ULSD blends (1 mark).

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, Adapted]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** With the help of a neat reaction scheme, explain the process of **transesterification** for biodiesel production. Discuss the role of the catalyst and the effect of the molar ratio of alcohol to oil.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** A batch of **500 kg jatropha oil** (MW = 870 g/mol, FFA = 4 %) is to be transesterified with **methanol** (MW = 32 g/mol, ρ = 792 kg/m³) using a **9:1 molar ratio** and **1.2 wt % KOH catalyst**. Determine: (i) volume of methanol needed, (ii) mass of KOH, (iii) theoretical mass of FAME produced (assume average FAME MW = 290 g/mol), and (iv) the mass of glycerol by-product (MW = 92 g/mol).

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

**Part (a) Solution — Transesterification Process (7 marks):**

**Reaction Scheme:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Triglyceride} &+ 3\,\text{CH}_3\text{OH} \xrightarrow{\text{KOH or NaOH, } 60\text{–}65\,°\text{C}} 3\,\text{FAME} + \text{Glycerol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mechanism Steps (3 marks for stepwise):**

1. **Catalyst + Methanol → Methoxide ion** — Base catalyst (KOH) dissociates and deprotonates methanol, generating the strongly nucleophilic **CH₃O⁻** ion.
2. **Nucleophilic Attack** — Methoxide attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of one of the triglyceride's three ester linkages.
3. **Tetrahedral Intermediate** — A 4-bonded carbon intermediate forms transiently.
4. **Collapse & Release** — The intermediate collapses, expelling the diglyceride alkoxide and forming one FAME molecule.
5. **Repeat × 2** — Same sequence occurs on diglyceride and monoglyceride intermediates, yielding total **3 FAME + 1 glycerol**.

**Role of Catalyst (2 marks):** The base catalyst (NaOH/KOH) accelerates the reaction by **orders of magnitude** (>1000×) by generating methoxide ions. Without catalyst, transesterification at 60 °C is negligibly slow. The catalyst does NOT appear in the stoichiometry — it is regenerated.

**Effect of Molar Ratio (2 marks):** Stoichiometric ratio is 3:1 (methanol:oil). Industrial practice uses **6:1 to 12:1** because excess methanol:
- Shifts equilibrium toward products (Le Chatelier's principle).
- Ensures > 98 % conversion within reasonable time (1–2 hours).
- Compensates for methanol losses to evaporation.

However, excess methanol (> 12:1) complicates glycerol separation and increases recovery costs.

---

**Part (b) Solution — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** m_oil = 500 kg, MW_oil = 870 g/mol, R = 9, MW_MeOH = 32 g/mol, ρ_MeOH = 792 kg/m³, MW_FAME = 290 g/mol, MW_glycerol = 92 g/mol.

**Step 1 — Moles of oil (1 mark):**

$$
n_{\text{oil}} = \dfrac{500{,}000\,\text{g}}{870\,\text{g/mol}} = 574.71\,\text{mol}
$$

**Step 2 — Total moles of methanol (1 mark):** With R = 9 (i.e., 9 mol methanol per 1 mol oil):

$$
n_{\text{MeOH}} = 9 \times 574.71 = 5172.4\,\text{mol}
$$

**Step 3 — Volume of methanol (1 mark):**

$$
V_{\text{MeOH}} = \dfrac{5172.4 \times 32}{792{,}000} = \dfrac{165{,}517}{792{,}000} = 0.2090\,\text{m}^3 = 209.0\,\text{L}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{\text{MeOH}} \approx 209\,\text{L}}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass of KOH catalyst (1 mark):**

$$
m_{\text{KOH}} = 0.012 \times 500 = 6.0\,\text{kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Theoretical mass of FAME (2 marks):** Each mole of triglyceride yields 3 mol FAME.

$$
n_{\text{FAME}} = 3 \times 574.71 = 1724.1\,\text{mol}
$$

$$
m_{\text{FAME}} = 1724.1 \times 290 = 499{,}989\,\text{g} \approx 500\,\text{kg}
$$

$$
\boxed{m_{\text{FAME}} \approx 500\,\text{kg}}
$$

**Step 6 — Mass of glycerol (1 mark):**

$$
m_{\text{glycerol}} = 574.71 \times 92 = 52{,}873\,\text{g} \approx 52.9\,\text{kg}
$$

$$
\boxed{m_{\text{glycerol}} \approx 52.9\,\text{kg}}
$$

**Mass Balance Check:** 500 kg oil + 165.5 kg methanol → 500 kg FAME + 52.9 kg glycerol + 6.0 kg catalyst loss ≈ 665.5 kg ≈ input (within 0.5 % rounding).

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4, Model]**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Explain the **feedstock generations** for biodiesel production. Compare **1st generation and 3rd generation** feedstocks in terms of yield, land use, food-vs-fuel conflict, and technological readiness.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** Discuss the **properties of biodiesel** with reference to cetane number, viscosity, flash point, and energy density. Compare these with petro-diesel and state the **ASTM D6751** specifications.

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

**Part (a) Solution — Feedstock Generations (7 marks):**

**Generations Overview (3 marks):**

| Generation | Source | Example |
|---|---|---|
| 1st | Edible vegetable oils | Soybean, sunflower, palm, rapeseed |
| 2nd | Non-edible / waste | Jatropha, Karanja, WCO, tallow |
| 3rd | Microalgae | Chlorella, Spirulina, Nannochloropsis |
| 4th | Engineered / synthetic | GM microbes, synthetic biology |

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Criterion | 1st Generation | 3rd Generation |
|---|---|---|
| Yield (L/ha) | 500–2,500 | 5,000–15,000 (10–20× higher) |
| Land Use | Conflicts with food crops | Can use non-arable land, ponds |
| Food vs Fuel | **MAJOR conflict** | No conflict |
| Tech Readiness (TRL) | TRL 9 (mature) | TRL 5–6 (pilot/demonstration) |
| Cost | Competitive (subsidized) | High (~$5–10/L currently) |
| CO₂ Lifecycle | ~50–60 % reduction | ~80–90 % reduction |

**Conclusion:** 1st generation is commercially dominant today (Brazil, USA, EU), but **3rd generation microalgae is the long-term sustainable path** because of its enormous per-hectare productivity and absence of food-vs-fuel conflict.

---

**Part (b) Solution — Biodiesel Properties & ASTM D6751 (7 marks):**

**Key Properties Comparison (4 marks):**

| Property | Biodiesel (B100) | Petro-diesel | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cetane Number | 45–65 | 40–55 | Higher CN → better cold-start, smoother combustion |
| Viscosity @ 40 °C (cSt) | 3.5–5.0 | 2.0–4.5 | Closer to diesel → no injector coking |
| Flash Point (°C) | 120–170 | 55–65 | **Much safer** to store & transport |
| Energy Density (MJ/kg) | ~37 | ~43 | ~14 % lower → ~6 % lower mileage on volume basis |
| Density (kg/m³) | ~880 | ~850 | Slightly heavier |
| Sulfur | ~0 ppm | 10–50 ppm | **Zero SOx emissions** |
| Oxygen | ~10–12 wt % | 0 % | Cleaner combustion, lower PM |

**ASTM D6751 Critical Specifications (3 marks):**

- **Cetane Number:** ≥ 47
- **Viscosity @ 40 °C:** 1.9–6.0 mm²/s
- **Flash Point:** ≥ 130 °C (closed cup)
- **Water & Sediment:** ≤ 0.05 % vol
- **Acid Value:** ≤ 0.5 mg KOH/g
- **Free Glycerol:** ≤ 0.02 % mass
- **Total Glycerol:** ≤ 0.24 % mass
- **Oxidation Stability (Rancimat):** ≥ 3 hours
- **Sulfated Ash:** ≤ 0.02 % mass

**Conclusion:** Biodiesel offers superior cetane, flash point, and sulfur profile, at the cost of slightly lower energy density — making it ideal as a **blending stock (B5, B20)** in existing diesel engines without modification.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 1–2 marks each):**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by 3:** When calculating FAME moles, students often write $n_{\text{FAME}} = n_{\text{oil}}$ instead of $3 \times n_{\text{oil}}$. This single error causes an answer to be off by **3×** — instant 0 marks in the numerical part.
> 2. **Mixing mass ratio and molar ratio:** "9:1 methanol to oil" almost always means **molar ratio**, not mass ratio. Read the problem carefully!
> 3. **Forgetting catalyst mass:** Students often quote volume of methanol but forget the NaOH/KOH mass — both are asked.
> 4. **Confusing saponification value with acid value:** SV measures total ester content (used for MW estimation); AV measures free fatty acid content (used to decide pretreatment).
> 5. **Forgetting the two-step process for FFA > 2 %:** If a numerical problem mentions FFA = 4 %, students must mention **acid pretreatment BEFORE base transesterification** — or they will lose the process-design mark.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision bullets for KTU exam — Bio Diesel:**

- **Biodiesel = FAME** (Fatty Acid Methyl Ester) — mono-alkyl ester of long-chain fatty acids (ASTM D6751 / EN 14214).
- **Transesterification** = triglyceride + 3 methanol → 3 FAME + glycerol, catalyzed by NaOH/KOH at **60–65 °C**.
- **Industrial molar ratio = 6:1 to 12:1** (NOT 3:1 — excess methanol drives equilibrium forward).
- **FFA > 2 % ⇒ Two-step process** (acid esterification FIRST, then base transesterification) — otherwise soap forms.
- **Catalyst loading = 0.5–1 wt %** of oil mass for base catalysts; **~5 wt %** for acid catalysts.
- **Saponification Value (SV)** = 56.1 × N × V / m; indicates average molecular weight (higher MW ⇒ lower SV).
- **Iodine Value (IV)** measures unsaturation — high IV ⇒ poor oxidation stability and lower cetane number.
- **Acid Value (AV)** measures free fatty acids — high AV ⇒ soap risk, needs pretreatment.
- **Biodiesel properties:** Viscosity ~3.5–5.0 cSt, Flash Point > 130 °C, Cetane > 47, Energy density ~37 MJ/kg.
- **B100** = 100 % biodiesel; **B20** = 20 % biodiesel + 80 % diesel (most common blend in fleets); **B5** = typical EU retail blend.
- **Generation hierarchy:** 1st (edible) → 2nd (non-edible/waste) → 3rd (algae — highest yield 5,000–15,000 L/ha) → 4th (synthetic biology).
- **Advantages:** Renewable, biodegradable, lower CO/PM/SOx, high cetane, high flash point, zero sulfur.
- **Disadvantages:** ~14 % lower energy density, NOx slightly higher, cold-flow issues (cloud point ~0 °C for soy), hygroscopic (water absorption).
- **Glycerol by-product** (~10 % by mass) is saleable to pharma industry if purified to USP grade — improves project economics.
- **Mass-balance equation:** $m_{\text{oil}} + m_{\text{MeOH}} = m_{\text{FAME}} + m_{\text{glycerol}} + \text{losses}$ — closure must be checked.
- **Key Kerala/Indian context:** National Policy on Biofuels targets **B5 by 2030**; jatropha and waste cooking oil are priority feedstocks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biogas Production from Waste Biomass
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT862 - RENEWABLE ENERGY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Biogas Production from Waste Biomass # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Biogas Production from Waste Biomass** is defined as the **biochemical conversion of organic waste materials into a combustible gas mixture (primarily methane and carbon dioxide)** through a process called **Anaerobic Digestion (AD)** — a series of microbial reactions occurring in the **complete absence of oxygen**, mediated by a synergistic consortium of hydrolytic, acidogenic, acetogenic, and methanogenic archaea. The gaseous end-product (**biogas**) typically contains **55–70% CH₄**, **30–40% CO₂**, and trace amounts of H₂S, NH₃, and water vapor, with a calorific value in the range of **20–26 MJ/m³** (≈ **5500–7200 kcal/m³**). The solid–liquid residue is termed **digestate**, a nutrient-rich biofertilizer. | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Anaerobic Digestion (AD)** | Microbial decomposition of organics without O₂ | | **Biogas** | Combustible gas from AD (CH₄ + CO₂) | | **Digestate** | Liquid/solid effluent from the digester | | **Volatile Solids (VS)** | Organic fraction of Total Solids (TS) | | **HRT** | Hydraulic Retention Time (days) | | **OLR** | Organic Loading Rate (kg VS/m³·day) | ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Cow's Stomach is a Biogas Plant" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-world analogy:** Consider a **cow's rumen** or a **stagnant pond bottom** covered with sludge. When organic matter (grass, food scraps, manure) is sealed underwater and left untouched, it does **not rot in the normal aerobic manner** (which produces CO₂ and heat). Instead, microbes slowly break it down and bubbles of combustible gas rise to the surface. **A biogas digester is simply an engineered, sealed, temperature-controlled, stirred version of a cow's stomach** — designed to capture and use those gas bubbles for cooking, electricity, or vehicle fuel. A simple comparison clarifies the idea: | Natural System | Engineered Equivalent | |---|---| | Cow's rumen | Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR) | | Lake bottom sludge | Covered Lagoon Digester | | Landfill gas vents | Sanitary Landfill with gas recovery | | Paddy soil methanogenesis | Fixed-Dome Biogas Plant | ## 1.3 Substrates Used in Biogas Production > [!NOTE] > Any **biodegradable organic matter** with a moisture content > 60% and balanced C/N ratio can serve as feedstock. | Category | Examples | Typical VS (% of TS) | Methane Yield (m³ CH₄/kg VS) | |---|---|---|---| | **Agricultural residue** | Paddy straw, bagasse, corn stover | 85–95 | 0.20–0.35 | | **Animal manure** | Cattle dung, pig slurry, poultry litter | 75–85 | 0.15–0.30 | | **Food waste** | Kitchen waste, vegetable market waste | 85–95 | 0.30–0.50 | | **Sewage sludge** | Primary/secondary sludge | 60–75 | 0.20–0.30 | | **MSW (organic fraction)** | Market waste, garden waste | 50–80 | 0.25–0.40 | | **Energy crops** | Maize silage, Napier grass | 90–95 | 0.30–0.45 | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Biological Stages of Anaerobic Digestion > [!IMPORTANT] > The AD process is **not a single reaction** but a tightly coupled, four-stage microbial food chain. Failure at any stage collapses the entire system. ### Stage 1 — Hydrolysis - **Function:** Complex biopolymers (carbohydrates, proteins, lipids) are broken into monomers (sugars, amino acids, fatty acids). - **Microorganisms:** *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*, *Streptococcus*, aerobic-facultative bacteria. - **Rate-limiting for:** Lignocellulosic waste (e.g., paddy straw) due to lignin sheath. - **Time scale:** Hours to several days. ### Stage 2 — Acidogenesis (Fermentation) - **Function:** Monomers are converted into volatile fatty acids (VFAs) like acetate, propionate, butyrate, plus H₂, CO₂, and alcohols. - **Microorganisms:** *Clostridium*, *Bacteroides*, *Bacillus*. - **Critical pH window:** **6.5 – 7.5** (drop in pH = sour digester). ### Stage 3 — Acetogenesis - **Function:** VFAs and alcohols are oxidized to acetate, H₂, and CO₂ by **obligate proton reducers**. - **Microorganisms:** *Syntrophobacter wolinii*, *Syntrophomonas*. - **Thermodynamic constraint:** Requires extremely low H₂ partial pressure (≤ 10⁻⁴ atm) — therefore syntrophic partnership with methanogens is essential. ### Stage 4 — Methanogenesis - **Function:** Acetate, H₂, and CO₂ are converted to CH₄. - **Two main pathways:** - **Acetoclastic (~70%):** CH₃COOH → CH₄ + CO₂ - **Hydrogenotrophic (~30%):** CO₂ + 4H₂ → CH₄ + 2H₂O - **Microorganisms:** Strict anaerobes — *Methanosarcina*, *Methanosaeta*, *Methanobacterium*. > [!NOTE] > **Why is the process so slow?** Because methanogens have a doubling time of **3–30 days** — they are the slowest growers. This is why digesters are designed for long HRTs (15–40 days) and why temperature shocks are devastating. ## 2.2 Critical Operating Parameters | Parameter | Mesophilic Range | Thermophilic Range | Effect of Deviation | |---|---|---|---| | **Temperature** | **30 – 40 °C** (optimum ≈ 37 °C) | **50 – 60 °C** (optimum ≈ 55 °C) | Sudden shift > 2 °C/day kills methanogens | | **pH** | **6.5 – 7.5** | **6.5 – 7.5** | Below 6.0 = acid crash | | **C/N ratio** | **20 : 1 to 30 : 1** | Same | High → low NH₃; Low → NH₃ toxicity | | **HRT** | 15 – 30 days | 10 – 20 days | Shorter HRT = washout of methanogens | | **OLR** | 1 – 4 kg VS/m³·day | 2 – 6 kg VS/m³·day | Higher → VFA accumulation | | **Toxicity thresholds** | NH₃ < 1700 mg/L; H₂S < 200 mg/L | Same | Excess = inhibition | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield) > [!NOTE] > Master these equations — Buswell equation and methane yield formula are **guaranteed KTU exam questions** in this module. $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{1. General Buswell Equation:} \quad C_n H_a O_b + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right)H_2O \;\longrightarrow\; \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right)CO_2 + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right)CH_4 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{2. Theoretical Methane Yield (BMP estimate):} \quad B_{0,CH_4} = \frac{(n/2 + a/8 - b/4) \times 22{,}415}{12n + a + 16b} \quad \left[\frac{L\,CH_4}{g\,VS}\right] \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{3. Practical Daily Methane Production:} \quad Q_{CH_4} = M \times TS \times VS_{frac} \times B_{0,CH_4} \times \eta_{digester} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{4. Energy Content of Biogas:} \quad E_{biogas} = Q_{biogas} \times CV_{biogas}, \qquad CV_{biogas} = 0.55 \times 35.88 + 0.45 \times 0 \approx 19.7 \text{ MJ/m}^3 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{5. Calorific Value of Pure Methane (HHV):} \quad CV_{CH_4} = 55.5 \text{ MJ/kg} = 39.7 \text{ MJ/m}^3 \text{ (NTP)} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \textbf{6. Digester Volume (CSTR design):} \quad V_{digester} = Q_{feed} \times HRT = \frac{Q_{feed} \times OLR_{design}}{VS_{feed}} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > In the Buswell equation, **n, a, b** are the stoichiometric coefficients of the empirical formula of the substrate. The factor **22,415 mL/mol** is the molar volume of an ideal gas at NTP (0 °C, 1 atm). ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Description | |---|---| | **Rural household cooking** | 1–4 m³ floating-dome plants in India (e.g., KVIC model) | | **Industrial electricity** | 0.5–5 MW plants co-digesting agri-residue + manure | | **Vehicle fuel (CBG)** | Compressed Biogas — 95–98% CH₄ upgraded from raw biogas | | **Municipal waste management** | Decentralized AD plants integrated with MSW processing | | **Cold storage / refrigeration** | Biogas-fired ammonia absorption systems (rural India) | | **Effluent treatment** | Anaerobic digesters as tertiary wastewater treatment | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Buswell Equation from First Principles > [!NOTE] > The Buswell equation gives the **stoichiometric maximum methane potential** of any organic compound with empirical formula C_n H_a O_b. Derivation assumes complete mineralization with H₂O as the only co-reactant and CO₂ + CH₄ as the only products. ### Step 1 — Set up the general reaction $$ \begin{aligned} C_n H_a O_b + \alpha H_2O \;\longrightarrow\; \beta CO_2 + \gamma CH_4 \end{aligned} $$ where α, β, γ are the unknown stoichiometric coefficients to be determined. ### Step 2 — Apply the three atom balances **Carbon balance** (input C = output C): $$ \begin{aligned} n = \beta + \gamma \end{aligned} $$ **Hydrogen balance** (input H = output H): $$ \begin{aligned} a + 2\alpha = 4\gamma \end{aligned} $$ **Oxygen balance** (input O = output O): $$ \begin{aligned} b + \alpha = 2\beta \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Solve the linear system for α, β, γ From the **Oxygen balance**: $$ \begin{aligned} \beta = \frac{b + \alpha}{2} \end{aligned} $$ Substituting into the **Carbon balance**: $$ \begin{aligned} \gamma = n - \beta = n - \frac{b + \alpha}{2} \end{aligned} $$ Substituting both into the **Hydrogen balance**: $$ \begin{aligned} a + 2\alpha &= 4\left(n - \frac{b + \alpha}{2}\right) \\ a + 2\alpha &= 4n - 2b - 2\alpha \\ 4\alpha &= 4n - 2b - a \\ \alpha &= n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Back-substitute α to get β and γ $$ \begin{aligned} \beta = \frac{b + (n - a/4 - b/2)}{2} = \frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \gamma = n - \beta = \frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Final generalized Buswell equation $$ \begin{aligned} C_n H_a O_b + \left(n - \frac{a}{4} - \frac{b}{2}\right)H_2O \;\longrightarrow\; \left(\frac{n}{2} - \frac{a}{8} + \frac{b}{4}\right)CO_2 + \left(\frac{n}{2} + \frac{a}{8} - \frac{b}{4}\right)CH_4 \end{aligned} $$ ## 3.2 Worked Example — Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) Substitute n = 6, a = 12, b = 6: $$ \begin{aligned} \alpha &= 6 - \frac{12}{4} - \frac{6}{2} = 6 - 3 - 3 = 0 \\ \beta &= \frac{6}{2} - \frac{12}{8} + \frac{6}{4} = 3 - 1.5 + 1.5 = 3 \\ \gamma &= 3 + 1.5 - 1.5 = 3 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} C_6 H_{12} O_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 3 CO_2 + 3 CH_4 \end{aligned} $$ Maximum methane volume at NTP = **3 × 22.4 = 67.2 L CH₄ per 180 g glucose = 0.373 L CH₄ per g glucose**. ## 3.3 Worked Example — Cellulose (C₆H₁₀O₅) Substitute n = 6, a = 10, b = 5: $$ \begin{aligned} \alpha &= 6 - \frac{10}{4} - \frac{5}{2} = 6 - 2.5 - 2.5 = 1 \\ \beta &= 3 - \frac{10}{8} + \frac{5}{4} = 3 - 1.25 + 1.25 = 3 \\ \gamma &= 3 + 1.25 - 1.25 = 3 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} C_6 H_{10} O_5 + H_2O \;\longrightarrow\; 3 CO_2 + 3 CH_4 \end{aligned} $$ Theoretical yield = **3 × 22.4 = 67.2 L CH₄ per 162 g cellulose = 0.415 L CH₄ per g cellulose**. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Theoretical CH₄ Potential of Common Substrates | Substrate | Empirical Formula | Mol. Wt. (g/mol) | CH₄ moles per mole | CH₄ yield (L/g VS at NTP) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Glucose | C₆H₁₂O₆ | 180 | 3 | 0.373 | | Cellulose | C₆H₁₀O₅ | 162 | 3 | 0.415 | | Starch | C₆H₁₀O₅ | 162 | 3 | 0.415 | | Protein (avg.) | C₅H₇NO₂ | 113 | 2.5 | 0.496 | | Fat (glyceryl trioleate) | C₅₇H₁₀₄O₆ | 884 | 52 | 1.318 | | Acetic acid | C₂H₄O₂ | 60 | 1 | 0.373 | | Cattle dung (approx.) | C₁₀H₁₅NO₆ | 245 | 4.0 | 0.366 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fats/lipids give the highest methane yield** (~3× that of carbohydrates) but are slow to hydrolyze and inhibit methanogens at high loadings. **Proteins are fast but release ammonia** — another inhibitor at > 2500 mg/L NH₃-N. ## 3.5 Numerical Practice — Daily Methane Production **Problem:** A dairy farm produces **800 kg/day of fresh cattle dung** with **18% total solids** and **80% volatile solids**. The digester operates with **η = 0.65** of theoretical yield. Calculate daily CH₄ production and energy content. **Step 1:** Volatile solids fed per day: $$ \begin{aligned} M_{VS} = 800 \times 0.18 \times 0.80 = 115.2 \text{ kg VS/day} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Theoretical CH₄ yield (using cattle dung value 0.366 L/g): $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{CH_4,th} = 115{,}200 \times 0.366 = 42{,}163 \text{ L/day} = 42.16 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Actual CH₄ (with 65% efficiency): $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{CH_4,act} = 42.16 \times 0.65 = 27.4 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Biogas volume (assuming 60% CH₄ in biogas): $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{biogas} = \frac{27.4}{0.60} = 45.7 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** Energy content per day (CV ≈ 21.5 MJ/m³ for typical biogas): $$ \begin{aligned} E_{daily} = 45.7 \times 21.5 = 982.6 \text{ MJ/day} \approx 273 \text{ kWh/day} \end{aligned} $$ This is sufficient to continuously run a **3–5 kW generator** and supply **40–50 rural households' cooking needs** (≈ 0.8 m³ biogas/day per family). # 4.
